passages.title: How Water Changes Rock
passages.text:
1. Have you ever noticed smooth rocks in a stream or deep canyons carved through the land? Water is one of the most powerful forces of weathering on Earth. Although water may seem gentle, it can slowly change Earth's surface over long periods of time.

2. One way water causes weathering is through a process called ice wedging. Water enters small cracks in rocks. When temperatures drop below freezing, the water turns into ice and expands. This expansion pushes against the sides of the crack. After many cycles of freezing and thawing, the rock can split apart.

3. Flowing water also contributes to weathering. Rivers and streams carry sand, pebbles, and other particles. As these materials move downstream, they scrape against rocks and gradually wear them down. This process can smooth rough surfaces and shape valleys and canyons.

4. Rainwater can cause chemical weathering as well. When rainwater mixes with gases in the atmosphere, it forms a weak acid. This acidic water can slowly dissolve certain types of rock, including limestone. Over thousands of years, chemical weathering can create caves and other unique landforms.

5. Water-related weathering helps form soil and shape landscapes around the world. Many of the landforms people see today were influenced by the steady action of water over long periods of time.

passages.title: How Living Things Change Rock
passages.text:
6. Water is not the only cause of weathering. Living things also play an important role in changing Earth's surface. Plants, animals, and even tiny organisms can contribute to weathering in different ways.

7. One common example involves plant roots. Seeds may settle into small cracks in rocks. As the plants grow, their roots expand and push deeper into the cracks. The pressure created by the roots can eventually break the rock into smaller pieces.

8. Animals can also contribute to weathering. Burrowing animals such as rabbits, prairie dogs, and moles dig tunnels beneath the ground. Their digging can expose rocks to air and water, increasing the rate at which weathering occurs.

9. Some living organisms cause chemical weathering. For example, certain plants and lichens release weak acids that slowly break down rock surfaces. These organisms help create small pieces of rock that can later become part of the soil.

10. Although living things may seem less powerful than rivers or storms, their effects can be significant. Over many years, plants and animals help shape Earth's surface and contribute to the formation of soil and landforms.

questions.text:

passages.title: Erosion Along the River
passages.text:
1. Rivers are constantly changing the landscapes through which they flow. One of the ways they do this is through erosion. Erosion is the process by which water, wind, ice, or gravity moves weathered rock and soil from one place to another.

2. As river water flows downstream, it carries sand, gravel, and small rocks. These materials scrape against the riverbed and banks, slowly wearing them away. Over many years, this process can make a river wider and deeper.

3. In some places, rivers carve valleys and canyons through solid rock. The Grand Canyon in Arizona is one of the most famous examples of a landform shaped by erosion. Scientists believe that the Colorado River gradually cut through layers of rock over millions of years.

4. Erosion can occur more quickly during floods. Fast-moving water has enough energy to pick up larger rocks and carry them downstream. After major storms, people may notice changes in the shape of riverbanks or stream channels.

5. Although erosion removes material from one location, it is only part of the story. The materials carried away by water eventually settle somewhere else. This process is called deposition.

passages.title: Building New Land Through Deposition
passages.text:
6. Deposition occurs when water, wind, ice, or gravity drops sediments in a new location. As moving water slows down, it loses energy and can no longer carry all the materials it once transported.

7. Rivers often deposit sand, soil, and gravel along their banks or near their mouths. Over time, these sediments can build fertile floodplains where plants grow well. Many farming communities are located near floodplains because the soil is rich in nutrients.

8. Deltas are another example of deposition. A delta forms when a river empties into a larger body of water, such as a lake or ocean. As the river slows, it drops sediment that gradually builds new land. The Mississippi River Delta is one of the largest deltas in North America.

9. Wind can also cause deposition. In deserts and along beaches, wind carries sand from one place to another. When the wind slows, the sand settles and forms dunes. These dunes may continue to grow and change shape over time.

10. Deposition helps create new landforms and enrich soil. While erosion removes material from Earth's surface, deposition builds up Earth's surface in other places. Together, these processes constantly reshape the planet.

questions.text:
Read this sentence from paragraph 6: "As moving water slows down, it loses energy and can no longer carry all the materials it once transported." Based on how it is used in paragraph 6, the word "transported" most nearly means [ ▾ ].

broke into smaller pieces

B  Deltas are the only landform created when rivers slow down and stop moving.

As you write your response: use your own words to explain your opinion; use evidence from the passages to support your points; and use clear language to express your opinion by making connections between your points and your evidence. Be sure to: include an introduction; create organized paragraphs that focus on your opinion; pay attention to the grammar, structure, and mechanics of your sentences; and include a conclusion. passages.title: Does Matter Disappear?
passages.text:
1. Imagine crushing a cracker into crumbs, watching an ice cube melt into water, or stirring sugar into a glass of lemonade. At first, it may seem as though some of the material disappears during these changes. However, scientists have discovered that matter does not simply vanish. Instead, matter is conserved, which means the total amount of matter remains the same even when it changes form.

2. Matter is anything that has mass and takes up space. Everything around us, including air, water, rocks, plants, and people, is made of matter. Matter can change in many ways, but the amount of matter stays constant unless some of it is added or removed from the system being observed.

3. One example of matter conservation occurs when an object breaks into smaller pieces. Suppose a large rock is struck with a hammer and breaks into several smaller rocks. The rock may look different after it breaks apart, but all the pieces together have the same total amount of matter as the original rock. The matter has simply been divided into smaller parts.

4. A similar process happens when food is crushed. If a cracker is broken into crumbs, the crumbs may be scattered across a table, making it seem as though the cracker has changed completely. However, if all the crumbs are collected and measured, they would have the same mass as the original cracker.

5. Matter is also conserved when substances dissolve. For example, when sugar is stirred into water, the sugar seems to disappear. In reality, the sugar particles spread throughout the water and become too small to see easily. The sugar is still present, even though it is no longer visible. Scientists can prove this by allowing the water to evaporate. Once the water is gone, sugar crystals remain.

6. Changes of state provide another example of matter conservation. Water can exist as a solid, liquid, or gas. When an ice cube melts, it changes from a solid to a liquid. When water boils, it changes from a liquid to a gas called water vapor. Although the appearance of the water changes, the amount of matter remains the same.

7. Sometimes people think matter disappears during evaporation because the liquid can no longer be seen. However, the water vapor remains in the air. If the vapor cools, it can condense back into liquid water. This process demonstrates that the matter was present the entire time.

8. Understanding matter conservation helps scientists explain many natural processes. It allows them to study how materials change while recognizing that the total amount of matter remains constant. Whether matter is broken into smaller pieces, dissolved, melted, frozen, evaporated, or condensed, the matter itself is not destroyed.

9. By carefully observing changes in matter, scientists have learned that appearances can sometimes be misleading. A substance may look different after a change, but the total amount of matter remains conserved. This important scientific principle helps explain how the physical world works.

questions.text:
What is the central idea of the passage?
A  Matter is anything that has mass and takes up space, such as air, water, rocks, and people.
B  Even when matter changes its form or appearance, the total amount of matter stays the same.
C  When sugar is stirred into water, the sugar particles spread out and become too small to see.
D  Scientists can make water evaporate in order to prove that sugar crystals are still present.

Read this sentence from paragraph 1: 'Instead, matter is conserved, which means the total amount of matter remains the same even when it changes form.' In this sentence, the word 'conserved' means [ ▾ ].
kept the same in amount
made into something new
broken into smaller pieces
removed from a system

In paragraph 5, how does the author support the point that sugar is still present after it dissolves in water?
A  By explaining that the sugar particles spread out and become too small to see easily.
B  By stating that matter has mass and takes up space.
C  By describing how the water can evaporate and leave sugar crystals behind.
D  By comparing dissolving sugar to crushing a cracker into crumbs.

The passage explains that the appearance of matter can be misleading. Select two details from the passage that show matter still exists even when it looks like it has disappeared.
A  'all the pieces together have the same total amount of matter as the original rock' (paragraph 3)
B  'Everything around us, including air, water, rocks, plants, and people, is made of matter' (paragraph 2)

passages.title: The Journey of Energy
passages.text:
1. Every day, people use energy without giving much thought to where it comes from or where it goes. Energy allows lights to shine, vehicles to move, devices to operate, and living things to survive. Although energy may appear in many forms, it follows an important rule: energy can be transferred from one location to another or transformed from one form into another.

2. Energy transfer occurs when energy moves between objects or places. For example, imagine holding a metal spoon in a cup of hot soup. After a few minutes, the handle of the spoon becomes warm. Thermal energy, often called heat energy, has transferred from the hot soup to the cooler spoon. The energy moved from one object to another without changing its form.

3. Another example of energy transfer can be observed when a person kicks a soccer ball. The player's muscles provide energy to the foot, and the moving foot transfers energy to the ball. As a result, the ball moves across the field. In this case, energy is transferred from one object to another through motion.

4. Energy can also move through electrical systems. When a lamp is plugged into an outlet and switched on, electrical energy travels through wires to the lamp. The energy is transferred from the power source to the device, allowing it to function.

5. In addition to transferring energy, many processes transform energy from one form into another. A transformation occurs when energy changes form while still being conserved. One common example is found in flashlights.

6. A flashlight contains batteries that store chemical energy. When the flashlight is turned on, the chemical energy is transformed into electrical energy. The electrical energy then transforms into light energy and a small amount of thermal energy. Although the forms of energy change, the total amount of energy remains conserved.

7. Solar panels provide another example of energy transformation. Sunlight contains radiant energy, which travels from the Sun to Earth. Solar panels capture this radiant energy and transform it into electrical energy that can power homes, schools, and businesses.

8. Plants also depend on energy transformations. During photosynthesis, plants absorb sunlight and transform radiant energy into chemical energy stored in sugars. The stored energy helps plants grow and provides energy for animals and people that consume plant-based foods.

9. Many devices rely on both energy transfer and energy transformation. Consider a smartphone. Electrical energy from a charger is transferred to the phone's battery. The battery stores chemical energy. Later, when the phone is used, the chemical energy transforms into electrical energy, which powers the screen, speakers, and other components.

10. Understanding energy transfer and transformation helps scientists explain how the world works. Whether energy is warming a spoon, moving a soccer ball, powering a flashlight, or helping plants grow, energy is constantly changing location or form. These processes make many everyday activities possible and demonstrate the important role energy plays in our lives.

questions.text:
What is the central idea of the passage?
A  Energy is always changing location or form, and these processes make many everyday activities possible.
B  Thermal energy moves from hot objects to cooler objects, such as from soup to a metal spoon.
C  Flashlights and smartphones both use batteries that store chemical energy until it is needed.
D  Plants are the only living things that can transform radiant energy from the Sun into another form.

The passage describes how a flashlight works in paragraph 6. Select two statements that correctly explain the order of energy changes in a flashlight.
A  Chemical energy stored in the batteries is transformed into electrical energy first.
B  Light energy is transformed back into chemical energy that recharges the batteries.
C  The electrical energy is then transformed into light energy and a small amount of thermal energy.
D  Thermal energy from the room is transferred into the batteries to start the process.
E  The total amount of energy is lost as the flashlight changes the forms of energy.

Read this sentence from paragraph 6: 'Although the forms of energy change, the total amount of energy remains conserved.' In this sentence, the word 'conserved' means the total amount of energy stays [ ▾ ].
the same

In paragraph 9, the author uses the example of a smartphone mainly to support which point?
A  Many devices rely on both energy transfer and energy transformation.
B  Electrical energy can only travel through wires to reach a device.
C  Chemical energy is the most powerful form of energy that exists.
D  Smartphones use more energy than flashlights or solar panels do.

passages.title: Where Did the Sugar Go?
passages.text:
1. Have you ever stirred sugar into a glass of water and watched it disappear? At first, it may seem as though the sugar is gone forever. However, scientists know that the sugar is still present. This observation helps demonstrate an important scientific principle called the conservation of matter.

2. Matter is anything that has mass and takes up space. According to the law of conservation of matter, matter is not created or destroyed during physical changes. Instead, it may change form or location while the total amount of matter remains the same.

3. When sugar is added to water, the sugar crystals break apart into tiny particles. These particles spread evenly throughout the liquid. Because the particles become so small and dispersed, they can no longer be seen with the naked eye.

4. Even though the sugar is invisible, it remains in the water. One way scientists can demonstrate this is by allowing the water to evaporate. As the liquid water changes into water vapor and enters the air, the sugar remains behind. Eventually, sugar crystals can be observed again.

5. Scientists have measured the mass of water and sugar before and after dissolving. Their results show that the total amount of matter stays the same. Dissolving changes the appearance of the materials, but it does not cause matter to disappear.

6. Understanding dissolving helps scientists study many natural and human-made processes. From making beverages to understanding how minerals dissolve in water, the conservation of matter remains an important concept.

passages.title: The Changing States of Water
passages.text:
7. Water provides another example of matter conservation. Unlike dissolving, this example involves changes of state. Matter can exist as a solid, liquid, or gas. Water is one of the few substances that people commonly observe in all three states.

8. When an ice cube melts, it changes from a solid into a liquid. The shape and appearance of the water change, but the amount of matter remains the same. No new matter is created, and none is lost during the melting process.

9. Water can also change from a liquid into a gas through evaporation. On a warm day, a puddle may seem to disappear after several hours. However, the water has not vanished. Instead, it has become water vapor and mixed with the air.

10. Later, water vapor in the atmosphere can cool and condense into liquid water. This process forms clouds, dew, and rain. Throughout these changes, the same water particles continue to exist even though their state changes.

11. Scientists often use sealed containers to study changes of state. When they measure the total mass before and after melting, freezing, evaporation, or condensation, they find that the amount of matter remains constant.

12. Changes of state occur throughout nature every day. These processes provide strong evidence that matter is conserved, even when substances appear very different from their original forms.

questions.text:
Read this sentence from paragraph 9: "On a warm day, a puddle may seem to disappear after several hours. However, the water has not vanished." In this sentence, the word vanished means most nearly [ ▾ ].
gone completely away

soaked into the ground

What is the central idea shared by both Passage 1 and Passage 2?

B  Water is the only substance that can exist as a solid, liquid, or gas.
C  Sugar crystals can always be seen with the naked eye in water.
D  Scientists prefer studying dissolving over studying changes of state.

Both passages use evaporation as part of their explanations. Select two ways evaporation is used in BOTH Passage 1 and Passage 2.

B  Evaporation is used to show that matter remains even when it cannot be seen.
C  Evaporation creates brand-new matter that did not exist before.

In Passage 2, what reason does the author give to support the claim that matter is conserved during changes of state?

C  Clouds, dew, and rain form when water vapor cools and condenses.
D  An ice cube changes its shape and appearance when it melts.

As you write your response: use your own words to explain your opinion; use evidence from the passages to support your points; and use clear language to express your opinion by making connections between your points and your evidence. Be sure to: include an introduction; create organized paragraphs that focus on your opinion; pay attention to the grammar, structure, and mechanics of your sentences; and include a conclusion. passages.title: Energy on the Move
passages.text:
1. Every day, energy moves from one place to another. This movement is called energy transfer. Without energy transfer, many of the activities people rely on would not be possible. Energy transfer occurs whenever energy travels between objects or locations.

2. One common example involves thermal energy. Imagine placing a metal spoon into a bowl of hot soup. After a few minutes, the handle of the spoon becomes warm. Thermal energy has transferred from the hot soup to the cooler spoon. The energy itself remains thermal energy, but it moves from one object to another.

3. Energy transfer can also occur through motion. When a baseball player hits a ball with a bat, energy is transferred from the moving bat to the ball. As a result, the ball travels across the field. The amount of energy transferred helps determine how far the ball will move.

4. Electrical systems depend on energy transfer as well. In a home, electrical energy travels through wires from a power source to appliances. When a television is turned on, energy is transferred through the electrical system to the device, allowing it to operate.

5. Scientists study energy transfer because it helps explain how objects interact. Whether energy is warming a spoon, moving a ball, or powering an appliance, energy transfer plays an important role in everyday life.

passages.title: Energy Changes Form
passages.text:
6. While energy is often transferred from one place to another, it can also change from one form to another. This process is called energy transformation. Energy transformations occur in countless natural and human-made systems.

7. A flashlight provides a useful example. Batteries store chemical energy. When the flashlight is switched on, the chemical energy changes into electrical energy. The electrical energy is then transformed into light energy and a small amount of thermal energy.

8. Solar panels also depend on energy transformation. Sunlight contains radiant energy that travels from the Sun to Earth. Solar panels capture this energy and transform it into electrical energy that can be used in homes and businesses.

9. Plants perform an important energy transformation through photosynthesis. They absorb sunlight and transform radiant energy into chemical energy stored in sugars. This stored energy supports plant growth and becomes a source of energy for animals and people.

10. Energy transformations allow many technologies and natural processes to function. By changing energy from one form to another, living things and machines can use energy in ways that meet their needs.

11. Together, energy transfer and energy transformation help explain how energy moves through the world. Sometimes energy changes location, and sometimes it changes form. Both processes are essential to the systems people depend on every day.

questions.text:
Read this sentence from paragraph 2: "Thermal energy has transferred from the hot soup to the cooler spoon." In this sentence, the word transferred means energy [ ▾ ].

What is the central idea of Passage 2, "Energy Changes Form"?
A  Energy can change from one form into another form to be useful.
B  Batteries are the only source of stored chemical energy.
C  Sunlight is the most powerful kind of energy on Earth.
D  Plants and machines both need thermal energy to work.

Select two ideas that are supported by BOTH Passage 1 and Passage 2.
A  Energy plays an important role in everyday life.
B  Energy can involve the Sun's radiant energy.
C  Energy is connected to systems people depend on.
D  Energy is stored inside batteries as chemical energy.
E  Energy always becomes light when it is used.

Based on Passage 2, why does a flashlight need more than one energy change to make light?

B  The flashlight must warm up with thermal energy before it can turn on.
C  The radiant energy from the Sun must reach the batteries first.
D  The light energy must be stored in the batteries before it can shine.

passages.title: Fossils: Clues from the Past
passages.text:
1. Imagine finding the imprint of a leaf in a rock or uncovering the bones of an animal that lived millions of years ago. These preserved remains or traces of ancient organisms are called fossils. Fossils provide important evidence about life on Earth long before humans existed. By studying fossils, scientists can learn what organisms looked like, how they lived, and how they compare to living organisms today.

2. Fossils form in several ways. Some are created when an organism is buried by sediment, such as mud, sand, or volcanic ash. Over time, layers of sediment build up and harden into rock. Minerals may replace parts of the organism, preserving its shape and structure. Other fossils form as impressions, footprints, or tracks left behind by ancient organisms.

3. Scientists often compare fossils to one another. By examining similarities and differences, they can identify patterns and learn how organisms may have changed over time. For example, fossils of ancient horses show that early horses were much smaller than modern horses. They also had different numbers of toes. These comparisons help scientists understand how certain traits changed as environments changed.

4. Fossils can also be compared to present-day organisms. Some fossilized organisms look remarkably similar to living species. Fossils of ancient sharks, for instance, reveal body structures that are similar to sharks swimming in today's oceans. These similarities suggest that some features have remained effective for survival over long periods of time.

5. In other cases, fossils reveal important differences. Scientists have discovered fossils of giant dragonfly-like insects that lived millions of years ago. These insects were much larger than modern dragonflies. By studying these differences, researchers can learn about environmental conditions that existed in the past.

6. Fossils of dinosaurs provide another opportunity for comparison. Some dinosaur fossils contain features that resemble those of modern birds. Scientists have found evidence of feathers on certain dinosaur fossils. These similarities have led many researchers to conclude that birds and some dinosaurs share common characteristics.

7. Comparing fossils and living organisms also helps scientists classify organisms into groups. Characteristics such as body structure, teeth, limbs, and protective coverings can provide clues about how organisms are related. Scientists use these observations to develop explanations about the history of life on Earth.

8. Fossils do not tell scientists everything about ancient organisms, but they provide valuable evidence. Because fossils are often incomplete, researchers combine fossil evidence with information from living organisms to make informed conclusions. New fossil discoveries sometimes lead scientists to revise their understanding of the past.

9. The study of fossils continues to help scientists answer questions about Earth's history. By comparing fossils to one another and to present-day organisms, researchers gain insights into similarities, differences, adaptations, and environmental changes. These comparisons help create a clearer picture of life across millions of years.

questions.text:
What is the central idea of the passage?

B  By comparing fossils to one another and to living organisms, scientists learn about life on Earth.

D  Scientists will never fully understand ancient organisms because fossils are always incomplete.

D  "They also had different numbers of toes." (paragraph 3)
E  "New fossil discoveries sometimes lead scientists to revise their understanding of the past." (paragraph 8)

Be sure to: include an introduction; create organized paragraphs that focus on your opinion; pay attention to the grammar, structure, and mechanics of your sentences; and include a conclusion. passages.title: When Environments Change
passages.text:
1. Every organism depends on its environment to survive. An environment includes all the living and nonliving things in an area, such as plants, animals, water, soil, sunlight, and weather conditions. However, environments do not remain the same forever. Natural events and human activities can cause environmental changes that affect the organisms living there.

2. Some environmental changes are beneficial. A beneficial change helps an organism survive, grow, or reproduce. For example, a period of steady rainfall can increase the amount of water available in an ecosystem. Plants may grow more successfully, providing additional food and shelter for animals. As resources become more abundant, some populations may increase.

3. Forest fires can also create beneficial changes under certain conditions. Although fires may seem entirely destructive, some plants depend on them. The heat from a fire can cause the seeds of certain pine trees to open and release seeds. After the fire, nutrients from burned plants return to the soil, helping new vegetation grow.

4. Changes in temperature can sometimes benefit organisms as well. If a region experiences slightly warmer conditions, certain plants may have a longer growing season. This change can provide more food for herbivores, which may then support larger populations of predators.

5. Not all environmental changes are beneficial. Some changes make survival more difficult. Droughts, for example, reduce the amount of available water. As ponds, streams, and rivers shrink, organisms may struggle to find the resources they need. Plants may die, and animals may be forced to move to new locations.

6. Severe storms can also create harmful environmental changes. Hurricanes, tornadoes, and floods can destroy habitats and remove food sources. Animals may lose shelter, and populations can decline if they are unable to adapt to the new conditions.

7. Human activities sometimes cause harmful environmental changes. Pollution can affect the quality of air, water, and soil. When pollutants enter an ecosystem, plants and animals may become sick or lose access to resources necessary for survival. Habitat destruction can have similar effects by reducing the amount of space available for organisms.

8. Different organisms respond to environmental changes in different ways. Some species adapt by changing their behaviors or moving to new areas. Others may struggle to survive if the changes occur too quickly or are too severe. The ability to respond to environmental changes often affects an organism's chances of survival.

9. Scientists study environmental changes to better understand ecosystems and the organisms that live within them. By examining both beneficial and harmful changes, researchers can learn how environments influence populations and biodiversity.

10. Environmental changes are a natural part of life on Earth. While some changes create new opportunities for organisms, others present challenges. Understanding these effects helps people appreciate the complex relationship between organisms and their environments.

questions.text:
Which sentence BEST states the central idea of the whole passage?

B  Predators increase first, which gives herbivores more food and lengthens the growing season for plants.
C  Herbivores move to new areas, then plants grow longer, and finally predator populations decline.
D  More predators cause plants to have a longer growing season, which then provides food for herbivores.

D  "nutrients from burned plants return to the soil, helping new vegetation grow" (paragraph 3)
E  "certain plants may have a longer growing season" (paragraph 4)

title: A New Pond Brings New Opportunities

passages.title: A New Pond Brings New Opportunities
passages.text:
1. After several weeks of heavy rain, a low area in a grassland filled with water and formed a pond. What had once been a dry field became a new habitat for many organisms. The environmental change quickly affected the plants and animals living nearby.

2. Within a short time, aquatic plants began growing along the edges of the pond. These plants provided food and shelter for insects, fish, and other organisms. Frogs soon appeared and laid eggs in the shallow water.

3. Birds also benefited from the new pond. Species that fed on insects and small aquatic animals visited the area more often. Some birds even built nests nearby because the pond provided a reliable source of food.

4. As the pond ecosystem developed, biodiversity increased. Biodiversity refers to the variety of living things in an area. The presence of water allowed many different species to survive where they could not before.

5. Although some organisms had to adjust to the wetter conditions, many benefited from the environmental change. The new pond created additional resources and opportunities for survival.

6. Scientists often study situations like this to understand how environmental changes can support populations. In this case, the change provided water, food, and shelter that helped many organisms thrive.

passages.title: A Drought Changes the Landscape
passages.text:
7. In another region, an ecosystem experienced a very different environmental change. A drought caused months of unusually dry conditions. Streams became smaller, ponds dried up, and many plants struggled to survive.

8. As water became scarce, animals faced new challenges. Some species traveled long distances in search of water. Others competed more intensely for the limited resources that remained.

9. Plants were also affected by the drought. Without enough water, many plants produced fewer leaves and flowers. Some plants died before they could reproduce. As plant populations decreased, animals that depended on them for food were affected as well.

10. The drought also changed habitats. Areas that once provided shelter became dry and less suitable for many organisms. As a result, some populations declined while others were forced to move to new locations.

11. Scientists observed that the drought created a chain reaction throughout the ecosystem. Changes affecting one group of organisms often influenced many others. The loss of water affected plants, animals, and entire food webs.

12. While some organisms were able to adapt to the dry conditions, many struggled to survive. This example demonstrates how harmful environmental changes can create significant challenges for living things.

questions.text:
Read this sentence from Passage 1: "As the pond ecosystem developed, biodiversity increased. Biodiversity refers to the variety of living things in an area." (paragraph 4) In this passage, the word biodiversity most nearly means the [ ▾ ] in a place.
range of different kinds of living things
amount of water available
number of birds building nests
size of the new habitat

Based on Passage 2, what happened to many animals as a direct result of the drought reducing the water supply?
A  They competed harder for resources or moved to find water.
B  They began eating aquatic plants growing along a pond's edge.
C  They built more nests because food became more reliable.
D  They adapted easily and were not affected by the dry conditions.

The two passages both describe environmental changes, but the changes affect organisms in opposite ways. Select two statements that correctly compare how the changes in Passage 1 and Passage 2 affected living things.
A  In Passage 1 the change increased resources, while in Passage 2 the change reduced resources.
B  In Passage 1 biodiversity increased, while in Passage 2 some populations declined.
C  Both passages show that the environmental change harmed nearly every organism.
D  Both passages show that animals had to travel long distances to find water.
E  In Passage 1 plants died before reproducing, while in Passage 2 new aquatic plants appeared.

Which sentence best states a central idea shared across both passages?
A  A change in an organism's environment can help some living things and harm others.
B  Ponds always form after several weeks of heavy rain in a grassland.
C  Scientists are the only people who can observe changes in ecosystems.
D  Birds are the organisms most affected by any environmental change.

writingPrompt.text: Which environmental change had a greater impact on organisms: the formation of the new pond or the drought? In your own words, write a multi-paragraph response that explains which environmental change had a greater impact on organisms. Include evidence from the passages that supports your opinion. As you write your response: use your own words to explain your opinion; use evidence from the passages to support your points; and use clear language to express your opinion by making connections between your points and your evidence. Be sure to: include an introduction; create organized paragraphs that focus on your opinion; pay attention to the grammar, structure, and mechanics of your sentences; and include a conclusion. Write your multi-paragraph response in the space provided.

title: Fossils from the Age of Dinosaurs

passages.title: Fossils from the Age of Dinosaurs
passages.text:
1. Fossils provide important clues about organisms that lived long ago. By comparing fossils to one another, scientists can learn how organisms may have changed over time. Fossils also help researchers identify similarities and differences among species that lived during different periods of Earth's history.

2. Dinosaur fossils are among the most well-known fossils ever discovered. Scientists have uncovered fossils of dinosaurs ranging from the size of chickens to animals longer than school buses. By studying their bones, researchers can estimate an organism's size, movement, and diet.

3. Some dinosaur fossils reveal surprising similarities. For example, several species possessed lightweight bones, three-toed feet, and specialized forelimbs. Scientists compare these features across different fossils to determine how certain groups of dinosaurs may have been related.

4. Fossils also reveal important differences. Some dinosaurs had sharp teeth for hunting prey, while others had flat teeth better suited for eating plants. Certain species walked on two legs, while others moved on four. These differences help scientists classify organisms into groups and understand how they adapted to different environments.

5. One of the most significant discoveries involved fossils showing evidence of feathers. Scientists found that some dinosaurs possessed feather-like structures long before modern birds appeared. These fossils provided new information about how certain characteristics developed over time.

6. By comparing fossils from different locations and time periods, researchers continue to build a better understanding of ancient life and the relationships among extinct organisms.

passages.title: Comparing Fossils to Living Organisms
passages.text:
7. Fossils do more than reveal information about extinct species. Scientists also compare fossils to present-day organisms. These comparisons help researchers understand how living things may be connected to organisms that existed millions of years ago.

8. Birds provide one example. Modern birds share several characteristics with certain dinosaur fossils, including lightweight bones, similar limb structures, and feathers. These similarities have led scientists to conclude that birds and some dinosaurs share important traits.

9. Sharks offer another interesting comparison. Fossilized shark teeth discovered in ancient rock layers often resemble the teeth of sharks living today. Although some ancient sharks were much larger, many basic features have remained similar for millions of years.

10. Scientists also compare fossilized plants to modern plants. Fossils may show leaf shapes, stem structures, or seed patterns that resemble those found in living species. These observations help researchers understand how plant life has changed and adapted over time.

11. Not all comparisons reveal similarities. Fossils sometimes show characteristics that no longer exist in present-day organisms. These differences provide evidence that environments and populations have changed throughout Earth's history.

12. By comparing fossils to living organisms, scientists gain valuable insights into adaptation, survival, and biodiversity. These comparisons help explain both the continuity and the changes that have occurred in life on Earth.

title: The Northwest Ordinance and the Growth of the United States

passages.title: The Northwest Ordinance and the Growth of the United States
passages.text:
1. After the American Revolution ended in 1783, the United States gained control of a large area of land west of the original thirteen states. Leaders faced an important question: How should this new territory be governed? They needed a system that would allow the nation to grow while maintaining fairness and order.

2. In 1787, Congress passed a law called the Northwest Ordinance. This law established rules for governing the Northwest Territory, a region that included land that would later become the states of Ohio, Indiana, Illinois, Michigan, Wisconsin, and part of Minnesota. The Northwest Ordinance became one of the most important laws in early American history.

3. Before the Northwest Ordinance, there was uncertainty about how new territories would join the nation. Some people worried that territories might remain under the control of existing states or receive fewer rights than other parts of the country. The Northwest Ordinance addressed these concerns by creating a clear process for territories to become states.

4. According to the law, a territory would first be governed by officials appointed by Congress. As the population grew, residents could begin electing representatives to help make decisions. This process allowed people living in the territory to gradually participate in government.

5. Once a territory reached a certain population, it could apply for statehood. If Congress approved the request, the territory would become a state. This process gave territories a clear path toward joining the Union.

6. One of the most significant features of the Northwest Ordinance was that new states would enter the Union on equal footing with the original states. They would have the same rights, responsibilities, and representation as the states that already existed. This reflected the democratic ideal that all states should be treated fairly.

7. The Northwest Ordinance also protected several important rights. It supported freedom of religion, encouraged public education, and provided for trial by jury. These protections reflected democratic principles that many Americans valued.

8. As settlers moved into the Northwest Territory, communities grew and new governments were established. Eventually, five states were created from the territory. The success of this process provided a model that was later used as the United States expanded westward.

9. The Northwest Ordinance demonstrated important democratic ideals. It gave citizens opportunities to participate in government, protected individual rights, and ensured that new states would be equal members of the Union. These ideas helped strengthen the growing nation.

10. Today, historians continue to study the Northwest Ordinance because of its lasting impact. By creating a fair process for territories to become states and by recognizing them as equals, the law helped shape the development of the United States and its democratic system of government.

questions.text:

title: Ohio Innovations That Changed the Nation

passages.title: Ohio Innovations That Changed the Nation
passages.text:
1. Throughout American history, inventors, entrepreneurs, and engineers have created technologies that changed the way people live and work. Many of these important innovations originated in Ohio. From transportation to communication, Ohio inventors helped solve problems and improve daily life for people across the United States.

2. One of the most famous Ohio innovations was the airplane. Wilbur and Orville Wright, often called the Wright brothers, lived and worked in Dayton, Ohio. Although their first successful powered flight took place in North Carolina in 1903, much of the research, design, and testing for their aircraft occurred in Ohio.

3. Before the Wright brothers' invention, people could travel only by land or water. The airplane transformed transportation by allowing people and goods to move quickly over long distances. Today, air travel connects communities, supports businesses, and enables emergency services to reach people in need.

4. Another important Ohio innovation was the cash register. In 1879, James Ritty of Dayton invented the first practical cash register. Ritty wanted a way to accurately record sales and reduce mistakes in his business. His invention helped store owners keep better records and manage their finances more effectively.

5. Over time, cash registers became common in stores across the country. Modern versions now include computers, scanners, and digital payment systems. The original invention helped create technologies that businesses still rely on today.

6. Ohio also played a role in the development of communication technology. In the early twentieth century, Charles Kettering and other inventors worked on technologies that improved automobiles and electrical systems. Their innovations contributed to advances that made transportation and communication more efficient.

7. Another Ohio inventor, Garrett Morgan of Cleveland, developed important safety devices. Among his inventions was an improved traffic signal that helped drivers and pedestrians move more safely through intersections. As automobiles became more common, traffic signals reduced confusion and improved public safety.

8. Morgan also developed a safety hood that protected people from smoke and harmful gases. Versions of this invention were later used by firefighters and rescue workers. His work demonstrated how technology could help save lives.

9. These innovations benefited not only Ohio residents but also people throughout the United States. Airplanes changed travel, cash registers improved business operations, and safety inventions protected communities. Each innovation addressed a need and provided practical solutions.

10. Technological innovation continues today, but many modern advances build upon ideas developed in the past. The inventions that originated in Ohio demonstrate how creativity, problem-solving, and perseverance can lead to changes that affect millions of people.

title: A Letter from the Flood of 1913

passages.title: A Letter from the Flood of 1913
passages.text:
1. Historians learn about the past by studying different types of sources. One type is a primary source. A primary source is a firsthand account created by someone who experienced an event. Letters, diaries, photographs, and newspapers from the time of an event are examples of primary sources.

2. The following is adapted from a letter written by a resident of Dayton, Ohio, after the Great Flood of 1913:

3. "The rain seemed endless. For days, the rivers continued to rise. Soon, water covered streets that were usually busy with wagons and people. Families worked together to move belongings to higher places."

4. "As the floodwaters spread, many people became trapped in their homes. Neighbors used boats to help rescue those in danger. Although the situation was frightening, people cooperated and supported one another."

5. "When the waters finally began to recede, much of the city had been damaged. Roads were covered with mud, and many buildings needed repairs. Still, community members immediately began helping rebuild."

6. This letter provides details from someone who witnessed the flood. It describes what people saw, experienced, and felt during the event. Because it was written by an eyewitness, it serves as a valuable primary source.

Passage 2: Writing History from Multiple Sources

7. Historians often use primary sources to create historical narratives. A historical narrative is an account that explains past events using evidence. However, historians rarely rely on only one source. Instead, they gather information from multiple primary and secondary sources.

8. Secondary sources are created after an event has occurred. Textbooks, biographies, documentaries, and encyclopedia articles are examples of secondary sources. These sources often summarize information gathered from many primary sources.

9. A historian studying the Great Flood of 1913 might begin by reading letters, diaries, photographs, and newspaper articles from people who experienced the flood. These primary sources provide direct evidence about what happened.

10. Next, the historian might consult books and articles written by researchers. These secondary sources help explain the broader causes and effects of the flood. They may also compare information from many eyewitness accounts.

11. By combining information from both primary and secondary sources, historians can create more complete and accurate historical narratives. Primary sources provide firsthand details, while secondary sources help organize and explain the evidence.

12. Together, these sources allow historians to tell the story of past events. Without primary and secondary sources, it would be much more difficult to understand history and share it with future generations.

passages.title: American Indians and Early Ohio
passages.text:
1. Long before Ohio became a state, American Indian groups lived throughout the region. These groups, including the Shawnee, Miami, Delaware, and Wyandot, developed communities, hunted, farmed, and traded with one another. Rivers, forests, and fertile land provided resources that supported their way of life.

2. American Indian groups often traded goods such as tools, animal hides, crops, and pottery. Trade allowed communities to obtain resources that were not available in their local areas. Through these exchanges, groups shared ideas, skills, and cultural traditions.

3. During the late 1700s and early 1800s, increasing numbers of settlers moved into Ohio. Many settlers were searching for farmland and new opportunities. As settlers established towns and farms, interactions between American Indians and settlers became more common.

4. Some interactions involved cooperation. American Indians sometimes taught settlers how to grow crops suited to the region and how to navigate local waterways. Trade continued in certain areas, benefiting both groups.

5. However, competition for land and resources also led to conflict. As more settlers arrived, American Indians often lost access to hunting grounds and traditional lands. Disagreements over territory sometimes resulted in violence and mistrust.

6. In some cases, leaders from different groups negotiated agreements and treaties. These compromises were intended to reduce conflict, although not all agreements satisfied everyone involved. The interactions among American Indians and settlers shaped the development of Ohio and influenced its history for generations.

passages.title: Immigrants Help Build Ohio Communities
passages.text:
7. During the 1800s and early 1900s, many immigrants arrived in Ohio from countries around the world. Some came from Germany, Ireland, Italy, Poland, and other parts of Europe. Others arrived from different regions seeking jobs, freedom, and new opportunities.

8. Immigrants settled in growing cities and towns throughout Ohio. They worked in factories, mines, farms, and businesses. Their labor helped industries expand and contributed to the state's economic growth.

9. Many immigrant communities established churches, schools, newspapers, and cultural organizations. These institutions helped preserve traditions while also supporting newcomers as they adapted to life in Ohio.

10. Interactions between immigrants and people already living in Ohio often involved cooperation. Communities worked together to build roads, businesses, and public services. Cultural traditions from different groups enriched Ohio's society and introduced new foods, celebrations, and ideas.

11. At times, immigrants faced challenges and discrimination. Some people worried that newcomers would compete for jobs or change established customs. These concerns sometimes created tension and conflict within communities.

12. Over time, many groups found ways to compromise and work together. The contributions of immigrants became an important part of Ohio's identity. Their experiences demonstrate how cooperation, conflict, and compromise have influenced the state's development throughout history.

questions.text:

title: Ohio's Role in the Nation's Economy

passages.title: Ohio's Role in the Nation's Economy
passages.text:
1. The economy of the United States is connected through a network of farms, factories, businesses, transportation systems, and natural resources. Ohio has played an important role in this network for many years. The state's agriculture, industries, and natural resources have contributed to economic growth, while changes in the national economy have influenced how Ohio produces goods and services.

2. Agriculture has long been an important part of Ohio's economy. Much of the state's land is suitable for farming because of its fertile soil and favorable climate. Ohio farmers grow crops such as corn, soybeans, and wheat. They also raise livestock, including cattle, pigs, and poultry.

3. The products grown and raised in Ohio are sold throughout the United States and around the world. As demand for food changes, farmers often adjust what they produce. New technologies, improved equipment, and scientific advances have helped farmers increase productivity while using resources more efficiently.

4. Industry has also contributed significantly to Ohio's economic development. During the nineteenth and twentieth centuries, Ohio became a center for manufacturing. Factories produced steel, automobiles, machinery, and other goods that supported economic growth across the nation.

5. Ohio's location helped make it an important manufacturing state. Rivers, railroads, highways, and access to Lake Erie allowed raw materials and finished products to be transported efficiently. These transportation networks connected Ohio businesses to markets throughout the country.

6. Natural resources have influenced Ohio's economy as well. The state contains resources such as limestone, sandstone, clay, coal, and natural gas. These resources have been used in construction, manufacturing, and energy production. Access to natural resources helped many industries develop and expand.

7. At the same time, Ohio's economy has been influenced by changes occurring across the United States. For example, advances in technology have changed the way factories operate. Many businesses now use computers, robotics, and automated equipment to improve efficiency and productivity.

8. Global trade has also affected Ohio industries. Companies often buy materials from different regions and sell products to customers around the world. As markets change, Ohio businesses adapt by developing new products and improving production methods.

9. Agriculture, industry, and natural resources continue to work together to support Ohio's economy. Farmers depend on equipment produced by manufacturers. Industries rely on natural resources and transportation systems. These connections demonstrate how different parts of the economy influence one another.

10. Today, Ohio remains an important contributor to the economic development of the United States. Its farms, industries, and natural resources both influence and are influenced by changes in the national economy. This ongoing relationship helps shape economic opportunities for businesses and communities throughout the state and the nation.

questions.text:

title: Ohio: A Crossroads for People, Products, and Ideas

passages.title: Ohio: A Crossroads for People, Products, and Ideas
passages.text:
1. Location plays an important role in how people, products, and ideas move from place to place. Ohio's location in the eastern-central part of the United States has helped make it an important transportation hub for more than two centuries. Because Ohio connects many regions of the country, its transportation systems continue to influence trade, travel, and communication.

2. Ohio is located within a day's drive of a large portion of the United States population. This central location allows businesses to ship products efficiently to customers in many states. As a result, companies often choose Ohio as a location for warehouses, distribution centers, and manufacturing facilities.

3. Rivers were among Ohio's earliest transportation routes. Settlers and traders used waterways such as the Ohio River to move goods and people. These rivers connected communities and supported economic growth by allowing products to travel more easily than they could over rough land routes.

4. During the 1800s, canals became important transportation systems. The Ohio and Erie Canal connected Lake Erie to the Ohio River, creating a route that linked different parts of the state. Farmers and businesses could transport crops, raw materials, and manufactured goods more efficiently, helping Ohio's economy expand.

5. Railroads later transformed transportation throughout the state. Trains carried large amounts of freight and transported passengers across long distances. Ohio's rail connections helped industries obtain raw materials and distribute products to markets throughout the nation.

6. Today, highways continue to play a major role in transportation. Interstate highways cross Ohio in multiple directions, connecting cities and states. Trucks use these roads to deliver food, clothing, electronics, and other products to stores and businesses across the country.

7. Air transportation has also strengthened Ohio's role in moving people and goods. Airports allow travelers to reach destinations quickly and enable companies to ship products over long distances. Some businesses depend on air transportation to deliver time-sensitive items such as medical supplies and technology equipment.

8. Ohio's transportation systems do more than move products. They also help spread ideas. People traveling for work, education, or tourism share information and experiences with others. Businesses communicate with partners in different regions, and communities benefit from connections to places throughout the country.

9. Advances in technology have improved transportation systems even further. Computerized logistics systems help companies track shipments, plan routes, and deliver products more efficiently. These innovations allow Ohio businesses to remain connected to national and global markets.

10. Ohio's location and transportation networks continue to influence the movement of people, products, and ideas throughout the United States. From rivers and canals to railroads, highways, and airports, transportation systems have helped shape Ohio's economy and its role in connecting communities across the nation.

questions.text:

D  Trucks use interstate highways to deliver food, clothing, and electronics to stores.

As you write your response: use your own words to explain your opinion; use evidence from the passage to support your points; and use clear language to express your opinion by making connections between your points and your evidence. Be sure to: include an introduction; create organized paragraphs that focus on your opinion; pay attention to the grammar, structure, and mechanics of your sentences; and include a conclusion. Write your multi-paragraph response in the space provided.

passages.title: The North's Growing Industries
passages.text:
1. During the early 1800s, different regions of the United States developed in unique ways. The physical environment of each region influenced how people lived and worked. In the North, rivers, forests, and growing cities helped support a rapidly changing economy.

2. Many northern rivers flowed swiftly from higher elevations to the Atlantic Ocean. These rivers provided power for watermills and factories. As new machines were invented, manufacturers used river power to produce textiles, tools, and other goods more efficiently.

3. The North's economy became increasingly focused on industry and manufacturing. Factories attracted workers from rural areas and from other countries. As cities grew, transportation systems such as canals and railroads expanded to connect communities and move products to markets.

4. Agriculture remained important in the North, but many farms were smaller than those found in other regions. Farmers often produced crops and livestock that supplied nearby cities. Improvements in transportation allowed food and manufactured goods to be traded more easily.

5. The North's combination of natural resources, waterways, and industry helped create a diverse economy. By the middle of the nineteenth century, many northern communities depended on manufacturing, trade, and transportation.

6. The physical environment provided opportunities for industrial growth, and the region's economy continued to develop as technology and transportation improved.

passages.title: The South and West Develop Differently
passages.text:
7. While industry expanded in the North, the South and West developed in ways that reflected their own environments and resources. The economies of these regions were shaped by different climates, landforms, and opportunities.

8. The South's warm climate and long growing season made agriculture the foundation of its economy. Farmers grew crops such as cotton, tobacco, rice, and sugarcane. Large areas of fertile land supported farming, and many southern communities depended on agricultural production.

9. Rivers played an important role in the South by providing transportation routes for crops and supplies. Products grown on farms were often shipped to other parts of the United States and to international markets.

10. Farther west, settlers encountered vast grasslands, forests, mountains, and rivers. The West offered opportunities for farming, ranching, trapping, and trade. Natural resources such as timber, minerals, and fertile soil attracted people seeking land and economic opportunity.

11. Transportation in the West was often more difficult because communities were spread across large distances. However, trails, rivers, and eventually railroads helped connect settlements and support economic growth.

12. Although the North, South, and West developed different economies, each region contributed to the growth of the United States. Their physical environments influenced the industries, resources, and activities that became most important to their development.

passages.title: Changing Rivers and Building Communities
passages.text:
1. Throughout history, people have modified the environment to meet their needs. In Ohio and across the United States, communities have changed rivers, forests, and landscapes to support transportation, agriculture, industry, and daily life. These modifications often brought benefits, but they sometimes created challenges as well.

2. During the 1800s, canals were built to connect communities and improve transportation. In Ohio, the Ohio and Erie Canal linked Lake Erie with the Ohio River. Workers dug channels, built locks, and altered parts of the landscape to create routes for boats carrying goods and passengers.

3. The canal system provided many positive results. Farmers could transport crops more efficiently, businesses gained access to new markets, and communities along canal routes experienced economic growth. Transportation became faster and less expensive than before.

4. People also built dams along rivers to control water flow, generate electricity, and create reservoirs. Dams provided reliable sources of energy and water for homes, farms, and industries. Many communities benefited from improved flood control and increased access to resources.

5. However, modifying rivers sometimes produced negative consequences. Dams changed natural water flow and affected fish populations. Some habitats were flooded when reservoirs were created, forcing plants and animals to adapt or relocate.

6. Even with these challenges, many environmental modifications helped support growing populations and expanding economies. Communities often viewed these projects as important investments in their future.

passages.title: The Effects of Industrial Growth
passages.text:
7. As Ohio and the United States became more industrialized, people continued modifying the environment. Factories, railroads, roads, and cities expanded to meet the needs of a growing population and economy.

8. New transportation systems allowed products and people to move more efficiently. Railroads connected regions of the country, while roads made travel easier for communities. These developments supported trade, communication, and economic growth.

9. Industrial growth created jobs and encouraged innovation. Factories produced goods more quickly, and cities became centers of business and manufacturing. Many people experienced improved access to products and services.

10. At the same time, some environmental consequences emerged. Factories sometimes released pollutants into the air and water. Forests were cleared to make room for buildings, roads, and agricultural land. As natural habitats decreased, some wildlife populations declined.

11. Over time, communities recognized the need to balance development with environmental protection. Laws and conservation efforts were introduced to reduce pollution, protect natural resources, and preserve important habitats.

12. The history of environmental modification demonstrates that human actions can produce both positive and negative results. While these changes often improve transportation, economic growth, and access to resources, they can also affect ecosystems and natural environments in lasting ways.

passages.title: Why Laws Matter
passages.text:
1. Every community has rules that help people live and work together. Some rules are created by families, schools, or organizations. Laws, however, are official rules established by governments. Laws help maintain order, protect individuals, provide benefits, and assign responsibilities to members of society.

2. One important purpose of laws is to protect rights. Rights are freedoms and protections that people are entitled to have. For example, laws help protect a person's right to own property, express opinions, practice a religion, and receive fair treatment under the law. Without legal protections, individuals could have difficulty exercising these freedoms.

3. Laws also help ensure public safety. Traffic laws require drivers to follow speed limits, obey traffic signals, and operate vehicles responsibly. These laws protect both drivers and pedestrians by reducing the risk of accidents and injuries.

4. Another role of laws is to provide benefits to citizens. Governments create laws that support public services such as education, transportation, emergency response, and public health programs. These services are designed to improve quality of life and help communities function effectively.

5. Public education is one example of a benefit supported by laws. State and local governments establish laws that provide educational opportunities for children. These laws help ensure that students have access to schools, teachers, and learning resources.

6. Laws can also help protect the environment. Environmental laws may limit pollution, protect natural resources, and preserve wildlife habitats. These protections benefit both current residents and future generations by helping maintain healthy ecosystems.

7. In addition to protecting rights and providing benefits, laws assign responsibilities. Citizens are expected to follow laws, respect the rights of others, and contribute to their communities. Responsibilities help ensure that society functions fairly and efficiently.

8. Paying taxes is one example of a responsibility assigned by law. Taxes help fund public services such as schools, roads, parks, libraries, and emergency services. Although paying taxes is a responsibility, it also helps support benefits that communities rely upon every day.

9. Jury service is another responsibility that some citizens may be asked to perform. Jurors help ensure that court cases are decided fairly according to the law. This responsibility supports the legal system and helps protect the rights of individuals.

10. Rights, benefits, and responsibilities are closely connected. Laws protect people's freedoms, provide services that improve daily life, and establish expectations for how citizens should contribute to society. When individuals fulfill their responsibilities, communities are often better able to provide benefits and protect rights.

11. Throughout history, laws have helped communities address challenges and adapt to changing needs. New laws are sometimes created when circumstances change, while existing laws may be revised to better serve the public.

12. Understanding the purpose of laws helps people recognize their importance. Laws protect rights, provide benefits, and assign responsibilities that contribute to safe, organized, and effective communities. Together, these functions help support the well-being of society as a whole.

passages.title: A Written Plan for Government
passages.text:
1. Every organization needs a plan to guide its actions and responsibilities. Schools have handbooks, sports teams have rules, and governments have constitutions. A constitution is a written plan for government. It explains how a government is organized, what powers it has, and how it serves the people.

2. The United States Constitution was written in 1787 and remains the highest law of the nation. It established the structure of the federal government and outlined the rights and responsibilities of citizens and government officials. The Constitution was designed to create a government strong enough to function effectively while preventing any one person or group from gaining too much power.

3. One way the Constitution accomplishes this goal is by separating government responsibilities among three branches. These branches are the legislative branch, the executive branch, and the judicial branch. Each branch has different duties and powers.

4. The legislative branch is responsible for making laws. At the federal level, this branch includes Congress, which is made up of the Senate and the House of Representatives. Lawmakers discuss issues, propose bills, and vote on whether new laws should be created.

5. The executive branch is responsible for carrying out and enforcing laws. The President leads the executive branch of the federal government. Executive agencies and departments help implement laws and provide services to citizens.

6. The judicial branch interprets laws and settles disputes. Courts determine whether laws are being applied fairly and whether government actions follow the Constitution. The Supreme Court is the highest court in the federal judicial system.

7. Like the United States Constitution, the Ohio Constitution serves as a written plan for state government. Ohio's Constitution explains how the state's government operates and identifies the responsibilities of its officials and institutions.

8. Ohio also separates government responsibilities among three branches. The Ohio General Assembly, which includes the Ohio Senate and Ohio House of Representatives, makes state laws. The governor leads the executive branch and helps enforce those laws. Ohio's court system serves as the judicial branch and interprets state laws.

9. Although the Ohio Constitution and the U.S. Constitution apply to different levels of government, they share many important principles. Both establish a system of government based on laws, define the powers of government officials, and divide responsibilities among three branches.

10. Separating powers among branches helps create a system of checks and balances. This means that each branch has ways to limit the power of the others. As a result, no single branch can easily control the entire government.

11. The constitutions also help citizens understand how their governments function. By providing a written plan, constitutions create stability and consistency. Government officials know their responsibilities, and citizens know how decisions are made.

12. Today, both the Ohio Constitution and the U.S. Constitution continue to guide government operations. Their systems of separated powers help ensure that laws are created, enforced, and interpreted in an organized and balanced manner.

passages.title: Rights and Responsibilities of Citizens
passages.text:
1. Citizens in Ohio and throughout the United States have important rights and responsibilities. Rights are freedoms and protections that people are entitled to have. Responsibilities are duties that help individuals contribute to their communities and government.

2. One important right of citizens is freedom of speech. This right allows people to express their opinions and share ideas. Citizens may discuss issues, write letters, speak at public meetings, or communicate with government leaders about topics they believe are important.

3. Citizens also have the right to vote when they reach the required age and meet legal requirements. Voting allows people to help choose leaders and influence decisions made by government officials. Elections provide citizens with an opportunity to participate directly in government.

4. Along with rights come responsibilities. Citizens are expected to obey laws, respect the rights of others, and stay informed about issues affecting their communities. Responsible citizenship helps governments function effectively.

5. Some citizens choose to volunteer in community organizations, participate in neighborhood projects, or help address local concerns. These actions contribute to the common good and strengthen communities.

6. Rights and responsibilities work together. Citizens use their rights to participate in government, while fulfilling responsibilities helps ensure that communities remain safe, organized, and productive.

passages.title: Influencing Government
passages.text:
7. Citizens have many opportunities to influence government at both the state and national levels. While voting is one of the most visible forms of participation, it is not the only way people can make their voices heard.

8. Many citizens contact elected officials to express opinions about laws and public policies. They may send letters, emails, or phone messages explaining their views on important issues. Government leaders often consider feedback from the people they represent.

9. Citizens can also attend public meetings where community issues are discussed. School board meetings, city council meetings, and other public gatherings allow people to learn about government decisions and share their opinions.

10. Some individuals participate in campaigns by volunteering, distributing information, or encouraging others to vote. These activities help citizens support candidates and causes they believe in.

11. Peaceful demonstrations and organized petitions provide additional ways for citizens to influence government. These activities allow groups of people to communicate concerns and advocate for change while exercising their rights.

12. Through voting, communication, volunteering, and civic participation, citizens can influence decisions made by state and national governments. These opportunities help ensure that government remains connected to the people it serves.

passages.title: Limited Government and the Constitution
passages.text:
1. When the leaders of the United States created the Constitution in 1787, they wanted a government that was strong enough to govern the nation but limited enough to protect individual freedoms. To achieve this goal, the Constitution established a system of limited government.

2. Limited government means that government officials must follow the law and can exercise only the powers granted to them by the Constitution. No person or branch of government has unlimited authority. This principle helps prevent the misuse of power and protects citizens from unfair treatment.

3. The Constitution also divides power among three branches of government: the legislative, executive, and judicial branches. Each branch has specific responsibilities and can limit the powers of the others through a system known as checks and balances.

4. In addition to organizing the government, the Constitution protects citizens' rights. The framers believed that individuals should have certain freedoms that the government could not easily take away. To strengthen these protections, ten amendments known as the Bill of Rights were added to the Constitution in 1791.

5. The Bill of Rights outlines important freedoms and protections for citizens. These amendments help ensure that the government respects individual rights while carrying out its responsibilities.

6. Through limited government, separated powers, and constitutional protections, the Constitution continues to provide a framework for balancing government authority with individual liberty.

passages.title: The First Amendment Protects Freedoms
passages.text:
7. The First Amendment is one of the most well-known parts of the Bill of Rights. It protects five important freedoms that citizens use in their daily lives. These freedoms help people express ideas, participate in government, and practice their beliefs.

8. The first freedom protected by the First Amendment is freedom of religion. Citizens may choose their own religious beliefs or choose not to follow a religion. The government cannot establish an official national religion or prevent people from practicing their faith.

9. The second freedom is freedom of speech. This right allows people to express opinions, share ideas, and discuss issues. Citizens often use this freedom when participating in public discussions and debates.

10. The third freedom is freedom of the press. Newspapers, television stations, websites, and other media organizations can report information and share news with the public. A free press helps citizens stay informed about current events and government actions.

11. The fourth freedom is the right to assemble peacefully. Citizens may gather in groups for meetings, celebrations, demonstrations, and other lawful activities. This freedom allows people to express their views collectively.

12. The fifth freedom is the right to petition the government. Citizens may contact government officials, sign petitions, or request changes to laws and policies. This right gives people a way to communicate concerns and seek action from their leaders.

13. Together, these five freedoms help protect individual liberty and encourage civic participation. The First Amendment remains an important part of the Constitution because it supports both personal freedoms and democratic government.
