title: Taking Risks to Build a Business

passages.title: Taking Risks to Build a Business
passages.text:
1. Every business begins with an idea. Some people notice a problem that needs solving, while others recognize an opportunity to provide a product or service that customers want. Individuals who start and manage businesses are called entrepreneurs. Entrepreneurs play an important role in Ohio's economy and in the economy of the United States.

2. Entrepreneurs organize productive resources to create goods and services. Productive resources include natural resources, human resources, and capital resources. Natural resources are materials found in nature, such as water, soil, and minerals. Human resources include the workers who provide labor and skills. Capital resources are tools, equipment, buildings, and technology used to produce goods and services.

3. Before opening a business, entrepreneurs must decide how to use these resources effectively. For example, a person starting a bakery may need ingredients, ovens, workers, and a building. The entrepreneur must organize these resources to produce baked goods that customers want to buy.

4. Entrepreneurs also take financial risks. Starting a business often requires investing money in equipment, supplies, advertising, and employee wages. There is no guarantee that customers will purchase the product or service. If the business is unsuccessful, the entrepreneur may lose money.

5. Ohio has been home to many successful entrepreneurs. The Wright brothers, who lived and worked in Dayton, Ohio, invested time and resources to develop the first successful powered airplane. Their work involved years of experimentation and uncertainty before they achieved success.

6. Entrepreneurs today continue to launch businesses throughout Ohio. Some develop new technologies, while others open restaurants, stores, farms, or service companies. Each entrepreneur hopes to earn a profit by meeting customer needs.

7. Profit is the money a business earns after paying its expenses. Entrepreneurs often use profits to expand their businesses, hire more workers, develop new products, or invest in additional equipment. However, earning a profit usually requires competing with other producers.

8. Competition occurs when multiple businesses offer similar goods or services. For example, several restaurants in the same community may compete for customers. Businesses often try to attract customers by offering quality products, lower prices, unique services, or convenient locations.

9. Competition can benefit consumers because businesses work to improve their products and services. Companies may introduce new ideas or technologies to gain an advantage over competitors. As a result, consumers often have more choices.

10. Not every entrepreneur succeeds on the first attempt. Some businesses fail because of changing customer preferences, economic conditions, or strong competition. Successful entrepreneurs often learn from mistakes and continue looking for new opportunities.

11. Entrepreneurs contribute to economic growth by creating jobs, producing goods and services, and encouraging innovation. Their willingness to organize resources and take risks helps communities grow and develop.

12. Throughout Ohio and the United States, entrepreneurs continue to shape the economy. By organizing productive resources, accepting financial risks, and competing with other producers, they help provide the products and services people use every day.

Write your multi-paragraph response in the space provided.

passages.title: The Value of Saving Money
passages.text:
1. Every day, people make choices about how to use their money. Some of their income is spent on needs such as food, housing, transportation, and clothing. Other income may be used for wants such as entertainment, hobbies, or special purchases. Financial experts often recommend that people save a portion of their income to help prepare for future needs and goals.

2. Income is the money a person earns from work, investments, or other sources. Once people receive income, they must decide how much to spend and how much to save. Saving means setting aside money instead of spending it immediately.

3. Saving contributes to an individual's financial well-being. Financial well-being refers to a person's ability to manage expenses, meet financial goals, and handle unexpected situations. People who regularly save money are often better prepared for future challenges and opportunities.

4. One reason saving is important is that unexpected expenses can occur at any time. A car may need repairs, a household appliance may stop working, or a medical expense may arise. Individuals who have savings may be able to cover these costs without borrowing money or going into debt.

5. Saving also helps people achieve long-term goals. Some individuals save for education, a home, a vehicle, travel, or retirement. By setting aside small amounts of money consistently, people can gradually build the funds needed to reach these goals.

6. Many people use savings accounts to store money safely. Banks and credit unions offer accounts that allow individuals to deposit money and access it when needed. Some accounts also earn interest, which is money paid by the financial institution for keeping funds in the account.

7. While saving is important, many people find it challenging because of spending habits. Expenses can quickly reduce the amount of income available for saving. As a result, financial experts often encourage people to examine their spending choices carefully.

8. One way individuals can save more money is by reducing spending. For example, a person might prepare meals at home instead of purchasing restaurant meals frequently. Another person might compare prices before making purchases or choose less expensive alternatives.

9. Creating a budget can also help people reduce spending. A budget is a plan for how income will be used. By tracking expenses and identifying unnecessary purchases, individuals can make informed decisions about where to save money.

10. Small changes in spending habits can have a significant effect over time. Spending a few dollars less each day may not seem important at first, but those savings can accumulate over weeks, months, and years.

11. Financial well-being often depends on balancing spending and saving. While people need to spend money on necessities and important activities, saving provides security and flexibility for the future.

12. By saving a portion of their income and reducing unnecessary spending, individuals can improve their financial well-being. These habits help people prepare for unexpected expenses, achieve personal goals, and make thoughtful financial decisions throughout their lives.

questions.text:

A  Saving money and reducing spending both help improve a person's financial well-being.

E  'Banks and credit unions offer accounts that allow individuals to deposit money and access it when needed' (paragraph 6).

Read this sentence from paragraph 6: 'Some accounts also earn interest, which is money paid by the financial institution for keeping funds in the account.' In this sentence, the word interest means [ ▾ ].
money paid to a person for keeping savings in an account
a strong feeling of wanting to learn about something
the amount of money a person spends each month
a plan that shows how income will be used

title: A Farmer with a New Idea

passages.title: A Farmer with a New Idea
passages.text:
1. Entrepreneurs are people who organize productive resources and take risks to start or grow businesses. Throughout Ohio and the United States, entrepreneurs help create jobs, provide goods and services, and contribute to economic growth. However, success often requires careful planning and a willingness to face uncertainty.

2. In a small Ohio town, Maya noticed that local grocery stores were selling vegetables shipped from far away. She believed customers might prefer fresh produce grown nearby. After researching the idea, she decided to start a small business growing vegetables in greenhouses.

3. To begin, Maya needed productive resources. She leased land, purchased seeds and equipment, and hired workers to help manage the greenhouses. She also invested money in irrigation systems and technology that monitored plant growth.

4. Starting the business involved risk. Maya spent much of her savings on equipment and supplies. She knew that weather conditions, changing customer preferences, or competition from larger farms could affect her success.

5. During the first year, Maya worked hard to build relationships with local stores and restaurants. Customers appreciated the fresh produce, and demand gradually increased. As her profits grew, she expanded the business and hired additional employees.

6. Maya's experience demonstrates how entrepreneurs organize resources and take risks in hopes of earning a profit. Her success depended on planning, investment, and the ability to compete with other producers.

passages.title: Competing in the Technology Market
passages.text:
7. Entrepreneurs can be found in many industries. While some businesses focus on agriculture, others develop products and services that rely on technology and innovation.

8. In Columbus, Ohio, a group of entrepreneurs created a company that designed educational software for schools. They believed teachers could benefit from programs that helped students practice important skills in engaging ways.

9. To launch the company, the entrepreneurs organized productive resources. They hired computer programmers, purchased equipment, rented office space, and secured funding from investors. These resources helped transform their idea into a working product.

10. The entrepreneurs faced significant competition. Many other companies already offered educational software. To attract customers, the team focused on creating unique features and improving the user experience.

11. Like Maya, the technology entrepreneurs accepted financial risks. If schools did not purchase their software, the company could lose money. However, they believed their product would meet an important need.

12. Over time, schools began adopting the software, and the business expanded. The company's success showed how entrepreneurs can use creativity, productive resources, and risk-taking to compete in the marketplace.

13. Whether they operate farms, factories, stores, or technology companies, entrepreneurs play an important role in the economy. By organizing resources and accepting risks, they create products and services while competing with other producers to earn a profit.

questions.text:

D  She had no experience and could not research how to grow vegetables nearby.

title: Building Savings One Step at a Time

passages.title: Building Savings One Step at a Time
passages.text:
1. Financial well-being refers to a person's ability to manage money, pay for needs, prepare for unexpected expenses, and work toward future goals. One of the most effective ways to improve financial well-being is by saving a portion of income on a regular basis.

2. Income is the money people earn from jobs, businesses, or other sources. After receiving income, individuals must decide how much to spend and how much to save. Financial experts often recommend setting aside part of each paycheck before spending money on other things.

3. Olivia, a fourth-grade student, decided to save money she received from chores and gifts. She wanted to buy a bicycle that cost much more than she could afford at one time. Instead of spending her money immediately, she deposited part of it into a savings account each month.

4. At first, Olivia's savings grew slowly. However, she continued adding money regularly. Over time, the amount increased enough for her to purchase the bicycle without borrowing money from anyone.

5. Saving also helped Olivia prepare for unexpected situations. When her backpack was damaged and needed to be replaced, she was able to use some of her savings to cover the cost. Because she had planned ahead, the expense was less stressful.

6. People save for many reasons, including emergencies, education, travel, large purchases, and future needs. By consistently saving a portion of their income, individuals can improve their financial security and achieve important goals.

passages.title: Reducing Spending to Reach Goals
passages.text:
7. While saving is important, many people find it difficult to set money aside. One reason is that spending can quickly use up available income. As a result, reducing unnecessary spending is another strategy that can improve financial well-being.

8. Marcus wanted to save money for a new gaming system. When he reviewed how he spent his allowance, he noticed that he often purchased snacks and small toys that he did not use for very long. Although each purchase seemed inexpensive, the costs added up over time.

9. Marcus decided to create a simple budget. A budget is a plan that helps people decide how to use their money. By tracking his spending, Marcus identified purchases he could reduce or avoid altogether.

10. Instead of buying snacks every week, Marcus brought snacks from home more often. He also waited several days before making purchases to decide whether he truly wanted an item. These changes helped him spend less money.

11. As Marcus reduced his spending, he had more money available to save. Within several months, he reached his goal and purchased the gaming system. He also learned that small spending decisions can have a significant effect on long-term savings.

12. Reducing spending does not mean never buying things people enjoy. Instead, it involves making thoughtful choices about how money is used. By controlling expenses, individuals can increase the amount of income available for saving and future goals.

questions.text:

E  He bought more small toys because each one seemed inexpensive.

passages.title: The Mountain Path
passages.text:
(1) I stood beside a mountain trail,(2) And watched the clouds drift by.(3) The winding path stretched far ahead,(4) Beneath the open sky.

(5) The rocks were rough beneath my feet,(6) The climb was steep and long.(7) At times I wondered if I should(8) Keep moving, brave and strong.

(9) The morning breeze seemed soft and kind,(10) It whispered through the trees.(11) "Take one more step," it seemed to say,(12) As it danced upon the leaves.

(13) I crossed a stream of sparkling blue,(14) Then climbed another hill.(15) The path grew narrow, bent, and curved,(16) Yet I kept walking still.

(17) At last I reached the mountain's top(18) And gazed across the land.(19) The valleys, forests, fields, and streams(20) Seemed carefully planned.

(21) The journey had not been easy work;(22) The climb had tested me.(23) But every challenge on the trail(24) Helped me learn and see.

(25) Sometimes the path ahead looks hard,(26) And progress may seem slow.(27) Yet steady steps can lead us far(28) More than we often know.

B  Steady effort and persistence can carry a person far, even when progress feels slow.

D  People should always travel outdoors in the morning when the breeze is soft.

B  The speaker comes to see the hard climb as a challenge that taught a valuable lesson.
C  The speaker believes the climb was easy and that worrying about it was foolish.
D  The speaker concludes that the view from the top was disappointing after all.

passages.title: The Sound of the Rain
passages.text:
(1) Rain taps softly on the roof,(2) Tap, tap, tap all day.(3) Tiny drops tumble through the air,(4) Then splash along the way.

(5) Rain taps softly on the roof,(6) Tap, tap, tap once more.(7) Puddles spread beside the path,(8) And gather near the door.

(9) Breezes blow and branches bend,(10) Dancing to the beat.(11) Water whispers down the street,(12) A rhythm clear and sweet.

(13) Rain taps softly on the roof,(14) Tap, tap, tap again.(15) The steady sound becomes a song,(16) Sung by falling rain.

(17) Children listen from inside(18) As clouds drift overhead.(19) The pattern of the rainy tune(20) Fills each heart and head.

(21) Soon the storm begins to fade,(22) The sky turns bright and blue.(23) Yet echoes of the rainy song(24) Still softly linger through.

passages.title: The Lantern
passages.text:
(1) A lantern glowed beside the road,(2) Its light both warm and bright.(3) It shone across the rocky path(4) Throughout the quiet night.

(5) A traveler walked beneath the stars,(6) Unsure of where to go.(7) The darkness stretched on every side,(8) The wind began to blow.

(9) Yet every time the traveler looked,(10) The lantern stood ahead.(11) Its steady beam seemed to whisper,(12) "Move forward, not with dread."

(13) The path was long, the climb was steep,(14) The traveler often sighed.(15) But with the lantern lighting the way,(16) Hope remained beside.

(17) At dawn, the traveler reached the hill(18) And watched the sunrise glow.(19) The lantern's light had guided each step(20) More than it seemed to show.

passages.title: The Oak Tree
passages.text:
(21) An oak tree stood upon a hill,(22) Its branches stretched up high.(23) Through storms and winds it held its ground(24) Beneath the changing sky.

(25) In spring, new leaves began to grow.(26) In summer, birds would rest.(27) In autumn, golden leaves would fall.(28) In winter, snow would nest.

(29) The years passed by, yet still it stood,(30) Strong through every season.(31) It bent but never broke apart,(32) Its roots the steady reason.

(33) Children played beneath its shade,(34) Travelers paused nearby.(35) The oak tree seemed to teach a lesson:(36) Stand tall and always try.

(37) Though storms may come and days may change,(38) Courage can remain.(39) Like roots beneath the mighty oak,(40) Strength can grow through strain.

questions.text:

B  Even when the way is hard and unclear, steady guidance can help a person keep going.

D  Reaching the top of a hill is the only reward worth the climb.

made a soft sound as it burned through the night
was too quiet for the traveler to notice
frightened the traveler into moving faster

passages.title: Marching Band Morning
passages.text:
(1) Step by step, the students stride,(2) Across the field so wide.(3) Boom, boom, boom, the drums all play,(4) To start the school-day ride.

(5) Step by step, the students stride,(6) Their instruments held high.(7) The steady beat helps keep the pace(8) As music fills the sky.

(9) Trumpets toot and tubas hum,(10) Their notes both strong and clear.(11) The rhythm helps the players stay(12) Together year by year.

(13) Step by step, the students stride,(14) Boom, boom, boom once more.(15) The repeated sounds help listeners(16) Know what the music's for.

(17) The beat unites the marching band(18) As they move down the street.(19) The repeated words and steady drum(20) Create a marching beat.

passages.title: The Busy Bees
passages.text:
(21) Busy bees buzz by the blooms,(22) Beneath the bright blue sky.(23) They flutter fast from flower to flower(24) As summer days drift by.

(25) Buzz, buzz, buzz, the bees all sing,(26) Their song both low and light.(27) They gather nectar through the day(28) From morning until night.

(29) Busy bees buzz by the blooms,(30) Returning time and again.(31) The repeated sounds remind us how(32) They work without an end.

(33) The buzzing bees and blooming buds(34) Use sounds that seem to play.(35) Their alliteration adds a rhythm(36) That brightens up the day.

(37) Buzz, buzz, buzz, the bees all sing,(38) A pattern we can hear.(39) The repeated words and matching sounds(40) Help bring the meaning near.

questions.text:

title: The Last Dragon of Ember Valley

passages.title: The Last Dragon of Ember Valley
passages.text:
1. Talia pulled her cloak tighter as she climbed the winding path through Ember Valley. Above her, jagged mountains pierced the clouds, and glowing red flowers lined the trail. Most people in the kingdom believed dragons had disappeared long ago, but Talia was not so sure.

2. For years, she had listened to stories told by her grandfather. He claimed that dragons once protected the valley with their powerful magic. According to the legends, the last dragon had hidden itself deep within the mountains after a great conflict divided the kingdom.

3. Talia carried a map that her grandfather had left behind. Faded markings led toward a place called the Crystal Cavern. She hoped the map would help her discover whether the stories were true.

4. As she climbed higher, the air grew colder. Suddenly, a bright flash of silver light appeared between the rocks. A small winged creature darted across the path and disappeared into a cave.

5. "Wait!" Talia called.

6. She hurried after it and entered the cave. Inside, crystals covered the walls and ceiling. Their colorful glow reflected across the stone floor like pools of light.

7. In the center of the cavern stood a massive dragon. Its scales shimmered with shades of silver and blue. Large wings folded neatly against its sides, and its golden eyes watched Talia carefully.

8. Talia froze. The stories had been true.

9. "Why have you come here?" the dragon asked in a deep voice.

10. Talia swallowed nervously. "I wanted to know if dragons still existed."

11. The dragon lowered its head. "Few people believe in us anymore."

12. As they talked, Talia learned that the dragon's name was Aerion. He explained that dragons possessed ancient magic connected to the land. When dragons disappeared, some of that magic faded as well.

13. Aerion showed Talia a crystal pool at the back of the cavern. The water displayed images of the kingdom. Rivers appeared smaller than before, forests seemed thinner, and fields looked dry.

14. "The kingdom is losing its magic," Aerion explained. "Without it, the land will struggle."

15. Talia frowned. "Can anything be done?"

16. Aerion nodded. "The magic can return if people choose to protect the land instead of taking from it without care."

17. The dragon stretched out one enormous wing. A glowing silver feather drifted into Talia's hands.

18. "Take this," Aerion said. "It will remind people that dragons once guarded this kingdom."

19. Talia carefully tucked the feather into her bag and promised to share what she had learned.

20. When she returned home, many people doubted her story. However, the silver feather shimmered with magical light, proving that her adventure had been real.

21. Inspired by the feather and Talia's message, villagers planted trees, cleaned rivers, and protected nearby forests. Over time, the land began to recover.

22. Years later, travelers claimed they sometimes saw a silver dragon soaring above the mountains at sunrise. Whenever Talia heard these stories, she smiled.

23. She knew that some legends were more than stories. They were reminders that courage, responsibility, and hope could bring about extraordinary change.

questions.text:
What is the central message of the story?
A  Dragons are dangerous creatures that people should avoid at all costs
B  People should keep ancient legends secret to protect them from doubters
C  Caring for the land and acting with courage can bring about important change
D  Maps left by family members always lead to hidden treasure

Select two details from the story that show how Talia changes the kingdom after she leaves the cavern.
A  "many people doubted her story" (paragraph 20)
B  "the silver feather shimmered with magical light, proving that her adventure had been real" (paragraph 20)
C  "Inspired by the feather and Talia's message, villagers planted trees, cleaned rivers, and protected nearby forests" (paragraph 21)
D  "Over time, the land began to recover" (paragraph 21)
E  "travelers claimed they sometimes saw a silver dragon soaring above the mountains at sunrise" (paragraph 22)

Read this sentence from paragraph 6. "Their colorful glow reflected across the stone floor like pools of light." The author compares the glow of the crystals to [ ▾ ] to help the reader picture how the light spread across the floor.
sharp, jagged rocks
spreading pools of water
a single bright lamp
cold mountain air

B  Talia thinks dragons may still be real, while most people believe they disappeared
C  Talia believes dragons are harmful, while most people think dragons protect the land
D  Talia wants to keep dragons hidden, while most people want to share dragon stories

passages.title: The Colony on Titan
passages.text:
1. In the year 2148, humans lived in places far beyond Earth. One of the most unusual settlements was New Horizon Colony, located on Titan, Saturn's largest moon. Titan's thick orange atmosphere and icy surface made it very different from Earth, but advanced technology allowed people to live and work there safely.

2. Twelve-year-old Aria lived with her family inside a large protective dome. The dome shielded residents from the extreme cold and provided clean air, water, and comfortable temperatures. Outside the dome, robotic vehicles explored the frozen landscape while scientists studied Titan's environment.

3. Aria's favorite part of the colony was the Learning Hub. Students attended classes using holographic technology that projected three-dimensional images into the room. During science lessons, students could examine virtual planets, explore microscopic organisms, or observe distant galaxies without leaving their seats.

4. One morning, an alert appeared on every screen in the colony. A research drone exploring a remote region of Titan had detected an unusual energy signal beneath the ice.

5. Scientists quickly gathered to study the data. The signal did not match any known natural process. Some researchers believed it might be caused by unusual underground activity. Others wondered whether it could be evidence of a previously undiscovered energy source.

6. Aria's mother was one of the engineers assigned to investigate. Using advanced sensors and robotic equipment, the team traveled to the location identified by the drone.

7. Several days later, the scientists made an exciting discovery. Beneath the ice was a large underground chamber filled with glowing crystals. The crystals released small amounts of energy that could be measured by scientific instruments.

8. The discovery attracted attention throughout the solar system. If the crystals could safely provide energy, they might help support future space exploration and reduce the need to transport fuel across vast distances.

9. However, the scientists faced an important question. Should they immediately begin collecting the crystals, or should they spend more time studying them first?

10. Some colony leaders wanted to use the crystals right away. They believed the energy source could benefit millions of people. Others argued that removing the crystals too quickly might damage Titan's environment or destroy important scientific evidence.

11. Aria listened carefully as experts debated the issue during a public broadcast. She was surprised to learn that scientific discoveries often required difficult decisions. Finding something valuable was only the first step. Scientists also had to consider possible risks and consequences.

12. After weeks of research, the colony reached a decision. The crystals would remain protected while scientists continued studying them. Small samples could be collected for testing, but large-scale mining would not begin until researchers understood more about the underground chamber.

13. The decision required patience, but many people supported the plan. By gathering additional information first, scientists could make more informed choices about how to use the discovery responsibly.

14. Months later, researchers learned that the crystals formed part of a complex natural system beneath Titan's surface. Careful study allowed them to develop methods for using the energy while minimizing environmental impacts.

15. As Aria looked out through the dome at Titan's orange sky, she realized that the future would depend not only on technology but also on wise decision-making. Scientific discoveries could create exciting opportunities, but understanding them fully was just as important.

questions.text:
Based on paragraph 11, how does Aria change after listening to the experts debate the issue?
A  She decides she wants to become an engineer like her mother.
B  She comes to understand that scientific discoveries often require difficult decisions.

B  Some leaders believed the crystals should be destroyed to protect Titan.
C  Some leaders argued that removing the crystals too quickly might damage Titan's environment.
D  Some leaders wanted to keep the discovery a secret from the rest of the solar system.
E  Some leaders thought the crystals were too dangerous to study at all.

Complete the sentence about the theme of the story.

The story teaches that [ ▾ ].
technology is the only thing that matters for the future of a colony
making wise decisions is just as important as making new discoveries
people should always use a new resource as quickly as possible
scientists should avoid exploring places that are dangerous

"By gathering additional information first, scientists could make more informed choices about how to use the discovery responsibly."

What does the word "informed" mean as it is used in this sentence?
A  Based on more knowledge and understanding
B  Decided very quickly
C  Kept secret from others
D  Made by only one person

passages.title: The New Canal
passages.text:
1. In the summer of 1830, twelve-year-old Samuel stood beside a section of the Ohio and Erie Canal that was still under construction. Workers dug long channels through the earth while teams of horses pulled heavy equipment along the muddy ground.

2. Samuel's father worked on the canal project. Every evening, he returned home tired but excited about the progress being made. He often explained that the canal would connect communities and make it easier to transport goods across Ohio.

3. Samuel had heard people talking about the canal for years. Some believed it would bring new opportunities for farmers and merchants. Others worried that the project was taking too long and costing too much money.

4. One morning, Samuel accompanied his father to the work site. He watched workers build locks, which were structures designed to help boats move through sections of water at different elevations.

5. "Why is everyone working so hard on this?" Samuel asked.

6. His father smiled. "Because this canal will help people travel and trade more easily. Communities that seem far apart today will soon be connected."

7. Months later, the first canal boat passed through the completed section near Samuel's town. Crowds gathered along the waterway to celebrate. The boat carried barrels of flour, tools, and other goods that would have taken much longer to transport by wagon.

8. As businesses began using the canal, the town grew. New stores opened, and more travelers passed through the area. Samuel realized that the canal was changing daily life for many people.

9. Although the work had been difficult, the canal helped create new economic opportunities throughout Ohio. Samuel felt proud knowing that his father had contributed to such an important project.

passages.title: A Day at the World's Fair
passages.text:
10. In 1893, thirteen-year-old Clara traveled with her family to Chicago to visit the World's Columbian Exposition, often called the World's Fair. The event celebrated technological achievements and attracted visitors from across the United States and around the world.

11. Clara had never seen such large buildings or crowds. As she entered the fairgrounds, she stared at the enormous exhibits displaying inventions, machinery, and scientific discoveries.

12. One attraction impressed her more than anything else—the Ferris wheel. Designed by engineer George Ferris, the giant wheel lifted passengers high above the fairgrounds, offering views of the city below.

13. At first, Clara felt nervous about riding it. The wheel seemed impossibly large. However, her older brother convinced her to give it a try.

14. As their car slowly rose into the air, Clara gazed across the fair. She could see exhibition halls, lakes, gardens, and thousands of visitors exploring the grounds.

15. Throughout the day, Clara learned about new technologies that were changing American life. She saw electric lights, advanced machines, and transportation innovations that many people had never experienced before.

16. "Imagine what life will be like in the future," Clara said as her family prepared to leave.

17. Her father nodded. "Events like this show how quickly our country is growing and changing."

18. Years later, Clara still remembered her visit to the World's Fair. The experience helped her understand how inventions and new ideas could influence communities across the nation.

questions.text:
In Passage 1, how does Samuel's understanding of the canal change from the beginning to the end of the story?
A  At first he does not understand why people work so hard, but later he sees how the canal changes daily life.
B  At first he is proud of his father, but later he worries the project cost too much money.
C  At first he wants to work on the canal, but later he decides to become a merchant instead.
D  At first he is excited about the boats, but later he becomes bored with the busy town.

Read this sentence from Passage 2: "At first, Clara felt nervous about riding it. The wheel seemed impossibly large." (paragraph 13) The word "impossibly" helps the reader understand that the wheel seemed [ ▾ ] to Clara.

smaller than she had expected

B  Children should always follow the advice of their parents and older siblings.
C  Hard work on a difficult project is usually not worth the effort it takes.
D  Traveling far from home helps young people make new friends.

Both Samuel's father (Passage 1) and Clara's father (Passage 2) share a similar view about the events their children witness. Select two statements that show this shared view.
A  "Communities that seem far apart today will soon be connected." (paragraph 6)
B  "Events like this show how quickly our country is growing and changing." (paragraph 17)
C  "Why is everyone working so hard on this?" (paragraph 5)
D  "Imagine what life will be like in the future," (paragraph 16)
E  "The wheel seemed impossibly large." (paragraph 13)

passages.title: The Community Garden
passages.text:
1. Maya looked out the window of her apartment building and frowned. The empty lot across the street was filled with weeds, litter, and broken fences. Every day, she walked past it on her way to school.

2. One afternoon, Maya attended a neighborhood meeting with her grandmother. Residents discussed ways to improve the community. During the meeting, someone suggested turning the vacant lot into a community garden.

3. Maya thought the idea sounded exciting. A garden could provide flowers, vegetables, and a place for neighbors to gather. She volunteered to help with the project.

4. Over the next several weeks, volunteers worked together to clean the lot. They removed trash, repaired fences, and prepared the soil for planting. Maya spent many Saturdays pulling weeds and carrying supplies.

5. When spring arrived, community members planted tomatoes, peppers, sunflowers, and herbs. Children painted signs, and local businesses donated gardening tools.

6. By summer, the garden was thriving. Neighbors visited regularly to water plants and harvest vegetables. People who had rarely spoken before began sharing gardening tips and working together.

7. One evening, Maya walked through the garden and smiled. The empty lot that had once been ignored was now filled with color and activity.

8. "It's amazing what people can accomplish when they work together," her grandmother said.

9. Maya nodded. She felt proud that her efforts had helped improve the neighborhood.

passages.title: The School Newspaper
passages.text:
10. Ethan loved writing. When he learned that his middle school planned to restart its newspaper, he immediately signed up to help.

11. At the first meeting, the newspaper advisor explained that students would be responsible for writing articles, conducting interviews, and designing each issue. Ethan was excited but also nervous. He had never written for a newspaper before.

12. His first assignment was to interview students about a new after-school program. Ethan carefully prepared questions and practiced taking notes.

13. The interview went better than he expected. Students shared their opinions, and Ethan used their responses to write an article. After several revisions, the article was published in the newspaper.

14. Soon, more students began reading the paper. Teachers used articles to discuss school events, and students learned about activities they might not have known about otherwise.

15. As the year continued, Ethan's confidence grew. He interviewed teachers, reported on sports events, and wrote feature stories about student achievements.

16. One day, a younger student approached him.

17. "I read your article about the science fair," she said. "It made me want to enter next year."

18. Ethan smiled. He realized that writing could help inform and inspire others.

19. By the end of the year, the school newspaper had become an important source of information for students and families. Ethan was proud to have contributed to its success.

questions.text:

D  Spending time outdoors is the best way to improve a neighborhood.

How are Maya in Passage 1 and Ethan in Passage 2 most alike?

B  Both contribute their effort to a project that ends up helping their whole community.
C  Both are asked by a teacher to lead a project they did not want to join.
D  Both give up partway through their projects before others finish the work.

title: How the Fireflies Got Their Light

passages.title: How the Fireflies Got Their Light
passages.text:
1. Long ago, before fireflies glowed in the night sky, the world became dark each evening when the Sun disappeared below the horizon. During the day, people worked, traveled, and gathered food. At night, however, they stayed close to home because it was difficult to see in the darkness.

2. In those days, the Moon provided only a faint silver glow. Travelers often lost their way, and animals struggled to find paths through forests and fields. Many people wished for a source of light that could help guide them after sunset.

3. According to an old legend, a young girl named Lila lived in a village near a great forest. She enjoyed exploring nature and often listened to stories told by the village elders. One evening, she heard them discussing the growing difficulties caused by the dark nights.

4. "If only we had small lights to guide us," one elder said. "The nights would not seem so long."

5. Lila thought about this problem for many days. One night, she climbed a hill overlooking the forest and called out to the Sky Spirit, who was said to watch over the land.

6. To her surprise, a soft voice answered.

7. "Why have you called for me?" asked the Sky Spirit.

8. Lila explained how people struggled in the darkness and wished for light during the night.

9. The Sky Spirit listened carefully. "The stars already shine," the spirit replied.

10. "They are beautiful," Lila said, "but they are too far away to help travelers on the ground."

11. The Sky Spirit smiled at her thoughtful answer. "You care not only for yourself but for others as well. Perhaps there is a way to help."

12. The spirit gathered a handful of starlight and scattered it across the forest. Tiny sparks drifted through the air like floating embers. As they touched small insects resting among the leaves, something magical happened.

13. The insects began to glow.

14. Soon, hundreds of tiny lights flickered among the trees. The glowing insects drifted through the night, lighting paths, fields, and forest trails.

15. Travelers could now follow the gentle lights through the darkness. Children watched in wonder as the glowing insects danced above the grass. The villagers celebrated the beautiful gift.

16. "What are these creatures called?" Lila asked.

17. "They will be known as fireflies," the Sky Spirit replied. "Their light will remind people that even the smallest acts of kindness can brighten the world."

18. From that night forward, fireflies filled the summer evenings with tiny flashes of light. People believed the insects carried pieces of starlight wherever they traveled.

19. Although scientists now explain that fireflies glow because of chemical reactions inside their bodies, the old legend remains popular. Many people still enjoy the story because it explains the appearance of fireflies while teaching a lesson about helping others.

20. And so, whenever fireflies twinkle across a field on a warm summer night, some people remember the tale of Lila and the gift of starlight that brought light to the darkness.

questions.text:
What is the most important lesson, or theme, of the myth?
A  Small acts of kindness can make a big difference in the world.
B  People should always stay close to home after the Sun goes down.
C  Stars are more beautiful when they shine far away in the sky.
D  Exploring nature is the best way to make new friends.

Select two details from the story that show Lila cares about other people.

B  "they are too far away to help travelers on the ground" (paragraph 10)
C  "a young girl named Lila lived in a village near a great forest" (paragraph 3)
D  "To her surprise, a soft voice answered" (paragraph 6)
E  "From that night forward, fireflies filled the summer evenings" (paragraph 18)

Read this sentence from paragraph 12.

"Tiny sparks drifted through the air like floating embers."

The author compares the sparks to floating embers to show that they [ ▾ ].
glowed and moved softly through the air
fell quickly to the ground and burned
were cold and could not be seen
were too heavy for the insects to carry

How does the way Lila views the stars differ from the way the Sky Spirit first views them?
A  Lila thinks the stars are too far away to help, while the Sky Spirit thinks they already give enough light.
B  Lila thinks the stars are frightening, while the Sky Spirit thinks they are friendly.
C  Lila wants to remove the stars, while the Sky Spirit wants to add more stars.
D  Lila believes the stars are fake, while the Sky Spirit believes the stars are real.

title: The Fox and the Crow's Shortcut

passages.title: The Fox and the Crow's Shortcut
passages.text:
1. A crow named Clara lived in a large oak tree near the edge of a forest. Each morning, she flew to a nearby meadow to gather berries and seeds. Although the trip took time, Clara enjoyed the peaceful journey.

2. One day, Clara met a fox named Felix sitting beside the path.

3. "Why do you fly all the way to the meadow every day?" Felix asked.

4. "That is where the best food grows," Clara replied.

5. Felix grinned. "I know a shortcut. If you follow the narrow trail through the thorns, you'll reach the meadow much faster."

6. Clara looked toward the trail. Thick bushes covered both sides, and sharp thorns stretched across the path.

7. "It doesn't look very safe," she said.

8. "You're worrying too much," Felix replied. "Think of all the time you'll save."

9. Clara decided to try the shortcut. At first, the trail seemed faster. However, the thorny branches quickly became a problem. Several feathers caught on the bushes, and Clara had to stop many times to free herself.

10. By the time she reached the meadow, she was tired and frustrated.

11. The next morning, Felix saw Clara again.

12. "Did you enjoy the shortcut?" he asked.

13. "Not really," Clara admitted. "It was difficult and uncomfortable."

14. Felix shrugged. "Maybe you should try it again. Perhaps you'll be luckier."

15. Clara considered his advice. The shortcut might save time if everything went perfectly. Still, she remembered how many problems it had caused.

16. Instead of taking the thorny trail, Clara returned to her usual route. The journey was longer, but it was safe and dependable.

17. As she flew over the forest, she noticed something interesting. Along the familiar path, she discovered a patch of berry bushes she had never seen before. The berries were ripe and plentiful.

18. Clara gathered enough food for several days.

19. Later, she met Felix again.

20. "You took the long route," he said. "Didn't it take more time?"

21. "Perhaps," Clara replied, "but the safest path turned out to be the most rewarding."

22. Felix thought about her words. He realized that he often looked for the easiest solution without considering possible consequences.

23. From then on, both animals paid closer attention to the choices they made. They learned that the quickest option is not always the best one.

questions.text:
What is the most important lesson, or theme, of the fable?
A  The fastest way to do something is not always the best way.
B  Foxes cannot be trusted to give honest directions.
C  Crows should always gather enough food for several days.
D  Friends should spend as much time together as possible.

Why does Clara return to her usual route in paragraph 16 instead of taking the shortcut again?
A  She remembered how many problems the thorny trail had caused.
B  She knew the shortcut was now blocked by thicker bushes.
C  She wanted to prove to Felix that she was not afraid.
D  She had already discovered the new berry bushes on the path.

How does Felix's view about how to make choices differ from Clara's view? Select two answers.
A  Felix believes the easiest, fastest option is best, while Clara believes safety matters more than speed.
B  Felix considers possible consequences carefully, while Clara ignores them completely.
C  Felix encourages Clara to keep trying the shortcut, while Clara decides to avoid it.
D  Felix thinks the meadow has the best food, while Clara thinks the forest does.
E  Felix wants to travel with Clara, while Clara prefers to travel alone.

Read this sentence from paragraph 16: 'The journey was longer, but it was safe and dependable.' The word 'dependable' helps the reader understand that Clara's usual route was [ ▾ ].
something she could count on
more exciting than before
shorter than the shortcut
harder to follow each day

title: Johnny Appleseed and the Endless Orchard

passages.title: Johnny Appleseed and the Endless Orchard
passages.text:
1. During the early 1800s, a man named John Chapman traveled across parts of the American frontier, including areas that would later become Ohio, Indiana, and Illinois. Because he planted apple trees wherever he went, people eventually gave him the nickname Johnny Appleseed.

2. Historical records show that Chapman established nurseries and helped settlers obtain apple trees. He believed that communities would benefit from having orchards that could provide fruit and other resources.

3. Over time, stories about Johnny Appleseed grew larger than life. According to one legend, he carried a giant sack filled with enough apple seeds to plant forests that stretched from one horizon to the other.

4. Some versions of the legend claim that wherever Johnny stepped, apple trees appeared overnight. Travelers supposedly followed trails of blooming orchards that marked his path across the wilderness.

5. Another popular tale says that wild animals gathered peacefully around him. Bears, deer, and birds were said to accompany him through forests as he planted seeds and shared stories with settlers.

6. Although these details are exaggerated, they reflect the admiration many people felt for Chapman. His efforts helped communities grow, and his kindness became an important part of the legend.

7. Today, Johnny Appleseed remains one of the most famous folk heroes associated with Ohio and the American frontier. The real man planted orchards, but the legend transformed him into a larger-than-life figure whose apple trees seemed to cover the entire frontier.

passages.title: Davy Crockett and the Roaring River
passages.text:
8. Davy Crockett was a real historical figure who lived during the early 1800s. He served as a soldier, hunter, and member of Congress. His adventures on the frontier made him famous during his lifetime.

9. After Crockett's death at the Battle of the Alamo in 1836, stories about him spread throughout the United States. Many of these stories blended fact with imagination.

10. One legend tells of a time when a river flooded after days of heavy rain. According to the tale, the rushing water threatened nearby homes and farms.

11. As the story goes, Crockett stood at the edge of the river and challenged the flood to stop. When the water continued rising, he supposedly grabbed a giant log and used it to redirect the entire river.

12. The legend claims that the river changed course immediately, saving the community from disaster. Some versions even say that fish applauded by splashing their tails against the water.

13. Another story describes Crockett wrestling enormous bears and outrunning storms across the frontier. These exaggerated adventures helped build his reputation as a fearless hero.

14. Historians know that many of these stories are not literally true. However, the legends reflect qualities people admired, including courage, determination, and leadership.

15. Like the stories about Johnny Appleseed, the legends surrounding Davy Crockett transformed a real person into a legendary figure whose accomplishments seemed almost impossible.

questions.text:
Read this sentence from paragraph 3.

"According to one legend, he carried a giant sack filled with enough apple seeds to plant forests that stretched from one horizon to the other."

What does the phrase "stretched from one horizon to the other" help the reader understand about the legend?
A  The forests Johnny planted were imaginary and never truly existed.
B  The legend exaggerates how much land Johnny's trees could cover.
C  Johnny preferred planting in open fields rather than thick forests.
D  The apple seeds were too heavy for one person to carry alone.

Complete the sentence about an event in Passage 2.

When the flooded river kept rising and threatened nearby homes, the legend says that Crockett [ ▾ ].
built a tall wall of stones to block the water
grabbed a giant log and used it to redirect the river
warned the families so they could escape to safety
waited until the heavy rain finally stopped on its own

Both passages tell legends about real people from the early 1800s frontier. Select two ideas that BOTH legends share.
A  A real person's actions were exaggerated into impossible feats.
B  Wild animals gathered peacefully around the hero as he traveled.
C  The hero changed the course of a river to save a community.
D  The legend reflects qualities that people admired in the hero.
E  The hero became famous only after dying at a famous battle.

How does the way the subject is shown in Passage 1 differ from the way the subject is shown in Passage 2?
A  Passage 1 shows a hero admired for kindness and helping communities grow, while Passage 2 shows a hero admired for courage and fearless action.
B  Passage 1 shows a hero whose deeds are exaggerated, while Passage 2 shows a hero whose deeds are completely true.
C  Passage 1 shows a hero who lived long ago, while Passage 2 shows a hero who is still alive today.
D  Passage 1 shows a hero feared by settlers, while Passage 2 shows a hero loved by settlers.

Include evidence from the passages that supports your opinion. Be sure to: include an introduction; create organized paragraphs that focus on your opinion; pay attention to the grammar, structure, and mechanics of your sentences; and include a conclusion. Write your multi-paragraph response in the space provided. Legends: based on real and/or events but exaggerated Write questions here

passages.title: The Glass Mountain
passages.text:
1. Long ago, in a kingdom surrounded by forests and rivers, there stood a mountain made entirely of shining glass. Its surface sparkled in the sunlight and reflected the stars at night.

2. At the top of the mountain grew a silver apple tree that produced one magical apple each year. According to legend, the apple could heal sickness and bring peace to anyone who possessed it.

3. For many years, no one could reach the tree because the mountain's smooth sides were impossible to climb. Knights, adventurers, and travelers all tried and failed.

4. One day, a kind young woman named Elara learned that the king had become seriously ill. She decided to attempt the climb, hoping to bring back the silver apple.

5. Before leaving, Elara helped an old woman gather firewood near the edge of the forest.

6. "Thank you for your kindness," the woman said. "Take this pair of enchanted boots. They will help you when the time is right."

7. Elara thanked her and continued her journey.

8. When she reached the Glass Mountain, she discovered that the enchanted boots allowed her to walk safely on the slippery surface. Step by step, she climbed higher until she reached the silver apple tree.

9. As she picked the apple, a golden bird appeared.

10. "You have shown kindness and courage," the bird said. "Because of that, the apple belongs to you."

11. Elara returned to the kingdom and gave the apple to the king. Soon, his health improved, and the kingdom celebrated.

12. The people praised Elara not only for her bravery but also for the kindness that had helped her succeed.

passages.title: The Prince and the Moonflower
passages.text:
13. In another kingdom, a prince named Rowan searched for a magical Moonflower that bloomed only once every hundred years. The flower was said to grant wisdom to anyone who found it.

14. Rowan wanted the flower because he hoped to become a wise ruler someday. Guided by an old map, he traveled through enchanted forests and across sparkling rivers.

15. Along the way, he encountered talking animals, friendly giants, and magical creatures. Each offered advice about finding the Moonflower.

16. One evening, Rowan reached a crystal lake where a fairy appeared.

17. "The Moonflower grows on an island in the center of the lake," she explained. "But only those willing to place others before themselves may reach it."

18. As Rowan prepared to cross the lake, he noticed a family of rabbits trapped by a fallen branch.

19. Although he worried about missing the flower's bloom, Rowan stopped to help them. By the time he finished, the Moonflower had already opened.

20. Rowan hurried to the island and found the flower glowing beneath the moonlight.

21. "You have already shown wisdom," the fairy told him. "True wisdom comes from helping others."

22. Rowan returned home carrying the Moonflower. As king, he became known for making fair decisions and caring about the needs of his people.

23. The kingdom prospered, and the story of the Moonflower was passed down for generations.

questions.text:
In Passage 1, what causes Elara to receive the enchanted boots?
A  She climbs the Glass Mountain and reaches the silver apple tree (paragraph 8).
B  She helps an old woman gather firewood near the forest (paragraph 5).
C  She gives the magical apple to the sick king (paragraph 11).
D  She is praised by the people for her bravery (paragraph 12).

A  Magical creatures cannot be trusted to tell the truth.
B  True success comes from putting others before yourself.
C  Long journeys are always more dangerous than they appear.
D  People should keep magical treasures for their own families.

passages.title: The Lost Notebook
passages.text:
1. Ava hurried down the school hallway, balancing a stack of books in her arms. The final bell had already rung, and students were rushing toward buses, cars, and after-school activities.

2. As she reached her locker, she suddenly stopped.

3. "My science notebook!" she exclaimed.

4. Ava searched through her backpack, but the notebook was nowhere to be found. Her heart sank. The notebook contained notes and observations she had collected for a science project that was due the following week.

5. Trying not to panic, Ava retraced her steps. First, she checked her science classroom. The room was empty except for her teacher, Mr. Hernandez.

6. "Have you seen a blue notebook?" Ava asked.

7. Mr. Hernandez shook his head. "Not today, but let's think about where you used it last."

8. Ava remembered writing notes during lunch while discussing her project with friends. She thanked her teacher and hurried toward the cafeteria.

9. The cafeteria tables were nearly empty. Ava looked under chairs, beside trash cans, and along the walls, but the notebook was not there.

10. As she prepared to leave, she noticed a small flyer hanging near the entrance. The flyer reminded students to check the school's lost-and-found box for missing items.

11. "Maybe someone turned it in," Ava thought.

12. She walked quickly to the main office and asked the secretary if she could look through the lost-and-found.

13. At first, she saw only jackets, lunch containers, and water bottles. Then she spotted something blue tucked beneath a sweatshirt.

14. "There it is!" Ava said.

15. She pulled out the notebook and checked the pages. Everything was still inside.

16. Relieved, Ava thanked the secretary and headed home.

17. That evening, she organized her project materials and placed her notebook in a special folder.

18. The next day, she shared her experience with her classmates.

19. "I learned that staying organized can save a lot of stress," Ava said.

20. Her friends nodded in agreement. Several students decided to organize their own materials before the next big assignment.

21. A week later, Ava successfully presented her science project. As she looked at her neatly organized notes, she was glad she had learned an important lesson before it was too late.

"Ava remembered writing notes during lunch while discussing her project with friends. She thanked her teacher and hurried toward the cafeteria."

D  She blames her friends for losing the notebook during lunch.

What is the theme, or central message, of the story?

B  Teachers always know where lost items can be found.

D  It is impossible to find something once it is lost.

D  She shares her experience with her classmates the next day. (paragraph 18)
E  She successfully presents her science project a week later. (paragraph 21)

title: The Day the Donuts Took Over

passages.title: The Day the Donuts Took Over
passages.text:
1. Nobody knew exactly when it started. One moment, the town square looked ordinary. The next, giant donuts seemed to be everywhere. Frosted donuts rolled slowly down sidewalks, sprinkled donuts bounced across parks, and powdered donuts stacked themselves into wobbly towers beside buildings.

2. Bright pink frosting gleamed in the morning sunlight. Chocolate glaze dripped down the sides of towering donut piles, while rainbow sprinkles sparkled like tiny jewels. The colorful scene stretched as far as anyone could see.

3. The air smelled like a bakery. Sweet scents of cinnamon, vanilla, and warm sugar drifted through the streets. Every breeze carried another delicious smell, making people stop and sniff the air in surprise.

4. In the center of town, a giant jelly-filled donut rested beside the fountain. Smaller donuts rolled around it in slow circles, as if they were protecting their leader. Nearby, a group of maple-glazed donuts balanced on top of one another to form a crooked archway.

5. People gathered on sidewalks and rooftops to watch. Some pointed toward donut towers that seemed to grow taller each hour. Others laughed as donut holes bounced across the grass like playful rubber balls.

6. A delivery truck sat completely surrounded by blueberry donuts. Across the street, a row of sprinkled donuts stretched from one corner to another, creating a colorful path through the town square.

7. The sounds were just as unusual as the sights. Soft thumps echoed whenever a giant donut rolled across the pavement. Sprinkles rattled against windows, and occasional puffs of powdered sugar drifted through the air like tiny clouds.

8. Above it all, a towering donut covered in bright blue frosting stood beside the clock tower. Its shadow stretched across the square as townspeople stared upward in amazement.

9. The scene felt both strange and wonderful. Everywhere people looked, donuts filled the streets, parks, and buildings. For one unforgettable day, it seemed as though the town belonged entirely to donuts.

questions.text:
Which detail from the passage best supports the idea that the donuts seemed to act with a purpose, almost like living creatures?
A  "Frosted donuts rolled slowly down sidewalks, sprinkled donuts bounced across parks" (paragraph 1)
B  "Smaller donuts rolled around it in slow circles, as if they were protecting their leader" (paragraph 4)
C  "Sprinkles rattled against windows, and occasional puffs of powdered sugar drifted through the air" (paragraph 7)
D  "a towering donut covered in bright blue frosting stood beside the clock tower" (paragraph 8)

What is the central message of the vignette?
A  Hard work is always rewarded in the end.
B  An ordinary place can become magical and joyful in surprising ways.
C  People should be careful about eating too many sweets.
D  It is important to clean up a mess as soon as it appears.

Read this sentence from paragraph 5: "Others laughed as donut holes bounced across the grass like playful rubber balls." The author uses this comparison to show that the donut holes moved in a way that was [ ▾ ].
heavy and slow
lively and bouncy
quiet and still
sharp and dangerous

Select two phrases from the passage that the author uses to make the donut-filled town seem beautiful and dazzling.
A  "rainbow sprinkles sparkled like tiny jewels" (paragraph 2)
B  "A delivery truck sat completely surrounded by blueberry donuts" (paragraph 6)
C  "Bright pink frosting gleamed in the morning sunlight" (paragraph 2)
D  "Soft thumps echoed whenever a giant donut rolled across the pavement" (paragraph 7)
E  "a giant jelly-filled donut rested beside the fountain" (paragraph 4)

writingPrompt.text: Which detail contributes most to the atmosphere of The Day the Donuts Took Over: the sights, the smells, or the sounds? As you write your response: use your own words to explain your opinion; use evidence from the passage to support your points; and use clear language to express your opinion by making connections between your points and your evidence. passages.title: Donut Parade
passages.text:
(1) Donuts rolled along the street,(2) A sweet and silly sight.(3) Frosting shimmered in the sun,(4) Pink and brown and white.

(5) Sprinkles sparkled everywhere,(6) Like confetti in the air.(7) Jelly donuts bounced along,(8) Without a single care.

(9) Powdered donuts drifted past,(10) Leaving snowy trails.(11) Maple donuts marched in lines,(12) Like ships with sugary sails.

(13) Children watched in wonderment,(14) Laughing as they passed.(15) Hoping that the donut parade(16) Might somehow always last.

(17) But as the evening shadows grew,(18) The donuts rolled away.(19) Leaving only happy thoughts(20) From that delicious day.

passages.title: Snow Day Morning
passages.text:
1. Snow covered everything in sight. The rooftops, sidewalks, and trees wore thick white blankets that sparkled beneath the early morning sun. Not a patch of grass could be seen.

2. The neighborhood looked quieter than usual. Tire tracks curved through the fresh snow, and small footprints crossed front yards where children had already begun exploring. Smoke drifted slowly from chimneys into the cold winter air.

3. The sounds were soft and distant. A shovel scraped against a driveway. A dog barked somewhere down the street. Every few moments, snow slipped from tree branches with a gentle whoosh.

4. Near the park, children bundled in colorful coats built snow forts and rolled giant snowballs across the ground. Bright red scarves and blue hats stood out against the snowy landscape.

5. The cold air tingled against faces, but the scene felt cheerful and peaceful. Everywhere people looked, the world seemed transformed into a sparkling winter wonderland.

passages.title: The County Fair at Sunset
passages.text:
6. The fairgrounds glowed beneath strings of colorful lights. As the sun dipped lower in the sky, gold and orange colors stretched across the horizon behind the rides and food stands.

7. The scent of popcorn, roasted corn, and sweet funnel cakes drifted through the warm evening air. Visitors carried prizes, chatted with friends, and wandered between booths decorated with banners and ribbons.

8. Music floated from the carousel while laughter echoed from the midway. Bells rang from game booths, and excited voices rose above the crowd whenever someone won a prize.

9. Near the center of the fairgrounds, the Ferris wheel slowly turned against the colorful sky. Its lights blinked in patterns that reflected across nearby windows and puddles.

10. Families gathered around picnic tables while others waited in line for rides. The scene felt lively and welcoming, filled with movement, color, and excitement.

11. As darkness settled over the fairgrounds, the glowing lights seemed even brighter. The fair buzzed with energy, creating a memorable scene that visitors would remember long after the evening ended.

questions.text:

C  The morning was too cold for anyone to go outside.
D  The trees had been decorated for a winter holiday.

C  Both narrators warn that their scenes may become dangerous as the day goes on.

title: Hayden Nickell: Finding Balance Above the Ground

passages.title: Hayden Nickell: Finding Balance Above the Ground
passages.text:
1. Many athletes compete on fields, courts, or tracks. Hayden Nickell chose a very different path. He became known as a professional slackliner, an athlete who performs tricks and balance skills on a narrow, flexible line suspended above the ground. Through years of practice and determination, Hayden helped introduce more people to the growing sport of slacklining.

2. Hayden Nickell grew up in Colorado, where he spent much of his time outdoors. Like many athletes, he enjoyed challenges that tested his physical and mental abilities. When he discovered slacklining, he became fascinated by the balance, focus, and coordination the sport required.

3. Slacklining involves walking, balancing, and performing tricks on a flat webbing line stretched between two anchor points. Unlike a tightrope, a slackline moves and bounces. Athletes must constantly adjust their movements to stay balanced. Hayden quickly realized that success required patience and many hours of practice.

4. As his skills improved, Hayden began competing against other slackliners. He became known for performing creative tricks and developing a unique style on the line. In national competitions, he demonstrated impressive athletic ability and earned recognition among some of the sport's top competitors.

5. Hayden also explored highlining, a form of slacklining performed high above the ground. Highliners often stretch lines across canyons, cliffs, or other elevated locations. These challenges require not only physical skill but also the ability to remain calm and focused in difficult conditions.

6. As slacklining became more popular, Hayden helped introduce the sport to larger audiences. He appeared in videos, demonstrations, and events that showcased the athleticism involved in slacklining. People who had never heard of the sport before were able to see what skilled slackliners could accomplish.

7. One of the most notable opportunities in Hayden's career came when he performed as part of Madonna's MDNA World Tour. Along with other professional slackliners, he helped bring the sport to audiences around the world. Millions of people were exposed to slacklining through these performances.

8. Hayden explained that slacklining differs from many other activities because the line constantly moves beneath the athlete. Instead of controlling a completely stable surface, slackliners must react to changing conditions and maintain balance moment by moment.

9. Beyond competition and performance, Hayden's story demonstrates the importance of persistence. Learning advanced slackline skills requires athletes to fall, try again, and continue improving over time. Progress often happens gradually through repeated practice.

10. Hayden's experiences also show how passion can turn a hobby into a career. What began as an outdoor activity eventually led to competitions, performances, and opportunities to travel and share the sport with others.

11. Today, many people recognize slacklining as a sport that combines balance, strength, creativity, and concentration. Athletes like Hayden Nickell helped increase awareness of the activity and inspire others to try something new.

12. Hayden's journey reminds people that success often comes from dedication and a willingness to keep learning. By developing his skills and sharing his passion with others, he helped bring an unusual sport to a much larger audience.

questions.text:
What is the central message of the passage?
A  Slacklining is a more difficult sport than walking on a tightrope.
B  Dedication and a willingness to keep learning can lead to success.
C  Athletes who grow up in Colorado often enjoy outdoor challenges.
D  Performing in front of large audiences is the goal of every athlete.

Based on the passage, why does slacklining require so much practice and patience?
A  Athletes must climb across canyons and cliffs to reach the line.
B  The line constantly moves and bounces, so balance must be adjusted moment by moment.
C  Slackliners compete against many other athletes in national contests.
D  The webbing line is stretched between two anchor points high in the air.

one of a kind
very dangerous
slow and careful
easy to copy

Select two events that show how Hayden's interest in slacklining grew into a career.

B  He began competing against other slackliners and earned recognition among top competitors. (paragraph 4)
C  He performed as part of Madonna's MDNA World Tour for audiences around the world. (paragraph 7)
D  He realized that success required patience and many hours of practice. (paragraph 3)
E  He learned that a slackline moves and bounces unlike a tightrope. (paragraph 3)

title: Alysa Liu: A Champion on the Ice

passages.title: Alysa Liu: A Champion on the Ice
passages.text:
1. Alysa Liu has become one of the most successful and recognizable figure skaters in the United States. Known for her athletic ability, determination, and love of skating, she achieved remarkable success at a young age and inspired many people through her accomplishments on the ice. She is recognized as a history-making athlete who helped bring renewed attention to American figure skating.

2. Alysa was born in California and began skating when she was very young. Like many athletes, she spent countless hours practicing skills, learning routines, and improving her technique. Figure skating requires a combination of strength, balance, flexibility, and artistry, and Alysa worked hard to develop all of these abilities.

3. In 2019, Alysa gained national attention when she won the U.S. Figure Skating Championships at just 13 years old. Her victory made her the youngest female skater ever to win a U.S. national title. During the competition, she performed difficult jumps that many older skaters could not complete successfully.

4. Alysa continued her success the following year by winning another U.S. national championship. Her performances demonstrated both technical skill and confidence. By this time, many people viewed her as one of the brightest young talents in figure skating.

5. Throughout her career, Alysa became known for accomplishing difficult skating skills. She was the first American woman to land a quadruple jump in competition and achieved several other milestones that showcased her talent and dedication. These accomplishments helped her stand out among competitors around the world.

6. In 2022, Alysa represented the United States at the Winter Olympics in Beijing. She also earned a bronze medal at the World Figure Skating Championships that same year. These achievements placed her among the top figure skaters in the world.

7. Shortly after the 2022 season, Alysa surprised many fans by announcing her retirement from competitive skating. At only 16 years old, she decided to step away from the sport and focus on other interests. Her decision showed the importance of balancing personal well-being with athletic competition.

8. After spending time away from skating, Alysa eventually returned to competition. She approached the sport with a renewed sense of enjoyment and confidence. Her comeback demonstrated resilience and a willingness to pursue her goals even after taking a break.

9. In 2025, Alysa won the World Figure Skating Championships, becoming the first American woman in many years to earn a world title. The victory was especially impressive because it came after her return to competitive skating.

10. Alysa's success continued on the international stage. Her achievements showed that hard work, perseverance, and a positive attitude can help athletes overcome challenges and reach ambitious goals. Many young skaters looked to her as a role model.

11. Beyond medals and championships, Alysa is admired for her enthusiasm and authenticity. She has often spoken about finding joy in skating and competing on her own terms. Her experiences remind others that success includes both achievement and personal growth.

12. Today, Alysa Liu is remembered as a groundbreaking figure skater who achieved success at a young age, returned from retirement, and continued to make history. Her journey demonstrates the value of determination, resilience, and a passion for pursuing one's dreams.

questions.text:
Based on paragraphs 3 and 4, why did many people begin to view Alysa as one of the brightest young talents in figure skating?
A  She won U.S. national championships two years in a row, including becoming the youngest female skater ever to win the title.
B  She was born in California and started skating when she was very young.
C  She earned a bronze medal at the World Figure Skating Championships in 2022.
D  She decided to retire from competitive skating at only 16 years old.

Which statement best expresses a central message of the passage?
A  Determination, resilience, and passion can help a person achieve and overcome challenges to reach ambitious goals.
B  Winning a national championship at a young age guarantees lifelong success in a sport.
C  Athletes should always retire early so they can focus on other interests.
D  Figure skating is the most difficult sport for young athletes to learn.

The passage describes Alysa's comeback as showing "resilience." Select two details from the passage that support the idea that Alysa showed resilience.
A  After taking a break from skating, she returned to competition with renewed enjoyment and confidence (paragraph 8).
B  In 2025, she won the World Championships after returning to competitive skating (paragraph 9).
C  She was born in California and began skating when she was very young (paragraph 2).
D  Figure skating requires strength, balance, flexibility, and artistry (paragraph 2).
E  She earned a bronze medal at the World Championships in 2022 (paragraph 6).

Read this sentence from paragraph 8: "Her comeback demonstrated resilience and a willingness to pursue her goals even after taking a break." In this sentence, the word "comeback" most nearly means a [ ▾ ].
return to competition after being away
decision to retire from a sport
reply given during an argument
type of difficult skating jump

writingPrompt.text: Which quality contributed most to Alysa Liu's success: determination, resilience, or passion for figure skating? title: Ryan Hickman and Recycling for Change

passages.title: Ryan Hickman and Recycling for Change
passages.text:
1. Ryan Hickman was only three years old when he developed an interest in recycling. During a visit to a recycling center in California, he learned how bottles and cans could be collected and reused instead of being thrown away. The experience inspired him to take action.

2. Ryan began collecting recyclable materials from family members and neighbors. He sorted bottles and cans and brought them to local recycling centers. At first, his efforts were small, but he remained committed to helping the environment.

3. As Ryan grew older, more people learned about his work. Neighbors began saving recyclable items for him, and local businesses joined the effort. What started as a simple project expanded into a much larger community activity.

4. Over the years, Ryan recycled hundreds of thousands of bottles and cans. The money he earned from recycling helped support environmental causes and community projects. He also encouraged others to reduce waste and protect natural resources.

5. Ryan often spoke at schools and community events about the importance of caring for the environment. He explained how small actions could make a difference when many people worked together.

6. His efforts earned recognition from environmental organizations and news outlets. However, Ryan continued focusing on his goal of helping the planet rather than seeking attention.

7. Today, Ryan's story reminds people that age does not limit a person's ability to create positive change. Through dedication and leadership, he inspired others to take responsibility for protecting the environment.

passages.title: Sammie Vance and Buddy Benches
passages.text:
8. Sammie Vance was a young student from Indiana who wanted to help children feel included at school. After learning about "buddy benches," she decided to bring the idea to her community.

9. A buddy bench is a special bench placed on a playground. Students who are feeling lonely or need someone to play with can sit on the bench. Other students are encouraged to invite them to join activities and games.

10. Sammie believed that buddy benches could help students feel welcome and reduce loneliness. To make her idea a reality, she collected plastic bottle caps that could be recycled into materials used to build the benches.

11. Gathering enough bottle caps required determination and teamwork. Sammie organized collection drives and encouraged schools, families, and community members to participate.

12. Her efforts were successful. Buddy benches were installed at schools, giving students a simple way to connect with one another. Many children benefited from the opportunity to make new friends and feel included.

13. As more people heard about Sammie's project, other schools became interested in creating buddy benches of their own. Her idea spread to communities beyond her hometown.

14. Sammie's work demonstrated that solving a problem does not always require complicated solutions. Sometimes a simple idea can have a meaningful impact on the lives of many people.

15. Through leadership, compassion, and perseverance, Sammie helped create opportunities for friendship and inclusion. Her story continues to inspire young people to look for ways they can help others.

D  It started large but shrank as fewer people became interested.

E  Change happens only when adults lead the effort.

B  Ryan wanted as much fame as possible, while the organizations tried to keep his work a secret.
C  Ryan refused all help, while the news outlets forced him to keep recycling.
D  Ryan cared only about earning money, while the organizations cared only about the environment.
