passages.title: LeBron James
passages.text:
1. LeBron James is one of the most successful basketball players in history. He was born in Akron, Ohio, in 1984 and showed athletic talent from a young age. As a student, he quickly gained attention for his basketball skills and became one of the most highly anticipated young athletes in the country.

2. LeBron attended St. Vincent–St. Mary High School in Akron. During his high school career, he helped lead his team to multiple state championships. His performances attracted national media attention, and many people believed he would become a successful professional athlete.

3. After graduating from high school, LeBron entered the National Basketball Association (NBA). Throughout his career, he earned numerous awards and championships while becoming one of the league's all-time leading scorers.

4. Although LeBron achieved success on the basketball court, he also focused on helping others. He established the LeBron James Family Foundation, which supports educational opportunities for students and families.

5. One of the foundation's most notable projects is the I PROMISE School in Akron. The school provides educational support, resources, and programs designed to help students succeed both in and out of the classroom.

6. LeBron's contributions to sports and education have made him an important figure in Ohio and throughout the United States. His story demonstrates how athletic success can be combined with community service and leadership.

passages.title: Neil Armstrong
passages.text:
7. Neil Armstrong is best known as the first person to walk on the Moon. He was born in Wapakoneta, Ohio, in 1930 and developed an interest in aviation at an early age. As a child, he enjoyed learning about airplanes and dreamed of flying.

8. Armstrong earned his pilot's license while he was still a teenager. Later, he studied engineering and served as a pilot before becoming involved in the nation's space program.

9. In the 1960s, Armstrong joined NASA as an astronaut. During this time, the United States was working to achieve the goal of landing humans on the Moon.

10. On July 20, 1969, Armstrong made history when he stepped onto the Moon during the Apollo 11 mission. Millions of people around the world watched the event, which became one of the most significant achievements in space exploration.

11. After returning to Earth, Armstrong continued contributing to science, engineering, and education. He shared his knowledge and encouraged future generations to pursue careers in science and technology.

12. Today, Armstrong is remembered as a pioneer whose achievements expanded human understanding of space. His accomplishments continue to inspire people to explore, discover, and innovate.

title: The Day the Wright Flyer Took Flight

passages.title: The Day the Wright Flyer Took Flight
passages.text:
1. The cold wind swept across the sandy dunes of Kitty Hawk, North Carolina, on the morning of December 17, 1903. Waves crashed against the nearby shore, and the sky was filled with gray clouds. A small group of people stood watching as two brothers prepared for an experiment that many believed would fail.

2. Wilbur and Orville Wright had spent years studying flight. The brothers, who were originally from Ohio, operated a bicycle shop in Dayton. In their spare time, they designed gliders, built models, and tested ideas that might allow humans to fly.

3. The Wright brothers understood that birds could soar through the air, but creating a machine capable of carrying a person was far more difficult. They built wings, tested engines, and made countless adjustments. Each failure taught them something new.

4. On that chilly December morning, the brothers wheeled their newest invention onto a wooden launch track. The aircraft, called the Wright Flyer, was made of wood, fabric, and a lightweight engine they had designed themselves.

5. A few local residents and members of the lifesaving station nearby gathered to watch. Some were curious. Others were doubtful. No one knew whether the machine would actually leave the ground.

6. Orville climbed onto the Flyer while Wilbur helped steady the aircraft against the strong wind. The engine sputtered to life, and the propellers began spinning.

7. Slowly, the Flyer moved forward along the track. Then, to the amazement of everyone watching, it lifted off the ground.

8. For twelve seconds, Orville flew through the air. The aircraft traveled about 120 feet before landing in the sand. Although the flight was brief, it was the first successful powered flight by a human in history.

9. The brothers were thrilled. They shook hands and quickly began preparing for additional flights. Later that day, they completed several more attempts, each one traveling farther than the last.

10. During the final flight of the day, Wilbur flew more than 850 feet in less than a minute. The achievement proved that controlled, powered flight was possible.

11. News of the accomplishment spread slowly at first. Many people found it difficult to believe that humans could fly. However, as the Wright brothers continued improving their designs, more people recognized the importance of their work.

12. In the years that followed, airplanes became faster, stronger, and more reliable. What began as a short flight above the sands of Kitty Hawk eventually transformed transportation around the world.

13. Airplanes connected distant cities, allowed people to travel across oceans, and made it possible to deliver supplies and aid to communities in need. The invention changed business, communication, science, and exploration.

14. Looking back, it is easy to see why the Wright brothers' achievement is remembered as one of the most important events in history. Their determination, creativity, and willingness to learn from failure helped turn a dream into reality.

15. On that windy morning in 1903, the spectators witnessed more than a successful experiment. They witnessed the beginning of a new era of transportation that would change the world forever.

questions.text:

passages.title: Lost in the Alaskan Wilderness
passages.text:
1. In July 1928, a pilot named Carl Ben Eielson prepared for a flight across the remote wilderness of Alaska. Eielson was known for flying in some of the most challenging conditions in the world. He often transported supplies, mail, and passengers to isolated communities that could not easily be reached by road.

2. On this particular journey, Eielson and his passenger traveled over vast stretches of forests, rivers, and mountains. Below them, the landscape seemed endless. Small settlements were separated by hundreds of miles of rugged terrain.

3. As the flight continued, weather conditions began to change. Thick clouds moved into the area, and strong winds made flying more difficult. Pilots in Alaska often faced sudden weather changes that could create dangerous situations.

4. Despite his experience, Eielson knew he needed to proceed carefully. Visibility decreased as clouds surrounded the aircraft. The landscape below became harder to see, making navigation more challenging.

5. When conditions worsened, the pilot searched for a safe place to land. Continuing through the storm could place both travelers at risk. After carefully assessing the situation, he guided the aircraft toward a flat area in the wilderness.

6. The landing was rough, but the plane reached the ground safely. Although they were unharmed, Eielson and his passenger now faced a new challenge. They were far from any town and surrounded by wilderness.

7. The pair understood that survival depended on staying calm and making good decisions. They gathered supplies from the aircraft and worked to protect themselves from the cold weather.

8. Days passed as they waited for help. Search teams knew the flight was overdue and began looking for signs of the missing aircraft. However, Alaska's enormous size made the search difficult.

9. During the wait, Eielson relied on his training and experience. He carefully rationed supplies and focused on maintaining shelter and safety. His actions helped increase the chances that both travelers would remain healthy until rescue arrived.

10. Meanwhile, search crews faced challenges of their own. Pilots searched from the air while others explored possible routes on the ground. Harsh weather and remote terrain slowed their efforts.

11. Eventually, rescuers located the stranded travelers. After days of uncertainty, Eielson and his passenger were safely brought back to civilization. Their survival was the result of preparation, patience, and sound decision-making.

12. Stories like this helped demonstrate the risks faced by early aviators. Pilots operating in remote regions often encountered conditions that modern travelers rarely experience.

13. Eielson continued contributing to aviation throughout his career. He became known as a pioneer who helped connect distant communities through air travel and demonstrated remarkable courage in difficult circumstances.

14. His experiences remind us that exploration and transportation often require perseverance. Even when unexpected challenges arise, careful planning and determination can help people overcome obstacles.

15. The story of Carl Ben Eielson's survival in the Alaskan wilderness remains an example of how preparation, patience, and good judgment can make a difference during a real-life adventure.

questions.text:

B  Careful planning and good judgment can help people overcome unexpected challenges.

title: Ernest Shackleton and the Endurance Expedition

passages.title: Ernest Shackleton and the Endurance Expedition
passages.text:
1. In 1914, explorer Ernest Shackleton set out on a journey to Antarctica. His goal was to lead the first expedition to cross the frozen continent on foot. Shackleton and his crew sailed aboard a ship called the Endurance.

2. As the ship traveled south, it entered fields of thick sea ice. The crew hoped the ice would eventually break apart and allow them to continue. Instead, the ice trapped the Endurance and prevented it from moving.

3. For months, the ship drifted with the ice. The crew waited through the long Antarctic winter while temperatures remained far below freezing. Supplies were carefully managed, and everyone worked together to survive.

4. Eventually, the pressure of the surrounding ice crushed the ship. The crew watched as the Endurance slowly sank beneath the frozen sea.

5. Stranded far from civilization, Shackleton and his crew established camps on the ice. They faced extreme cold, strong winds, and limited resources. Despite these challenges, Shackleton focused on keeping the group united and hopeful.

6. When the ice began breaking apart, the crew used lifeboats to reach a small island. However, the island offered little chance of rescue.

7. Determined to save his men, Shackleton selected several crew members and began a dangerous journey across rough seas in a small boat. After traveling hundreds of miles through stormy waters, they reached South Georgia Island.

8. Shackleton then crossed mountains and glaciers to reach a whaling station and organize a rescue mission.

9. Months after the ship was lost, every member of the expedition was rescued. Although the expedition failed to cross Antarctica, Shackleton's leadership helped ensure the survival of his entire crew.

passages.title: Juliane Koepcke's Survival in the Rainforest
passages.text:
10. On December 24, 1971, seventeen-year-old Juliane Koepcke boarded a flight over the Amazon rainforest in Peru. She was traveling with her mother to visit family during the holiday season.

11. During the flight, a powerful storm developed. Lightning struck the aircraft, causing severe damage. Moments later, the plane broke apart high above the rainforest.

12. Juliane survived the accident but found herself alone in one of the largest rainforests in the world. Surrounded by dense vegetation, she had no easy way to contact rescuers.

13. Although injured, Juliane remembered survival skills she had learned from her parents, who were scientists working in the rainforest. She knew that following water could lead to signs of people.

14. Using this knowledge, Juliane began walking along small streams. She faced many dangers, including insects, heat, hunger, and exhaustion. Yet she continued moving forward each day.

15. For more than a week, Juliane traveled through the rainforest. She carefully used the survival knowledge she had learned and focused on staying calm despite her situation.

16. Eventually, she discovered a small boat near a remote shelter used by local workers. Soon afterward, people found her and helped her return to safety.

17. Juliane's remarkable survival story became known around the world. Her experience demonstrated the importance of knowledge, determination, and remaining calm during emergencies.

passages.title: The Flood of 1913
passages.text:
1. In March 1913, heavy rain fell across much of Ohio. At first, many people welcomed the rain because it followed a snowy winter. However, as the storms continued day after day, rivers and streams began rising rapidly.

2. In Dayton, Ohio, residents watched the Great Miami River climb higher and higher. Water spilled over riverbanks and flooded nearby streets. People grew concerned, but few expected what would happen next.

3. On March 25, floodwaters rushed through the city. Entire neighborhoods became submerged. Families climbed to upper floors and rooftops as water filled homes and businesses. Boats moved through streets that were normally crowded with wagons and pedestrians.

4. Telephone lines stopped working, making communication difficult. Rescue workers struggled to reach stranded residents. Volunteers worked around the clock, rowing through dangerous currents to bring people to safety.

5. As the floodwaters spread, thousands of people were forced to leave their homes. Schools, churches, and public buildings became temporary shelters for families who had nowhere else to go.

6. Despite the destruction, community members helped one another. Neighbors shared food, clothing, and supplies. Rescue teams risked their own safety to save others.

7. When the flood finally receded, residents faced the enormous task of rebuilding. Streets were covered in mud and debris. Homes and businesses required extensive repairs.

8. In response, Ohio leaders developed new flood-control systems, including dams and levees. These improvements helped reduce the risk of future flooding and protected communities throughout the region.

9. The Great Flood of 1913 remains one of the most significant natural disasters in Ohio's history. It demonstrated both the power of nature and the determination of Ohio communities.

passages.title: Ohio Celebrates the Moon Landing
passages.text:
10. On July 20, 1969, people across Ohio gathered around televisions to witness a historic event. Millions of Americans were preparing to watch astronaut Neil Armstrong become the first person to walk on the Moon.

11. Armstrong had grown up in Wapakoneta, Ohio. Because of his Ohio roots, many residents felt a special connection to the Apollo 11 mission.

12. Families crowded into living rooms, schools, and community centers. Some people stayed awake late into the night because they did not want to miss the moment.

13. As the lunar module landed on the Moon's surface, excitement spread throughout the state. Television announcers described every step of the mission while viewers watched carefully.

14. Then came the moment everyone had been waiting for. Neil Armstrong stepped onto the Moon and spoke words that would be remembered around the world.

15. Cheers erupted in homes and public gathering places across Ohio. Many people felt proud that someone from their state had helped achieve such an extraordinary accomplishment.

16. Newspapers quickly printed special editions. Schools discussed the mission, and students imagined future careers in science, engineering, and space exploration.

17. The Moon landing inspired many young people to study mathematics, technology, and science. It also demonstrated what could be accomplished through innovation, teamwork, and determination.

18. Today, museums and memorials throughout Ohio continue to celebrate Armstrong's achievement. The Moon landing remains one of the most important events connected to Ohio's history.

D  Residents in both passages wanted to leave Ohio and live somewhere else.

passages.title: Exploring the Solar System
passages.text:
1. The solar system is a vast collection of objects that travel through space around the Sun. At the center of the solar system is the Sun, a massive star that provides light and heat to the planets and other celestial bodies that orbit it. The solar system includes eight planets, dwarf planets, moons, asteroids, comets, and countless smaller objects.

2. Scientists believe the solar system formed about 4.6 billion years ago from a giant cloud of gas and dust. Over time, gravity pulled material together to form the Sun and the many objects that now orbit it.

3. Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun. It is a rocky planet with a surface covered in craters. Because Mercury has very little atmosphere, temperatures can become extremely hot during the day and very cold at night.

4. Venus is often called Earth's "sister planet" because it is similar in size. However, Venus has a thick atmosphere that traps heat, making it the hottest planet in the solar system. Its surface temperature is hot enough to melt some metals.

5. Earth is the third planet from the Sun and the only known planet that supports life. Liquid water covers much of Earth's surface, and its atmosphere contains the oxygen that many living things need to survive.

6. Mars, sometimes called the Red Planet, appears reddish because of iron-rich dust on its surface. Scientists have discovered evidence that liquid water may have existed on Mars long ago. Today, robotic spacecraft continue exploring the planet.

7. Beyond Mars lies a group of giant planets known as the outer planets. Jupiter is the largest planet in the solar system. It is a gas giant with powerful storms, including the Great Red Spot, a storm that has lasted for hundreds of years.

8. Saturn is famous for its spectacular rings, which are made mostly of ice and rock particles. Although other planets have rings, Saturn's are the largest and most visible.

9. Uranus is unique because it rotates on its side compared to most other planets. Its blue-green color comes from methane gas in its atmosphere.

10. Neptune is the farthest planet from the Sun. Strong winds race through its atmosphere at speeds faster than those of many hurricanes on Earth. Neptune's deep blue appearance also results from methane gas.

11. In addition to planets, the solar system contains many other celestial bodies. Dwarf planets such as Pluto orbit the Sun but do not meet all the requirements needed to be classified as planets. Asteroids, comets, and moons also contribute to the solar system's diversity.

12. Each planet has unique characteristics that help scientists understand how the solar system formed and changed over time. By studying planets and other celestial bodies, researchers continue to learn more about the universe beyond Earth.

questions.text:

title: Our Sun and the Stars Beyond

passages.title: Our Sun and the Stars Beyond
passages.text:
1. On a clear night, thousands of stars can be seen shining in the sky. Although they may appear small and similar from Earth, stars vary greatly in size, temperature, color, and brightness. Scientists have discovered that the Sun, which seems much larger than the other stars, is actually one of billions of stars that exist in the universe.

2. A star is a massive ball of hot gases that produces its own light and energy. Deep inside a star, powerful reactions release enormous amounts of heat and light. This energy travels through space and can be seen from great distances.

3. The Sun is the star at the center of our solar system. It provides the light and warmth that make life on Earth possible. Without the Sun's energy, plants could not grow, water would freeze, and most living things would not survive.

4. The Sun appears larger and brighter than other stars because it is much closer to Earth. On average, the Sun is about 93 million miles (150 million kilometers) away from our planet. In comparison, the next closest star beyond the Sun is more than 25 trillion miles away.

5. Although the Sun is very large compared to Earth, it is not one of the largest stars in the universe. Some stars are many times larger than the Sun. For example, certain giant stars are so enormous that millions of Earths could fit inside them.

6. Stars come in different colors, including red, orange, yellow, white, and blue. A star's color often indicates its temperature. Blue stars are among the hottest, while red stars are generally cooler. The Sun is classified as a yellow star.

7. Scientists use powerful telescopes to study stars. These instruments collect light from distant objects and allow researchers to learn about stars that are far beyond our solar system. Telescopes on Earth and in space have helped scientists discover billions of stars in our galaxy alone.

8. Our galaxy, the Milky Way, contains hundreds of billions of stars. The Milky Way is just one of countless galaxies in the universe. Because there are so many galaxies, scientists estimate that the universe contains an enormous number of stars.

9. Not all stars are the same age. Stars form from large clouds of gas and dust. Over millions of years, gravity pulls the material together until a new star begins producing energy. Eventually, stars change as they age and may end their lives in different ways depending on their size.

10. Some stars exist alone, while others are part of star systems that contain two or more stars orbiting one another. Scientists continue to study these systems to better understand how stars form and interact.

11. The Sun is an ordinary star in many ways, but it plays an extraordinary role in our lives. Its energy supports life on Earth and drives many natural processes, including weather patterns and the water cycle.

12. By studying the Sun and other stars, scientists learn more about the universe and our place within it. The stars that fill the night sky remind us that Earth is part of a much larger cosmic system that continues to inspire exploration and discovery.

questions.text:
Which main ideas are developed in the passage?

B. All stars are the same size, and the Sun is the largest star in the universe.
C. Stars are planets that orbit the Milky Way, and they cannot be studied.
D. The Sun is close to Earth, so it is not a real star.

C. By describing only the Sun's distance from Earth.
D. By giving examples of planets in the solar system.

passages.title: Why Day and Night Happen
passages.text:
1. Every morning, the Sun appears to rise in the east and move across the sky. By evening, it seems to disappear below the western horizon. For thousands of years, people observed this daily pattern and wondered why it happened.

2. Today, scientists know that the Sun is not actually moving around Earth each day. Instead, Earth rotates on its axis. An axis is an imaginary line that runs through the planet from the North Pole to the South Pole.

3. Earth completes one full rotation approximately every 24 hours. As the planet turns, different parts of Earth's surface face the Sun.

4. When a location on Earth faces the Sun, it experiences daylight. Sunlight reaches that area, making the sky appear bright and allowing people to see clearly.

5. As Earth continues rotating, that location gradually turns away from the Sun. When it no longer faces the Sun, nighttime begins.

6. This cycle repeats every day. Because Earth's rotation is predictable, people can determine when the Sun will rise and set in different locations throughout the year.

7. Earth's rotation also helps explain why people in different parts of the world experience day and night at different times. While one side of Earth faces the Sun, the opposite side faces away from it.

8. The regular pattern of Earth's rotation creates one of the most predictable cycles on our planet. Clocks, calendars, and daily schedules are all based on this dependable motion.

passages.title: Why Seasons Change
passages.text:
9. Another predictable pattern involving Earth and the Sun is the cycle of seasons. Unlike day and night, which result from Earth's rotation, seasons are caused by Earth's movement around the Sun and the tilt of its axis.

10. Earth revolves around the Sun once every year. As it travels along its orbit, different parts of the planet receive varying amounts of sunlight.

11. Earth's axis is tilted at an angle. Because of this tilt, one hemisphere receives more direct sunlight during part of the year, while the other hemisphere receives less.

12. When the Northern Hemisphere is tilted toward the Sun, it experiences summer. Days are generally longer, and sunlight reaches the surface more directly.

13. At the same time, the Southern Hemisphere experiences winter because it is tilted away from the Sun. Sunlight reaches the surface less directly, and days are often shorter.

14. About six months later, the situation reverses. The Northern Hemisphere experiences winter while the Southern Hemisphere experiences summer.

15. Spring and autumn occur during the transition periods between these seasons. During these times, the amount of sunlight received by each hemisphere becomes more balanced.

16. Because Earth's orbit and tilt remain consistent, the seasons occur in a predictable pattern year after year. Scientists can accurately predict when each season will begin.

17. Together, Earth's rotation, revolution, and tilted axis create cycles that affect daily life. These predictable motions influence everything from weather patterns and agriculture to school calendars and holiday traditions.

questions.text:
How do the two passages work together to explain Earth-Sun cycles?
A. Passage 1 explains day and night through rotation, while Passage 2 explains seasons through revolution and Earth's tilt. [CORRECT]
B. Passage 1 explains seasons, while Passage 2 explains why the Moon changes shape.
C. Both passages say the Sun moves around Earth each day.
D. Both passages focus only on calendars and holidays.

B. Earth's tilt makes the Sun move around Earth.
C. Earth's tilt causes day and night every 24 hours.
D. Earth's tilt prevents scientists from predicting seasons.

passages.title: Following the Shadow
passages.text:
1. Long before modern clocks and calendars existed, people carefully observed the Sun's movement across the sky. They noticed that shadows changed position throughout the day and that the Sun appeared to follow the same path day after day.

2. Ancient farmers depended on these observations. By watching the Sun's position, they could estimate the time of day and plan important tasks such as planting, harvesting, and caring for crops.

3. Scientists now understand that the Sun's apparent movement across the sky is caused by Earth's rotation. Earth spins on its axis approximately once every 24 hours.

4. As Earth rotates, the Sun appears to rise in the east, move across the sky, and set in the west. This pattern repeats every day in a highly predictable cycle.

5. Because the cycle is consistent, people can accurately predict sunrise and sunset times. Calendars and scientific observations make it possible to determine these times months or even years in advance.

6. The predictable pattern of daylight and darkness influences many aspects of life. School schedules, transportation systems, work hours, and recreational activities are often planned around the daily cycle of sunlight.

7. Many living things also respond to this pattern. Birds, insects, plants, and other organisms rely on the regular cycle of day and night to guide their behavior.

8. Earth's rotation creates one of the most dependable natural patterns on the planet. Every day, the cycle begins again, providing a predictable rhythm that affects people and ecosystems around the world.

passages.title: Measuring the Year
passages.text:
9. While Earth's rotation creates the daily cycle of day and night, another predictable motion affects life on a much longer timescale. Earth revolves around the Sun once every year, completing a journey that takes about 365 days.

10. As Earth travels along its orbit, the tilt of its axis causes different parts of the planet to receive varying amounts of sunlight throughout the year.

11. This changing sunlight creates the seasons. In the Northern Hemisphere, summer occurs when that part of Earth is tilted toward the Sun. Winter occurs when it is tilted away.

12. Because Earth's orbit and tilt remain consistent, the seasons follow a predictable pattern. Scientists can determine the dates of solstices and equinoxes years in advance.

13. The seasonal cycle affects agriculture, weather patterns, tourism, and many cultural traditions. Farmers often use seasonal changes to decide when crops should be planted and harvested.

14. Animals also respond to seasonal patterns. Some birds migrate, while certain mammals prepare for winter by storing food or changing their behavior.

15. Throughout history, people have created calendars based on Earth's yearly motion around the Sun. Holidays, celebrations, and important events are often connected to seasonal changes.

16. The predictable cycle of seasons allows communities to prepare for changing weather conditions and plan activities throughout the year.

17. Together, Earth's daily rotation and yearly revolution create patterns that people can predict with remarkable accuracy. These cycles help humans understand time and organize life on Earth.

questions.text:
How are the two passages structured?

B. Both list planets in order from the Sun.
C. Passage 1 is a personal story, while Passage 2 is a poem.
D. Passage 1 gives only problems, while Passage 2 gives only solutions.

passages.title: Force, Mass, and Motion
passages.text:
1. Every day, people observe objects moving. A soccer ball rolls across a field, a shopping cart moves through a store, and a bicycle travels down a sidewalk. Although these movements may seem simple, scientists have learned that changes in motion depend on several factors, including force and mass.

2. A force is a push or a pull that can change an object's motion. Forces can cause objects to start moving, stop moving, speed up, slow down, or change direction. When a person kicks a soccer ball, pushes a wagon, or pulls a sled, a force is being applied.

3. Mass is the amount of matter in an object. Objects with greater mass contain more matter than objects with less mass. For example, a bowling ball generally has more mass than a tennis ball.

4. The amount of change in an object's motion depends on both the force applied and the object's mass. In general, a larger force causes a greater change in motion. A smaller force causes a smaller change.

5. Imagine two identical soccer balls resting on a field. If one ball is kicked gently and the other is kicked with much greater force, the second ball will usually move faster and travel farther. The stronger kick creates a greater change in motion.

6. Mass also affects how objects respond to force. Suppose a student pushes an empty shopping cart and a fully loaded shopping cart using the same amount of force. The empty cart will likely accelerate more quickly because it has less mass.

7. Scientists often observe this relationship during experiments. By changing either the amount of force or the mass of an object, they can study how motion changes. These investigations help researchers understand how objects move in different situations.

8. Vehicles provide another example. A small car and a large truck may both be traveling on a highway. If the same braking force is applied to each vehicle, the smaller car often changes its motion more easily because it has less mass.

9. Engineers use their understanding of force and mass when designing products. They consider how much force machines can produce and how different materials affect the mass of an object. These decisions help create safer and more efficient vehicles, equipment, and tools.

10. Athletes also rely on these principles. A baseball pitcher applies force to throw a ball. A golfer applies force to a golf ball. In each case, the amount of force influences how the object moves.

11. Even in space, force and mass play important roles. Rockets must generate enormous amounts of force to overcome Earth's gravity and lift their massive structures into orbit. Scientists carefully calculate these forces before every launch.

12. Understanding the relationship between force, mass, and motion helps explain many events that occur in everyday life. Whether moving a toy car, riding a bicycle, or launching a spacecraft, the same scientific principles apply.

13. By studying how force and mass affect motion, scientists and engineers can make predictions, solve problems, and design technologies that improve the way people live and work.

questions.text:

B. Objects with greater mass always move faster than objects with less mass.

C. A smaller car often changes its motion more easily than a large truck with the same braking force. [CORRECT]
D. A pitcher applies force to throw a baseball.
E. Rockets generate force to lift off.

B. By explaining that all objects have the same mass.
C. By describing only the history of bicycles.
D. By listing planets that orbit the Sun.

title: The Predictable Patterns of Light and Sound

passages.title: The Predictable Patterns of Light and Sound
passages.text:
1. Every day, people use their senses to gather information about the world around them. We see objects because of light, and we hear sounds because of sound energy. Although light and sound are different forms of energy, both behave in predictable ways that scientists can study and explain.

2. Light is a form of energy that travels in waves. One important characteristic of light is that it can travel through empty space. This is why sunlight is able to reach Earth from the Sun, even though space contains very little matter.

3. When light strikes an object, several things can happen. Some light may be absorbed, some may pass through the object, and some may be reflected. Reflection occurs when light bounces off a surface.

4. Mirrors provide a common example of reflection. When light reflects from a mirror, it creates an image that allows people to see themselves. Smooth, shiny surfaces generally reflect light more effectively than rough surfaces.

5. Light can also be refracted. Refraction occurs when light changes direction as it passes from one material into another. For example, a straw placed in a glass of water may appear bent because light travels differently through air and water.

6. Scientists rely on these predictable behaviors when designing tools such as eyeglasses, microscopes, telescopes, and cameras. These devices help people observe objects that are too small, too distant, or too difficult to see clearly with the naked eye.

7. Sound is another form of energy that behaves in predictable ways. Unlike light, sound cannot travel through empty space. Sound requires matter, such as air, water, or solids, to carry its vibrations.

8. Sound begins when an object vibrates. These vibrations create waves that move through a material. When the waves reach a person's ears, the brain interprets them as sound.

9. Different sounds have different characteristics. Some sounds are loud, while others are quiet. Some sounds are high-pitched, while others are low-pitched. These differences depend on the way the sound waves are produced.

10. Sound waves can also reflect from surfaces. An echo occurs when sound waves bounce off an object and return to the listener. People may hear echoes in large rooms, caves, or canyons.

11. Scientists and engineers use their understanding of sound to design concert halls, classrooms, theaters, and recording studios. By controlling how sound waves travel and reflect, they can improve the quality of what people hear.

12. Although light and sound behave differently, both follow predictable patterns. Scientists can use these patterns to make observations, solve problems, and develop technologies that improve daily life.

13. From mirrors and cameras to musical instruments and communication systems, many tools depend on the predictable behavior of light and sound energy. Understanding these forms of energy helps people better understand the world around them.

questions.text:
Which main ideas are developed in the passage?
A. Light and sound are forms of energy that behave in predictable ways and are used in many tools. [CORRECT]
B. Light and sound travel the same way through empty space.
C. Sound can be seen in mirrors, and light begins with vibrations.
D. Energy cannot be studied by scientists.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.

Drop-down options: light changing direction as it passes into another material [CORRECT] / sound bouncing back to a listener / light disappearing inside a mirror / vibrations moving through air

D. Echoes can be heard in caves or canyons.
E. Cameras depend on predictable light behavior.

How does understanding predictable energy patterns help people?
A. It allows scientists and engineers to design tools that improve seeing, hearing, and communication. [CORRECT]
B. It proves sound can move through empty space.
C. It prevents light from reflecting off surfaces.
D. It makes all sounds have the same pitch.

passages.title: The Science of Soccer
passages.text:
1. During a soccer match, players constantly apply force to change the motion of the ball. A gentle tap may cause the ball to roll slowly across the field, while a powerful kick can send it soaring through the air toward the goal.

2. The amount of force applied affects how much the ball's motion changes. When a player kicks the ball harder, the ball usually moves faster and travels farther. A smaller force produces a smaller change in motion.

3. Coaches often teach players how to control the amount of force they use. A short pass to a nearby teammate requires less force than a long pass across the field.

4. Scientists explain these changes using the relationship between force and motion. The greater the force applied to an object, the greater the change in its movement is likely to be.

5. Soccer players rely on this principle throughout every game. Whether passing, shooting, or stopping the ball, they adjust the amount of force they use to achieve different results.

6. Even goalkeepers use force when they block or catch a fast-moving ball. By applying force with their hands, they can reduce the ball's motion and bring it to a stop.

7. Understanding how force affects motion helps athletes improve their performance and make more accurate movements during competition.

passages.title: Moving Heavy Loads
passages.text:
8. Imagine two wagons sitting side by side. One wagon is empty, while the other is filled with bags of soil. Although both wagons appear similar, they have different amounts of mass.

9. When a person pushes the empty wagon, it begins moving fairly easily. However, pushing the loaded wagon requires much more effort.

10. Mass plays an important role in determining how an object responds to force. Objects with greater mass are generally more difficult to speed up, slow down, or change direction.

11. If the same amount of force is applied to both wagons, the empty wagon will usually experience a greater change in motion because it has less mass.

12. Workers, engineers, and transportation companies consider mass when moving heavy materials. Large trucks, trains, and construction equipment must generate greater forces to move heavier loads.

13. The relationship between force and mass affects many activities in everyday life. It influences how vehicles operate, how machines are designed, and how people move objects from one place to another.

14. Scientists study these patterns because they help explain how motion works. By understanding both force and mass, people can predict how objects are likely to move under different conditions.

questions.text:
How do the two passages present factors that affect motion?
A. Passage 1 focuses on the amount of force applied, while Passage 2 focuses on the mass of an object. [CORRECT]
B. Passage 1 focuses on light, while Passage 2 focuses on sound.
C. Both passages say mass and force do not affect motion.
D. Passage 1 explains wagons, while Passage 2 explains soccer.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In Passage 2, mass means ( ).
Drop-down options: the amount of matter in an object [CORRECT] / the speed of a wagon / the distance a ball travels / a type of soccer kick

A. A powerful kick can send a ball farther than a gentle tap. [CORRECT]
B. Wagons can be empty or filled with soil.
C. Goalkeepers use force to reduce a ball's motion and stop it. [CORRECT]
D. Large trucks need greater forces to move heavy loads.
E. Scientists study patterns of motion.

What conclusion can be drawn from both passages?
A. Predicting motion requires considering both the force on an object and the object's mass. [CORRECT]
B. Objects with more mass are always easier to move.
C. Athletes do not use scientific principles during games.
D. The same amount of force changes every object's motion equally.

passages.title: The Science of Light
passages.text:
1. Light is a form of energy that allows people to see the world around them. Whether sunlight shines through a window or a flashlight illuminates a dark path, light behaves in predictable ways that scientists can observe and study.

2. One important property of light is that it travels in straight lines. This predictable behavior helps explain why shadows form. When an object blocks light, a shadow appears on the surface behind it.

3. Light can also be reflected. Reflection occurs when light bounces off a surface. Smooth surfaces, such as mirrors, reflect light in a way that creates clear images. Rough surfaces reflect light in many directions, making images difficult to see.

4. Another predictable behavior of light is refraction. Refraction occurs when light changes direction as it moves from one material to another. For example, a pencil placed in a glass of water may appear bent because light travels differently through air and water.

5. Scientists use their understanding of light to create useful technologies. Cameras, microscopes, telescopes, and eyeglasses all depend on the predictable behavior of light.

6. Light can travel through empty space, which allows sunlight to reach Earth from millions of miles away. Without light energy from the Sun, life on Earth would not be possible.

7. Because light follows consistent patterns, scientists can predict how it will behave in different situations and use that knowledge to solve problems and make discoveries.

passages.title: The Science of Sound
passages.text:
8. Like light, sound is a form of energy that behaves in predictable ways. However, sound travels differently from light. Sound requires matter, such as air, water, or solids, to carry its energy.

9. Sound begins when an object vibrates. These vibrations create waves that move through a material. When the waves reach a person's ears, they are interpreted as sound.

10. Sound waves can travel through many materials, but they do not travel at the same speed in each one. For example, sound generally travels faster through solids than through air.

11. Sound also reflects from surfaces. When sound waves bounce back to a listener, an echo is produced. Echoes can often be heard in canyons, caves, and large empty rooms.

12. The pitch and loudness of sounds depend on the characteristics of the sound waves. A whistle produces a different sound from a drum because the vibrations are different.

13. Engineers use their understanding of sound when designing buildings, theaters, and concert halls. By controlling how sound waves travel and reflect, they can improve what audiences hear.

14. Scientists also use sound waves in technologies such as sonar and medical imaging equipment. These tools help people explore underwater environments and examine parts of the human body.

15. Because sound behaves in predictable ways, people can use it to communicate, gather information, and develop technologies that improve everyday life.

questions.text:
How are the two passages structured similarly?
A. Each passage explains a form of energy by describing its properties and useful technologies. [CORRECT]
B. Each passage tells a story about one inventor.

passages.title: Roles in an Ecosystem
passages.text:
1. An ecosystem is a community of living and nonliving things that interact with one another. Forests, deserts, wetlands, grasslands, and oceans are all examples of ecosystems. Within each ecosystem, organisms perform different roles that help keep the system functioning.

2. One important group of organisms is producers. Producers are living things that make their own food, usually through photosynthesis. During this process, plants use sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide to create the food they need to survive.

3. Producers include grasses, trees, shrubs, wildflowers, and algae. Because they create their own food, producers serve as the primary source of energy for most ecosystems.

4. Another group of organisms is consumers. Consumers cannot make their own food, so they must obtain energy by eating plants, animals, or both. Herbivores, such as deer and rabbits, eat plants. Carnivores, such as hawks and wolves, eat other animals. Omnivores, such as bears and humans, eat both plants and animals.

5. Consumers play an important role in transferring energy throughout an ecosystem. When one organism eats another, energy moves through the food web.

6. A third group of organisms is decomposers. Decomposers break down dead plants, animals, and waste materials. Common decomposers include fungi, bacteria, earthworms, and certain insects.

7. As decomposers break down organic matter, they return nutrients to the soil. Plants can then absorb these nutrients and use them to grow. This process helps recycle materials within an ecosystem.

8. Each role is connected to the others. Producers provide energy for consumers. Decomposers recycle nutrients that producers need. Consumers help move energy through the ecosystem. Together, these interactions create a balanced system.

9. Scientists often study food webs to understand how organisms depend on one another. A change affecting one group can influence many other organisms. For example, if a disease destroys large numbers of plants, herbivores may struggle to find food, which can also affect predators.

10. Ecosystems remain healthy when producers, consumers, and decomposers perform their roles successfully. Each group contributes to the flow of energy and the cycling of matter.

11. Although their jobs are different, all three groups are important. The survival of many organisms depends on the interactions among producers, consumers, and decomposers.

12. By studying these roles, scientists can better understand how ecosystems function and how living things depend on one another for survival.

questions.text:
Which main ideas are developed in the passage?
A. Organisms have different roles in ecosystems, and those roles are connected. [CORRECT]
B. Only decomposers are important in ecosystems.
C. Consumers make their own food through photosynthesis.
D. Ecosystems contain living things but not nonliving things.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In paragraph 6, decomposers are organisms that ( ).
Drop-down options: break down dead plants, animals, and waste [CORRECT] / make their own food from sunlight / eat only living animals / stop energy from moving

Select TWO relationships among ecosystem roles.

B. Herbivores eat only other animals.
C. Decomposers return nutrients to the soil that producers need. [CORRECT]
D. Ecosystems remain healthy when one role disappears.
E. Algae are always consumers.

How does the author support the point that ecosystem roles are connected?
A. By explaining how producers, consumers, and decomposers move energy and recycle matter. [CORRECT]
B. By describing one forest animal in detail.
C. By saying ecosystems never change.
D. By focusing only on nonliving parts of ecosystems.

passages.title: Energy: The Power Behind Life
passages.text:
1. Every living organism requires energy to survive. From the smallest bacterium to the largest whale, all living things depend on energy to carry out the processes that keep them alive. Without a continuous supply of energy, organisms would be unable to grow, move, reproduce, or respond to their environments.

2. Energy is needed for all of the activities that occur within an organism. Even when an animal appears to be resting, its body is still using energy. The heart pumps blood, the lungs move oxygen, and cells carry out countless chemical reactions.

3. Plants also require energy. Through photosynthesis, plants capture energy from sunlight and use it to produce food. This food provides the energy plants need for growth, repair, and reproduction.

4. Animals cannot produce their own food like plants. Instead, they obtain energy by consuming plants, other animals, or both. During digestion, food is broken down into smaller substances that cells can use as a source of energy.

5. Cells are often called the basic units of life. Every cell in an organism requires energy to function properly. Cells use energy to transport materials, build new structures, remove waste, and carry out chemical processes necessary for survival.

6. Growth is one process that depends heavily on energy. Young organisms require energy to develop new tissues and increase in size. As organisms grow, their cells divide and produce additional structures, all of which require energy.

7. Movement is another activity that depends on energy. Animals use energy to walk, run, swim, fly, and perform other actions. Even plants use energy for movements such as opening flowers, growing toward sunlight, or responding to environmental changes.

8. Organisms also need energy to maintain internal stability. For example, many animals use energy to regulate body temperature, repair damaged tissues, and fight diseases.

9. Reproduction requires energy as well. Producing seeds, eggs, offspring, or other reproductive structures involves many biological processes that depend on a steady supply of energy.

10. Energy moves through ecosystems as organisms obtain food. Plants capture energy from the Sun. Herbivores obtain energy by eating plants, and carnivores obtain energy by eating other animals. In this way, energy flows from one organism to another.

11. Scientists study how organisms use energy because it helps explain how living systems function. By understanding energy use, researchers can learn more about growth, behavior, health, and survival.

12. Although organisms may look very different from one another, they all share one important characteristic: they require energy. Every process that takes place within a living organism depends on energy in some way.

13. Whether a tree is growing, a bird is flying, or a cell is dividing, energy is the driving force behind life. Without it, the processes necessary for survival could not occur.

questions.text:
Which main idea is developed throughout the passage?
A. All living organisms require energy for the processes that keep them alive. [CORRECT]
B. Only animals need energy to survive.
C. Energy is used only when organisms are moving.
D. Photosynthesis prevents plants from needing food.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In paragraph 8, internal stability means ( ).
Drop-down options: balanced conditions inside an organism's body [CORRECT] / movement from place to place / sunlight stored in food / a plant's outer structure

Select TWO details that show organisms use energy even when they are not moving.
A. The heart pumps blood and the lungs move oxygen. [CORRECT]
B. Animals use energy to walk and swim.
C. Cells transport materials, build structures, and remove waste. [CORRECT]
D. Plants grow toward sunlight.
E. Food is broken down during digestion.

How does energy move through ecosystems according to paragraph 10?
A. Plants capture energy from the Sun, and animals obtain energy by eating plants or other animals. [CORRECT]
B. Animals produce sunlight, and plants eat animals.
C. Energy stops moving after plants use it.
D. Cells create energy without food or sunlight.

writingPrompt.text: Which process requires energy in the most important way: growth, movement, or maintaining internal stability? As you write your response: use your own words to explain your opinion; use evidence from the passage to support your points; and use clear language to express your opinion by making connections between your points and your evidence. passages.title: The Role of Pollinators
passages.text:
1. In many ecosystems, plants depend on animals called pollinators to help them reproduce. Pollinators move pollen from one flower to another, allowing plants to produce seeds and fruits.

2. Bees are among the most well-known pollinators. As a bee gathers nectar from a flower, pollen sticks to its body. When the bee visits another flower, some of the pollen is transferred.

3. Butterflies, hummingbirds, bats, and certain beetles also serve as pollinators. Although these organisms differ in appearance and behavior, they all help plants reproduce.

4. Pollinators play an important role in ecosystems because many plants rely on them. Without pollinators, some plants would struggle to produce seeds and new generations of plants.

5. The work of pollinators also benefits other organisms. Many animals depend on fruits, seeds, and plants for food. By helping plants reproduce, pollinators support entire food webs.

6. Scientists have found that healthy pollinator populations contribute to biodiversity, which is the variety of living things within an ecosystem.

7. Because pollinators support plant reproduction and food production, they are considered one of the most important groups of organisms in many ecosystems.

passages.title: The Role of Decomposers
passages.text:
8. While pollinators help plants reproduce, decomposers perform a very different role. Decomposers break down dead plants, animals, and waste materials that accumulate in ecosystems.

9. Organisms such as fungi, bacteria, earthworms, and certain insects are common decomposers. They feed on dead organic matter and gradually break it apart.

10. As decomposition occurs, nutrients are released into the soil. Plants absorb these nutrients through their roots and use them to grow.

11. Without decomposers, nutrients would remain trapped in dead organisms. Over time, fewer nutrients would be available for living plants.

12. Decomposers also help prevent dead material from building up in ecosystems. Fallen leaves, dead trees, and animal remains are continually recycled through decomposition.

13. This recycling process allows matter to move through ecosystems. Nutrients released by decomposers can be used repeatedly by future generations of organisms.

14. Scientists recognize decomposers as essential because they help maintain healthy soil and support the continued growth of plants.

15. By recycling nutrients and breaking down organic matter, decomposers help ecosystems remain balanced and productive.

questions.text:
How are pollinators and decomposers similar?
A. Both perform roles that help plants and support healthy ecosystems. [CORRECT]
B. Both make their own food through photosynthesis.
C. Both move pollen from flower to flower.

C. Decomposers prevent dead material from building up in ecosystems. [CORRECT]
D. Pollinators help plants produce fruits.
E. Butterflies and hummingbirds can be pollinators.

How are the passages structured?
A. Each passage explains one ecosystem role by describing what the organisms do and why the role matters. [CORRECT]
B. Passage 1 is a timeline, while Passage 2 is a personal account.
C. Each passage lists only problems with ecosystems.
D. Passage 1 compares climates, while Passage 2 compares planets.

passages.title: Energy for Growth and Repair
passages.text:
1. Every living organism requires energy to survive. One of the most important uses of energy is growth and repair. From the moment an organism begins life, energy is needed to build new cells and tissues.

2. Plants use energy from sunlight to produce food through photosynthesis. The energy stored in this food allows plants to grow roots, stems, leaves, flowers, and fruits.

3. Animals obtain energy by consuming food. The nutrients in food are broken down and used by cells throughout the body. These cells rely on energy to grow and divide.

4. Young organisms often require large amounts of energy because they are increasing in size. A growing tree, a developing bird, and a young human all need energy to build new tissues.

5. Energy is also necessary for repair. When organisms become injured or when cells wear out, new cells must be produced to replace damaged ones. This process depends on a steady supply of energy.

6. Even after organisms reach adulthood, growth and repair continue. Skin cells are replaced, bones heal after injuries, and plants produce new leaves and branches.

7. Without energy, organisms would be unable to grow, recover from damage, or maintain healthy tissues. Growth and repair are essential processes that support survival throughout an organism's life.

passages.title: Energy for Movement and Internal Functions
passages.text:
8. While energy supports growth and repair, it is also needed for movement and many internal processes. Organisms use energy every moment of every day, even when they appear to be resting.

9. Animals require energy to move. Walking, running, swimming, flying, and even blinking all depend on energy. Muscles use energy whenever they contract and produce motion.

10. However, not all energy use is visible. Inside every organism, cells perform countless activities that require energy. These activities include transporting materials, removing waste, and carrying out chemical reactions.

11. Many animals use energy to maintain stable internal conditions. For example, mammals use energy to regulate body temperature, keeping their bodies warm even when environmental temperatures change.

12. The heart continuously pumps blood throughout the body, and the lungs move oxygen into and out of the bloodstream. Both processes require energy.

13. Plants also use energy for internal functions. They transport water and nutrients, produce new cells, and respond to environmental changes such as sunlight and temperature.

14. Scientists have found that all life processes depend on energy. Whether an organism is moving, growing, reproducing, or maintaining internal stability, energy makes these activities possible.

15. The constant need for energy explains why organisms must continually obtain or produce food. Without energy, the processes necessary for life would stop.

questions.text:
What idea is developed in both passages?
A. Energy is required for all major life processes, whether they are visible or happening inside organisms. [CORRECT]
B. Only young organisms need energy.
C. Plants use energy only for movement.
D. Energy is not needed after organisms reach adulthood.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In Passage 1, tissues means ( ).
Drop-down options: groups of cells that make up parts of an organism [CORRECT] / food made only by animals / sunlight used for photosynthesis / movements made by muscles

Select TWO details from Passage 2 that show energy is used for internal functions.
A. Cells transport materials and remove waste. [CORRECT]
B. Young organisms increase in size.
C. Mammals use energy to regulate body temperature. [CORRECT]
D. Plants grow roots, stems, and leaves.
E. Injured organisms produce new cells.

How do the two passages organize information differently?
A. Passage 1 focuses on growth and repair, while Passage 2 focuses on movement and internal functions. [CORRECT]
B. Passage 1 explains animals only, while Passage 2 explains plants only.
C. Both passages are organized as fictional stories.
D. Both passages argue that food is unnecessary for organisms.

passages.title: Early Civilizations of the Americas
passages.text:
1. Long before Europeans arrived in the Western Hemisphere, advanced civilizations flourished throughout North, Central, and South America. These societies developed unique governments, social structures, religions, technologies, and agricultural systems that allowed them to build large communities and support growing populations.

2. One of the most well-known civilizations was the Maya, who lived in parts of present-day Mexico, Guatemala, Belize, Honduras, and El Salvador. The Maya built impressive cities with stone temples, pyramids, and public plazas. They developed a system of writing, studied mathematics, and created detailed calendars that helped them track time and plan agricultural activities.

3. The Maya relied heavily on farming. They grew crops such as corn, beans, and squash. Farmers adapted to the tropical environment by developing techniques that helped them make productive use of available land.

4. Farther south, the Inca civilization controlled a vast empire along the Andes Mountains in South America. The Inca built an extensive network of roads and bridges that connected distant communities across challenging mountain terrain.

5. Inca leaders organized a strong government that managed resources and coordinated large construction projects. The civilization is also known for its agricultural innovations. Farmers created terraces, or flat areas carved into hillsides, allowing crops to be grown on steep mountain slopes.

6. The Aztec civilization developed in central Mexico. Their capital city, Tenochtitlán, was built on islands in a large lake and became one of the largest cities in the world at that time.

7. Aztec society included rulers, priests, warriors, merchants, artisans, and farmers. Religion played an important role in daily life and influenced many ceremonies and traditions.

8. To support their large population, the Aztecs developed chinampas, sometimes called "floating gardens." These human-made farming areas increased the amount of land available for growing food and helped provide crops for the city's residents.

9. In North America, the Mississippian culture developed near major river systems, especially along the Mississippi River and its tributaries. These communities built large earthen mounds that served as centers for government, religion, and public gatherings.

10. Cahokia, located near present-day St. Louis, became one of the largest Mississippian settlements. Thousands of people lived there, and the community included homes, public spaces, and large ceremonial structures.

11. Like the Maya, Inca, and Aztec civilizations, the Mississippian people relied on agriculture. Corn was an especially important crop and helped support growing populations.

12. Although these civilizations developed in different regions and environments, they shared several characteristics. Each created organized governments, established social structures, practiced religious traditions, and developed technologies that helped communities thrive.

13. Their accomplishments demonstrate that sophisticated societies existed throughout the Americas long before European exploration. The achievements of the Maya, Inca, Aztec, and Mississippian peoples continue to be studied today because they provide valuable insights into history, culture, and human innovation.

questions.text:
Which main ideas are developed in the passage?
A. Several advanced civilizations existed in the Americas before Europeans arrived, and each developed systems that helped its society grow. [CORRECT]
B. Only one civilization existed in the Americas before Europeans arrived.
C. All early civilizations in the Americas lived in the same environment.
D. European colonies created the Maya, Inca, Aztec, and Mississippian cultures.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.

Drop-down options: highly developed or complex [CORRECT] / recently discovered / located near oceans / simple and unorganized

C. The Aztecs developed chinampas to increase land for growing food. [CORRECT]
D. Aztec society included priests and warriors.
E. Cahokia was near present-day St. Louis.

A. It helped support growing populations and allowed large communities to develop. [CORRECT]
B. It prevented governments from forming.
C. It made roads and bridges unnecessary.
D. It caused all civilizations to develop in the same region.

title: Exploration, Colonization, and a Changing Hemisphere

passages.title: Exploration, Colonization, and a Changing Hemisphere
passages.text:
1. During the 1400s, many European nations became interested in exploring new lands. Advances in shipbuilding, navigation, and mapmaking made long ocean voyages more possible than ever before. Countries such as Spain, Portugal, England, France, and the Netherlands hoped to find new trade routes, gain wealth, and expand their influence.

2. In 1492, Christopher Columbus sailed across the Atlantic Ocean while searching for a route to Asia. Although he did not reach Asia, his voyage connected Europe with the Americas in ways that would permanently change the Western Hemisphere.

3. Following Columbus's journey, other explorers traveled to the Americas. Some searched for valuable resources, while others claimed land for their home countries. These expeditions increased European knowledge of the Western Hemisphere and encouraged additional exploration.

4. As exploration continued, European nations established colonies throughout North, Central, and South America. Colonies were settlements controlled by a distant country. Colonists built towns, farms, and trading centers that allowed European nations to strengthen their presence in the region.

5. Colonization brought significant changes to the lives of American Indians. European settlers often claimed lands already occupied by Indigenous peoples. In some areas, cooperation and trade developed between groups. In other areas, conflicts arose over land, resources, and power.

6. Europeans also introduced new plants, animals, and technologies to the Americas. Horses, cattle, wheat, and other resources became part of life in many regions. At the same time, crops that originated in the Americas, such as corn, potatoes, and tomatoes, were introduced to Europe and other parts of the world.

7. This exchange of plants, animals, goods, and ideas became known as the Columbian Exchange. The Columbian Exchange transformed diets, agriculture, and economies on both sides of the Atlantic Ocean.

8. However, not all changes were positive. Europeans brought diseases such as smallpox to the Americas. Because Indigenous peoples had not previously been exposed to these diseases, many communities experienced devastating population losses.

9. Colonization also led to the movement of people across the Atlantic. Millions of Africans were forcibly transported to the Americas through the transatlantic slave trade. Their labor contributed to the growth of colonial economies, while their cultures influenced the development of societies throughout the Western Hemisphere.

10. Over time, European languages, religions, customs, and systems of government became established in many parts of the Americas. These influences can still be seen today in place names, laws, languages, and cultural traditions.

11. The effects of exploration and colonization continue to shape the modern world. Many countries in the Western Hemisphere speak languages introduced by European settlers, including English, Spanish, Portuguese, and French.

12. Economic systems, transportation networks, agricultural practices, and cultural traditions were also influenced by interactions among Indigenous peoples, Europeans, and Africans during this period.

13. Historians study exploration and colonization because these events help explain how societies in the Western Hemisphere developed. Understanding this history provides insight into the connections among cultures, economies, and governments today.

14. The exploration and colonization of the Americas during the 1400s through 1600s changed the course of history. The lasting effects of these events can still be observed throughout the Western Hemisphere, making this period an important part of understanding the world today.

questions.text:
Which summary best reflects the passage?
A. European exploration and colonization connected continents, changed cultures and economies, and had lasting effects on the Western Hemisphere. [CORRECT]
B. Columbus reached Asia in 1492 and ended European exploration.
C. Colonization affected only European languages and had no other effects.
D. The Columbian Exchange moved only plants from Europe to the Americas.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In paragraph 4, colonies are ( ).
Drop-down options: settlements controlled by a distant country [CORRECT] / ships used for ocean travel / crops from the Americas / diseases carried by insects

Select TWO lasting effects of exploration and colonization described in the passage.
A. European languages became widely spoken in parts of the Americas. [CORRECT]
B. All Indigenous communities gained land.
C. Crops, animals, goods, and ideas moved between continents. [CORRECT]
D. The transatlantic slave trade ended immediately.
E. European nations stopped building colonies after 1492.

How does the author support the point that colonization had lasting effects?
A. By giving examples such as languages, laws, cultural traditions, and agricultural practices that remain visible today. [CORRECT]
B. By focusing only on one explorer's childhood.
C. By saying the Western Hemisphere did not change.
D. By listing planets that were discovered by Europeans.

writingPrompt.text: Which lasting effect of European exploration and colonization had the greatest impact on the Western Hemisphere: the Columbian Exchange, the spread of European cultures and governments, or the movement of people across the Atlantic? In your own words, write a multi-paragraph response that explains which effect you think had the greatest impact. Include evidence from the passage that supports your opinion. As you write your response: use your own words to explain your opinion; use evidence from the passage to support your points; and use clear language to express your opinion by making connections between your points and your evidence. Be sure to: include an introduction; create organized paragraphs that focus on your opinion; pay attention to the grammar, structure, and mechanics of your sentences; and include a conclusion. Write your multi-paragraph response in the space provided.

passages.title: The Maya and Aztec Civilizations
passages.text:
1. Long before Europeans arrived in the Americas, advanced civilizations developed throughout the Western Hemisphere. Among the most influential were the Maya and the Aztec, whose achievements in government, religion, technology, and agriculture helped shape life in Mesoamerica, a region that includes parts of present-day Mexico and Central America.

2. The Maya civilization flourished for hundreds of years in areas that are now Mexico, Guatemala, Belize, Honduras, and El Salvador. The Maya built large cities with stone temples, pyramids, and public gathering places. These cities served as centers of government, religion, and trade.

3. Maya society was organized into city-states, each ruled by its own leader. Although the city-states sometimes cooperated, they also competed with one another for power and resources.

4. The Maya made important advances in mathematics, astronomy, and writing. They developed a complex calendar system that helped them track time and plan religious ceremonies and agricultural activities. Their writing system allowed them to record historical events and communicate information.

5. Agriculture was central to Maya life. Farmers grew crops such as corn, beans, and squash. They developed farming techniques that allowed them to adapt to the tropical environment and support large populations.

6. Centuries later, the Aztec civilization became one of the most powerful societies in the region. The Aztec capital, Tenochtitlán, was built on islands in a large lake and became one of the largest cities in the world during its time.

7. Aztec society included rulers, priests, warriors, merchants, artisans, and farmers. Religion influenced many aspects of daily life and played an important role in government and culture.

8. To feed their growing population, the Aztecs created chinampas, often called floating gardens. These human-made farming areas increased food production and allowed the city to support hundreds of thousands of residents.

passages.title: The Inca and Mississippian Civilizations
passages.text:
9. While the Maya and Aztec civilizations developed in Mesoamerica, other advanced societies thrived elsewhere in the Western Hemisphere. Two examples are the Inca civilization of South America and the Mississippian culture of North America.

10. The Inca controlled a vast empire that stretched along the Andes Mountains. Their territory included parts of present-day Peru, Ecuador, Bolivia, Chile, and Argentina. Governing such a large region required strong leadership and organization.

11. The Inca developed an extensive network of roads and bridges that connected distant communities. Messengers traveled these routes to deliver information and help maintain communication throughout the empire.

12. Farming in the Andes Mountains presented challenges because of the steep terrain. To solve this problem, the Inca built terraces, which were flat areas carved into hillsides. These terraces allowed crops to be grown efficiently in mountainous regions.

13. Farther north, the Mississippian culture developed near major rivers, especially the Mississippi River and its tributaries. These waterways supported trade, transportation, and agriculture.

14. One of the largest Mississippian settlements was Cahokia, located near present-day St. Louis. Thousands of people lived there, making it one of the largest communities in North America before European contact.

15. The Mississippian people built enormous earthen mounds that served as centers for government, religion, and community activities. Corn agriculture helped support their growing population and contributed to the success of their settlements.

16. Although the Maya, Aztec, Inca, and Mississippian civilizations developed in different environments, each created organized societies with unique governments, technologies, religions, and agricultural systems that allowed their communities to thrive.

questions.text:
Which idea is supported by both passages?
A. Early American civilizations developed organized societies and used technology or agriculture to adapt to their environments. [CORRECT]
B. All four civilizations used the same writing system.
C. The Maya and Inca lived in the same mountain region.
D. The Mississippian culture built chinampas in a lake.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In Passage 2, territory means ( ).
Drop-down options: land controlled or occupied by a group [CORRECT] / a type of calendar / a farming tool / a religious ceremony

Select TWO details that show how civilizations solved environmental challenges.
A. The Aztecs created chinampas to increase food production. [CORRECT]
B. Maya city-states sometimes competed for power.
C. The Inca built terraces to farm steep mountain land. [CORRECT]
D. Mississippian mounds served as community centers.
E. Maya cities had public gathering places.

How are the passages organized?
A. Passage 1 compares the Maya and Aztec, while Passage 2 compares the Inca and Mississippian cultures. [CORRECT]
B. Both passages are organized as a day-by-day timeline.
C. Passage 1 explains only agriculture, while Passage 2 explains only religion.
D. Both passages describe European exploration first.

writingPrompt.text: Which civilization made the most impressive adaptations to its environment: the Maya, Aztec, Inca, or Mississippian civilization?
