Write your multi-paragraph response in the space provided.

passages.title: The Columbian Exchange
passages.text:
1. In 1492, Christopher Columbus's voyage across the Atlantic Ocean connected Europe, Africa, and the Americas in new ways. As exploration increased, people, goods, plants, animals, and ideas began moving between continents. Historians call this movement the Columbian Exchange.

2. Before European exploration, many plants and animals were found only in certain parts of the world. After contact between continents increased, these resources began spreading to new regions.

3. Crops that originated in the Americas, including corn, potatoes, tomatoes, and cacao, were introduced to Europe. These foods eventually became important parts of diets around the world and helped support growing populations.

4. Europeans brought wheat, rice, sugarcane, horses, cattle, pigs, and sheep to the Americas. Some of these introductions transformed farming and transportation. For example, horses became valuable for travel, hunting, and trade in many Indigenous communities.

5. The Columbian Exchange also included the movement of knowledge, technologies, and cultural practices. As people interacted, they shared ideas that influenced daily life in both the Eastern and Western Hemispheres.

6. However, not all exchanges were beneficial. Europeans brought diseases such as smallpox, measles, and influenza to the Americas. Indigenous peoples had little immunity to these diseases, and many communities experienced severe population losses.

7. Despite its challenges, the Columbian Exchange permanently changed the Western Hemisphere. The crops, animals, and cultural influences introduced during this period continue to affect agriculture, economies, and daily life today.

passages.title: Building New Colonies
passages.text:
8. As exploration continued during the 1400s through 1600s, several European nations established colonies throughout the Americas. Spain, England, France, Portugal, and the Netherlands claimed land and built settlements that would influence the future of the Western Hemisphere.

9. Colonies were created for many reasons. Some settlers sought economic opportunities, while others hoped to spread their religion or increase their country's power.

10. Over time, European languages became common in many parts of the Americas. Today, English, Spanish, Portuguese, and French are spoken throughout the Western Hemisphere because of colonization.

11. European systems of government also influenced the development of many countries. Ideas about laws, citizenship, and representative government shaped political institutions that continue to exist today.

12. Colonization brought people from different cultures into contact with one another. Indigenous peoples, Europeans, and Africans contributed traditions, foods, languages, music, and customs that helped create diverse societies.

13. The movement of millions of Africans through the transatlantic slave trade had a profound impact on the Americas. African cultures, traditions, and knowledge became important parts of life throughout the Western Hemisphere.

14. Although colonization created opportunities for some groups, it also led to conflicts, displacement, and hardships for many Indigenous communities. These effects continue to influence discussions about history and society today.

15. The governments, languages, cultures, and populations found throughout the Western Hemisphere were shaped in significant ways by European colonization. Understanding these developments helps explain many features of modern societies.

questions.text:
How do the passages describe different lasting effects of exploration?
A. Passage 1 focuses on the movement of goods and diseases, while Passage 2 focuses on colonies, culture, language, and government. [CORRECT]
B. Passage 1 focuses on modern maps, while Passage 2 focuses on planets.
C. Both passages say exploration caused no lasting changes.
D. Passage 1 explains colonization only, while Passage 2 explains photosynthesis.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In Passage 1, immunity means ( ).
Drop-down options: protection against a disease [CORRECT] / a trade route across the ocean / a crop introduced to Europe / a government system

Select TWO details that show colonization changed the Western Hemisphere.
A. European languages such as English, Spanish, Portuguese, and French became common. [CORRECT]
B. Crops such as potatoes and tomatoes were introduced to Europe.
C. European systems of government influenced later political institutions. [CORRECT]
D. Horses became valuable in many Indigenous communities.
E. Corn and cacao originated in the Americas.

How are the two passages structured?
A. Each explains causes and effects of contact between continents during exploration and colonization. [CORRECT]
B. Both tell events in the life of one explorer.
C. Passage 1 lists only benefits, while Passage 2 lists only inventions.
D. Both are organized as instructions for building a colony.

writingPrompt.text: Which had a greater lasting impact on the Western Hemisphere: the Columbian Exchange or European colonization? In your own words, write a multi-paragraph response that explains which development you think had a greater lasting impact. Include evidence from both passages that supports your opinion. As you write your response: use your own words to explain your opinion; use evidence from the passage to support your points; and use clear language to express your opinion by making connections between your points and your evidence. Be sure to: include an introduction; create organized paragraphs that focus on your opinion; pay attention to the grammar, structure, and mechanics of your sentences; and include a conclusion. Write your multi-paragraph response in the space provided.

passages.title: A Hemisphere of Many Cultures
passages.text:
1. The Western Hemisphere includes North America, Central America, South America, and the Caribbean. Although these regions are connected by geography, they are home to many different cultures. The cultural diversity found throughout the Western Hemisphere developed over centuries as people from various parts of the world interacted, exchanged ideas, and influenced one another.

2. Long before European exploration, Indigenous peoples lived throughout the Western Hemisphere. Groups such as the Maya, Inca, Aztec, and many others developed unique languages, traditions, art forms, governments, and religious beliefs. These cultures continue to influence life in many countries today.

3. Beginning in the late 1400s, European explorers and settlers arrived in the Americas. People from Spain, England, France, Portugal, and other nations established colonies throughout the Western Hemisphere. As a result, European languages became widely spoken. Today, Spanish, English, Portuguese, and French are among the most common languages in the region.

4. European influences can also be seen in architecture, government systems, education, and religious practices. Many countries in the Western Hemisphere continue to celebrate holidays and traditions that originated in Europe.

5. Another important influence came from Africa. During the transatlantic slave trade, millions of Africans were forcibly transported to the Americas. Although this migration occurred under tragic circumstances, African cultures had a lasting impact on the Western Hemisphere.

6. African influences can be seen in music, dance, storytelling, art, language, and food. Musical styles such as jazz, samba, reggae, and many forms of popular music developed from a combination of African traditions and other cultural influences.

7. Food provides another example of cultural exchange. Many dishes found throughout the Western Hemisphere combine ingredients and cooking techniques from Indigenous, European, African, and later Asian cultures. Foods such as corn, potatoes, tomatoes, rice, and spices reflect centuries of interaction among different groups.

8. Religion also demonstrates cultural diversity. Christianity is practiced widely throughout the hemisphere, but many communities also follow Judaism, Islam, Hinduism, Buddhism, Indigenous spiritual traditions, and other belief systems. In some areas, religious practices combine elements from multiple cultural traditions.

9. Immigration has continued to shape the Western Hemisphere. During the 1800s and 1900s, many people arrived from Asia, the Middle East, and other parts of the world seeking new opportunities. These newcomers brought additional languages, customs, foods, and traditions.

10. Art and literature often reflect the blending of cultures. Artists, writers, musicians, and performers draw inspiration from their diverse backgrounds and experiences. Their work helps preserve cultural traditions while also creating new forms of expression.

11. Cities throughout the Western Hemisphere often contain neighborhoods where multiple cultures are represented. Festivals, museums, restaurants, and community events celebrate the traditions of different groups and encourage cultural understanding.

12. The interactions among Indigenous peoples, Europeans, Africans, Asians, and many other groups have created societies that are rich in cultural diversity. These influences can be seen in everyday life, from the languages people speak to the foods they eat and the music they enjoy.

13. Understanding cultural diversity helps people appreciate the many contributions different groups have made to society. It also highlights the importance of respecting traditions, perspectives, and experiences that may differ from one's own.

14. The Western Hemisphere's cultural diversity is the result of centuries of movement, interaction, and exchange among people from around the world. This rich mixture of cultures continues to shape the region today and contributes to its unique identity.

questions.text:
Which main ideas are developed in the passage?
A. The Western Hemisphere is culturally diverse because many groups interacted and influenced language, food, religion, art, and music. [CORRECT]
B. The Western Hemisphere has only one culture.
C. Cultural diversity ended after European exploration.
D. Food and music are not connected to culture.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In paragraph 12, interaction means ( ).
Drop-down options: people or groups affecting one another [CORRECT] / a single language spoken everywhere / a map of a country / a type of farming

Select TWO details that support the idea that cultures blended in the Western Hemisphere.
A. Many dishes combine ingredients and cooking techniques from Indigenous, European, African, and Asian cultures. [CORRECT]
B. The Western Hemisphere includes the Caribbean.
C. Some religious practices combine elements from multiple cultural traditions. [CORRECT]
D. European explorers arrived in the late 1400s.
E. Cities often contain neighborhoods.

How does the author support the point that many cultures shaped the region?
A. By giving examples from language, music, food, religion, art, and immigration. [CORRECT]
B. By describing only one city in the Western Hemisphere.
C. By arguing that cultural traditions should not be studied.
D. By listing only countries in Europe.

writingPrompt.text: Which cultural influence has had the greatest impact on the Western Hemisphere: Indigenous cultures, European cultures, African cultures, or later immigrant cultures? In your own words, write a multi-paragraph response that explains which cultural influence you think has had the greatest impact. Include evidence from the passage that supports your opinion. As you write your response: use your own words to explain your opinion; use evidence from the passage to support your points; and use clear language to express your opinion by making connections between your points and your evidence. Be sure to: include an introduction; create organized paragraphs that focus on your opinion; pay attention to the grammar, structure, and mechanics of your sentences; and include a conclusion. Write your multi-paragraph response in the space provided.

passages.title: Understanding Regions
passages.text:
1. People often divide large areas into smaller regions to better understand the world. A region is an area that shares one or more common characteristics. These characteristics may be related to landforms, climate, population, culture, or economic activities. By studying regions, geographers can compare different places and understand how people interact with their environments.

2. One way regions are identified is by their landforms. Landforms are natural features on Earth's surface, such as mountains, plains, deserts, valleys, and plateaus. For example, the Rocky Mountain region of North America is known for its high elevations and rugged terrain. In contrast, the Great Plains region is characterized by broad, flat grasslands.

3. Climate is another important factor used to define regions. Climate refers to the long-term weather patterns of an area. Some regions have hot, dry climates, while others experience cold winters, heavy rainfall, or mild temperatures throughout the year.

4. The climate of a region often influences how people live. In tropical regions, people may grow crops that require warm temperatures and abundant rainfall. In colder regions, homes are often built to withstand snow and freezing conditions.

5. Population is also used to identify regions. Population refers to the number of people living in a particular area. Some regions are densely populated, meaning many people live within a relatively small space. Others are sparsely populated and have fewer residents spread across large areas.

6. Large cities often develop in regions with access to transportation, resources, and economic opportunities. Urban regions may contain millions of people, while rural regions typically have lower population densities and more open land.

7. Cultural characteristics help define regions as well. Culture includes language, religion, traditions, art, music, food, and other shared practices. People living within a cultural region may share similar customs and ways of life.

8. For example, certain regions may be recognized for the languages commonly spoken there, while others may be known for unique celebrations, architectural styles, or artistic traditions. Cultural regions can sometimes cross political boundaries because people with similar traditions may live in different countries.

9. Economic characteristics are another way regions can be classified. Economic activities include the production, distribution, and consumption of goods and services. Some regions are known for agriculture, while others specialize in manufacturing, mining, technology, tourism, or trade.

10. The availability of natural resources often influences economic development. Regions with fertile soil may focus on farming, while regions with valuable minerals may develop mining industries.

11. Geographers frequently combine several types of data when identifying regions. A region may be defined by a combination of climate, population, culture, and economic activity rather than by a single characteristic.

12. Understanding regions helps people analyze patterns and relationships across the world. Governments, businesses, scientists, and educators use regional information to make decisions, plan projects, and study human activities.

13. Regions are not always permanent. Population growth, economic changes, migration, technological developments, and environmental conditions can alter the characteristics of a region over time.

14. By examining landforms, climate, population, cultural traits, and economic activities, geographers gain a clearer understanding of how places are similar and different. These characteristics help organize information about the world and explain why regions develop unique identities.

questions.text:
Which main ideas are developed in the passage?
A. Regions are defined by shared characteristics, and geographers use several kinds of data to understand them. [CORRECT]
B. Regions are permanent and never change.
C. Regions can be identified only by mountains and rivers.
D. Culture and economics are not useful for studying places.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In paragraph 5, densely populated means ( ).
Drop-down options: having many people living in a small area [CORRECT] / having few residents spread out / having only farms / having no transportation

Select TWO characteristics geographers may use to define regions.
A. Landforms such as mountains, plains, deserts, and valleys. [CORRECT]
B. The number of pages in a map book.
C. Cultural traits such as language, religion, traditions, and food. [CORRECT]
D. The names of all planets.
E. The age of a single building.

What relationship does the passage explain between climate and human activities?
A. Climate can influence crops, homes, clothing, and how people live in a region. [CORRECT]
B. Climate is chosen by governments.
C. Climate has no effect on human life.
D. Climate depends only on population density.

writingPrompt.text: Which characteristic is most useful for defining a region: landforms, climate, population, cultural characteristics, or economic characteristics? In your own words, write a multi-paragraph response that explains which characteristic you think is most useful for defining a region. Include evidence from the passage that supports your opinion. As you write your response: use your own words to explain your opinion; use evidence from the passage to support your points; and use clear language to express your opinion by making connections between your points and your evidence. Be sure to: include an introduction; create organized paragraphs that focus on your opinion; pay attention to the grammar, structure, and mechanics of your sentences; and include a conclusion. Write your multi-paragraph response in the space provided.

passages.title: Reading the World with Maps
passages.text:
1. Every day, people use geographic tools to learn about places, people, and environments. One of the most common geographic tools is a map. Maps help people understand where things are located and how different places are connected.

2. Maps can provide many types of information. A political map shows countries, states, and cities. A physical map shows natural features such as mountains, rivers, deserts, and plains. Other maps may display climate zones, population patterns, transportation routes, or land use.

3. Geographers use maps to gather and analyze information. For example, a population map can help researchers identify areas where many people live. A climate map can show regions that receive large amounts of rainfall or experience dry conditions.

4. Modern technology has expanded the ways people use maps. Global Positioning System (GPS) devices, satellite images, and digital mapping tools allow users to collect and view information with remarkable accuracy.

5. Emergency responders use maps to locate people during natural disasters. Scientists use maps to study environmental changes. Businesses use maps to determine where new stores should be built.

6. Maps also help people understand relationships between human activities and the environment. By examining different types of maps, geographers can identify patterns and make informed decisions.

7. Because maps organize large amounts of information into visual formats, they remain one of the most valuable geographic tools available today.

passages.title: The Work of Cartographers
passages.text:
8. Although maps may seem straightforward, creating them requires careful planning. The people who design maps are called cartographers. Cartographers must decide which information to include and how that information should be displayed.

9. No map can show every detail about a location. As a result, cartographers choose information based on the map's purpose. A road map emphasizes highways and streets, while a weather map focuses on temperature, precipitation, and storm systems.

10. Cartographers also select symbols, colors, and labels that make information easier to understand. Blue is often used to represent water, while green may indicate forests or parks.

11. Scale is another important consideration. A map of an entire country cannot display as much detail as a map of a single city. Cartographers must decide how much information can fit while keeping the map readable.

12. Modern cartographers use computer software, satellite images, and geographic data to create maps. These technologies allow maps to be updated more quickly than in the past.

13. The choices made by cartographers can influence how people interpret information. For example, emphasizing certain features may help users focus on specific topics, such as transportation, climate, or population.

14. Because maps are designed for different purposes, people often use multiple maps when studying a place. Comparing information from several maps can provide a more complete understanding of an area.

15. Through their decisions about what information to include and how to present it, cartographers help people gather, process, and communicate geographic information about the world.

questions.text:
How do the passages work together?
A. Passage 1 explains how maps are used, while Passage 2 explains how cartographers make choices when creating maps. [CORRECT]
B. Passage 1 explains ancient farming, while Passage 2 explains weather on planets.
C. Both passages say maps should include every possible detail.
D. Passage 1 describes only road maps, while Passage 2 describes only population maps.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In Passage 2, scale means ( ).
Drop-down options: the relationship between distance on a map and actual distance [CORRECT] / a color used to show water / a type of satellite image / the number of cartographers

Select TWO reasons cartographers must make careful choices.
A. No map can show every detail about a location. [CORRECT]
B. Political maps show countries, states, and cities.
C. The choices made by cartographers can influence how people interpret information. [CORRECT]
D. GPS devices are a modern mapping tool.
E. Businesses use maps to choose store locations.

How are the passages organized differently?
A. Passage 1 describes types and uses of maps, while Passage 2 describes decisions involved in making maps. [CORRECT]
B. Passage 1 is a timeline, while Passage 2 is a fictional story.
C. Passage 1 focuses on only symbols, while Passage 2 focuses only on mountains.
D. Both passages are organized as directions for drawing one map.

writingPrompt.text: Which is more important when creating a useful map: the information included on the map or the way the information is presented? In your own words, write a multi-paragraph response that explains which factor you think is more important. Include evidence from both passages that supports your opinion. As you write your response: use your own words to explain your opinion; use evidence from the passage to support your points; and use clear language to express your opinion by making connections between your points and your evidence. Be sure to: include an introduction; create organized paragraphs that focus on your opinion; pay attention to the grammar, structure, and mechanics of your sentences; and include a conclusion. Write your multi-paragraph response in the space provided.

title: How Physical Environments Influence Human Activities

passages.title: How Physical Environments Influence Human Activities
passages.text:
1. The Western Hemisphere contains a wide variety of physical environments, including mountains, deserts, grasslands, forests, rivers, and coastal regions. These environments often influence how people live, work, and use resources.

2. In the Andes Mountains of South America, steep slopes and high elevations create challenges for farming. To adapt, people have built terraces into hillsides, creating flat areas where crops can be grown. This practice has been used for centuries and allows communities to make productive use of mountainous land.

3. In the Great Plains of North America, vast stretches of flat land and fertile soil support large-scale agriculture. Farmers grow crops such as wheat, corn, and soybeans, helping supply food to millions of people.

4. Coastal regions also influence human activities. Access to oceans and waterways encourages fishing, trade, transportation, and tourism. Many large cities have developed near coastlines because ships can transport goods efficiently.

5. Climate plays an important role as well. People living in areas with long growing seasons may produce different crops than those living in colder regions. Weather conditions often affect housing, clothing, transportation, and economic activities.

6. Natural resources found in different environments also shape human decisions. Forests provide timber, rivers supply water, and mineral-rich areas support mining industries.

7. Throughout the Western Hemisphere, people adapt their activities to the opportunities and challenges presented by their physical environments.

passages.title: How Human Activities Modify Physical Environments
passages.text:
8. While physical environments influence people, human activities also change the environment. Throughout history, people have modified landscapes to meet their needs.

9. One common example is agriculture. Farmers clear land, plant crops, and build irrigation systems that bring water to fields. These changes allow food production to increase, but they also alter natural ecosystems.

10. Cities provide another example of environmental modification. As populations grow, people construct roads, buildings, bridges, and transportation systems. Urban development can replace forests, grasslands, and wetlands with human-made structures.

11. Dams are often built to control rivers, generate electricity, and store water. While dams provide important benefits, they can also change water flow patterns and affect plants and animals living nearby.

12. Mining activities remove valuable resources from the ground. These resources help support industries and technology, but mining can also reshape landforms and affect local environments.

13. In some areas, people work to reduce environmental impacts by planting trees, protecting wildlife habitats, and restoring damaged ecosystems. These efforts help balance human needs with environmental conservation.

14. Scientists study the relationship between people and the environment because the two are closely connected. Human activities can improve living conditions, but they can also create challenges that require careful management.

15. The Western Hemisphere continues to demonstrate the ongoing relationship between physical environments and human activities. Environments influence how people live, and people continually modify environments to meet their needs.

questions.text:
What idea is developed across both passages?
A. People and physical environments affect one another in the Western Hemisphere. [CORRECT]
B. People never change the environments where they live.
C. Mountains and coastlines prevent all human activity.
D. Human activities always improve environments without challenges.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In Passage 2, modify means ( ).
Drop-down options: change something [CORRECT] / study from far away / leave untouched / predict the weather

Select TWO details that show physical environments influence human activities.
A. Terraces allow people in the Andes Mountains to farm steep slopes. [CORRECT]
B. Cities can replace forests and wetlands.
C. Coastal regions encourage fishing, trade, transportation, and tourism. [CORRECT]
D. Dams can change water flow patterns.
E. Mining can reshape landforms.

How are the passages structured?
A. Passage 1 explains how environments influence people, while Passage 2 explains how people modify environments. [CORRECT]
B. Both passages list only types of maps.
C. Passage 1 describes a problem, while Passage 2 gives a recipe.
D. Both passages focus only on deserts.

writingPrompt.text: Which has a greater impact on the Western Hemisphere: physical environments influencing human activities or human activities modifying physical environments? In your own words, write a multi-paragraph response that explains which influence you think has a greater impact. Include evidence from both passages that supports your opinion. As you write your response: use your own words to explain your opinion; use evidence from the passage to support your points; and use clear language to express your opinion by making connections between your points and your evidence. Be sure to: include an introduction; create organized paragraphs that focus on your opinion; pay attention to the grammar, structure, and mechanics of your sentences; and include a conclusion. Write your multi-paragraph response in the space provided.

passages.title: Becoming an Informed Citizen
passages.text:
1. Every day, people encounter information about events, issues, and decisions that affect their communities. News reports, websites, books, interviews, surveys, and government publications all provide information about public issues. To understand these issues accurately, individuals must gather information from multiple sources and evaluate what they learn carefully.

2. A public issue is a topic or problem that affects a community, region, or nation. Examples include transportation, education, environmental protection, public health, and the use of natural resources. Public issues often involve different viewpoints and complex information.

3. When researching a public issue, it is important to gather information from a variety of sources. Different sources may provide different details, perspectives, or types of evidence. Using multiple sources can help people develop a more complete understanding of a topic.

4. For example, a person studying water conservation might read newspaper articles, examine scientific reports, review government data, and listen to community discussions. Each source may contribute valuable information.

5. Gathering information is only the first step. People must also interpret the information they find. Interpreting information involves examining facts, identifying patterns, and considering what the information means.

6. Individuals should also check information for accuracy. Not all sources provide reliable or complete information. One way to verify accuracy is by comparing information from multiple trustworthy sources. If several reliable sources report similar facts, confidence in the information may increase.

7. Evaluating sources involves asking questions. Who created the information? What evidence is provided? When was the information published? Does the source appear to present information fairly and accurately? These questions can help people determine whether information is trustworthy.

8. Data often plays an important role in understanding public issues. Data may include numbers, measurements, survey results, or observations collected during research.

9. Because large amounts of data can be difficult to understand, information is often displayed graphically. Graphs, charts, maps, and tables help organize information and make patterns easier to recognize.

10. A bar graph can compare quantities, while a line graph can show changes over time. Pie charts can illustrate how parts relate to a whole. Maps can display information about locations, populations, or environmental conditions.

11. Graphical displays allow people to communicate information efficiently. Instead of reading large amounts of text, viewers can quickly identify trends and relationships.

12. Scientists, journalists, business leaders, and government officials frequently use graphs and charts when sharing information with the public. These visual tools help audiences understand complex information more easily.

13. However, readers should still examine graphs carefully. The way data is presented can influence how people interpret information. Labels, scales, and categories should be reviewed to ensure the information is represented accurately.

14. Informed citizens gather information from multiple sources, evaluate the accuracy of that information, and use evidence to form conclusions. They also understand how data displays can help communicate important information.

15. By carefully gathering, interpreting, and checking information, individuals can better understand public issues and make informed decisions about the world around them.

questions.text:
Which main ideas are developed in the passage?
A. People understand public issues by using multiple reliable sources and by interpreting data displays carefully. [CORRECT]
B. Public issues should be studied with only one source.
C. Graphs always tell the full story without labels or scales.
D. Data is useful only to scientists.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In paragraph 5, interpreting information means ( ).
Drop-down options: examining facts and deciding what they mean [CORRECT] / copying data without checking it / choosing one source at random / removing labels from graphs

Select TWO questions that help evaluate whether a source is trustworthy.
A. Who created the information? [CORRECT]
B. What evidence is provided? [CORRECT]
C. How many colors are in the graph?
D. Can the source be read quickly?
E. Does the topic involve transportation?

How does the author support the point that data displays are useful?
A. By explaining that graphs, charts, maps, and tables organize information and make patterns easier to recognize. [CORRECT]
B. By saying data displays should replace all sources.
C. By describing only newspaper articles.
D. By claiming labels and scales do not matter.

writingPrompt.text: Which is more important when learning about a public issue: gathering information from multiple sources or displaying data clearly with graphs and charts? In your own words, write a multi-paragraph response that explains which practice you think is more important. Include evidence from the passage that supports your opinion. As you write your response: use your own words to explain your opinion; use evidence from the passage to support your points; and use clear language to express your opinion by making connections between your points and your evidence. Be sure to: include an introduction; create organized paragraphs that focus on your opinion; pay attention to the grammar, structure, and mechanics of your sentences; and include a conclusion. Write your multi-paragraph response in the space provided. Standard: Individuals can better understand public issues by gathering, interpreting and checking information for accuracy from multiple sources. Data can be displayed graphically to effectively and efficiently communicate information.

title: Understanding Information in a Data-Driven World

passages.title: Understanding Information in a Data-Driven World
passages.text:
1. People make decisions every day based on information. Communities decide how to improve transportation, schools determine how to support students, and governments develop plans to address public issues. To make informed decisions, individuals need accurate information and a clear understanding of the facts.

2. Public issues are topics that affect groups of people and often require discussion, planning, or action. Examples include protecting natural resources, improving public health, managing traffic, and expanding community services. Because public issues can be complex, it is important to gather information from multiple sources.

3. Different sources often provide different pieces of information. News articles may explain recent events, government reports may provide statistics, and interviews may offer personal experiences. By examining several sources, individuals can gain a broader understanding of an issue.

4. Gathering information from multiple sources also helps people identify errors or incomplete information. If one source presents a claim, it can be compared with other reliable sources to determine whether the information is accurate.

5. Interpreting information is another important skill. Interpreting means examining evidence, identifying patterns, and determining what the information suggests. Two people may read the same information but reach different conclusions if they do not carefully evaluate the evidence.

6. Individuals should also consider the reliability of their sources. Questions such as "Who created this information?" and "What evidence supports these claims?" can help determine whether a source is trustworthy.

7. Much of the information used to understand public issues is collected as data. Data may include survey results, population counts, scientific measurements, or economic statistics. Large amounts of data can be difficult to understand when presented only as numbers.

8. For this reason, data is often displayed graphically. Graphs, charts, maps, and tables help organize information and make it easier to recognize trends and patterns.

9. A line graph can show how something changes over time, such as population growth or temperature changes. A bar graph can compare different groups or categories. Pie charts can show how parts contribute to a whole.

10. Maps are another useful way to display information. Geographic data can be presented on maps to show population density, weather patterns, transportation systems, or environmental conditions.

11. Visual displays allow people to communicate information quickly and efficiently. Instead of reading pages of statistics, viewers can often understand important trends by examining a graph or chart.

12. However, graphical displays must be interpreted carefully. The design of a graph can influence how information appears. Labels, scales, and categories should be examined to ensure the data is presented accurately.

13. Scientists, journalists, business leaders, and government officials frequently use both data and visual displays when sharing information with the public. These tools help audiences understand complicated topics more clearly.

14. Informed citizens gather information from multiple sources, evaluate its accuracy, interpret evidence carefully, and understand how data can be communicated visually. These skills help people make thoughtful decisions about issues that affect their communities.

15. In a world filled with information, the ability to gather, interpret, verify, and communicate data is an essential skill. By using reliable sources and understanding graphical displays, individuals can better understand public issues and participate more effectively in society.

questions.text:

passages.title: Investigating a Community Issue
passages.text:
1. Imagine that a town is considering building a new community park. Some residents support the project because they believe it will provide recreation opportunities and improve quality of life. Others are concerned about the cost and the use of available land.

2. To better understand the issue, community members begin gathering information from multiple sources. They read newspaper articles, attend public meetings, review government reports, and interview local residents.

3. Each source provides different types of information. Newspaper articles may explain recent developments, while government reports might contain information about costs and funding. Interviews can reveal how residents feel about the proposal.

4. Gathering information from several sources helps people gain a more complete understanding of the issue. Relying on only one source may lead to an incomplete picture.

5. Community members also check information for accuracy. They compare facts reported by different sources and look for evidence that supports claims. This process helps identify mistakes, misunderstandings, or incomplete information.

6. Interpreting information is equally important. People must consider what the facts mean and how different pieces of evidence relate to one another.

7. By gathering, interpreting, and verifying information, individuals are better prepared to make informed decisions about public issues that affect their communities.

passages.title: Communicating Information with Data
passages.text:
8. After information has been collected and analyzed, it must often be shared with others. One effective way to communicate information is through data displays such as graphs, charts, maps, and tables.

9. Suppose town officials conduct a survey about the proposed park. Hundreds of residents participate, creating a large amount of data. Presenting all of the survey results as a list of numbers could be difficult to understand.

10. Instead, officials might create a bar graph showing how many residents support or oppose the project. A pie chart could display the percentage of responses in each category.

11. Graphical displays help people recognize patterns and trends quickly. Viewers can often understand important information in seconds rather than spending time reading long reports.

12. Maps can also communicate information effectively. A map might show where residents live in relation to the proposed park site or identify areas that currently lack recreational facilities.

13. However, data displays must be designed carefully. Labels, scales, and categories should be accurate so that viewers can interpret the information correctly.

14. Scientists, journalists, businesses, and government agencies frequently use graphs and charts because they allow complex information to be communicated efficiently.

15. When information is presented clearly and accurately, data displays help people understand public issues and make informed decisions.

questions.text:

D. Passage 1 and Passage 2 give opposite definitions of data.

passages.title: Solving a School Transportation Problem
passages.text:
1. A school district noticed that many students were arriving late to school. Teachers reported that late arrivals were disrupting lessons, and administrators wanted to understand the cause of the problem.

2. Rather than making assumptions, school officials decided to gather information from multiple sources. They reviewed attendance records, examined bus schedules, interviewed drivers, and surveyed families.

3. Each source provided different information. Attendance records showed when students arrived. Bus schedules identified planned routes and pickup times. Driver interviews revealed traffic challenges, and family surveys described concerns about transportation reliability.

4. As officials compared information from these sources, they noticed patterns. Several bus routes experienced delays because of construction projects, while some routes required longer travel times due to increased traffic.

5. School leaders checked the information for accuracy by comparing reports from different sources. When multiple sources identified the same problems, officials became more confident in their findings.

6. By gathering, interpreting, and verifying information, the district gained a clearer understanding of the issue. As a result, transportation schedules were adjusted, and arrival times improved.

7. This example demonstrates how using multiple sources helps people better understand public issues and make informed decisions.

passages.title: Turning Information into Visual Data
passages.text:
8. After gathering information about the transportation problem, school officials needed a way to share their findings with parents, teachers, and community members. They decided to use graphical displays.

9. A bar graph was created to compare the number of late arrivals before and after schedule changes. The graph allowed viewers to quickly see that the number of late students had decreased.

10. Officials also created a line graph showing arrival patterns over several months. This graph helped viewers recognize trends and determine whether improvements continued over time.

11. In addition, a map displayed bus routes throughout the district. The map highlighted areas affected by construction and showed where transportation adjustments had been made.

12. Presenting information visually helped community members understand the data more easily than reading pages of statistics and reports.

13. However, school officials carefully checked the graphs before sharing them. They made sure labels, scales, and titles accurately represented the information.

14. Graphical displays can be powerful communication tools, but they must be designed responsibly. Misleading scales or incomplete information can cause viewers to misunderstand the data.

15. When used correctly, graphs, charts, and maps allow people to communicate information clearly and efficiently. These tools help communities understand public issues and evaluate possible solutions.

questions.text:

passages.title: The Impact of Choices
passages.text:
1. Every day, individuals and governments make choices that affect their lives and communities. Some decisions have immediate results, while others create consequences that may not become visible for many years. Understanding the connection between choices and consequences helps people make informed decisions and plan for the future.

2. Individuals make choices about education, health, spending, and the use of resources. For example, a student who studies regularly may improve academic skills and perform better in school. The immediate consequence is increased knowledge, while a future consequence may be greater educational and career opportunities.

3. Financial decisions provide another example. A person who saves a portion of their income may have fewer funds available for spending today. However, that choice can provide financial security and flexibility in the future. On the other hand, spending all available income may offer short-term satisfaction but create challenges later if unexpected expenses arise.

4. Governments also make decisions that affect both present and future generations. Local, state, and national governments determine how resources are used and which projects receive funding.

5. For example, a government may decide to build new roads, bridges, or public transportation systems. These projects can improve travel and create jobs in the present. In the future, they may support economic growth by helping people and goods move more efficiently.

6. Governments often make choices about education as well. Funding schools, libraries, and educational programs may require significant investments today, but these decisions can help prepare future generations with the knowledge and skills needed for success.

7. Environmental decisions frequently involve balancing present needs with future consequences. A community may choose to protect forests, wetlands, and water resources. While conservation efforts sometimes limit certain activities in the short term, they can help preserve natural resources for future generations.

8. Similarly, decisions about energy production can have long-lasting effects. Governments and individuals must consider how their choices affect the environment, available resources, and future energy needs.

9. Technology also demonstrates the relationship between choices and consequences. Decisions to invest in research and innovation can lead to new inventions, improved communication, advances in medicine, and economic opportunities. The benefits of these choices may continue for decades.

10. Not all consequences are easy to predict. Some decisions produce unexpected results that require additional planning and adjustment. For this reason, leaders often gather information, consider different viewpoints, and evaluate possible outcomes before making important decisions.

11. Responsible decision-making involves thinking about both immediate and long-term effects. People and governments must weigh the benefits and challenges of different options before choosing a course of action.

12. Historians often study past decisions to understand how they shaped societies. Many of the transportation systems, laws, schools, and public services used today exist because of choices made by earlier generations.

13. Likewise, the decisions people make today will influence future communities. Choices about education, technology, natural resources, and public policies can affect opportunities, quality of life, and economic conditions for years to come.

14. Understanding the consequences of decisions encourages thoughtful planning and responsible citizenship. By considering both present and future impacts, individuals and governments can make choices that contribute to long-term success and well-being.

questions.text:
Which main ideas are developed in the passage?

B. Only governments make choices that affect the future.
C. Consequences of decisions are always easy to predict.
D. Environmental decisions have no future effects.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.

D. Some decisions produce unexpected results.

B. Future consequences happen before choices are made.
C. Saving money always creates problems later.
D. Technology decisions affect only the person who makes them.

passages.title: Building Skills for Future Success
passages.text:
1. Most people earn income by working. The amount of income a worker earns often depends on many factors, including education, skills, experience, and the demand for certain types of work. Because jobs and industries continue to change over time, workers often seek opportunities to gain new knowledge, skills, and experiences that can improve their earning potential.

2. Knowledge is one of the most valuable resources a worker can develop. People gain knowledge through education, training programs, reading, and hands-on learning. As workers learn more about their fields, they are often able to perform tasks more effectively and take on additional responsibilities.

3. Many careers require specialized knowledge. Teachers study education, engineers learn mathematics and science, and healthcare workers receive training related to medicine and patient care. The knowledge gained through education helps workers prepare for their careers and qualify for employment opportunities.

4. Skills are another important factor that can influence income. Skills include abilities that allow people to perform specific tasks. Some skills, such as communication, teamwork, and problem-solving, are useful in many different jobs. Other skills are more specialized and relate to particular occupations.

5. Workers often improve their skills through practice and training. A computer programmer may learn a new programming language, while a mechanic may receive training on new technologies used in vehicles. Developing additional skills can increase a worker's value to employers.

6. Experience also plays a significant role in earning income. As workers spend time performing job duties, they often become more efficient and knowledgeable. Experience helps people apply what they have learned and solve problems more effectively.

7. Employers frequently value experience because it demonstrates that workers have successfully performed tasks in real-world situations. Workers with more experience may qualify for leadership roles, promotions, or positions with greater responsibility.

8. Technology has changed many workplaces. Some jobs that existed in the past now require different skills because of new tools and equipment. Workers who continue learning are often better prepared to adapt to these changes.

9. Lifelong learning has become increasingly important in many careers. Some workers attend workshops, earn certifications, complete additional education programs, or participate in professional development opportunities throughout their careers.

10. Apprenticeships and internships provide another way to gain valuable experience. These opportunities allow people to learn from experienced professionals while developing practical workplace skills.

11. Workers who invest time in gaining knowledge, improving skills, and building experience often increase their employment opportunities. They may become qualified for a wider range of positions and have more options when pursuing career goals.

12. Employers benefit from skilled and knowledgeable workers because they can contribute to productivity, innovation, and problem-solving within an organization.

13. Although success depends on many factors, education, training, and experience often help workers improve their ability to earn income. These investments can create opportunities for advancement and professional growth.

14. In a changing economy, workers who continue learning and developing new skills are often better prepared for future challenges. By gaining knowledge, skills, and experiences, individuals can strengthen their ability to achieve career success and increase their earning potential.

questions.text:
Which main ideas are developed in the passage?

B. Income is based only on luck.

B. By saying old skills are always enough.
C. By describing only one kind of career.
D. By arguing that employers do not value knowledge.

passages.title: The Power of Productive Resources
passages.text:
1. Every business depends on productive resources to create goods and services. Productive resources include natural resources, human resources, and capital resources. The availability of these resources can have a major impact on how much a business is able to produce.

2. Natural resources are materials that come from nature, such as water, timber, minerals, and fertile soil. A company that manufactures paper depends on access to trees, while a farm depends on healthy soil and water.

3. Human resources refer to the people who perform work. Workers contribute knowledge, skills, and labor that help businesses operate effectively. A company with trained and experienced employees may be able to produce goods more efficiently than a company with fewer skilled workers.

4. Capital resources include tools, machinery, buildings, and technology used to produce goods and services. Modern equipment often allows businesses to increase production and reduce the time needed to complete tasks.

5. Consider two furniture companies. One company has access to high-quality wood, skilled workers, and advanced machinery. The other company has fewer resources and older equipment. The first company is likely able to produce more furniture in less time.

6. However, limited resources can reduce productive capacity. A shortage of materials, workers, or equipment may slow production and make it difficult for businesses to meet demand.

7. Because productive resources influence how much can be produced, businesses often look for ways to acquire and use resources efficiently.

passages.title: The Impact of the Division of Labor
passages.text:
8. Another factor that affects productive capacity is the division of labor. Division of labor occurs when workers specialize in specific tasks rather than each person completing every step of production.

9. Imagine a factory that produces bicycles. Instead of having one worker build an entire bicycle, different workers perform different jobs. One worker assembles the frame, another installs the wheels, and another adds the brakes.

10. Specialization often increases efficiency because workers become highly skilled at their assigned tasks. As workers gain experience, they may complete their jobs more quickly and accurately.

11. The division of labor can also reduce training time. Workers need to learn only a portion of the production process rather than every step.

12. As a result, businesses are often able to produce more goods in less time. Increased productivity can lower costs and make products more available to consumers.

13. However, division of labor can sometimes create challenges. If one part of the production process experiences delays, the entire system may slow down. Workers who perform the same task repeatedly may also find their jobs less varied.

14. Despite these challenges, many businesses use specialization because it can significantly increase productive capacity. Factories, restaurants, hospitals, and technology companies often rely on workers performing specialized roles.

15. Together, productive resources and the division of labor influence how efficiently goods and services are produced. The availability of resources and the way work is organized can both have positive and negative effects on productive capacity.

questions.text:
How do the passages explain productive capacity?
A. Passage 1 explains how resources affect production, while Passage 2 explains how organizing workers through division of labor affects production. [CORRECT]
B. Passage 1 explains trade, while Passage 2 explains climate regions.
C. Both passages say businesses produce more when they have fewer resources.
D. Passage 1 and Passage 2 focus only on consumers buying goods.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.

Drop-down options: tools, machinery, buildings, and technology used to produce goods [CORRECT] / workers who perform labor / materials from nature / products bought by consumers

B. Natural resources include water, timber, and minerals.
C. Workers become highly skilled and may complete jobs more quickly and accurately. [CORRECT]
D. Limited resources can reduce productive capacity.
E. A furniture company may need high-quality wood.

How are the two passages structured?

B. Passage 1 is a timeline, while Passage 2 is a map description.
C. Both passages explain only negative effects.
D. Both passages describe one worker's career path.

As you write your response: use your own words to explain your opinion; use evidence from the passage to support your points; and use clear language to express your opinion by making connections between your points and your evidence. Be sure to: include an introduction; create organized paragraphs that focus on your opinion; pay attention to the grammar, structure, and mechanics of your sentences; and include a conclusion. Write your multi-paragraph response in the space provided.

passages.title: Why Regions Specialize
passages.text:
1. Around the world, different regions have access to different resources, climates, and skills. Because of these differences, regions often specialize in producing certain goods or services that they can create efficiently.

2. Specialization occurs when a region, country, business, or worker focuses on producing a particular product or service. Instead of trying to produce everything they need, regions often concentrate on what they do best.

3. For example, a region with fertile soil and a favorable climate may specialize in growing crops. Another region with large deposits of minerals may focus on mining. Coastal regions often develop industries related to fishing, shipping, or tourism.

4. Specialization can increase productivity because producers gain experience, improve techniques, and make efficient use of available resources. As production becomes more efficient, larger quantities of goods can often be produced.

5. Consider two countries. One country has ideal conditions for growing coffee, while another has advanced factories that manufacture electronics. If each country focuses on what it produces best, both can produce greater amounts of their specialized products.

6. However, specialization also creates dependence on goods and services produced elsewhere. A country that focuses heavily on agriculture may still need machinery, technology, or manufactured products from other regions.

7. Because no region can efficiently produce every good and service, specialization often leads to trade with others.

passages.title: The Benefits of Trade and Interdependence
passages.text:
8. Trade allows regions and countries to exchange goods and services. Through trade, producers can obtain items that may be difficult, expensive, or impossible to produce locally.

9. When countries specialize and trade, they become interdependent. Interdependence means that regions rely on one another for certain goods, services, or resources.

10. Returning to the example of the coffee-producing country and the electronics-producing country, each benefits from trade. The coffee-producing country can obtain electronics, while the electronics-producing country can purchase coffee.

11. As trade expands, consumers often gain access to a greater variety of products. Grocery stores may sell foods grown in distant countries, while stores may offer clothing, technology, and household goods produced around the world.

12. Trade can also support economic growth. Businesses gain access to larger markets, allowing them to sell products to more customers. This increased demand may create jobs and encourage investment.

13. However, interdependence can also create challenges. If a natural disaster, conflict, or transportation problem disrupts production in one region, other regions that depend on those goods may be affected.

14. Despite these challenges, trade remains an important part of the global economy. Most countries rely on goods and services from other regions while also exporting products they produce efficiently.

15. Specialization and trade allow regions and countries to increase both the amount and variety of goods and services available. As a result, people often have access to more choices than would be possible if every region tried to produce everything on its own.

questions.text:
How are specialization and trade connected?
A. Specialization helps regions produce what they do best, and trade lets them exchange for goods and services from other regions. [CORRECT]
B. Specialization prevents regions from trading with others.
C. Trade makes resources and climate the same everywhere.
D. Specialization means every region produces everything it needs.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In Passage 2, interdependent means ( ).
Drop-down options: relying on one another for goods, services, or resources [CORRECT] / producing everything alone / refusing to exchange products / having no specialized skills

Select TWO details that support the benefits of trade.
A. Consumers often gain access to a greater variety of products. [CORRECT]
B. Specialization creates dependence on goods from elsewhere.
C. Businesses can sell products to larger markets, which may create jobs and encourage investment. [CORRECT]
D. Natural disasters can disrupt production.
E. Coastal regions may develop fishing or tourism.

How do the passages organize the information?
A. Passage 1 explains why regions specialize, and Passage 2 explains how trade and interdependence result from specialization. [CORRECT]
B. Passage 1 lists only challenges, while Passage 2 lists only climates.
C. Both passages are organized as fictional stories.
D. Both passages focus only on one country growing coffee.

Write your multi-paragraph response in the space provided.

passages.title: First Tracks
passages.text:
(1) Before the sun begins to rise,(2) The mountain waits beneath the sky.(3) Fresh snow sparkles silver-white,(4) Like scattered stars that tumbled by.

(5) The chairlift hums above the trees,(6) Climbing slowly toward the peak.(7) The world below grows small and still,(8) While snowy slopes seem wide and sleek.

(9) Cold air rushes across my face,(10) Sharp and crisp as winter's song.(11) My skis point toward the open trail;(12) I've waited for this all year long.

(13) I push ahead and start to glide,(14) The powder swirls beneath my feet.(15) Each turn draws lines across the snow,(16) Smooth and curved and quick and neat.

(17) The mountain challenges my skill.(18) Some trails twist and some are steep.(19) A few mistakes may slow me down,(20) But I refuse to stop or weep.

(21) Instead, I stand and try again,(22) Learning more with every run.(23) By afternoon, the trails that seemed(24) So difficult have become fun.

(25) As evening paints the sky with gold,(26) I take one final ride downhill.(27) The mountain taught me something new:(28) Great rewards can follow will.

questions.text:
Which theme is best supported by the poem?
A. Trying difficult things can lead to growth and reward. [CORRECT]
B. Winter sports are safer when people avoid steep trails.
C. Skiers should only choose easy trails.
D. Mountains are more beautiful in the evening than in the morning.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In line 14, powder means ( ).
Drop-down options: soft, fresh snow [CORRECT] / a rocky trail / cold winter air / marks left by skis

Select TWO details that show the speaker keeps trying.
A. "Some trails twist and some are steep."
B. "A few mistakes may slow me down, / But I refuse to stop or weep." [CORRECT]
C. "The chairlift hums above the trees."
D. "Instead, I stand and try again, / Learning more with every run." [CORRECT]
E. "As evening paints the sky with gold"

How does the structure of the poem help develop its meaning?
A. It moves from anticipation, to challenge, to a final lesson about effort. [CORRECT]
B. It describes the same moment on the mountain several times.
C. It begins with the lesson and then explains why it is wrong.
D. It focuses only on the speaker's equipment.

writingPrompt.text: What is the most important message of the poem? In your own words, write a multi-paragraph response that explains what you think is the most important message of the poem. Include evidence from the poem that supports your interpretation. As you write your response: use your own words to explain your opinion; use evidence from the passage to support your points; and use clear language to express your opinion by making connections between your points and your evidence. Be sure to: include an introduction; create organized paragraphs that focus on your opinion; pay attention to the grammar, structure, and mechanics of your sentences; and include a conclusion. Write your multi-paragraph response in the space provided.

passages.title: Race the Rapids
passages.text:
(1) Paddle, paddle, through the spray,(2) Through the rushing river's roar.(3) Paddle, paddle, through the spray,(4) Toward the bend and distant shore.

(5) White waves whirl and wildly leap,(6) Splashing, spinning, side to side.(7) Fast and fierce, the river runs;(8) We match its rhythm as we glide.

(9) Stroke by stroke, we stay in sync.(10) Stroke by stroke, we keep our pace.(11) Every paddle pulls us on(12) Through the water's twisting race.

(13) The current calls, "Come test your skill!"(14) The rocks reply, "Be strong and brave!"(15) We listen as the river speaks(16) In every rolling, racing wave.

(17) Paddle, paddle, through the spray,(18) Though the rapids toss and churn.(19) Paddle, paddle, through the spray,(20) And trust the lessons that you learn.

(21) Soon the river slows its song.(22) Calm reflections fill the stream.(23) We smile and watch the sunlight dance(24) Like glitter from a golden dream.

(25) The rapids fade behind us now,(26) But one thing lingers in our minds:(27) Working hard and working together(28) Helps us face the toughest times.

passages.title: Above the Clouds
passages.text:
(1) The mountain rises sharp and high,(2) Its snowy peak cuts through the sky.(3) The trail is steep, the climb is long,(4) Yet every step makes my legs strong.

(5) The wind grows colder as I climb,(6) But I keep moving, one step at a time.(7) Below, the forests fade from view;(8) Above, the sky turns brighter blue.

(9) At last I reach the rocky crest,(10) And pause a moment there to rest.(11) The world below seems far away,(12) Like a painted picture on display.

(13) I came to conquer stone and height,(14) To test my courage, strength, and might.(15) But standing here, I finally see(16) The mountain also changed me.

(17) It taught me patience on the climb,(18) And how to face challenges with time.(19) The greatest prize was not the view—(20) It was discovering what I could do.

passages.title: The Old Oak Tree
passages.text:
(21) At the edge of a grassy field(22) Stands an oak both tall and wide.(23) Through countless years and changing seasons,(24) It has remained with quiet pride.

(25) Birds have nested in its branches.(26) Children played beneath its shade.(27) Storms have bent its sturdy limbs,(28) Yet still it stands, unafraid.

(29) Spring brings leaves of shining green.(30) Summer offers cooling rest.(31) Autumn paints the branches gold,(32) While winter puts its strength to test.

(33) The oak does not climb mountains high(34) Or race across the land.(35) Its greatness comes from staying true,(36) And choosing firmly where to stand.

(37) Though it never speaks a word,(38) The old tree seems to say:(39) "Strength is not just moving forward.(40) Sometimes it means choosing to stay."

questions.text:
How do the poems present strength differently?
A. "Above the Clouds" connects strength to climbing and growth, while "The Old Oak Tree" connects strength to staying steady. [CORRECT]
B. Both poems show strength by describing a race.
C. "Above the Clouds" rejects patience, while "The Old Oak Tree" rejects courage.
D. Both poems suggest that strength means avoiding challenges.

B. The forests fade from view.
C. The oak's limbs bend in storms, yet it still stands. [CORRECT]
D. Birds nest in the oak's branches.
E. The oak stands at the edge of a grassy field.

Which theme is developed across both poems?
A. Strength can appear in different ways, including effort, patience, and steadiness. [CORRECT]
B. People should always choose stillness over movement.
C. Nature is beautiful but cannot teach lessons.
D. The best rewards are always views from high places.

passages.title: The Mountain Bike Trail
passages.text:
(1) Pedal, pedal, up the hill,(2) Pedal, pedal, higher still.(3) Wheels whirl wildly through the trees,(4) Dancing with the mountain breeze.

(5) Rocky ridges rush below.(6) Twisting trails curve to and fro.(7) Fast and free, I find my flow,(8) Watching forests blur and go.

(9) Bump by bump and bend by bend,(10) The winding pathway seems to bend.(11) Bump by bump and bend by bend,(12) Adventure waits around each bend.

(13) The tires hum a steady beat.(14) My heart keeps rhythm with my feet.(15) Pedal, pedal, never slow—(16) There's always farther still to go.

(17) When the ride is finally done,(18) I think about the trail I won.(19) The repeated turns and steady pace(20) Helped me finish every race.

passages.title: Campfire Song
passages.text:
(21) Crackle, crackle, sparks take flight.(22) Crackle, crackle through the night.(23) Flames flicker, flashing gold,(24) Chasing away the evening cold.

(25) Friends gather close and sing along.(26) Each voice joins the growing song.(27) Round and round the chorus goes,(28) Familiar words that everyone knows.

(29) Softly, sweetly, stories start,(30) Filling every listening heart.(31) Softly, sweetly, tales are told,(32) Mixing new adventures with the old.

(33) Rhymes and rhythms guide the way,(34) Helping every singer stay.(35) The repeated lines feel warm and strong,(36) Like the steady beat of a favorite song.

(37) Long after the fire grows dim,(38) The words remain, remembered within.(39) The rhythm helps the message stay,(40) Even when the night fades away.

D. Both poems use rhythm only to describe weather.

passages.title: The Newsboy's Race
passages.text:
(1) Cincinnati, Ohio, 1908. Jacob Miller tightened his grip on the stack of newspapers tucked beneath his arm as he hurried down a busy city street. The morning air felt cool, and the sounds of electric streetcars echoed between the buildings. Newsboys shouted headlines on every corner while workers rushed toward factories, shops, and offices. At twelve years old, Jacob had delivered newspapers before school for nearly two years to help support his family.

(2) Cincinnati was changing quickly. New buildings seemed to appear every month, and construction projects stretched across the city. Jacob especially enjoyed watching crews work along the Ohio River. He often stopped after completing his route to observe workers building a large bridge that would help connect parts of the growing city. The bridge fascinated him. He admired the engineers who designed it and the workers who carefully assembled it high above the water.

(3) "One day, I'm going to build something important," Jacob often told his friends. Most of them laughed and reminded him that he was only a newspaper seller. Even so, Jacob never stopped paying attention to how things worked. Whenever he had free time, he visited the library and read books about bridges, machines, and inventions.

(4) One afternoon, a large crowd gathered near the bridge construction site. Curious, Jacob worked his way through the group until he could see a man standing beside a table covered with blueprints and sketches.

(5) "Attention, students!" the man called. "The bridge company is sponsoring a design contest. We want young people to develop an idea that could help workers move tools and supplies more safely across the bridge."

(6) Jacob's eyes widened. He listened carefully as the rules were explained. Contest entries would be judged by engineers working on the project. The winning design would earn a small cash prize and be displayed at the construction office.

(7) That evening, Jacob sat at the kitchen table long after dinner. He sketched ideas while studying a map of the bridge. Over the next several days, he carefully observed workers carrying heavy tools across narrow beams. He noticed how much time was spent moving supplies from one section of the bridge to another.

(8) Gradually, an idea began to take shape. Instead of carrying tools by hand, workers could use small carts attached to overhead cables. The carts could slide across the bridge and transport equipment more efficiently. Jacob spent hours improving his design and labeling each part of the drawing.

(9) When the contest deadline arrived, he submitted his project and tried not to think about it. Weeks passed. Then a letter arrived inviting contestants to attend an awards ceremony.

(10) Jacob sat nervously among dozens of students as an engineer stepped onto the stage. "This year's winning design," the engineer announced, "belongs to Jacob Miller."

(11) For a moment, Jacob could hardly believe what he had heard. Then applause filled the room. The engineer explained that Jacob's design showed creativity, careful observation, and practical problem-solving. After the ceremony, several engineers spoke with him about their work and encouraged him to continue studying mathematics and science.

(12) As Jacob walked home that evening, he carried more than a certificate and prize money. He carried a new sense of confidence. Winning the contest had not made him an engineer, but it showed him that his ideas mattered. Looking up at the unfinished bridge stretching across the river, Jacob smiled. For the first time, his dream felt less like a wish and more like a goal he could actually achieve.

questions.text:

B. A person must be wealthy to invent something useful.

passages.title: The Colony on Europa
passages.text:
(1) The year was 2148, and twelve-year-old Aria Patel had never seen a tree. She lived on Europa, one of Jupiter's icy moons, where thousands of people worked and lived inside a massive underground colony. Thick layers of ice protected the colony from harmful radiation while advanced technology supplied air, water, and food.

(2) Most students at Europa Learning Center studied science, engineering, and environmental systems. Everyone understood that the colony's survival depended on technology working correctly. If even one major system failed, the consequences could be serious.

(3) One morning, Aria was helping monitor the colony's algae farms. The farms produced oxygen and helped recycle carbon dioxide. As she reviewed data on her tablet, she noticed something unusual. Oxygen production had decreased in several sections of the facility.

(4) Aria immediately reported the problem to her instructor. Engineers soon discovered that microscopic ice particles had entered part of the filtration system, reducing the algae's ability to grow.

(5) The colony's maintenance teams began working to solve the problem, but repairs could take several days. During that time, oxygen production might continue to decline.

(6) Aria remembered reading about a backup system that had been designed years earlier but never fully tested. The system used specialized bacteria to help support oxygen production during emergencies.

(7) After reviewing old research files, scientists agreed that the backup system might work. Teams quickly activated the dormant equipment.

(8) Within twenty-four hours, oxygen levels began to stabilize. The colony avoided a serious crisis.

(9) Later, the colony director thanked Aria for paying attention to the data and sharing her observations. "Technology helps us survive here," he said, "but people still need to think carefully and solve problems."

(10) Looking out through an observation window at the frozen landscape of Europa, Aria felt proud. Life on a distant moon was challenging, but science and teamwork made extraordinary things possible.

passages.title: The Floating City
passages.text:
(11) In the year 2185, rising sea levels had changed many coastlines on Earth. To adapt, engineers built floating cities that could move slowly across the oceans while generating their own energy from wind, waves, and sunlight.

(12) Twelve-year-old Kai Torres lived in Ocean Haven, one of the largest floating cities ever constructed. The city included schools, parks, farms, research centers, and thousands of homes connected by elevated walkways.

(13) One afternoon, Kai noticed that several solar towers were not rotating properly. The towers normally adjusted their positions throughout the day to capture the maximum amount of sunlight.

(14) Curious, Kai examined public maintenance reports available through the city's network. He discovered that a software update had caused communication problems between the towers and the city's energy system.

(15) Although engineers were already investigating the issue, the malfunction reduced the city's energy production. If the problem continued, some nonessential services might need to be temporarily shut down.

(16) While reviewing the data, Kai recognized a pattern. The communication errors occurred only when certain weather-monitoring sensors transmitted information at the same time.

(17) He shared his findings with city technicians, who used the information to trace the source of the problem. After making several adjustments to the software, the solar towers returned to normal operation.

(18) The following week, Kai was invited to visit the city's control center. Engineers explained how thousands of connected systems worked together to keep Ocean Haven functioning safely.

(19) "Technology gives us incredible opportunities," one engineer told him, "but solving problems still depends on observation, creativity, and cooperation."

(20) As Kai looked across the floating city stretching toward the horizon, he realized that the future would belong to people willing to learn, adapt, and innovate.

questions.text:
