passages.title: The Team Project
passages.text:
(1) Maya glanced at the clock for the third time in ten minutes. The science fair was only two weeks away, and her group still had not finished building their project. Their assignment was to design a model that demonstrated how renewable energy could power a small community.

(2) At first, Maya had been excited to work with her classmates Jordan and Elena. However, the project quickly became frustrating. Jordan often forgot to bring materials, and Elena preferred to work independently rather than meet with the group.

(3) During one meeting, Maya found herself doing most of the work while the others argued about which design would look best. By the end of the afternoon, very little progress had been made.

(4) "At this rate, we'll never finish," Maya said.

(5) Instead of giving up, Maya decided to organize the group's efforts. She created a list of tasks and divided responsibilities among the team members. Jordan would gather materials, Elena would create the display board, and Maya would assemble the model.

(6) The next week, the group met again. This time, everyone arrived prepared. Jordan had collected the necessary supplies, and Elena brought several creative design ideas. As they worked together, the project began to take shape.

(7) There were still disagreements, but the group learned how to listen to one another and compromise. When one idea did not work, they tried another.

(8) By the day of the science fair, the model was complete. Their project successfully demonstrated how solar panels and wind turbines could provide electricity for a community.

(9) Although the group did not win first place, the judges praised their teamwork and problem-solving skills. Maya realized that success often depends on cooperation rather than individual effort alone.

passages.title: The New Student
passages.text:
(10) Ethan adjusted the straps of his backpack as he walked into school on the first day after winter break. His family had recently moved to a new town, and everything felt unfamiliar.

(11) During lunch, Ethan sat alone at a table near the back of the cafeteria. Students chatted with friends while he quietly looked around the room.

(12) Across the cafeteria, Sofia noticed him sitting by himself. She remembered how nervous she had felt when she transferred schools a few years earlier.

(13) Gathering her courage, Sofia walked over and introduced herself. "Would you like to sit with my friends?" she asked.

(14) Ethan smiled with relief and followed her to another table. Throughout the week, Sofia introduced him to classmates, explained school routines, and answered questions about the community.

(15) Gradually, Ethan became more comfortable. He joined the robotics club, participated in class discussions, and started making friends.

(16) A few weeks later, the robotics club entered a regional competition. Ethan's experience with computer programming helped the team solve a difficult problem during the final round.

(17) Thanks to the efforts of every team member, the club earned second place. After the competition, Ethan thanked Sofia for welcoming him when he was new.

(18) "I just treated you the way I hoped someone would treat me," Sofia replied.

(19) Ethan realized that small acts of kindness can make a significant difference in someone's life. Sofia's decision to reach out had helped him feel like he belonged.

questions.text:

A. Cooperation and kindness can help people succeed and feel supported. [CORRECT]
B. Success always means winning first place.
C. New situations should be handled alone.
D. Group work is impossible when people disagree.

B. Maya ignores her partners, while Sofia refuses to talk to Ethan.
C. Maya works alone, while Sofia wins a science fair.
D. Maya and Sofia both solve the same robotics problem.

title: How the Northern Lights Found the Sky

passages.title: How the Northern Lights Found the Sky
passages.text:
(1) Long ago, in a village near the frozen northern seas, people believed that the sky was a giant blanket stretched across the world. During the day, the Sun traveled across the blanket, bringing warmth and light. At night, the Moon and stars appeared, but the sky remained dark and still.

(2) In that village lived a curious girl named Anika. She loved asking questions that no one could answer. Why did the stars twinkle? Where did the wind come from? Why was the night sky so dark? Whenever she asked the village elders, they smiled and said, "Some mysteries belong to the sky."

(3) One winter evening, Anika noticed something unusual. A pale green glow appeared on the horizon. It shimmered for only a moment before disappearing.

(4) The next day, she asked everyone in the village if they had seen it. Most people had not. Others said it was probably a reflection from the snow.

(5) But Anika was certain she had seen something magical.

(6) Determined to discover the truth, she climbed a nearby mountain after sunset. The air was cold, and snow crunched beneath her boots. As darkness spread across the land, she waited.

(7) Suddenly, ribbons of green light appeared above her. They twisted and danced across the sky like giant glowing streams.

(8) Anika stared in amazement.

(9) Then she heard a voice.

(10) "You are the first person to find us," said the voice.

(11) Standing before her was a spirit made entirely of light. Its shape shifted constantly, glowing green, blue, and purple.

(12) "Who are you?" Anika asked.

(13) "We are the Sky Dancers," the spirit replied. "For centuries, we have hidden beyond the horizon. We bring color, wonder, and joy wherever we travel."

(14) "Then why do you stay hidden?" Anika asked.

(15) The spirit's colors dimmed. "Long ago, people became so busy that they stopped looking up. They hurried through their days and ignored the beauty around them. We feared our dances no longer mattered."

(16) Anika thought carefully.

(17) "Maybe people have forgotten," she said, "but they can learn to look again."

(18) The spirit remained silent for a moment. Then its colors brightened.

(19) "Perhaps you are right."

(20) The spirit raised its glowing arms. Instantly, streams of green, blue, and purple light spread across the sky. They swirled above the mountains and reflected on the snowy landscape below.

(21) Back in the village, people rushed from their homes. They pointed upward and gasped in wonder.

(22) The lights danced all night long.

(23) The next morning, villagers could talk about little else. Children drew pictures of the glowing sky. Elders shared stories about beauty and gratitude. Families spent more time outdoors, watching sunsets and admiring the stars.

(24) The Sky Dancers returned many times after that. Whenever they appeared, people paused their work and looked upward.

(25) According to the old legend, the Northern Lights exist because the Sky Dancers continue their celebration across the heavens. They remind people that even during long winters and difficult times, there is beauty waiting to be noticed.

(26) And that is why, whenever green and purple lights ripple across the northern sky, people stop, look up, and remember the lesson Anika taught long ago: wonder can be found by those who take the time to see it.

questions.text:
Which theme is best supported by the myth?
A. Wonder and beauty can be found when people take time to notice the world. [CORRECT]
B. People should ignore mysteries they cannot explain.
C. The night sky is less important than daily work.
D. Curiosity always causes danger.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In paragraph 3, horizon means the ( ).
Drop-down options: place where the sky seems to meet the land [CORRECT] / center of the village / top of the mountain / path through the snow

B. Anika climbs the mountain after sunset.
C. Children draw pictures, and families spend more time outdoors watching the sky. [CORRECT]
D. The elders say some mysteries belong to the sky.
E. Snow crunches beneath Anika's boots.

How does the ending contribute to the myth?
A. It connects the Northern Lights to a lesson about wonder and paying attention. [CORRECT]
B. It explains the Northern Lights using only scientific facts.
C. It shows that the Sky Dancers decide never to return.
D. It proves Anika was wrong to ask questions.

title: The Fox and the Falling Acorns

passages.title: The Fox and the Falling Acorns
passages.text:
(1) On the edge of a large forest lived a fox who believed he was the smartest animal in the woods. Whenever the animals gathered to solve a problem, the fox was always the first to offer advice. "Listen to me," he would say proudly. "I always know the best answer." Sometimes his ideas were helpful, but other times he simply liked being the center of attention. Even so, many animals listened because he sounded confident.

(2) One crisp autumn morning, the fox was resting beneath a tall oak tree. The leaves above him rustled in the breeze, and the forest seemed peaceful. Suddenly, an acorn dropped from a branch and landed on his tail. Startled, the fox leaped into the air. At that same moment, a strong gust of wind shook the tree, causing dozens of acorns to rain down through the branches. The fox stared upward in alarm.

(3) "The sky is falling!" he shouted. Without stopping to investigate, he raced through the forest as fast as he could. Before long, he encountered a rabbit nibbling clover near a stream. "Run!" cried the fox. "The sky is falling! Pieces of it are crashing down from above!" The rabbit gasped and immediately joined him.

(4) As they hurried through the woods, they met a deer. "What's happening?" asked the deer. "The sky is falling!" shouted the rabbit, repeating what the fox had said. The deer trusted the rabbit and began running as well. Soon they passed a squirrel, a raccoon, and several other animals. Each one heard the warning and joined the growing crowd. None of them had seen the sky falling, but all of them believed the story.

(5) The frightened animals ran until they reached the edge of the forest, where an old turtle rested beside a pond. The turtle watched the rushing crowd with curiosity. "Why is everyone running?" he asked calmly. "The sky is falling!" the animals shouted. The turtle blinked slowly and asked, "Who actually saw it happen?"

(6) The deer pointed to the rabbit. The rabbit pointed to the fox. The fox shifted uncomfortably. "Well," he admitted, "I didn't exactly see the sky falling. Something hit my tail, and then many things started dropping from above." The turtle nodded thoughtfully. "Show me where it happened," he said.

(7) Reluctantly, the animals followed the turtle back through the forest. When they arrived at the oak tree, acorns still covered the ground. The turtle picked one up and held it out to the fox. "Is this what struck your tail?" he asked. The fox examined the acorn and slowly nodded.

(8) The turtle smiled. "The sky is not falling," he explained. "Acorns fall from oak trees every autumn. You became frightened before taking time to discover what had really happened." The animals looked around and realized the turtle was right. The branches stretched peacefully overhead, and nothing unusual was happening in the sky.

(9) Embarrassed, the fox lowered his head. "I should have checked the facts before spreading the story," he admitted. The rabbit, deer, and the others agreed. They realized they had accepted the rumor without asking questions or looking for evidence.

(10) From that day forward, the animals learned to investigate information before believing it. They also learned that confidence is not the same as wisdom. As for the fox, he continued sharing his ideas, but he made sure to learn the truth before speaking. The animals never forgot the lesson they learned beneath the oak tree.

questions.text:

C. Running away is the best way to solve problems.
D. Questions make problems worse.

passages.title: The Glass Mountain
passages.text:
(1) Long ago, in a kingdom surrounded by forests and rivers, there stood a mountain made entirely of glass. Its shining sides reflected the sunlight during the day and the stars at night. At the top of the mountain grew a single silver apple tree that was said to grant wisdom to anyone who tasted its fruit.

(2) Many people attempted to climb the Glass Mountain, but none succeeded. The surface was too smooth and slippery. Knights, adventurers, and even kings tried to reach the top, yet each one slid back down before reaching the tree.

(3) In a small village nearby lived a kind girl named Elara. Unlike the others, she did not dream of becoming famous. She spent her days helping neighbors, caring for animals, and sharing what little she had with others.

(4) One winter evening, an old woman appeared at Elara's cottage. The traveler looked tired and cold. Although Elara had little food for herself, she invited the woman inside, shared her supper, and offered her a warm place to rest.

(5) The next morning, the old woman revealed that she was actually a powerful enchantress. To thank Elara for her kindness, she gave her a pair of crystal slippers.

(6) "These slippers will help you climb the Glass Mountain," the enchantress said. "But remember, kindness is more valuable than any treasure you may find."

(7) Elara thanked her and began the journey. The crystal slippers allowed her to walk steadily across the slippery surface. As she climbed higher, strong winds and icy storms tried to force her back, but she continued upward.

(8) At last, she reached the silver apple tree. Instead of taking all the fruit for herself, she picked only one apple. When she returned home, she shared its wisdom with others, helping people solve problems throughout the kingdom.

(9) News of her generosity spread far and wide. Soon, people realized that Elara's kindness had earned her the opportunity that strength and ambition alone could not achieve.

passages.title: The Enchanted Rose Garden
passages.text:
(10) In another kingdom, a beautiful rose garden surrounded an ancient castle. The roses bloomed in every season and glowed softly beneath the moonlight. According to legend, a magical golden rose grew at the center of the garden. Whoever cared for the rose would bring prosperity to the kingdom.

(11) For many years, the golden rose flourished. Then one day, it began to wilt. As the flower weakened, crops produced less food, rivers shrank, and the kingdom's fortunes declined.

(12) The king offered a reward to anyone who could save the rose. Wealthy merchants, skilled gardeners, and famous scholars traveled to the castle. Each tried a different method, but the flower continued to fade.

(13) Among those who heard about the problem was a young gardener named Theo. He was not wealthy or famous, but he loved plants and spent much of his time tending flowers in his village.

(14) When Theo arrived at the castle, he noticed something others had overlooked. The golden rose was surrounded by thorny vines that prevented sunlight from reaching its leaves.

(15) Carefully and patiently, Theo trimmed the vines. He watered the rose each day and protected it from insects. Gradually, the flower began to recover.

(16) Weeks later, the golden rose bloomed brighter than ever before. The kingdom's crops improved, rivers filled with water, and prosperity returned.

(17) The king offered Theo a chest filled with gold, but Theo accepted only a small reward. He explained that helping others was more important than becoming rich.

(18) The people celebrated Theo's success, and the story became a favorite fairy tale throughout the kingdom. Many believed the magic restored the rose, but others believed Theo's patience and care were the true source of its recovery.

questions.text:
How are the two fairy tales similar?
A. Both show that kindness, patience, and care can succeed where fame or ambition fails. [CORRECT]
B. Both show that wealth is the only path to success.
C. Both focus on characters who use magic selfishly.
D. Both suggest that helping others is unimportant.

D. The roses glow beneath the moonlight.
E. The king offers Theo a chest of gold.

A. Elara's kindness earns magical help, while Theo's careful observation and patient work restore the rose. [CORRECT]
B. Elara and Theo both use strength to defeat an enemy.
C. Elara ignores advice, while Theo refuses to help.
D. Elara and Theo both take all the treasure they can find.

passages.title: The Last Run
passages.text:
(1) Ethan tightened the straps on his helmet and looked up at the mountain. Snow covered the slopes, and the afternoon sun cast long shadows across the trails. It was the final day of his family's ski trip, and he had spent the entire week working toward one goal: skiing Falcon Ridge, the most challenging trail at the resort.

(2) Earlier in the week, Ethan had watched experienced skiers glide down Falcon Ridge with confidence. The steep turns and narrow sections looked intimidating, but they also looked exciting. He wanted to prove that he was ready for the challenge.

(3) Each day, Ethan practiced on easier trails. He focused on controlling his speed, improving his turns, and staying balanced on uneven terrain. Whenever he made a mistake, he tried again. By the end of the week, he felt more confident than ever.

(4) Now, standing near the chairlift, Ethan was finally ready to attempt Falcon Ridge. At least, that was what he thought.

(5) As he approached the lift, he noticed a ski patrol worker speaking with several guests. The worker pointed toward the mountain and explained that strong winds had moved into the area. Conditions on the upper slopes were becoming unpredictable.

(6) Ethan frowned. He had waited all week for this opportunity.

(7) "Maybe the winds will calm down," he said.

(8) His father looked at the clouds gathering near the summit. "Maybe," he replied, "but sometimes the smartest choice is not the most exciting one."

(9) Ethan crossed his arms and stared at the mountain. Part of him wanted to ignore the warning. He felt prepared, and he did not want to leave without skiing Falcon Ridge.

(10) Just then, a group of advanced skiers returned from the trail. Their jackets were covered with snow, and they described icy patches, poor visibility, and gusts strong enough to push them off balance.

(11) Ethan listened carefully. The more he heard, the more uncertain he became. He realized that being ready for a challenge also meant understanding when conditions were not safe.

(12) After several minutes, he made his decision.

(13) "I'm not going today," he said.

(14) His father smiled. "That sounds like a difficult choice."

(15) "It is," Ethan admitted. "But I'd rather wait for the right conditions than take a risk just because I want to prove something."

(16) Instead of heading to Falcon Ridge, Ethan spent the afternoon exploring other trails. He discovered a winding route through a forest of snow-covered pine trees and found several sections he had never skied before.

(17) By the time the lifts closed, he had enjoyed one of the best days of the trip. Although he never skied Falcon Ridge, he felt proud of the decision he had made.

(18) As the family packed their equipment that evening, Ethan looked back toward the mountain. Falcon Ridge would still be there another day. He realized that success was not always about reaching a goal immediately. Sometimes it meant having the patience and judgment to wait for the right opportunity.

questions.text:
Which theme is developed in the story?
A. Good judgment can matter more than proving yourself right away. [CORRECT]
B. People should ignore warnings when they feel prepared.
C. Reaching a goal is always worth any risk.
D. Easy trails are always better than challenging ones.

D. Ethan discovers a route through pine trees.

A. He changes from wanting to prove himself immediately to feeling proud of a patient, safe decision. [CORRECT]
B. He changes from confident to unwilling to ski ever again.
C. He changes from cautious to careless.
D. He changes from experienced to unable to enjoy other trails.

passages.title: Saturday at the Skate Park
passages.text:
(1) The skate park came alive long before noon. Under a bright blue sky, riders rolled through the entrance carrying skateboards, scooters, and helmets covered with colorful stickers. The smooth concrete stretched across the park in a series of ramps, rails, bowls, and platforms. Some riders headed immediately toward their favorite features, while others paused to watch friends practice new tricks. From every corner came a mixture of sounds—the rattle of wheels, the scrape of boards against rails, and the cheers that followed a successful landing.

(2) Near the center of the park stood the largest ramp. Its curved surface towered above the others and attracted some of the most experienced riders. One teenager soared into the air, spinning before landing smoothly back on the ramp. A small crowd gathered nearby, watching each attempt. Every time a trick worked, applause erupted. Every time someone stumbled, encouraging voices called out, "Try again!" The atmosphere felt competitive and supportive at the same time.

(3) Along one edge of the park stretched a long mural painted on a concrete wall. Bright colors covered the surface from end to end. Mountains, rivers, forests, and city skylines blended together in a scene that seemed to tell dozens of stories at once. Riders often stopped there between turns. Some sat on the low wall beneath the artwork and talked about school, sports, and upcoming competitions. Others simply rested in the shade and watched the activity around them.

(4) The air carried the scent of freshly cut grass from a nearby field. A gentle breeze rustled the leaves of the trees surrounding the park. From beyond the fence came the distant sounds of soccer games and families enjoying the weekend. Even with so much activity nearby, the skate park felt like its own small world. Everyone seemed connected by a shared interest, whether they were attempting advanced tricks or stepping onto a board for the very first time.

(5) At one corner of the park, a young rider stood quietly at the top of a ramp. He adjusted his helmet and stared down at the curved surface below. For several moments, he remained perfectly still while riders passed around him. Then he took a deep breath, pushed forward, and rolled down the ramp. Although the trick was simple compared to what others were attempting, his smile afterward was impossible to miss. Nearby riders nodded and offered words of encouragement. Small victories seemed just as important as spectacular ones.

(6) As the afternoon continued, shadows stretched across the concrete. New riders arrived while others packed up their equipment and headed home. Friends exchanged high-fives and made plans to meet again the following weekend. The energy of the park never seemed to disappear completely. It simply shifted from one group to the next as people came and went.

(7) Looking across the park, it was easy to notice more than just ramps and rails. The skate park was a place where people practiced skills, built confidence, and challenged themselves. It was also a place where friendships grew through shared experiences. Whether someone arrived hoping to master a difficult trick or simply spend time with others, the park offered a space where both goals could exist side by side.

questions.text:

B. The skate park is only for expert riders.

B. It follows one character through a complete conflict and solution.
C. It lists rules for riders to follow.
D. It begins with the ending and then explains the cause.

passages.title: Waiting for the Storm
passages.text:
(1) The beach was unusually quiet for a summer afternoon. Thick gray clouds stretched across the horizon, and the bright blue water had turned a darker shade of steel. The air felt heavy and still. Along the shoreline, colorful umbrellas remained planted in the sand, but most people had packed their belongings and left. The distant cry of a seagull echoed across the nearly empty beach.

(2) Near a weathered lifeguard stand, a family lingered for a few more minutes, watching the waves roll toward shore. The waves seemed larger than they had been earlier in the day. Each one crashed with a deep roar before retreating across the wet sand. A young child pointed toward the horizon, where flashes of lightning flickered inside the clouds.

(3) Palm trees lining the boardwalk swayed gently at first. Then a stronger gust of wind swept across the beach, lifting grains of sand into the air. The scent of saltwater mixed with the smell of rain. Store owners began pulling down awnings while cyclists hurried past on the boardwalk.

(4) The beach seemed to hold its breath. Everything felt suspended between calm and chaos. The storm had not yet arrived, but its presence could be felt in every dark cloud, every gust of wind, and every glance toward the sky. For a moment, the shoreline became a place of anticipation, where people quietly observed nature preparing to change.

passages.title: The Farmers Market
passages.text:
(5) On a sunny Saturday morning, the town square buzzed with activity. Rows of white tents filled the plaza, and shoppers moved from booth to booth carrying baskets and canvas bags. Bright displays of tomatoes, peaches, flowers, and handmade crafts created splashes of color in every direction.

(6) Vendors greeted customers with cheerful smiles. Some offered samples of fresh fruit, while others explained how they grew vegetables on nearby farms. Conversations blended together into a steady hum of voices, laughter, and friendly greetings. A local musician played guitar near the fountain, adding a gentle rhythm to the scene.

(7) Children stopped to examine jars of honey and bouquets of sunflowers. Friends paused beside tables to catch up on recent news. A baker arranged warm loaves of bread while a flower seller carefully misted colorful arrangements. Everywhere people looked, there was something to notice.

(8) Above the crowd, strings of small flags fluttered in the breeze. The scent of baked goods drifted through the square. Although hundreds of people moved through the market, the atmosphere felt relaxed and welcoming. The market was more than a place to shop. It was a place where neighbors gathered, stories were shared, and the community came together for a few hours each week.

questions.text:

D. Both passages focus on sadness after a disaster.

B. Both tell a full story with a main problem and solution.
C. Both are organized as step-by-step instructions.
D. Both focus only on what one character says.

passages.title: The Lost Drone
passages.text:
(1) Marcus had been saving money for nearly a year to buy a drone. He completed extra chores, walked dogs for neighbors, and carefully placed part of every birthday gift into a savings jar. When he finally purchased the drone, he spent weeks learning how to fly it safely.

(2) One Saturday morning, Marcus carried the drone to a large park near his house. The sky was clear, and only a light breeze moved through the trees. After completing a safety check, he launched the drone into the air.

(3) The drone climbed smoothly above the field. Through the controller's screen, Marcus could see winding trails, a nearby pond, and rows of trees stretching across the park. Excited by the view, he guided the drone farther than he had ever flown before.

(4) Suddenly, a warning message flashed across the screen. The battery level was lower than expected. Marcus immediately turned the drone around, but strong winds had begun pushing it away from the park.

(5) He watched nervously as the drone drifted toward a wooded area beyond the field. Then the signal disappeared completely.

(6) Marcus searched for nearly two hours. He walked along trails, checked open spaces, and carefully looked beneath trees. Despite his efforts, he could not find the drone.

(7) Disappointed, he sat on a bench near the parking lot. Just as he was about to leave, an elderly man approached carrying something in his hands.

(8) "Looking for this?" the man asked.

(9) Marcus's eyes widened. It was his drone.

(10) The man explained that he had discovered it resting in a patch of tall grass while walking his dog. Relieved and grateful, Marcus thanked him repeatedly.

(11) As he headed home, Marcus realized he had learned an important lesson. Owning something valuable also meant taking responsibility for using it carefully. The next time he flew the drone, he would pay closer attention to weather conditions and battery levels.

passages.title: The Championship Game
passages.text:
(12) Ava adjusted her soccer cleats as she stepped onto the field. Her team had worked hard all season to reach the championship game. Parents filled the bleachers, teammates warmed up nearby, and a steady buzz of excitement filled the air.

(13) The match began at a fast pace. Both teams moved the ball quickly, and scoring opportunities were rare. Ava played midfield, helping on both offense and defense whenever she could.

(14) By halftime, the score remained tied. The coach reminded the players to stay focused and continue working together.

(15) During the second half, the opposing team scored a goal. With only a few minutes remaining, Ava's team trailed by one point.

(16) Determined not to give up, Ava sprinted after a loose ball near the sideline. She passed it to a teammate, who crossed it toward the goal. Another player scored, tying the game just before time expired.

(17) The championship would be decided in overtime.

(18) The players looked exhausted, but no one wanted to quit. A few minutes into overtime, Ava intercepted a pass and dribbled toward the goal. Two defenders moved to block her path.

(19) Instead of taking the shot herself, Ava passed the ball to an open teammate. The teammate kicked the ball into the net.

(20) The crowd erupted with cheers. Ava's team had won the championship.

(21) After the celebration, the coach praised the players for their teamwork. Ava smiled as she accepted her medal. Although scoring the winning goal might have brought more attention, she knew the right decision had been to pass the ball. Success came from working together rather than seeking individual recognition.

questions.text:
Which idea is developed in both stories?
A. Responsible choices can matter more than personal excitement or attention. [CORRECT]
B. Expensive equipment is needed to solve every problem.
C. Winning is the only way to learn a lesson.
D. People should avoid listening to others.

E. Ava adjusts the straps of her cleats.

A. Marcus must learn to handle a valuable object carefully, while Ava must choose teamwork during a game. [CORRECT]
B. Marcus and Ava both lose a championship.
C. Marcus solves a problem for his team, while Ava searches for a drone.
D. Marcus and Ava both face the same weather warning.

title: Gitanjali Rao: Solving Problems Through Innovation

passages.title: Gitanjali Rao: Solving Problems Through Innovation
passages.text:
(1) When many students were spending their free time playing games or watching television, Gitanjali Rao was thinking about ways to solve real-world problems. Born in 2005, Gitanjali grew up in Colorado with a curiosity about science, technology, and engineering. She enjoyed asking questions, conducting experiments, and learning how inventions could improve people's lives.

(2) One of the events that inspired Gitanjali happened when she learned about concerns regarding lead contamination in drinking water. She wondered whether there might be a faster and easier way to detect harmful substances in water supplies. Instead of simply reading about the issue, she began researching possible solutions. She studied scientific information, tested ideas, and worked with mentors who helped guide her learning.

(3) After many months of effort, Gitanjali developed a device called Tethys. The invention was designed to detect lead in water more quickly than some traditional testing methods. Creating the device required patience and persistence. Not every experiment worked as planned, and many ideas had to be revised before she made progress.

(4) Gitanjali continued looking for other ways to use technology to help people. She became interested in issues such as cyberbullying and communication challenges faced by individuals with certain medical conditions. Rather than focusing on a single project, she explored multiple ways technology could address problems in everyday life.

(5) As her work gained attention, Gitanjali received several honors. In 2017, she was named America's Top Young Scientist after presenting her lead-detection invention. Later, she was recognized by organizations around the world for her creativity and commitment to helping others through innovation.

(6) In 2020, TIME magazine named Gitanjali Rao its first-ever Kid of the Year. The recognition highlighted not only her inventions but also her belief that young people can make meaningful contributions to society. During interviews, she often encouraged students to identify problems they care about and look for ways to create solutions.

(7) Despite her accomplishments, Gitanjali frequently emphasizes that innovation is not limited to scientists or inventors. She believes anyone can develop ideas that improve their community. According to Gitanjali, solving problems begins with curiosity, a willingness to learn, and the determination to keep trying when challenges arise.

(8) Today, Gitanjali continues to study science and technology while inspiring young people around the world. Her story demonstrates that age does not determine a person's ability to make a difference. Through creativity, persistence, and a desire to help others, she has shown that even young people can tackle important challenges and contribute meaningful ideas.

questions.text:
Which central idea is developed in the biography?
A. Curiosity, persistence, and a desire to help others can allow young people to make a difference. [CORRECT]
B. Inventors succeed only when every experiment works the first time.
C. Young people cannot contribute useful ideas.
D. Technology is important only when it wins awards.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In the passage, innovation means ( ).
Drop-down options: creating new ideas or solutions [CORRECT] / repeating old answers / avoiding difficult problems / winning every contest

Select TWO details that show Gitanjali's persistence.
A. Creating Tethys required many months of effort. [CORRECT]
B. She grew up in Colorado.
C. Not every experiment worked, and many ideas had to be revised. [CORRECT]
D. TIME magazine named her Kid of the Year.
E. She enjoyed learning about engineering.

How does the chronological structure support the central idea?
A. It shows how Gitanjali's curiosity led to research, invention, recognition, and inspiring others. [CORRECT]
B. It hides the order of events to create mystery.
C. It describes only one day in her life.
D. It focuses on awards before explaining why her work matters.

writingPrompt.text: Which quality contributed more to Gitanjali Rao's success: her curiosity about problems or her determination to keep working when challenges arose? passages.title: Mark Rober: Making Science Fun
passages.text:
(1) Many people know Mark Rober as a popular science creator who builds unusual inventions and shares experiments online. Millions of viewers watch his videos to see projects involving engineering, problem-solving, and creativity. Before becoming a well-known creator, however, Rober spent years working as an engineer and learning how science can be used to solve real-world problems.

(2) Mark Rober grew up with a strong interest in figuring out how things worked. As a child, he enjoyed building, experimenting, and finding solutions to challenges. Instead of simply accepting that something could not be done, he often looked for ways to improve it. This curiosity would later become an important part of his career.

(3) After studying engineering in college, Rober began working at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory. There, he contributed to projects related to space exploration. One of the most notable projects he worked on was the Curiosity rover, which was designed to explore the surface of Mars. The rover traveled millions of miles through space before successfully landing on the planet in 2012.

(4) Working on such a complex mission required patience, teamwork, and careful attention to detail. Engineers spent years planning, testing, and improving the technology before launch. Even small mistakes could create major problems. Through this experience, Rober learned the importance of persistence and preparation.

(5) Although he enjoyed engineering, Rober also wanted to help more people become interested in science. He began creating videos that explained scientific concepts through entertaining demonstrations and experiments. His goal was to show that science is not just a subject taught in school—it is a way of understanding and exploring the world.

(6) Over time, his videos reached millions of viewers. Some featured creative inventions, while others focused on solving everyday problems. One of his most famous projects involved designing a device that used glitter and harmless spray to discourage package thieves. The invention combined engineering, creativity, and humor to address a real problem.

(7) Rober's videos often show projects that fail before they succeed. Instead of hiding mistakes, he explains what went wrong and how he improved the design. He believes that failure is often an important part of learning. By sharing both successes and setbacks, he encourages others to keep trying when challenges arise.

(8) In addition to creating videos, Rober has participated in educational and environmental projects that encourage people to use science and technology to improve their communities. Through these efforts, he has inspired many students to explore engineering, problem-solving, and innovation.

(9) Today, Mark Rober continues to create content that combines education and entertainment. His work demonstrates that curiosity, creativity, and perseverance can help people solve problems and make complex ideas easier to understand. Whether designing technology for space exploration or building inventions for a video, he approaches each challenge with the same goal: learning something new and sharing that knowledge with others.

passages.title: Mikaila Ulmer
passages.text:
(1) When Mikaila Ulmer was four years old, she was stung by two bees within a short period of time. At first, the experience frightened her. However, instead of developing a dislike for bees, Mikaila became curious about them. She began learning about the important role bees play in pollinating plants and helping ecosystems thrive.

(2) Around the same time, Mikaila and her family discovered a recipe for flaxseed lemonade that had been passed down through generations. As she learned more about the challenges facing bee populations, she came up with an idea. She would start a lemonade business and use part of the profits to support organizations that protect bees.

(3) Mikaila launched her business, Me & the Bees Lemonade, while she was still a child. Like many entrepreneurs, she faced challenges. She had to learn about creating products, speaking with customers, and managing a growing business. Each new obstacle required patience and problem-solving.

(4) Her determination paid off. Over time, her lemonade became available in stores across the United States. She also spoke at events and shared information about the importance of protecting pollinators. Through her business, Mikaila demonstrated that young people can combine creativity and purpose to address real-world issues.

(5) Today, Mikaila continues to advocate for bees and environmental awareness. Her story shows how curiosity and action can transform a simple idea into an opportunity to make a positive difference.

passages.title: Ryan Kaji
passages.text:
(6) Ryan Kaji became known to millions of people through videos that began with a simple idea: sharing toys, games, and activities that interested him. As a young child, Ryan enjoyed watching online videos and eventually asked his parents why he could not create his own.

(7) With support from his family, Ryan began making videos that featured toy reviews, science activities, challenges, and educational content. At first, only a small audience watched. However, his enthusiasm and personality helped attract more viewers over time.

(8) As his audience grew, Ryan and his family expanded their projects. They developed educational products, books, games, and learning experiences designed for children. What started as a small creative project eventually became a large business and media brand.

(9) Despite his success, creating content required consistency and effort. New ideas had to be developed, videos needed to be planned and recorded, and projects required teamwork. Ryan's success did not happen overnight. It developed through years of creativity, persistence, and a willingness to try new things.

(10) Today, Ryan continues to create content while encouraging children to explore, learn, and have fun. His story demonstrates how pursuing an interest can lead to opportunities that were never imagined at the beginning.

passages.title: Coco Gauff
passages.text:
(1) Coco Gauff was introduced to tennis at a young age and quickly developed a passion for the sport. Growing up in Florida, she spent countless hours practicing and improving her skills. Her parents supported her goals, but success required much more than talent alone. Coco had to commit herself to training, learning, and improving every day.

(2) As she competed against stronger and more experienced players, Coco faced many challenges. Some matches ended in disappointment, and some goals took longer to achieve than she expected. Instead of giving up, she used each experience as an opportunity to learn. Coaches and teammates often praised her determination and willingness to keep working even when things became difficult.

(3) In 2019, Coco gained international attention when she defeated one of her tennis heroes, Venus Williams, at Wimbledon. At just fifteen years old, she became one of the youngest athletes to make such an impact at a major tournament. The victory introduced her to fans around the world, but it also brought new expectations and pressure.

(4) Rather than becoming distracted by her growing fame, Coco continued focusing on improvement. She worked to strengthen different parts of her game and remained committed to her long-term goals. Over the following years, she earned additional victories and established herself as one of the top players in professional tennis.

(5) Today, Coco Gauff is admired not only for her athletic achievements but also for her maturity, work ethic, and leadership. Her story demonstrates how dedication and perseverance can help people achieve ambitious goals.

passages.title: Ryan Trahan
passages.text:
(6) Ryan Trahan grew up in Texas and enjoyed creating videos that shared his interests and experiences. What began as a hobby eventually grew into a career as millions of people started watching his content online. However, Ryan's success did not happen immediately. Building an audience required creativity, consistency, and a willingness to experiment with new ideas.

(7) Throughout his career, Ryan developed a reputation for creating unusual challenges and adventures. Many of his projects involved setting difficult goals and documenting the steps required to achieve them. Some ideas worked well, while others failed to attract attention. Instead of becoming discouraged, Ryan learned from those experiences and continued improving his content.

(8) One of Ryan's most well-known projects involved traveling across the United States while starting with only a small amount of money. Along the way, he met new people, solved unexpected problems, and raised money for charitable causes. The challenge required planning, adaptability, and persistence.

(9) As his audience continued to grow, Ryan used his platform to support fundraising campaigns and community projects. His videos often encourage viewers to think creatively, stay positive during challenges, and work toward meaningful goals.

(10) Today, Ryan Trahan continues creating content while inspiring others through storytelling, entrepreneurship, and philanthropy. His journey shows how creativity and persistence can turn a simple hobby into an opportunity to help others.

title: The Day the Wright Flyer Took Flight

passages.title: The Day the Wright Flyer Took Flight
passages.text:
(1) The wind swept across the sandy dunes near Kitty Hawk, North Carolina, on the morning of December 17, 1903. The weather was cold, and the sky was mostly gray. A small group of people stood nearby, watching two brothers make final adjustments to an unusual machine resting on the ground. At first glance, the aircraft looked fragile. Its wooden frame, fabric-covered wings, and exposed engine seemed unlikely to achieve what many inventors had spent years attempting.

(2) Wilbur and Orville Wright had traveled a long road to reach that moment. The brothers owned a bicycle shop in Ohio, but their interests extended far beyond repairing and selling bicycles. For years, they had studied the problem of human flight. They read about earlier experiments, tested ideas using kites and gliders, and carefully recorded the results of countless trials.

(3) Success did not come easily. Many of their early experiments failed. Some gliders did not perform as expected, and strong winds occasionally damaged their equipment. Instead of abandoning their goal, the brothers analyzed what went wrong and made improvements. Each setback taught them something new about balance, control, and aerodynamics.

(4) On that December morning, the brothers believed they were finally ready to test their most advanced invention: the Wright Flyer. Unlike earlier gliders, the Flyer included an engine and propellers designed to provide powered flight. If the machine worked, it could become the first aircraft to achieve sustained, controlled flight with a pilot on board.

(5) Shortly after 10:00 a.m., Orville climbed onto the lower wing of the Flyer and prepared for takeoff. Wilbur steadied the aircraft as it rested on a launching rail. The engine rattled loudly as it came to life. Nearby observers watched closely, uncertain whether the machine would rise into the air or collapse onto the sand.

(6) Then the Flyer began to move. Slowly at first, it rolled forward along the rail. As its speed increased, the aircraft lifted from the ground. For twelve remarkable seconds, Orville flew above the sand, traveling 120 feet before landing. Although the flight was brief, it marked a historic achievement.

(7) The brothers completed several additional flights that day. The longest lasted fifty-nine seconds and covered more than 850 feet. By modern standards, those distances may seem small. However, the flights demonstrated that powered, controlled flight was possible.

(8) News of the accomplishment did not spread immediately. Many people remained skeptical, and some newspapers paid little attention to the event. Over time, however, the significance of the Wright brothers' achievement became clear. Their work helped launch a new era of transportation that would eventually connect people and places around the world.

(9) Looking back, historians recognize that the success of the Wright Flyer was not the result of a single lucky moment. It was the product of years of experimentation, problem-solving, and perseverance. The brothers' willingness to learn from failure allowed them to accomplish something that many people believed was impossible.

passages.title: Alone in the Canyon
passages.text:
(1) In April 2003, an experienced outdoorsman named Aron Ralston set out to explore Bluejohn Canyon in southeastern Utah. The narrow canyon, with its towering red rock walls and winding passages, attracted hikers and climbers from around the world. Ralston had visited remote areas many times before and felt confident in his ability to navigate challenging terrain.

(2) The day began like many of his previous adventures. The weather was clear, and the canyon was quiet except for the sound of wind moving through the rocky passageways. As he climbed, hiked, and explored, Ralston enjoyed the freedom of traveling through one of the most rugged landscapes in the American Southwest.

(3) During the afternoon, however, everything changed. While descending through a narrow section of the canyon, Ralston dislodged a large boulder. The rock suddenly shifted and pinned his right arm against the canyon wall. Despite repeated efforts, he could not free himself.

(4) At first, Ralston believed he would find a way to escape. He pushed against the boulder, pulled on his trapped arm, and searched for weaknesses in the rock. Hours passed without success. As daylight faded, he realized the situation was far more serious than he had imagined.

(5) Over the next several days, Ralston remained trapped and alone. He carefully rationed his limited supply of food and water. To stay focused, he recorded video messages for family and friends and documented his experience in a journal. He also continued searching for possible solutions, refusing to give up despite the increasingly difficult circumstances.

(6) The canyon became both beautiful and unforgiving. During the day, sunlight illuminated the red sandstone walls. At night, temperatures dropped, and the canyon grew silent. With each passing day, Ralston faced physical exhaustion, dehydration, and uncertainty about whether rescue would arrive in time.

(7) Eventually, he realized that waiting was no longer an option. After carefully considering his situation, Ralston made an extraordinary decision. He took action to free himself and begin the difficult journey back to safety. Although the choice required tremendous courage and determination, it ultimately allowed him to survive.

(8) Even after escaping the canyon, Ralston still faced major challenges. He had to hike across rough terrain before eventually encountering other people who helped him reach medical care. His survival depended not only on physical endurance but also on his ability to remain calm and think clearly during an emergency.

(9) News of Ralston's experience spread around the world. Many people were amazed by his resilience and determination. His story later became the subject of books, interviews, and a feature film. While the details of his survival were extraordinary, the broader lesson resonated with many people: difficult challenges often require perseverance, problem-solving, and the courage to make difficult decisions.

(10) Today, Aron Ralston's experience remains one of the most remarkable survival stories in modern history. His time in Bluejohn Canyon serves as a reminder that preparation is important, but so is the ability to adapt when circumstances change unexpectedly. Faced with an overwhelming challenge, Ralston refused to surrender and continued searching for a path forward.

passages.title: Lost in the Alaskan Wilderness
passages.text:
(1) In 1928, a young trapper named Leon Crane survived one of the most remarkable wilderness ordeals in Alaska's history. Crane was traveling by small aircraft to a remote area when the plane encountered problems and crashed in the rugged wilderness. The pilot did not survive, and Crane suddenly found himself alone hundreds of miles from the nearest town.

(2) The Alaskan landscape was both beautiful and dangerous. Snow-covered mountains stretched across the horizon, temperatures frequently dropped below freezing, and food was difficult to find. Crane had limited supplies and no reliable way to communicate with rescuers.

(3) At first, he stayed near the crash site, hoping search teams would find him quickly. Days passed, however, and no rescue arrived. Realizing he could not depend entirely on being found, Crane began developing a plan to survive.

(4) He carefully rationed food and searched the area for anything useful. Using materials from the wreckage, he created shelter from the harsh weather. He also learned how to travel safely through unfamiliar terrain while conserving energy.

(5) As the weeks passed, Crane faced constant challenges. Deep snow slowed his progress, storms reduced visibility, and the isolation tested his determination. Despite these difficulties, he continued moving toward areas where he believed help might be found.

(6) After spending months in the wilderness, Crane eventually reached a remote cabin. Inside, he discovered supplies that helped him recover his strength. From there, he continued his journey until he finally encountered people who could assist him.

(7) By the time he was rescued, Crane had survived for more than eighty days. His experience became one of Alaska's most famous survival stories and demonstrated the importance of perseverance, planning, and adaptability during an emergency.

passages.title: Ernest Shackleton and the Endurance Expedition
passages.text:
(8) In 1914, explorer Ernest Shackleton led an expedition to Antarctica with an ambitious goal: crossing the frozen continent. His ship, the Endurance, carried a crew of twenty-eight men into one of the harshest environments on Earth.

(9) For months, the expedition traveled through icy waters. Then disaster struck. The Endurance became trapped in sea ice that surrounded the ship and prevented it from moving. The pressure from the ice steadily increased until the vessel was crushed and eventually sank.

(10) Stranded thousands of miles from home, Shackleton and his crew faced an uncertain future. They established camps on drifting ice and salvaged as many supplies as possible before the ship disappeared beneath the frozen ocean.

(11) Conditions were extremely difficult. Temperatures remained bitterly cold, food was limited, and the crew had no way to call for help. Nevertheless, Shackleton worked constantly to maintain morale and organize survival efforts. He understood that leadership would be just as important as physical endurance.

(12) After months on the ice, the crew launched small lifeboats and navigated dangerous seas to reach a remote island. Even then, they were not safe. Shackleton and several crew members undertook another perilous journey across the ocean and through mountainous terrain to seek rescue.

(13) Eventually, after numerous attempts, Shackleton succeeded in returning with help. Remarkably, every member of the expedition survived. Historians continue to study the expedition because of the leadership, teamwork, and resilience displayed during the crisis.

(14) Although the expedition failed to achieve its original goal, Shackleton's ability to guide his crew through extraordinary challenges transformed the journey into one of history's greatest survival stories.

D. Both stories take place in the same Alaskan cabin.

passages.title: The Great Boston Molasses Flood
passages.text:
(1) On January 15, 1919, residents of Boston, Massachusetts, were going about their normal routines. Workers moved through the busy streets of the North End neighborhood, children played nearby, and horses pulled wagons through the city. Few people paid much attention to the enormous steel tank that stood near the harbor. The tank held more than two million gallons of molasses, a thick syrup used to make food and industrial products.

(2) Shortly after noon, a loud rumbling sound echoed through the neighborhood. Witnesses later described hearing noises that resembled a train rushing down the tracks. Without warning, the massive tank burst apart. Steel plates tore away, and a tremendous wave of molasses surged into the streets.

(3) The wave moved with surprising speed, knocking over buildings, wagons, and utility poles. People scrambled to escape as the sticky liquid swept through the area. Some climbed to higher ground, while others helped neighbors who were trapped by the flood.

(4) Rescue efforts began almost immediately. Police officers, firefighters, sailors, and ordinary citizens worked together to search for survivors. The thick molasses made movement difficult, and rescuers often struggled to reach people who needed help. Despite the challenging conditions, many individuals risked their own safety to assist others.

(5) In the days that followed, cleanup crews faced an enormous task. Molasses coated streets, buildings, and equipment throughout the neighborhood. Historians estimate that it took weeks to fully clean the affected area. Investigations later revealed concerns about the tank's construction and maintenance.

(6) Although the event was tragic, it led to changes in safety regulations and engineering practices. Today, the Great Boston Molasses Flood remains one of the most unusual disasters in American history. The story serves as a reminder of the importance of safety, planning, and community cooperation during emergencies.

passages.title: The Race to Save Nome
passages.text:
(7) During the winter of 1925, the remote town of Nome, Alaska, faced a serious crisis. A dangerous disease called diphtheria threatened many residents, especially children. The town needed medicine quickly, but harsh winter conditions made transportation extremely difficult.

(8) Airplanes could not safely fly through the severe weather, and ships were unable to reach the frozen region. With time running short, officials developed a new plan. Teams of dog sled drivers, known as mushers, would relay the medicine across hundreds of miles of snow-covered wilderness.

(9) The journey began as temperatures dropped far below zero. Strong winds reduced visibility, and blizzards swept across the landscape. Despite the dangers, mushers and their sled dogs pushed forward. Each team carried the medicine part of the distance before passing it to the next group.

(10) One of the most famous mushers was Leonhard Seppala, who traveled through some of the most dangerous sections of the route with his lead dog, Togo. Other mushers also played critical roles, navigating frozen rivers, icy trails, and fierce storms.

(11) Day and night, the relay continued. Communities along the route followed the progress closely, hoping the medicine would arrive in time. Finally, after traveling more than 600 miles, the serum reached Nome.

(12) The successful delivery helped prevent a much larger outbreak and saved countless lives. The event became known as the Serum Run and remains one of the most celebrated examples of courage, teamwork, and determination in American history. Each participant contributed to the effort, proving that extraordinary challenges can sometimes be overcome when people work together toward a common goal.

D. Both events happen in Boston during the same year.
