Be sure to: include a thesis statement; address counterclaims; use evidence from multiple passages; and avoid overly relying on one passage.

title: Ancient Ideas in a Modern World

passages.title: Ancient Ideas in a Modern World
passages.text:
1. Thousands of years ago, some of the world's earliest civilizations developed in Mesopotamia, Egypt, India, and China. These societies created governments, economic systems, social structures, religions, technologies, and agricultural practices that helped large populations survive and thrive. Although these civilizations existed long ago, many of their ideas continue to influence life in the Eastern Hemisphere today.

2. Early civilizations often developed near major rivers. The Nile River in Egypt, the Tigris and Euphrates Rivers in Mesopotamia, the Indus River in India, and the Huang He (Yellow River) in China provided water for farming. Fertile soil allowed farmers to grow crops that supported growing populations and encouraged the development of cities.

3. As populations increased, governments became necessary to organize society. Leaders established laws, collected taxes, managed resources, and organized public projects. One famous example is Hammurabi's Code in Mesopotamia, one of the earliest known written legal systems. Written laws helped create order and establish expectations for citizens.

4. Religion also played an important role in daily life. Ancient Egyptians built temples and pyramids connected to their religious beliefs. In India, Hinduism emerged and influenced cultural practices that continue today. Ancient Chinese philosophies such as Confucianism emphasized respect, responsibility, and education, shaping social relationships for generations.

5. These civilizations also developed technologies that changed how people lived. Mesopotamians created writing systems that allowed information to be recorded and shared. Egyptians developed advanced engineering techniques that made large construction projects possible. Chinese inventors created innovations such as paper, which improved communication and record keeping.

6. Agriculture remained at the center of these societies. Farmers developed irrigation systems to move water to crops during dry periods. These systems increased food production and helped support larger populations. Successful farming allowed people to specialize in other occupations, including trade, construction, government, and scholarship.

7. Historians often study ancient civilizations to understand how their ideas continue to influence the modern world. Some scholars believe governments today can learn valuable lessons from ancient civilizations. They argue that examining early legal systems, public works projects, and methods of organizing society can help leaders address modern challenges.

8. Supporters of this view point out that many ancient ideas have endured for centuries because they solved important problems. Early systems of law helped maintain order, while organized governments coordinated large projects that benefited entire communities. They believe studying these successes can provide useful guidance today.

9. Others believe modern societies face challenges that are very different from those of ancient civilizations. Rapid technological change, global trade networks, and large urban populations create issues that ancient leaders never encountered. They argue that while history remains important, modern governments should focus primarily on developing new solutions.

10. Some historians note that ancient societies were often structured differently than modern societies. Social classes, political systems, and economic opportunities varied greatly. Because of these differences, they caution against applying ancient ideas directly to present-day situations.

11. Supporters of learning from ancient civilizations respond that the goal is not to copy the past exactly. Instead, they believe understanding how earlier societies solved problems can provide insights that inspire modern solutions. They argue that studying history helps people recognize patterns and avoid repeating mistakes.

12. Ancient civilizations of Mesopotamia, Egypt, India, and China left lasting influences on governments, economies, religions, technologies, and agriculture. Historians continue to examine these contributions to better understand the Eastern Hemisphere today. Whether ancient civilizations should play a major role in shaping modern decisions remains a topic of discussion among scholars and leaders.

questions.text:
Which statement best expresses a central idea of the passage?

D. River valleys made technology unnecessary.

writingPrompt.text: Should modern societies use lessons from ancient civilizations to help guide important decisions today? Be sure to: include a thesis statement; address counterclaims; use evidence from the passage; and avoid overly relying on one part of the passage.

passages.title: What Makes a Civilization Successful?
passages.text:
1. More than 4,000 years ago, some of the world's earliest civilizations developed in Mesopotamia, Egypt, India, and China. Although these societies formed in different locations, they shared a common challenge: finding ways to support growing populations. To survive and prosper, each civilization developed governments, economic systems, social structures, religions, technologies, and agricultural practices that met the needs of its people.

2. Mesopotamia developed between the Tigris and Euphrates Rivers in an area often called the "Fertile Crescent." Farmers used irrigation systems to direct river water to their crops. These agricultural innovations helped communities produce enough food to support cities, merchants, artisans, and government officials.

3. Mesopotamian leaders established some of the earliest known legal systems. One famous example is Hammurabi's Code, a collection of written laws that addressed trade, property, crime, and family relationships. Written laws helped create order and allowed governments to manage increasingly complex societies.

4. Ancient Egypt flourished along the Nile River. Each year, flooding deposited nutrient-rich soil that helped farmers grow crops. Egyptian rulers organized large public projects, including temples, monuments, and irrigation systems. The government played a major role in coordinating resources and labor throughout the kingdom.

5. Religion influenced nearly every aspect of Egyptian society. Religious beliefs inspired the construction of pyramids and other monumental structures. These projects demonstrated both the importance of religion and the ability of the government to organize large numbers of workers.

6. In ancient India, communities developed along the Indus River. Archaeologists have discovered evidence of carefully planned cities that included streets arranged in grids and advanced drainage systems. These features suggest that city leaders placed a strong emphasis on organization and public infrastructure.

7. Ancient Chinese civilization developed near the Huang He, or Yellow River. Chinese farmers adapted to changing environmental conditions while producing crops that supported growing populations. Chinese societies also developed systems of government and philosophies that emphasized social order, responsibility, and education.

8. All four civilizations depended heavily on agriculture. Reliable food production allowed populations to grow and created opportunities for people to specialize in occupations beyond farming. As a result, trade expanded, technologies advanced, and societies became increasingly complex.

9. Historians often debate which factors contributed most to the success of early civilizations. Some scholars believe agriculture was the most important factor. They argue that without stable food supplies, civilizations could not have supported large populations, governments, or specialized workers.

10. Supporters of this view point out that each civilization developed near rivers that supported farming. Irrigation systems, fertile soil, and agricultural knowledge helped societies produce food surpluses. These surpluses allowed people to focus on trade, government, religion, and technological innovation.

11. Other historians argue that governments and social organization played a larger role. They believe strong leadership, written laws, public works projects, and organized labor allowed civilizations to thrive. According to this perspective, successful governments helped communities manage resources and solve problems.

12. Some scholars also emphasize the importance of technology and innovation. Writing systems, engineering projects, transportation networks, and agricultural tools helped civilizations expand their influence and improve daily life. These advancements allowed societies to preserve knowledge and adapt to changing conditions.

13. The cultural practices and products of these early civilizations continue to influence the Eastern Hemisphere today. Legal systems, religious traditions, agricultural methods, technologies, and social ideas developed thousands of years ago still shape many societies around the world.

14. Although historians agree that agriculture, government, social organization, religion, and technology all contributed to the success of early civilizations, they continue to debate which factor was most important. Understanding these civilizations helps people better understand both the ancient world and the societies that exist today.

questions.text:

E. Religions influenced Egyptian society.

B. Mesopotamia was the only successful early civilization.

D. Ancient governments did not organize resources.

writingPrompt.text: Which factor was most important to the success of early civilizations: agriculture, government, or technology? Be sure to: include a thesis statement; address counterclaims; use evidence from the passage; and avoid overly relying on one part of the passage.

passages.title: Ancient Innovations That Still Matter
passages.text:
1. Thousands of years ago, early civilizations developed in Mesopotamia, Egypt, India, and China. Although these societies existed long before modern technology, they created systems and inventions that helped people solve everyday problems. Many of their ideas continue to influence life in the Eastern Hemisphere today.

2. Mesopotamia, often called the "Cradle of Civilization," developed between the Tigris and Euphrates Rivers. Farmers used irrigation systems to move water to crops, helping communities grow enough food to support large populations. Mesopotamians also developed one of the earliest writing systems, known as cuneiform, which allowed people to record information, conduct trade, and preserve knowledge.

3. Ancient Egypt thrived along the Nile River. Egyptian farmers relied on fertile soil deposited by annual floods. The government organized large construction projects, including temples and pyramids. Egyptians also developed calendars based on observations of the stars and the flooding cycle of the Nile, helping farmers determine the best times to plant and harvest crops.

4. In ancient India, communities developed sophisticated urban centers. Archaeologists have discovered cities with carefully planned streets and advanced drainage systems. These innovations improved sanitation and helped support large populations. Trade networks connected communities and encouraged the exchange of goods and ideas.

5. Ancient China contributed important inventions and philosophies. Chinese farmers developed agricultural techniques that increased food production. Chinese inventors later created paper, which improved communication and record keeping. Philosophies such as Confucianism emphasized education, responsibility, and respect for others, influencing social relationships and government systems.

6. Supporters of studying ancient civilizations argue that many modern societies continue to benefit from these contributions. Written laws, organized governments, irrigation systems, calendars, transportation networks, and methods of recording information all have roots in ideas developed by early civilizations.

7. They point out that understanding these innovations helps people recognize how societies solve problems. Ancient civilizations faced challenges involving food production, government organization, trade, and communication. Many of their solutions influenced later societies throughout Asia, Africa, and the Middle East.

8. Supporters believe the achievements of early civilizations demonstrate how knowledge from the past can remain useful long after the civilizations themselves have changed or disappeared.

9. For this reason, many historians argue that studying ancient civilizations remains one of the best ways to understand the development of societies in the Eastern Hemisphere.

passages.title: New Challenges in a Changing World
passages.text:
1. Early civilizations made important contributions to human history, but some historians believe modern societies face challenges that require new solutions. While ancient innovations remain important, they argue that today's world differs greatly from the societies of Mesopotamia, Egypt, India, and China.

2. Ancient civilizations were largely agricultural societies. Most people worked as farmers and lived near the resources they depended on. In contrast, many modern societies are connected through global trade, digital communication, and advanced transportation systems. These developments have created opportunities and challenges that ancient civilizations never experienced.

3. Modern governments also operate on a much larger scale. National governments may serve millions or even billions of people. They must address issues such as cybersecurity, global health concerns, climate change, and international cooperation. Some historians argue that these challenges require approaches that go beyond lessons from the ancient world.

4. Technological innovation continues to transform daily life. Computers, satellites, smartphones, and artificial intelligence have changed how people communicate, work, and learn. While ancient inventions laid important foundations, many modern developments are the result of recent scientific discoveries.

5. Some scholars believe modern societies should focus primarily on preparing for future challenges rather than relying too heavily on historical examples. They argue that solutions developed thousands of years ago may not always apply to today's rapidly changing world.

6. Others note that cultural practices evolve over time. Religions, governments, economic systems, and social structures have adapted as societies have changed. As a result, modern cultures often combine influences from ancient traditions with new ideas and technologies.

7. Supporters of this view are not suggesting that ancient civilizations should be ignored. Instead, they believe history should be studied alongside current events, scientific advancements, and emerging technologies. Understanding both the past and the present may help societies make informed decisions.

8. They argue that while ancient civilizations help explain how societies developed, modern challenges often require innovative solutions that reflect current circumstances.

9. As people continue to study the Eastern Hemisphere, historians, leaders, and citizens must decide how much guidance should come from the past and how much should come from new ideas designed for the future.

questions.text:
How do the two passages differ?
A. Passage 1 emphasizes ancient innovations that still matter, while Passage 2 emphasizes modern challenges that may require new solutions. [CORRECT]
B. Passage 1 focuses on cybersecurity, while Passage 2 focuses on irrigation.
C. Passage 1 argues ancient societies had digital communication, while Passage 2 argues writing never mattered.
D. Both passages say history should be ignored.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
Passage 2 develops its argument by comparing ancient agricultural societies with ( ).

Select TWO details that support studying ancient civilizations.
A. Many modern societies continue to benefit from ancient contributions such as laws, irrigation, calendars, and record keeping. [CORRECT]
B. Modern governments must address cybersecurity and global health concerns.
C. Studying ancient solutions helps people recognize how societies solve problems. [CORRECT]
D. Ancient civilizations never relied on agriculture.
E. Modern cultures never combine old and new ideas.

Which claim is supported by evidence from both passages?
A. Societies can make informed decisions by studying history while also considering present conditions and new ideas. [CORRECT]
B. Ancient inventions solve every modern problem.
C. Modern technology has no effect on daily life.
D. Historians agree that the past should be copied exactly.

writingPrompt.text: When trying to solve modern challenges, should societies rely more on lessons from ancient civilizations or on developing new solutions for today's world? Write a multi-paragraph response in which you make and support a claim about whether societies should rely more on lessons from ancient civilizations or on developing new solutions for today's world. Your response must be based on ideas and information that can be found in the passages. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passages; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: include a thesis statement; address counterclaims; use evidence from multiple passages; and avoid overly relying on one passage.

passages.title: Ancient Traditions in Modern Life
passages.text:
1. The earliest civilizations of Mesopotamia, Egypt, India, and China developed thousands of years ago, yet their influence can still be seen throughout the Eastern Hemisphere today. These civilizations established governments, created economic systems, developed technologies, practiced religions, and built social structures that shaped the lives of millions of people.

2. In Mesopotamia, communities developed systems of writing, trade, and law. One of the most famous examples is Hammurabi's Code, a collection of written laws that helped govern society. Historians believe written laws provided consistency and helped governments manage growing populations.

3. Ancient Egypt relied on the Nile River to support agriculture. Egyptian farmers used fertile soil left behind by annual floods to grow crops. The government organized large construction projects, including temples and pyramids, which demonstrated both engineering skill and political organization.

4. In India, early civilizations along the Indus River developed carefully planned cities with drainage systems and organized streets. Religious traditions such as Hinduism later became important parts of life for millions of people and continue to influence culture today.

5. Ancient Chinese civilizations contributed technologies, agricultural practices, and philosophies that shaped East Asian societies. Confucianism emphasized education, respect, responsibility, and social harmony. These ideas continue to influence cultural values in many regions.

6. Supporters of preserving traditional cultural practices argue that these ancient civilizations provide important connections to the past. Festivals, religious customs, languages, art forms, and social traditions help people understand their history and identity.

7. They believe that maintaining these traditions helps preserve knowledge that has been passed down through generations. Ancient customs can teach people about the beliefs, values, and experiences of their ancestors.

8. Supporters also point out that cultural traditions attract visitors interested in history and heritage. Historic sites, religious celebrations, and traditional arts often contribute to tourism and local economies.

9. For these reasons, many people believe societies should place a strong emphasis on preserving the cultural practices and products that originated in early civilizations.

passages.title: Balancing Tradition and Change
passages.text:
1. While many cultural practices can be traced to ancient civilizations, societies continue to change over time. New technologies, economic systems, and forms of communication have transformed daily life across the Eastern Hemisphere.

2. Some historians argue that cultures remain strong because they adapt. Throughout history, societies have adopted new ideas through trade, migration, exploration, and innovation. These influences often combined with older traditions to create new cultural practices.

3. Economic systems today differ greatly from those of ancient civilizations. Modern economies rely on global trade, digital technology, manufacturing, and services in addition to agriculture. Supporters of cultural adaptation argue that traditions should evolve alongside these changes.

4. Governments have also changed significantly. Ancient rulers often held centralized authority, while many modern governments include elected representatives and expanded opportunities for public participation. Some people believe cultural practices should reflect these modern realities.

5. Technology has accelerated cultural change. Digital communication allows people to share ideas instantly across continents. Social media, online learning, and global entertainment influence how people interact and express themselves.

6. Supporters of adaptation argue that societies should not feel obligated to preserve every tradition exactly as it existed in the past. Instead, they believe cultures should continue developing in ways that meet current needs and challenges.

7. Others note that younger generations often create new traditions while maintaining connections to their heritage. This process allows cultures to remain relevant while still honoring important historical influences.

8. These individuals are not suggesting that ancient civilizations are unimportant. Rather, they believe the most successful societies are those that learn from the past while embracing innovation and change.

9. As the Eastern Hemisphere continues to evolve, people will likely continue debating how much influence ancient civilizations should have on modern life. The discussion reflects broader questions about the relationship between history, culture, and progress.

questions.text:
What is one similarity between the passages?

B. Both argue traditions should never change.
C. Both focus only on digital communication.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
As used in Passage 2, evolve means ( ).

B. Digital communication lets people share ideas instantly.

D. Modern economies rely on services and manufacturing.
E. Younger generations often create new traditions.

Which central idea is developed across both passages?
A. Societies debate how much to preserve traditions from ancient civilizations and how much to adapt them for modern life. [CORRECT]
B. Ancient civilizations have no influence on modern life.
C. Traditions are useful only for tourism.
D. Technology prevents all cultural change.

Your response must be based on ideas and information that can be found in the passages. Be sure to: include a thesis statement; address counterclaims; use evidence from multiple passages; and avoid overly relying on one passage.

passages.title: Comparing Places, Choosing Priorities
passages.text:
1. Geographers study regions to better understand the world. A region is an area that shares certain characteristics. These characteristics may include landforms, climate, population, culture, economic activities, or other features. By examining data related to these characteristics, geographers can classify and compare different regions.

2. Some regions are defined by physical features. Mountain regions, coastal regions, desert regions, and river valleys all have distinct landforms. These features often influence how people live, travel, and use resources. For example, communities in mountainous areas may face different transportation challenges than communities located on flat plains.

3. Climate is another important factor used to classify regions. Temperature, precipitation, and seasonal weather patterns affect agriculture, water supplies, and daily life. Tropical regions often experience warm temperatures throughout the year, while polar regions are much colder. These differences can influence where people choose to live and work.

4. Population data also helps geographers compare regions. Some areas contain densely populated cities with millions of residents, while others have small populations spread across large distances. Population patterns can affect housing, transportation systems, public services, and economic opportunities.

5. Cultural characteristics contribute to regional identity as well. Language, religion, traditions, food, music, and celebrations often help distinguish one region from another. These cultural traits may develop over long periods of time and reflect the history of the people who live there.

6. Economic activities provide another way to compare regions. Some regions rely heavily on agriculture, while others focus on manufacturing, tourism, technology, or natural resources. Economic characteristics often influence employment opportunities and standards of living.

7. Governments and organizations frequently use regional data when making decisions. Information about population growth, climate conditions, transportation networks, and economic activity can help leaders determine where resources should be directed. These decisions may affect schools, roads, healthcare facilities, and other public services.

8. Some people believe regional data should play the largest role in decision-making. They argue that objective information provides a clear picture of a region's needs. If population data shows rapid growth in a certain area, for example, leaders may be able to justify building additional schools, roads, or public facilities.

9. Supporters of this approach also point out that data allows regions to be compared fairly. Climate records, population statistics, economic reports, and geographic information can help leaders identify challenges and opportunities. They believe decisions based on measurable evidence are more likely to address real needs.

10. Others argue that data alone cannot tell the whole story. They believe local knowledge, community traditions, and cultural values should also play an important role in decision-making. Residents may understand regional needs in ways that statistics cannot fully capture.

11. Some community leaders point out that regions with similar data may still have different priorities. Two regions with comparable populations and climates may value different industries, traditions, or development goals. They argue that decisions should reflect the perspectives of the people who live in those regions.

12. Supporters of community input also note that cultural and historical factors often influence how people respond to change. Projects that appear beneficial based on data may face challenges if local residents believe important traditions or community values are being overlooked.

13. Many experts suggest that the most effective decisions combine both approaches. Regional data can help identify needs and opportunities, while local perspectives can provide important context. Together, these sources of information may help leaders make more informed choices.

14. Geographers use landforms, climate, population, cultural characteristics, and economic activities to classify and compare regions. As communities continue to grow and change, leaders must decide how best to use this information when making decisions that affect the people who live there.

questions.text:
Which statement best expresses a central idea of the passage?
A. Regional data helps classify and compare places, but local perspectives also provide important context for decisions. [CORRECT]
B. Regions can be classified only by landforms.

D. Cultural values should replace all evidence.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In paragraph 8, objective information means information that is ( ).

Select TWO details that support using regional data in decision-making.

B. Residents may understand needs statistics cannot fully capture.
C. Climate records, population statistics, economic reports, and geographic information can help compare regions fairly. [CORRECT]
D. Local traditions may influence responses to change.
E. Regions with similar data may have different priorities.

How does paragraph 13 contribute to the passage?
A. It presents a balanced conclusion by explaining how data and local perspectives can work together. [CORRECT]
B. It introduces the definition of a region.
C. It argues that leaders should never use statistics.
D. It lists only physical features of regions.

writingPrompt.text: When making important decisions about a region, should leaders rely more on regional data or on the perspectives of local communities? Write a multi-paragraph response in which you make and support a claim about whether leaders should rely more on regional data or on the perspectives of local communities when making important decisions about a region. Your response must be based on ideas and information that can be found in the passage. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passage; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: include a thesis statement; address counterclaims; use evidence from the passage; and avoid overly relying on one part of the passage.

title: Shaping the Land, Shaping Our Lives

passages.title: Shaping the Land, Shaping Our Lives
passages.text:
1. The Eastern Hemisphere includes a wide variety of physical environments. Vast deserts, fertile river valleys, mountain ranges, grasslands, forests, and coastal regions stretch across Africa, Asia, Europe, and Australia. These environments influence where people live, how they travel, what crops they grow, and the types of jobs they perform.

2. For thousands of years, people have adapted to the physical environments around them. Communities near rivers often developed farming systems because water and fertile soil supported agriculture. Ancient civilizations in regions such as Egypt, Mesopotamia, India, and China depended on nearby rivers to help grow food and support growing populations.

3. Mountains can also influence human activities. Mountain ranges may provide natural resources, attract tourists, and offer protection from harsh weather. However, steep terrain can make transportation and construction more difficult. As a result, people living in mountainous regions often develop different lifestyles than those living in flat coastal areas.

4. Climate affects human activities as well. Regions with warm temperatures and reliable rainfall may support agriculture throughout much of the year. In contrast, deserts and extremely cold regions often require people to develop special methods for obtaining water, food, and shelter.

5. Coastal environments have historically supported trade and transportation. Ports allow goods to move between regions and countries, connecting people through commerce and cultural exchange. Many of the Eastern Hemisphere's largest cities developed near coastlines because of these advantages.

6. While physical environments influence people, human activities also change the environment. Farmers clear land to grow crops, communities build roads and cities, and industries use natural resources to manufacture products. These activities can alter landscapes in significant ways.

7. One example can be seen in river systems. People build dams to generate electricity, store water, and reduce flooding. Dams can provide important benefits for communities, but they may also change the flow of rivers and affect plants, animals, and people who depend on those waterways.

8. Expanding cities can also transform physical environments. As populations grow, forests, grasslands, and agricultural areas may be converted into neighborhoods, businesses, and transportation networks. These developments can create economic opportunities while also changing local ecosystems.

9. Some people believe societies should continue modifying physical environments when doing so improves people's lives. They point to projects such as dams, transportation systems, irrigation networks, and urban development as examples of how humans can use resources to support growing populations.

10. Supporters of this view argue that many improvements in health, transportation, communication, and food production have been possible because people adapted and modified their environments. They believe responsible development can help communities meet current and future needs.

11. Others believe communities should be more cautious when altering physical environments. They argue that environmental changes sometimes produce unintended consequences. Habitat loss, reduced biodiversity, soil erosion, and water-quality concerns can occur when development is not carefully managed.

12. Some environmental experts note that natural systems are often interconnected. Changes made in one location may affect ecosystems, water supplies, or wildlife populations in other areas. Because of these connections, they believe environmental impacts should be carefully considered before major projects begin.

13. Supporters of a balanced approach suggest that communities can both protect the environment and meet human needs. New technologies, environmental regulations, and sustainable planning practices may help reduce negative impacts while still allowing development to occur.

14. Across the Eastern Hemisphere, physical environments continue to influence human activities, while human activities continue to modify the environment. As populations grow and technology advances, communities must decide how much environmental change is appropriate and how best to balance development with conservation.

questions.text:
How do paragraphs 2-5 develop the idea that physical environments influence human activities?
A. They give examples involving rivers, mountains, climate, and coasts. [CORRECT]
B. They explain only how dams affect animals.
C. They argue that all environments are the same.
D. They focus only on modern technology.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In paragraph 11, unintended consequences are effects that are ( ).
Drop-down options: not planned or expected [CORRECT] / required by every community / easy to predict exactly / always helpful to wildlife

Select TWO details that support being cautious when modifying environments.
A. Habitat loss, reduced biodiversity, soil erosion, and water-quality concerns can occur. [CORRECT]
B. Coastal environments have supported trade and transportation.
C. Natural systems are interconnected, so changes in one place may affect other areas. [CORRECT]
D. Dams can generate electricity and store water.
E. Cities may create economic opportunities.

Which claim is best supported by paragraph 13?
A. Communities may be able to meet human needs while reducing environmental harm through technology, regulations, and sustainable planning. [CORRECT]
B. Development and conservation can never occur together.
C. All environmental change should be banned.
D. Natural systems are not connected.

writingPrompt.text: Should communities continue modifying physical environments to meet human needs, or should they place greater emphasis on protecting natural environments? Write a multi-paragraph response in which you make and support a claim about whether communities should continue modifying physical environments to meet human needs or place greater emphasis on protecting natural environments. Your response must be based on ideas and information that can be found in the passage. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passage; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: include a thesis statement; address counterclaims; use evidence from the passage; and avoid overly relying on one part of the passage.

title: The Benefits of Movement and Exchange

passages.title: The Benefits of Movement and Exchange
passages.text:
1. Throughout history, people, products, and ideas have moved across the Eastern Hemisphere. These movements have been influenced by political decisions, environmental conditions, social interactions, and economic opportunities. As people traveled and traded, they connected regions that might otherwise have remained isolated.

2. Ancient trade routes played an important role in this process. One of the most famous examples was the Silk Road, a network of routes that connected parts of Asia, the Middle East, and Europe. Merchants transported goods such as silk, spices, metals, and textiles across vast distances.

3. Economic factors often encouraged movement. Merchants sought new markets where they could buy and sell goods. Farmers looked for fertile land, while workers sometimes moved to regions with greater job opportunities. These movements helped spread products and resources across different areas.

4. Ideas also traveled alongside people and goods. Religious beliefs, scientific knowledge, technologies, languages, and artistic traditions spread through trade and cultural exchange. For example, innovations such as paper-making and printing spread from one region to another, influencing societies far beyond their places of origin.

5. Environmental factors have also caused movement. Droughts, floods, and other natural events sometimes forced communities to relocate. In other cases, favorable climates and natural resources attracted settlers to particular regions.

6. Political factors influenced movement as well. Governments sometimes encouraged trade, built transportation networks, or established policies that promoted migration. Stable governments often made travel and commerce easier by improving security and infrastructure.

7. Supporters of movement and exchange argue that these interactions have helped societies grow and prosper. Trade allows regions to access products that may not be available locally. The exchange of ideas can lead to technological innovation and cultural understanding.

8. They also point out that many important discoveries and advancements spread because people were willing to travel, communicate, and share knowledge. Supporters believe movement continues to strengthen connections between regions of the Eastern Hemisphere today.

9. For these reasons, many historians view the movement of people, products, and ideas as a major force behind human progress.

passages.title: Challenges Created by Movement and Exchange
passages.text:
1. Although movement and exchange have created opportunities, they have also presented challenges throughout history. Some historians argue that the movement of people, products, and ideas can create difficulties that societies must address.

2. When people move into new regions, competition for resources may increase. Housing, jobs, water supplies, and public services can become strained if communities are unprepared for population growth. Leaders often face difficult decisions about how to manage these changes.

3. Trade can also create economic challenges. Regions that depend heavily on products from distant locations may become vulnerable if transportation routes are disrupted. Changes in trade patterns can affect businesses, workers, and entire economies.

4. Environmental concerns sometimes accompany movement as well. Increased transportation can contribute to pollution, while expanding settlements may alter natural habitats. Some environmental experts believe these impacts should be considered carefully.

5. The spread of ideas can create disagreements when different cultural beliefs and traditions interact. Throughout history, societies have sometimes struggled to balance new influences with existing customs and values.

6. Political factors may also complicate movement. Governments often establish borders, immigration policies, and trade regulations to address concerns related to security, economics, or public welfare. These policies can influence who and what moves between regions.

7. Critics do not necessarily oppose movement and exchange. Instead, they argue that communities should carefully manage these processes to reduce potential problems. They believe thoughtful planning can help societies benefit from movement while minimizing negative effects.

8. Others suggest that successful societies find ways to balance openness with stability. They support trade, migration, and the exchange of ideas but believe these activities should be guided by policies that address economic, environmental, and social concerns.

9. As people, products, and ideas continue moving throughout the Eastern Hemisphere, communities will likely continue debating how to balance the opportunities and challenges that accompany these exchanges.

questions.text:
How do the two passages present movement and exchange differently?
A. Passage 1 emphasizes benefits, while Passage 2 emphasizes challenges and the need for management. [CORRECT]
B. Passage 1 focuses on borders, while Passage 2 focuses only on the Silk Road.

D. Both passages say trade routes were unimportant.

B. Movement has only negative effects on societies.
C. Trade affects products but never ideas.
D. Political factors do not influence movement.

passages.title: How Ideas and Innovations Spread
passages.text:
1. Throughout history, people have shared ideas, products, technologies, and beliefs across the Eastern Hemisphere. This process, known as diffusion, occurs when something spreads from one group or region to another. Diffusion has played an important role in shaping societies, influencing how people live, work, communicate, and practice their beliefs.

2. Agricultural practices were among the earliest ideas to spread. Farmers learned new methods for growing crops, managing water, and improving harvests. As these techniques moved between regions, communities were often able to produce more food and support larger populations.

3. Products also traveled across long distances. Spices, silk, tea, and other goods moved along trade routes connecting Asia, the Middle East, Africa, and Europe. Merchants exchanged not only products but also knowledge about farming, manufacturing, and transportation.

4. Technology spread through diffusion as well. Paper-making techniques developed in China eventually reached other parts of Asia, the Middle East, and Europe. This innovation made it easier to record information, share ideas, and preserve knowledge.

5. Major world religions also spread throughout the Eastern Hemisphere. Buddhism originated in India and spread to regions of East and Southeast Asia. Christianity expanded from the Middle East into Europe, Africa, and parts of Asia. Islam spread throughout the Middle East, North Africa, and beyond. Hinduism remained influential in South Asia, while Judaism continued to shape religious traditions and communities in various regions.

6. Supporters of cultural and religious diffusion believe these exchanges have benefited societies. They argue that the spread of new ideas encourages learning, innovation, and cooperation between different groups of people.

7. They point out that many technological advances became widely useful only after they spread beyond their places of origin. Similarly, the exchange of agricultural knowledge often improved food production and quality of life.

8. Supporters also note that exposure to different beliefs and traditions can increase understanding among cultures. They believe diffusion helps people learn from one another while contributing to social and economic development.

9. For these reasons, many historians view diffusion as a powerful force that helped shape civilizations throughout the Eastern Hemisphere.

passages.title: Preserving Local Traditions
passages.text:
1. While diffusion has connected societies throughout history, some historians emphasize the importance of preserving local traditions and cultural identities. They argue that communities should carefully consider how outside influences affect long-established customs and practices.

2. As agricultural methods spread, some traditional farming practices became less common. In certain regions, communities replaced local techniques with methods developed elsewhere. While these changes sometimes increased production, they also led to the decline of practices that had been passed down through generations.

3. The spread of products and technologies has produced similar effects. New inventions often improve efficiency and communication, but they may also reduce the use of traditional crafts, tools, and methods. Some cultural historians worry that unique local knowledge can be lost when communities rapidly adopt outside innovations.

4. Religious diffusion has also influenced societies in different ways. As major world religions spread, they often interacted with existing beliefs and customs. In some places, traditions blended together, while in others, cultural changes created disagreements or tensions among groups.

5. Some people believe communities should place a strong emphasis on protecting cultural heritage. They argue that languages, customs, celebrations, and local practices help define a group's identity and connect people to their history.

6. Others point out that diversity among cultures enriches the Eastern Hemisphere. When unique traditions disappear, communities may lose important parts of their heritage. Preserving cultural practices can help future generations understand where they came from and how their societies developed.

7. Supporters of preservation are not necessarily opposed to diffusion. Instead, they believe communities should carefully evaluate new ideas and technologies while continuing to protect important traditions and cultural practices.

8. They argue that societies benefit most when they balance innovation with preservation. By doing so, communities can enjoy the advantages of new ideas while maintaining connections to their cultural roots.

9. As ideas, technologies, agricultural practices, products, and religions continue to spread, societies throughout the Eastern Hemisphere must decide how to balance change with the preservation of local traditions.

questions.text:

B. Passage 1 focuses on government labels, while Passage 2 focuses on economic scarcity.
C. Both passages argue diffusion should never occur.
D. Passage 2 says cultural heritage has no value.

passages.title: Labels and Leadership
passages.text:
1. Governments help societies maintain order, provide services, create laws, and make decisions that affect citizens. Throughout history, people have developed different forms of government to meet the needs of their communities. Political scientists often classify governments into categories such as monarchies, theocracies, dictatorships, and democracies. However, understanding how a government functions is often more complicated than applying a simple label.

2. In a monarchy, a king, queen, emperor, or other ruler serves as the head of state. Some monarchies give rulers significant authority over government decisions, while others limit the ruler's powers through constitutions and elected officials. As a result, monarchies can differ greatly from one another.

3. A theocracy is a government in which religious leaders or religious laws play a central role in governing society. Religious beliefs may influence laws, public policies, and expectations for citizens. The degree of religious influence can vary from one theocratic government to another.

4. In a dictatorship, power is concentrated in the hands of a single leader or a small group. These governments often make decisions without broad public participation. In some cases, citizens have limited opportunities to influence government policies or leadership.

5. Democracies generally allow citizens to participate in government through voting and other civic activities. Citizens may elect leaders, express opinions, and take part in public discussions. However, democracies can differ in how elections are conducted and how government power is distributed.

6. Although these categories help people understand governments, many governments combine characteristics from more than one type. A country may have a monarch while also holding democratic elections. Another government may include religious influences while maintaining elected representatives. Because governments are complex, labels do not always provide a complete picture.

7. Political scientists often examine how governments actually function rather than relying solely on their official classifications. They may study who holds power, how decisions are made, and how laws are enforced. These factors can reveal important differences between governments that share the same label.

8. One area often examined is the extent of citizens' liberties. Liberties may include freedoms related to speech, religion, the press, assembly, and participation in government. Different governments place different limits on these freedoms.

9. Some people believe governments should place a strong emphasis on protecting individual liberties. They argue that citizens should have broad freedoms to express ideas, participate in public discussions, and influence government decisions. Supporters of this view believe these freedoms encourage innovation, accountability, and civic engagement.

10. Others argue that governments sometimes need greater authority to maintain security, social order, and stability. They believe certain limits on individual freedoms may help governments respond to emergencies, reduce conflict, or address national challenges more effectively.

11. Supporters of stronger governmental authority point out that governments are often responsible for protecting citizens and maintaining public safety. They argue that leaders may need flexibility to make decisions that benefit society as a whole.

12. Critics respond that granting too much authority to governments can reduce citizen participation and limit important freedoms. They believe citizens should have opportunities to question government decisions and contribute to public discussions about important issues.

13. Many societies attempt to balance these competing priorities. Governments may establish laws and institutions designed to protect citizens while also preserving individual rights and responsibilities. The balance between authority and liberty often reflects a society's values, history, and goals.

14. Governments can be classified in different ways, but understanding how they function requires looking beyond labels. Around the world, people continue debating how much authority governments should have and how best to balance governmental power with individual freedoms.

questions.text:

passages.title: Whose Information Should You Trust?
passages.text:
1. Every day, people encounter information from many different sources. News articles, books, interviews, websites, videos, podcasts, and social media posts all provide information about events and issues. Because people have access to more information than ever before, it is important to understand how to evaluate sources and determine whether information is accurate and credible.

2. Historians and researchers regularly examine multiple sources when studying a topic. A source is any material that provides information about an event, person, idea, or issue. Sources may be historical, such as letters, diaries, government records, photographs, and newspaper articles from the past. They may also be contemporary, including current news reports, interviews, research studies, and digital media.

3. Different sources often present different perspectives. A perspective is a particular way of viewing or interpreting an event or issue. People's experiences, beliefs, roles, and goals can influence the perspectives they share. As a result, two sources discussing the same topic may emphasize different details or reach different conclusions.

4. For example, a newspaper article about a new transportation project might focus on potential economic benefits. An interview with nearby residents might emphasize concerns about traffic or environmental impacts. Both sources provide useful information, but each presents a different perspective.

5. Researchers often compare multiple sources to develop a more complete understanding of a topic. Examining several perspectives can help identify areas of agreement and disagreement. It may also reveal information that is missing from a single source.

6. Accuracy is another important consideration. Accurate sources provide information that is supported by evidence and can be verified through reliable methods. Researchers often compare facts across multiple sources to determine whether information is consistent and trustworthy.

7. Credibility refers to how reliable and trustworthy a source is. When evaluating credibility, people may consider factors such as the author's expertise, the quality of the evidence provided, the date of publication, and whether the information has been reviewed by experts or reputable organizations.

8. In today's digital world, evaluating sources can be challenging. Information can spread quickly through websites and social media platforms. Some information is carefully researched and verified, while other information may contain errors, incomplete evidence, or misleading claims.

9. Some people believe individuals should rely primarily on sources that have been reviewed by experts and established organizations. They argue that professional researchers, journalists, historians, and scientists often follow procedures designed to verify information before it is shared with the public.

10. Supporters of this view point out that established sources frequently provide citations, evidence, and opportunities for fact-checking. They believe these practices help reduce errors and improve the quality of information available to the public.

11. Others argue that people should also consider a wider range of perspectives, including personal accounts, local sources, and independent creators. They believe these sources can provide valuable information and viewpoints that may not always appear in larger organizations or publications.

12. Supporters of broader source use note that firsthand experiences can help people better understand how events affect individuals and communities. They argue that relying only on large institutions may sometimes overlook important perspectives.

13. Many researchers suggest combining both approaches. They recommend examining multiple sources, checking evidence carefully, and considering different perspectives before reaching conclusions. By doing so, people may be able to build stronger claims supported by reliable information.

14. Whether studying historical events or current issues, understanding different perspectives and evaluating sources for accuracy and credibility are important skills. These skills help people communicate effectively, defend claims with evidence, and make informed decisions in a world filled with information.

questions.text:

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.

Be sure to: include a thesis statement; address counterclaims; use evidence from the passage; and avoid overly relying on one part of the passage.

passages.title: Why Government Labels Matter
passages.text:
1. Governments around the world are often grouped into categories such as monarchies, theocracies, dictatorships, and democracies. These labels help people understand how governments are organized and how power is distributed. Political scientists use these categories as a starting point for comparing different nations and understanding how governments function.

2. In a monarchy, a king, queen, or other ruler serves as the head of state. Some monarchies grant significant authority to the ruler, while others limit the ruler's powers through constitutions and elected representatives. Because monarchies vary widely, political scientists often examine how much authority a monarch actually has.

3. Theocracies are governments in which religious leaders or religious laws play a major role in governing society. In these systems, religious beliefs may influence laws, public policies, and expectations for citizens. The extent of religious influence can differ from one country to another.

4. Dictatorships concentrate power in the hands of a single leader or a small group. Citizens in these governments may have limited opportunities to participate in decision-making. Government leaders often exercise significant authority over political and social activities.

5. Democracies generally allow citizens to participate in government through voting, civic engagement, and public discussion. Citizens often elect representatives who help make laws and policies. Democracies may differ in how elections are conducted and how governmental powers are organized.

6. Supporters of government classification systems argue that labels provide a useful framework for understanding political systems. They believe categories help students, researchers, and citizens compare governments and recognize important differences in how power is distributed.

7. These supporters also note that classifications can help people examine the extent of citizens' liberties and responsibilities. By understanding a government's structure, people may better understand how decisions are made and how citizens participate in public life.

8. For these reasons, many political scientists continue to use traditional government categories as valuable tools for understanding and comparing nations.

passages.title: Looking Beyond Government Labels
passages.text:
1. While government categories can be useful, many political scientists believe labels alone do not provide a complete picture of how governments actually function. They argue that governments are often more complex than a single classification suggests.

2. Some governments combine characteristics from multiple categories. A nation may have a monarch while also holding democratic elections. Another government may include both religious influences and elected officials. As a result, a single label may not fully describe how power is exercised.

3. Political scientists often study what governments do rather than relying only on official classifications. They examine who makes decisions, how laws are enforced, and what rights and responsibilities citizens have in practice.

4. The extent of citizens' liberties can vary greatly even among governments that share the same label. For example, two countries classified as democracies may differ in how much freedom citizens have to express opinions, organize groups, or participate in public discussions.

5. Responsibilities can vary as well. Citizens in different countries may have different expectations regarding voting, military service, taxation, education, or community participation. These differences may influence how governments function on a daily basis.

6. Some scholars argue that focusing too heavily on labels can lead to misunderstandings. A country's official government type may not always reflect how authority is exercised or how citizens experience government in their daily lives.

7. Others believe understanding citizens' liberties and governmental authority provides a more accurate picture. They argue that examining freedoms, responsibilities, and limits on power reveals information that labels alone cannot provide.

8. These scholars are not suggesting that classifications should be abandoned. Instead, they believe government labels should be viewed as starting points rather than complete descriptions. Additional evidence is often needed to understand how governments truly operate.

9. As political systems continue to evolve, many experts believe it is important to look beyond simple categories and examine how governments function in practice. Doing so may provide a deeper understanding of the relationship between governmental authority and citizens' rights and responsibilities.

questions.text:
What information does Passage 2 add to the ideas in Passage 1?

B. Monarchies always have elected leaders.

D. Theocracies are the only governments with laws.

E. Theocracies involve religious leaders or laws.

Which central idea is developed across both passages?

C. All governments protect the same liberties.
D. Citizens have identical responsibilities in every country.

writingPrompt.text: When trying to understand a government, is it more important to know its official category or to examine how it functions in practice? Write a multi-paragraph response in which you make and support a claim about whether it is more important to know a government's official category or to examine how it functions in practice. Your response must be based on ideas and information that can be found in the passages. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passages; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: include a thesis statement; address counterclaims; use evidence from multiple passages; and avoid overly relying on one passage. Standard: Governments can be categorized as monarchies, theocracies, dictatorships or democracies, but categories may overlap and labels may not accurately represent how governments function. The extent of citizens’ liberties and responsibilities varies according to limits on governmental authority.

passages.title: Why Reliable Sources Matter
passages.text:
1. Every day, people encounter information from a variety of sources. News articles, books, interviews, websites, documentaries, government records, and social media posts all provide information about events and issues. Because so much information is available, it is important to determine whether a source is accurate and credible.

2. Historians, scientists, journalists, and researchers often rely on evidence from multiple sources when investigating a topic. Historical sources may include letters, diaries, photographs, newspaper articles, maps, and government documents. Contemporary sources may include recent reports, interviews, research studies, and digital media.

3. Different sources can provide different perspectives. For example, two newspaper articles covering the same event may focus on different details or reach different conclusions. A government report and a personal interview may also present different viewpoints about the same issue.

4. Because perspectives vary, researchers often compare information from several sources before reaching conclusions. Looking at multiple perspectives can help identify agreements, disagreements, and missing information.

5. Supporters of relying on highly credible sources argue that expertise and evidence are essential. They believe sources produced by trained researchers, established organizations, historians, and professional journalists are often more trustworthy because they follow procedures designed to verify information.

6. These sources frequently provide citations, references, and supporting evidence. Researchers can examine this information to determine whether claims are supported by facts. Supporters argue that this process helps reduce errors and increases confidence in the information being presented.

7. In addition, highly credible sources are often reviewed by editors, experts, or organizations before publication. This review process may help identify mistakes and improve accuracy.

8. Supporters believe that when people build claims using reliable evidence from credible sources, they are more likely to communicate effectively and defend their arguments successfully.

9. For these reasons, many researchers believe that carefully evaluating credibility should be one of the most important steps in gathering information and supporting claims.

Passage 2: The Value of Multiple Perspectives

1. While credibility is important, some researchers emphasize the value of considering a wide range of perspectives. They argue that understanding an issue often requires more than examining information from a small group of sources.

2. Historical events and current issues frequently affect different people in different ways. As a result, individuals may have experiences and viewpoints that do not always appear in official reports, academic studies, or large news organizations.

3. Personal accounts, interviews, local newspapers, community organizations, and independent creators can provide information that helps people better understand how events affect individuals and communities. These sources may offer details that larger institutions overlook.

4. Supporters of this approach believe examining multiple perspectives helps people develop a more complete understanding of a topic. They argue that no single source can capture every aspect of a complex issue.

5. They also point out that even credible sources can have limitations. Researchers may choose to focus on certain questions, journalists may emphasize particular aspects of a story, and organizations may collect only specific types of data.

6. By comparing information from different sources, people can identify areas of agreement and disagreement. This process may reveal additional evidence and help researchers evaluate claims more carefully.

7. Supporters of broader source use do not suggest ignoring credibility. Instead, they believe people should evaluate all sources while remaining open to multiple perspectives. A source's viewpoint can provide useful information even if it requires additional verification.

8. They argue that effective claims are often built by combining evidence from several types of sources. This approach may help people better understand complicated issues and communicate more balanced arguments.

9. As access to information continues to grow, researchers must decide how to balance credibility and perspective. Many believe the strongest conclusions come from carefully evaluating sources while considering a variety of viewpoints.

questions.text:
How do the two passages differ?
A. Passage 1 emphasizes credibility and evidence, while Passage 2 emphasizes considering multiple perspectives. [CORRECT]
B. Passage 1 focuses on local newspapers, while Passage 2 focuses only on expert review.
C. Passage 1 says sources should never be checked, while Passage 2 says credibility is unimportant.
D. Both passages argue one source is enough for research.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In Passage 2, limitations means ( ).
Drop-down options: weaknesses or boundaries in what a source provides [CORRECT] / facts that have been fully proven / sources reviewed by experts / complete agreement among researchers

Select TWO details that support considering a wide range of perspectives.
A. Personal accounts, local newspapers, and community organizations may reveal details larger institutions overlook. [CORRECT]
B. Highly credible sources are often reviewed before publication.
C. Comparing sources can reveal areas of agreement, disagreement, and additional evidence. [CORRECT]
D. Citations help researchers check claims.
E. Experts follow procedures designed to verify information.

Which claim is supported by both passages?
A. Strong research often requires evaluating source credibility while also considering different viewpoints. [CORRECT]
B. A source's viewpoint is never useful.
C. Credible sources cannot have limitations.
D. Personal accounts should always replace evidence.

writingPrompt.text: When researching a topic, is it more important to prioritize highly credible sources or to consider a wide range of perspectives? Write a multi-paragraph response in which you make and support a claim about whether it is more important to prioritize highly credible sources or to consider a wide range of perspectives when researching a topic. Your response must be based on ideas and information that can be found in the passages. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passages; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: include a thesis statement; address counterclaims; use evidence from multiple passages; and avoid overly relying on one passage.

passages.title: Making Economic Choices
passages.text:
1. Every day, individuals, businesses, and governments make decisions about how to use limited resources. People decide how to spend their money, businesses determine what products to sell, and governments choose how to allocate public funds. Economists study these decisions because resources such as time, labor, materials, and money are limited.

2. One of the most important ideas in economics is scarcity. Scarcity means that people do not have enough resources to satisfy every want and need. Because resources are limited, choices must be made. Every choice involves trade-offs, which means giving up one option in order to pursue another.

3. Economists often describe decision-making using three fundamental questions: What should be produced? How should it be produced? For whom should it be produced? These questions help societies determine how resources will be used.

4. The first question, what to produce, focuses on deciding which goods and services should be created. A community might need food, housing, healthcare, transportation, and education. However, limited resources make it impossible to produce unlimited amounts of everything. Individuals, businesses, and governments must decide which goods and services are most important.

5. The second question, how to produce, involves determining the methods used to create goods and services. For example, a company might choose between using advanced technology, hiring additional workers, or combining both approaches. Different methods can affect costs, efficiency, and the use of resources.

6. The third question, for whom to produce, addresses how goods and services will be distributed. Some products may be available to anyone who can purchase them, while others may be provided through government programs or charitable organizations. Different societies answer this question in different ways.

7. These economic questions affect many decisions in the real world. Farmers decide which crops to grow. Businesses determine which products to manufacture. Governments decide how much money to spend on transportation, education, healthcare, and public safety.

8. Consider a city that receives funding to improve community services. City leaders may not have enough money to complete every project residents want. As a result, they must decide whether to invest more in parks, libraries, public transportation, road repairs, recreation centers, or other services.

9. Some people believe decision-makers should focus primarily on projects that benefit the greatest number of people. They argue that limited resources should be directed toward services used by the largest portion of the population. Supporters of this approach believe it allows communities to maximize the benefits of available resources.

10. These individuals point out that public transportation systems, road improvements, and large community facilities may serve thousands of residents. They believe investing in projects with broad impacts can improve efficiency and provide the greatest overall benefit.

11. Others argue that decision-makers should place greater emphasis on serving groups with the greatest needs, even if fewer people benefit directly. They believe resources should be directed toward programs and services that support vulnerable populations or address important community challenges.

12. Supporters of this view note that some services may help smaller groups but provide significant benefits. Programs that support public health, housing assistance, specialized education, or community outreach may improve quality of life for people who need help the most.

13. Many economists and community leaders recognize that both approaches have advantages. They often seek ways to balance broad public benefits with support for groups facing specific challenges. The choices communities make reflect their priorities and values.

14. The fundamental economic questions of what to produce, how to produce, and for whom to produce influence decisions at every level of society. Because resources are limited, people must continually decide how best to use them to meet competing needs and wants.

passages.title: The Power of Specialization
passages.text:
1. Imagine trying to make everything you need by yourself. You would have to grow your own food, make your own clothes, build your own furniture, and create every tool you use. Most people would find this difficult and time-consuming. Instead, individuals, businesses, and countries often focus on producing certain goods or services and then trade for the things they need. This process is known as specialization.

2. Specialization occurs when a person, business, region, or country concentrates on producing particular goods or services. Specialization allows producers to develop skills, improve efficiency, and make better use of available resources. As a result, they may be able to produce goods more effectively than others.

3. Different regions have different advantages. Climate, natural resources, technology, labor, and geographic location can influence what a region produces. For example, some regions have fertile soil that supports agriculture, while others have access to important minerals or advanced manufacturing facilities.

4. Countries often specialize in products they can produce efficiently. Some countries export agricultural products such as coffee, rice, or fruit. Others specialize in automobiles, electronics, textiles, or technology services. These products are then traded with other countries.

5. Global trade occurs when countries exchange goods and services across international borders. Through trade, countries gain access to products that may be difficult or expensive to produce on their own. Consumers often benefit from a wider variety of products and services.

6. Specialization and trade have connected economies around the world. A smartphone, for example, may contain materials from several countries, be assembled in another country, and then sold in markets around the world. Modern transportation and communication systems have made these connections possible.

7. Supporters of specialization believe it creates many benefits. They argue that countries can focus on what they do best, increase productivity, and lower production costs. These efficiencies may lead to lower prices and greater economic growth.

8. Supporters also note that trade can create opportunities for businesses to reach new markets. By selling products internationally, companies may expand their operations and create jobs. In many countries, exports contribute significantly to economic activity.

9. Others point out that specialization can create challenges. If a country depends heavily on a small number of products, economic problems may occur if demand changes. A region that relies mainly on one industry may face difficulties if that industry declines.

10. Some economists also note that global trade can make countries dependent on goods produced elsewhere. Disruptions caused by natural disasters, conflicts, transportation issues, or other events may affect the availability of important products.

11. Supporters of a more balanced approach argue that countries should participate in global trade while also maintaining the ability to produce some important goods domestically. They believe this strategy can help reduce risks while preserving many of the benefits of specialization.

12. Others respond that the advantages of specialization generally outweigh the risks. They argue that international trade has helped improve living standards, expand consumer choices, and encourage innovation in many parts of the world.

13. Economists continue to study how specialization affects countries, businesses, and consumers. While opinions differ about the best balance between specialization and self-sufficiency, most agree that trade plays an important role in the global economy.

14. When regions and countries specialize, global trade occurs. The decisions societies make about what to produce, what to trade, and how much to rely on other countries continue to shape economies around the world.
