Be sure to: include a thesis statement; address counterclaims; use evidence from multiple passages; explain how the evidence supports your claim; and avoid overly relying on one passage.

title: The Keeper of the Northern Lights

passages.title: The Keeper of the Northern Lights
passages.text:
(1) Long ago, before maps covered the world and before people understood the stars, the people of the Far North told stories about the lights that danced across the night sky. They believed the lights were created by a powerful spirit named Alera, the Keeper of the Sky.

(2) According to the legend, Alera lived beyond the tallest mountains, in a palace made of ice and crystal. During the day, she watched over the world from her throne of shimmering snow. At night, she traveled across the heavens wearing a cloak woven from threads of silver light.

(3) Alera had one important responsibility. She was charged with carrying hopes from the people below to the stars above. Whenever someone wished for courage, kindness, wisdom, or peace, their hopes floated upward like tiny sparks. Alera gathered these sparks and carried them across the sky.

(4) One winter, a young hunter named Niko became concerned. The nights seemed darker than usual, and the brilliant lights that normally filled the sky had not appeared for many weeks. The elders worried that Alera might have abandoned her duties.

(5) Determined to discover the truth, Niko traveled northward through forests of pine and across frozen rivers. After many days, he reached the edge of the world, where the sky appeared to meet the snow-covered land.

(6) There he found Alera sitting beside a frozen lake. Her silver cloak lay folded beside her, and the sparks she normally carried drifted quietly around her feet.

(7) "Why have the lights disappeared?" Niko asked. "The people are worried. They look to the sky each night and see only darkness."

(8) Alera sighed. "For many years, I have carried the hopes of others," she replied. "But recently, I have noticed something troubling. Many people make wishes and wait for change, yet few are willing to take action themselves."

(9) Niko considered her words. He thought about neighbors who wished for stronger friendships but never reached out to others. He remembered people who hoped for successful harvests but sometimes neglected their fields. Perhaps Alera had a point.

(10) "Hope is important," Niko said carefully, "but so is effort. People need both. Without hope, they may stop trying. Without effort, their hopes may never become reality."

(11) Alera remained silent for a long moment. Then she smiled. "Perhaps you understand something that many have forgotten."

(12) She rose and lifted her silver cloak. As she swung it across the sky, thousands of sparks scattered overhead. Streams of green, blue, and violet light danced above the horizon. The sky shimmered brighter than Niko had ever seen.

(13) "I will continue carrying people's hopes," Alera said, "but they must remember that hope is only the beginning. The future depends on the choices they make after they wish."

(14) Niko returned home and shared what he had learned. From that day forward, the people believed the northern lights appeared whenever Alera carried their hopes across the sky. Whenever they saw the lights dancing overhead, they remembered that hope and action worked best together.

(15) Some people believed the lights were a reminder to dream about what could be. Others believed the lights encouraged people to take responsibility for making those dreams come true. As the story spread from generation to generation, both interpretations became part of the legend.

(16) And even today, when colorful lights ripple across the northern sky, some still tell the tale of Alera, the Keeper of the Sky, who taught that hope may guide the way, but action determines where the journey leads.

questions.text:
Which theme is developed in the myth?
A. Hope is useful, but action is needed to make change happen. [CORRECT]
B. People should stop wishing for better futures.
C. Alera refuses to help people because she dislikes them.
D. The northern lights appear only when people ignore problems.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In paragraph 3, Alera's responsibility is her ( ).
Drop-down options: duty or important job [CORRECT] / hidden mistake / favorite game / personal wish

Select TWO details that support the myth's lesson.
A. Alera notices that many people wish for change but do not act. [CORRECT]
B. Alera lives in a palace made of ice and crystal.
C. Niko says people need both hope and effort. [CORRECT]
D. The sparks drift quietly around Alera's feet.
E. The lights are green, blue, and violet.

How does the myth's ending contribute to the passage?
A. It connects the explanation of the northern lights to a lesson about hope and action. [CORRECT]
B. It shows that Niko forgets what Alera taught him.
C. It proves that the elders were completely wrong.
D. It changes the story into a scientific article.
Argumentative

writingPrompt.text: What is more important for achieving success: having hope for the future or taking action to create change? Write a multi-paragraph response in which you make and support a claim about whether having hope for the future or taking action to create change is more important for achieving success. Your response must be based on ideas and information that can be found in the myth. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passage; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: include a thesis statement; address counterclaims; use evidence from the passage; explain how the evidence supports your claim; and avoid overly relying on one part of the passage.

passages.title: The Fox and the Finch
passages.text:
(1) In a wide meadow bordered by tall oak trees, a fox named Rowan was known for being clever. Whenever animals needed advice, Rowan was eager to offer it. He enjoyed explaining how others could solve problems, avoid mistakes, and improve their lives.

(2) Near the edge of the meadow lived a finch named Wren. Although Wren was small, she worked hard each day. She gathered seeds, repaired her nest, and prepared for changing seasons. Unlike Rowan, she spent little time giving advice because she was usually busy completing her own tasks.

(3) One spring morning, Rowan noticed Wren carrying twigs to strengthen her nest.

(4) "You're working much too hard," Rowan called. "The weather is beautiful. You should spend more time enjoying yourself."

(5) Wren politely thanked him but continued gathering materials. "The weather is beautiful now," she replied, "but storms may come later."

(6) Rowan shook his head and wandered off. Throughout the season, he offered similar advice to many animals. He told the rabbits they worried too much about finding food. He told the squirrels they spent too much time collecting nuts. He even told the beavers they worked too hard maintaining their dam.

(7) The animals listened respectfully, but most continued their preparations. They knew that seasons could change quickly and that planning ahead often prevented problems later.

(8) As summer approached, Rowan spent his days resting beneath shady trees. He explored the meadow, chatted with neighbors, and admired his own clever observations. Whenever someone praised his advice, he felt proud.

(9) Meanwhile, Wren continued preparing. She strengthened her nest, stored extra seeds, and learned which trees offered the best shelter during storms. Some animals wondered whether she was being overly cautious.

(10) Late one afternoon, dark clouds gathered above the meadow. Strong winds swept across the fields, and heavy rain soon followed. The storm lasted through the night.

(11) By morning, branches covered the ground. Several animals sheltered safely because they had prepared in advance. Wren's nest remained secure among sturdy branches, and she had enough food to remain protected while the weather improved.

(12) Rowan was not as fortunate. A fallen branch had damaged the den where he usually rested. Because he had spent little time preparing, he struggled to find shelter and food after the storm.

(13) Embarrassed, Rowan visited Wren. "I spent so much time telling others what they should do," he admitted, "that I forgot to follow my own advice about solving problems."

(14) Wren nodded kindly. "Advice can be helpful," she said, "but it matters little if we do not apply wisdom to our own lives."

(15) Rowan thought carefully about her words. He realized that understanding what should be done was not the same as actually doing it.

(16) From that day forward, Rowan still shared ideas with others, but he also focused on his own responsibilities. The animals noticed that his advice became more practical because it came from experience rather than observation alone.

(17) As the seasons passed, Rowan earned a different kind of respect. The animals no longer admired him simply because he sounded wise. They respected him because his actions reflected the lessons he shared.

(18) And whenever young animals asked Wren how Rowan had changed, she smiled and repeated the lesson they had both learned: knowing the right thing to do is valuable, but doing it is what truly makes a difference.

questions.text:
Which moral is best supported by the fable?
A. Giving advice is valuable only when actions show the same wisdom. [CORRECT]
B. Clever animals never need to prepare.
C. Resting is always better than working.
D. Storms teach animals not to help one another.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
When Wren says wisdom must be applied, she means it must be ( ).
Drop-down options: used in one's own actions [CORRECT] / admired from a distance / spoken loudly to others / kept secret from neighbors

Select TWO details that show Wren's actions are wiser than Rowan's advice.
A. Wren strengthens her nest and stores extra seeds before the storm. [CORRECT]
B. Rowan is known for being clever.
C. Rowan struggles to find shelter and food after the storm. [CORRECT]
D. The meadow is bordered by oak trees.
E. Other animals listen respectfully.

How does Rowan change by the end of the fable?
A. He stops speaking to the other animals.
B. He begins matching his advice with responsible actions. [CORRECT]
C. He blames Wren for the storm.
D. He decides preparation is unnecessary.
Argumentative

writingPrompt.text: Is it more important to give good advice or to set a good example through actions? Write a multi-paragraph response in which you make and support a claim about whether it is more important to give good advice or to set a good example through actions. Your response must be based on ideas and information that can be found in the fable. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passage; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: include a thesis statement; address counterclaims; use evidence from the passage; explain how the evidence supports your claim; and avoid overly relying on one part of the passage.

title: The Legend of Elias and the River

passages.title: The Legend of Elias and the River
passages.text:
(1) More than two hundred years ago, the town of Briar Crossing sat beside a wide river that provided water, transportation, and fish for the people who lived there. According to local records, a ferryman named Elias Hart operated a small ferry that carried travelers across the river long before a bridge was built.

(2) Historical documents confirm that Elias was known for helping travelers during difficult weather. However, over time, stories about his bravery grew far beyond the facts. Generations of townspeople retold tales of his adventures, and Elias gradually became the hero of one of the region's most famous legends.

(3) According to the legend, a massive storm once struck Briar Crossing without warning. Thunder shook the valley, powerful winds uprooted trees, and the river rose higher than anyone had ever seen. As the storm intensified, several travelers became stranded on the opposite bank.

(4) The story claims that Elias launched his small ferry into waves as tall as houses. Witnesses supposedly watched him guide the boat through crashing water while lightning flashed across the sky. Some versions of the tale even suggest that the river itself tried to stop him, sending giant waves directly into his path.

(5) Despite the danger, Elias reportedly made multiple trips across the river. Each journey brought more stranded travelers to safety. By dawn, everyone had returned home unharmed.

(6) Historians believe a storm likely occurred and that Elias probably helped several people reach safety. However, they note that no evidence supports the more dramatic details. The towering waves, repeated crossings, and extraordinary dangers may have been added as the story was passed from one generation to the next.

(7) Even so, many residents value the legend. They believe the exaggerated details help communicate an important message about courage, determination, and helping others during difficult times.

(8) Every year, Briar Crossing celebrates Founders Day with a festival that includes stories about Elias Hart. Children learn about his willingness to help others, and many people view the legend as an important part of the town's identity.

(9) To these residents, the precise details matter less than the lesson the story teaches. They believe legends remain meaningful because they inspire people to demonstrate the same qualities that made the original person memorable.

passages.title: The Legend of Clara and the Mountain Pass
passages.text:
(1) In another region, a legend tells the story of Clara Bennett, a guide who lived near a mountain pass during the late 1800s. Historical records confirm that Clara helped travelers navigate difficult terrain and was respected throughout the area for her knowledge of the mountains.

(2) Over time, stories about Clara became increasingly dramatic. According to the legend, she could predict storms simply by feeling the wind. Some versions claim she could hear distant avalanches long before anyone else. Others suggest she never became lost, even during blizzards that left experienced travelers disoriented.

(3) One popular tale describes a severe winter storm that trapped a group of travelers in the mountains. The story says Clara led dozens of people through deep snow while visibility was nearly impossible. Along the way, she supposedly recognized hidden landmarks buried beneath the snow and guided everyone safely to shelter.

(4) Historians agree that Clara likely helped travelers during difficult weather conditions. However, they point out that many details in the legend cannot be verified. Some scholars believe storytellers added extraordinary elements to make the story more memorable and exciting.

(5) Not everyone believes this is a problem. Supporters of the legend argue that exaggeration can help preserve important stories. They note that people are often more likely to remember tales that include dramatic events, larger-than-life characters, and exciting challenges.

(6) Others disagree. They believe legends should stay as close to historical facts as possible. According to this view, adding too many fictional details can make it difficult to understand what really happened and what the historical figure actually accomplished.

(7) During a local history event, community members discussed Clara's story. Some argued that the legend's purpose was to inspire people through examples of courage and leadership. Others argued that preserving accurate history was equally important.

(8) Despite these disagreements, most people agreed that Clara Bennett made important contributions to her community. Whether the more dramatic details were true or not, her story continued to be shared throughout the region.

(9) Today, the legend remains popular because it encourages people to think about the relationship between history and storytelling. It also raises questions about how much a story can change while still honoring the real person who inspired it.

questions.text:
How are the two legends similar?
A. Both are based on real helpers whose stories became more dramatic over time. [CORRECT]
B. Both prove that all legendary details are historically accurate.
C. Both focus on rulers who wanted more power.
D. Both describe people who refused to help travelers.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In Passage 2, details that cannot be verified are details that cannot be ( ).
Drop-down options: proven true with evidence [CORRECT] / repeated in a story / remembered by a community / made exciting

Select TWO details that support the idea that exaggerated legends can still be meaningful.
A. Residents value Elias's legend because it teaches courage and helping others. [CORRECT]
B. Historians find no evidence for waves as tall as houses.
C. Supporters say dramatic events and larger-than-life characters make stories memorable. [CORRECT]
D. Clara lived near a mountain pass in the late 1800s.
E. Some scholars believe storytellers added extraordinary elements.

What different perspectives about legends are presented in the passages?
A. Some people value legends for inspiration, while others worry that exaggeration can blur historical truth. [CORRECT]
B. Everyone agrees legends should remove all dramatic details.
C. Historians and community members both reject Elias and Clara.
D. The passages show that legends have no connection to real people.
Argumentative

writingPrompt.text: When telling stories about real people, is it better to emphasize historical accuracy or to include exaggerated details that make the story more memorable? Write a multi-paragraph response in which you make and support a claim about whether it is better to emphasize historical accuracy or to include exaggerated details that make a story more memorable. Your response must be based on ideas and information that can be found in the passages. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passages; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: include a thesis statement; address counterclaims; use evidence from multiple passages; explain how the evidence supports your claim; and avoid overly relying on one passage.

passages.title: The Garden of Golden Seeds
passages.text:
(1) Long ago, in the Kingdom of Valoria, a magnificent garden grew behind the royal palace. Unlike ordinary gardens, this one contained magical plants said to have been planted by the kingdom's first ruler. Golden roses bloomed through winter snow, silver apples grew year-round, and glowing vines climbed crystal trellises. The garden provided food and medicine for the kingdom and was considered one of its greatest treasures.

(2) Queen Celestine, who had ruled Valoria wisely for many years, was preparing to choose a successor. Many people assumed she would select her nephew, Prince Rowan. He was intelligent, confident, and eager to lead. However, the queen believed leadership required more than intelligence alone.

(3) One morning, she invited Rowan into the palace conservatory. Hundreds of rare flowers surrounded them as sunlight streamed through stained-glass windows.

(4) "I have a task for you," the queen said, placing a small golden seed in his hand. "Plant this seed and return in one month. What you learn from it will help me decide whether you are ready to rule."

(5) Rowan carefully planted the seed in the finest soil he could find. He watered it daily and placed it in perfect sunlight. Yet after a week, nothing happened. Two weeks passed. Still nothing.

(6) Concerned, Rowan consulted gardeners, scholars, and merchants. One merchant sold him enchanted fertilizer. Another offered a potion that promised rapid growth. Rowan tried everything he could think of, but the pot remained empty.

(7) As the deadline approached, Rowan became increasingly anxious. He imagined disappointing the queen and losing his opportunity to become ruler. Then, while visiting a market, he noticed a vendor selling beautiful golden flowers.

(8) An idea  his mind. If he replaced the seed with a flowering plant, no one would know. After all, he reasoned, the task was probably meant to test his gardening skills. Surely the queen wanted results.

(9) On the day of the presentation, Rowan carried a magnificent golden blossom into the palace. The flower shimmered in the sunlight, drawing admiration from everyone in the room.

(10) Queen Celestine studied the plant quietly. Then she asked a simple question.

(11) "Did this flower grow from the seed I gave you?"

(12) Rowan hesitated. At first he considered continuing the deception. But as he looked around the room, he remembered the trust people placed in their leaders.

(13) "No," he admitted. "The seed never grew. I became afraid of failing, so I replaced it."

(14) The queen nodded. "The seed was enchanted. It could never sprout. The purpose of the task was not to grow a flower but to reveal character."

(15) Some members of the court gasped. Others shook their heads. Rowan lowered his eyes, expecting the queen to dismiss him.

(16) Instead, she smiled slightly.

(17) "You made a mistake," she said. "But you also chose honesty when it mattered most. A leader will face many situations where taking shortcuts seems easier than telling the truth."

(18) The story spread throughout Valoria. Some people believed Rowan's final honesty proved he had learned an important lesson. Others argued that a future ruler should never have attempted deception in the first place.

(19) For years afterward, parents told the story of the golden seed to their children. Yet people often disagreed about its lesson. Was the most important quality a person's willingness to be honest, even after making mistakes? Or was it the patience to trust the process from the beginning?

(20) The debate became part of the fairy tale itself, and the people of Valoria continued discussing it long after Queen Celestine's reign had ended.

passages.title: The Door of Starlight
passages.text:
(1) In the neighboring Kingdom of Liora stood an ancient tower hidden deep within an enchanted forest. According to legend, the tower contained the Door of Starlight, a magical gateway that opened only for those willing to leave behind certainty and embrace the unknown.

(2) Most people avoided the tower. Stories claimed that travelers who  returned changed by their experiences. Some came back wiser, while others returned with unexpected challenges. No one could predict what awaited beyond the door.

(3) Princess Mira had heard these stories since childhood. Unlike many members of the royal court, she was fascinated by them. She spent hours reading maps, studying distant lands, and listening to travelers' tales.

(4) "Why would anyone leave a comfortable life to face uncertainty?" her advisors often asked.

(5) Mira never had a simple answer. She only knew she felt drawn toward places she had never seen.

(6) On her sixteenth birthday, a tiny silver dragon appeared in her chamber carrying a shining key.

(7) "The Door of Starlight is waiting," the dragon said. "But once you enter, there is no guarantee the journey will be easy."

(8) The next morning, Mira traveled to the tower. At its highest level stood the legendary door. When she inserted the key, the doorway filled with swirling light.

(9) Beyond it, she discovered floating islands, rivers that flowed through the sky, and forests where trees sang softly in the wind. The world was more extraordinary than she had imagined.

(10) Yet the journey was not easy. Mira crossed fragile bridges suspended above clouds. She solved riddles presented by magical creatures and navigated unfamiliar landscapes. Several times she considered turning back.

(11) During one challenge, she became lost inside a valley of shifting mist. The safest choice would have been to wait for help. Instead, she trusted her observations and continued forward.

(12) Gradually, Mira developed confidence she had never possessed before. Each obstacle taught her new skills. Each mistake taught her something valuable.

(13) Months later, she returned to Liora. Although she looked the same, people quickly noticed changes. She spoke with greater confidence, solved problems more independently, and approached challenges with determination.

(14) When the silver dragon appeared again, it asked whether she regretted leaving the safety of the kingdom.

(15) Mira thought carefully before answering.

(16) "The journey was difficult," she said. "I made mistakes and faced uncertainty. But if I had stayed where everything felt safe, I never would have discovered what I was capable of becoming."

(17) Her story soon became one of Liora's most beloved fairy tales. Many people viewed it as a lesson about courage and growth.

(18) Others, however, pointed out that Mira's success depended on taking risks that could easily have ended badly. They wondered whether wisdom sometimes meant remaining where responsibilities already existed rather than pursuing uncertain opportunities.

(19) As the tale spread across kingdoms, people debated its meaning. Some believed personal growth required courage and exploration. Others believed stability and careful decision-making were equally important.

(20) And so the story of the Door of Starlight remained a favorite fairy tale—not because everyone agreed on its lesson, but because it encouraged people to think about how growth truly occurs.

questions.text:
How do the two fairy tales present success differently?
A. The first tale connects success to honesty and patience, while the second connects success to courage and risk-taking. [CORRECT]
B. Both tales show that magic removes all consequences.
C. The first tale praises travel, while the second tale praises deception.
D. Both tales argue that leaders should avoid difficult choices.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In Passage 2, "certainty" most nearly means ( ).
Drop-down options: a safe and predictable outcome [CORRECT] / a magical doorway / a difficult riddle / a dragon's command

Select TWO details that show important character growth.
A. Rowan admits that the flower did not grow from the queen's seed. [CORRECT]
B. Rowan plants the seed in good soil.
C. Mira returns with more confidence and independence. [CORRECT]
D. The tower is hidden in an enchanted forest.
E. The queen's garden has golden roses.

Which theme is developed across both passages?
A. Growth can come from difficult choices that reveal or build a person's character. [CORRECT]
B. People should avoid honesty when they are afraid.
C. Safety is always more important than learning.
D. Magical places prevent people from changing.
Argumentative

writingPrompt.text: Which quality is more important for success: honesty and patience or courage and a willingness to take risks? Write a multi-paragraph response in which you make and support a claim about whether honesty and patience or courage and a willingness to take risks are more important for success. Your response must be based on ideas and information that can be found in the passages. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passages; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: include a thesis statement; address counterclaims; use evidence from multiple passages; explain how the evidence supports your claim; and avoid overly relying on one passage.

title: The Last Spot on the Team

passages.title: The Last Spot on the Team
passages.text:
(1) Ethan Collins had been thinking about the robotics team tryouts for weeks. Ever since the school announced that only twelve students would be selected for the regional competition team, he had spent afternoons practicing programming and weekends building prototype robots in his garage. On the day the results were posted, dozens of students crowded around a bulletin board outside the gym. Ethan pushed through the crowd and searched for his name.

(2) Relief washed over him when he found it. He had made the team. A smile spread across his face as he looked over the list. Then he noticed that his name appeared second-to-last. The final position belonged to Ava Patel, another student who had worked hard throughout the tryout process. Across the hallway, Ava celebrated with her friends while students congratulated the new team members.

(3) Later that afternoon, Coach Ramirez stopped Ethan before he left school. "Could you come by my office after classes?" she asked. Her serious expression immediately made Ethan wonder whether something was wrong. All day, he replayed the conversation in his mind.

(4) When Ethan arrived at the office, Coach Ramirez closed the door behind him. She explained that while reviewing the tryout results, she had discovered an error in the scoring system. One of Ethan's programming assessments had been  incorrectly. After correcting the mistake, his overall score increased significantly. Although he was already on the team, the corrected rankings changed the order of the final selections.

(5) "The updated scores show that you actually earned the last spot on the team," Coach Ramirez explained. "Unfortunately, that means Ava's score falls just below the cutoff." Ethan stared at the desk, unsure how to respond. Technically, the corrected rankings reflected what each student had earned. Yet he immediately thought about how excited Ava had been when she saw her name on the list.

(6) That evening, Ethan sat at the kitchen table while his parents prepared dinner. After hearing the story, his father believed the corrected results should stand. "The purpose of a competition is to reward performance," he said. "If the scores were wrong, they should be fixed." Ethan's mother agreed that accuracy was important, but she also understood why the situation felt unfair. "Ava didn't cause the mistake," she pointed out. "That matters too."

(7) The discussion continued long after dinner. Ethan found himself agreeing with both sides. Rules existed for a reason, and the corrected rankings reflected the actual results. However, he also imagined how disappointed Ava would feel if she lost her place because of an error she could not control. The more he thought about the problem, the less certain he became.

(8) The next day, Coach Ramirez called a meeting with the students who had qualified for the team. When everyone arrived, she carefully explained the scoring error and the revised rankings. The room became quiet. Some students immediately believed the corrected scores should determine the final roster. Others exchanged uncertain glances.

(9) Ava listened carefully before speaking. "If Ethan earned the position, then he deserves it," she said. Several students nodded. Ethan was surprised by her response. He had expected frustration or disappointment, not acceptance. Still, he could tell that losing the opportunity would be difficult.

(10) Coach Ramirez then revealed another possibility. The competition organization allowed schools to add one alternate team member under special circumstances. If approved, both Ethan and Ava could participate. However, doing so would require additional fundraising, longer practice sessions, and extra responsibilities for the team. Some students immediately supported the idea, while others worried about the challenges it might create.

(11) For the next thirty minutes, the group discussed the situation. Students raised questions about fairness, effort, responsibility, and teamwork. Ethan noticed that reasonable people often reached different conclusions even when they shared the same goal. Some believed following the corrected results was the fairest choice. Others believed the team should find a solution that acknowledged the impact of the mistake.

(12) As the meeting ended, Coach Ramirez thanked the students for their thoughtful discussion. She promised to make a decision after considering everyone's perspectives. Walking out of the library, Ethan realized the issue was larger than a robotics competition. It was about how people respond when rules, fairness, and compassion seem to point in different directions.

(13) Outside, the late afternoon sun cast long shadows across the parking lot. Ava caught up to Ethan as he headed home. Neither of them knew what decision Coach Ramirez would make. Yet both understood that difficult choices rarely have perfect answers.

(14) As Ethan walked away, he reflected on everything he had heard. Should people always follow the corrected results when mistakes occur, or should they consider the impact those decisions have on others? The answer seemed more complicated than he had first imagined, and he wondered what choice he would make if the final decision were his.

questions.text:
Which central idea is developed in the story?
A. Fairness can be complicated when rules, mistakes, and people's feelings all matter. [CORRECT]
B. Corrected results should always be ignored.
C. Ethan and Ava do not care about the robotics team.
D. Competition is useful only when no one discusses rules.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In paragraph 5, cutoff means the ( ).
Drop-down options: score or position needed to qualify [CORRECT] / door to the coach's office / final practice before tryouts / list posted outside the gym

Select TWO details that show why the decision is difficult.
A. Correcting the scoring error means Ethan earned the last spot. [CORRECT]
B. Ethan practiced programming for weeks.
C. Ava did not cause the mistake but may lose her opportunity. [CORRECT]
D. The results were posted outside the gym.
E. Students crowded around the bulletin board.

How does the possibility of adding Ava as an alternate affect the plot?
A. It adds a possible compromise but also creates new responsibilities for the team. [CORRECT]
B. It ends the conflict immediately with no costs.
C. It proves Ethan should quit the team.
D. It shows Coach Ramirez refuses to consider solutions.
Argumentative

writingPrompt.text: When a mistake affects an important outcome, should people strictly follow the corrected results or consider the impact on those affected by the error? Write a multi-paragraph response in which you make and support a claim about whether people should strictly follow corrected results or consider the impact on those affected by the error. Your response must be based on ideas and information that can be found in the story. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passage; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: include a thesis statement; address counterclaims; use evidence from the passage; explain how the evidence supports your claim; and avoid overly relying on one part of the passage.

passages.title: Saturday Morning at Mercado Square
passages.text:
(1) By eight o'clock, Mercado Square was already awake. Vendors rolled open colorful awnings while the scent of fresh bread drifted through the cool morning air. A musician near the fountain tuned his guitar, and shoppers wandered between rows of tables stacked with fruit, flowers, handmade crafts, and baked goods.

(2) Twelve-year-old Sofia liked arriving early. The market felt different before the crowds appeared. She could hear the splash of water from the fountain and the calls of birds perched on the rooftops. For a little while, the square belonged to the people setting up rather than the people shopping.

(3) Sofia's grandmother sold homemade jams at a small booth near the center of the market. Every Saturday, Sofia helped arrange jars of strawberry, peach, and blackberry preserves into neat rows. Her grandmother insisted that the display mattered just as much as the product.

(4) "People notice effort," her grandmother often said. "Even before they taste the jam."

(5) Across the walkway stood another booth owned by Mr. Alvarez. He sold hand-carved wooden toys. Some were painted bright colors, while others showed the natural grain of the wood. Children often stopped to watch him work as he carved small animals from blocks of cedar.

(6) By midmorning, the square had transformed. Families filled the walkways. Friends greeted one another near the fountain. Musicians performed beneath strings of colorful flags hanging overhead. Conversations blended into a steady hum that echoed across the plaza.

(7) Sofia enjoyed watching people. Some visitors moved quickly from booth to booth, focused on finding the best prices. Others wandered slowly, stopping to ask questions and listen to stories. Many vendors seemed to know their customers by name.

(8) Near noon, Sofia noticed a new grocery store opening across the street. Large signs advertised low prices and fast service. Throughout the morning, several shoppers discussed the store while browsing the market.

(9) Some believed the grocery store would make shopping easier. Customers could purchase many items in one place without spending hours walking through the market. Lower prices might also help families save money.

(10) Others worried that the new store could change the square. Local vendors depended on weekly sales. Many had spent years building relationships with customers. Some residents feared fewer people would visit the market if shopping became more convenient elsewhere.

(11) Sofia listened as shoppers debated the issue. One customer argued that progress often requires change. Another responded that places like Mercado Square offered something larger than products alone. They created opportunities for neighbors to connect, share traditions, and support local businesses.

(12) The discussion continued throughout the day. Some visitors seemed excited about the grocery store. Others preferred the market's slower pace and personal connections. Few people completely agreed.

(13) As afternoon sunlight stretched across the square, Sofia helped her grandmother pack the remaining jars. The musicians were finishing their final songs, and vendors began folding tables and loading supplies into trucks.

(14) Before leaving, Sofia looked around the square one last time. Children laughed near the fountain. Vendors exchanged farewells. Friends stopped to finish conversations before heading home. The market would return next Saturday, just as it always had.

(15) Yet Sofia wondered how the square might change in the future. Would people choose the convenience of the new grocery store, or would they continue supporting the market and the traditions it represented? Watching the last customers leave, she realized that both choices offered benefits, but deciding which mattered more would not be simple.

questions.text:
Which central idea is developed in the vignette?
A. Community spaces can offer connection and tradition, even when convenient new options appear. [CORRECT]
B. Low prices always matter more than relationships.
C. Sofia dislikes helping her grandmother.
D. Mercado Square is empty for most of the day.

Complete the sentence using the drop-down menu.
In paragraph 6, transformed most nearly means ( ).
Drop-down options: changed greatly [CORRECT] / closed permanently / became quieter / lost all color

Select TWO details that support the value of Mercado Square.
A. Vendors and customers know one another and share stories. [CORRECT]
B. A grocery store opens across the street.
C. The market creates opportunities for neighbors to connect and support local businesses. [CORRECT]
D. Large signs advertise low prices.
E. Some shoppers want to purchase many items in one place.

How does the vignette's structure help develop its meaning?
A. It uses scenes from morning to afternoon to show the square as a living community space. [CORRECT]
B. It begins with a final decision and then explains the rules.
C. It focuses only on the new grocery store's prices.
D. It avoids sensory details so readers can form no opinion.
Argumentative

writingPrompt.text: When making purchasing decisions, should people prioritize convenience and lower prices or support local businesses and community traditions? Write a multi-paragraph response in which you make and support a claim about whether people should prioritize convenience and lower prices or support local businesses and community traditions when making purchasing decisions. Your response must be based on ideas and information that can be found in the passage. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passage; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: include a thesis statement; address counterclaims; use evidence from the passage; explain how the evidence supports your claim; and avoid overly relying on one part of the passage.

passages.title: The Empty Lot
passages.text:
(1) Every afternoon, twelve-year-old Marcus walked past the empty lot on the corner of Maple Street and Third Avenue. The lot had once held an old grocery store, but the building had been torn down years ago. Now, weeds pushed through cracks in the pavement, and a chain-link fence surrounded the property.

(2) Most people barely noticed the lot anymore. Drivers passed it without slowing down, and pedestrians hurried by on their way to work or school. To many residents, it was simply an unused space waiting for something new.

(3) Recently, however, a large sign had appeared on the fence. It announced plans for a new shopping center that would include restaurants, stores, and a small movie theater. Construction was expected to begin later that year.

(4) The proposal quickly became a popular topic of conversation throughout the neighborhood. Some residents were excited. They imagined having more places to shop, eat, and spend time with friends. Others believed the project could create jobs and attract new businesses to the area.

(5) Marcus often overheard these discussions while waiting for the bus. One morning, a group of neighbors talked enthusiastically about the movie theater. Another afternoon, local business owners discussed how additional visitors might help nearby shops.

(6) Yet not everyone shared the excitement. Several longtime residents remembered when the lot had served as an informal gathering place. Community events were occasionally held there, and neighborhood organizations sometimes used the open space for festivals, food drives, and outdoor activities.

(7) One evening, Marcus saw volunteers setting up tables for a community meeting. Curious, he stopped to listen. Residents discussed how the neighborhood had changed over the years and what they hoped it would become in the future.

(8) Some speakers supported the shopping center because they believed growth would benefit the community. Others worried that replacing every open space with new development could gradually change the character of the neighborhood.

(9) As the meeting ended, Marcus looked across the lot. He could easily imagine the bright lights and busy sidewalks that might one day fill the space. He could also understand why some people valued the lot for reasons that had little to do with business or convenience.

(10) Walking home, Marcus realized that different people often viewed the same place in very different ways. What looked like an opportunity for progress to some looked like a loss to others.

passages.title: The Old Library
passages.text:
(1) Across town, twelve-year-old Lena spent most Saturday mornings at the Riverside Library. The building was nearly a century old. Its brick walls, tall windows, and wooden shelves made it feel different from newer buildings in the city.

(2) Visitors often moved quietly through the aisles, pausing to browse books or read near the large windows overlooking the river. The library hosted reading clubs, tutoring sessions, and community programs throughout the year.

(3) Recently, however, city officials announced plans to replace the aging structure with a modern learning center. The new facility would include advanced technology, collaborative workspaces, and expanded resources for students.

(4) Many residents welcomed the proposal. They argued that the current building required expensive repairs and lacked features found in newer educational facilities. Supporters believed the modern center would better serve future generations.

(5) Others felt differently. They viewed the library as more than a building. To them, it represented local history, community traditions, and shared memories.

(6) Lena listened as visitors discussed the issue. One afternoon, she heard a teacher explain how upgraded technology could help students complete research projects and access information more efficiently. Later that day, an elderly resident described learning to read in the very same building decades earlier.

(7) The conversations continued for weeks. Newspapers published letters from residents supporting both perspectives. Some focused on preparing for the future, while others emphasized preserving important parts of the past.

(8) One rainy afternoon, Lena sat beside a window and watched raindrops slide down the glass. Around her, students worked on homework, parents read with children, and community members exchanged greetings.

(9) She wondered whether the atmosphere she enjoyed could be recreated in a new building. At the same time, she understood why many people wanted updated resources and expanded opportunities.

(10) As Lena left the library that evening, she noticed visitors studying a display that showed drawings of the proposed learning center. Some people seemed excited by the possibilities. Others appeared reluctant to let go of something familiar. Looking back at the old brick building, Lena realized that both perspectives reflected a desire to improve the community, even if people disagreed about the best way to do it.

questions.text:
How are the two passages similar?
A. Both show communities weighing future improvements against preserving places with shared meaning. [CORRECT]
B. Both show that old buildings cannot serve communities.
C. Both focus on students who want to leave their neighborhoods.
D. Both argue that development always destroys history.

How do Marcus and Lena respond to the debates in their communities?

passages.title: The Championship Shot
passages.text:
(1) The scoreboard glowed above the gymnasium floor: Eagles 48, Wildcats 48. Only six seconds remained in the championship game. The crowd filled the bleachers, and the noise echoed off the walls as players rushed into position.

(2) Seventh-grader Noah Thompson stood near the sideline waiting for the inbound pass. For months, he had imagined what it might feel like to take the final shot in an important game. He practiced before school, after school, and on weekends. Now the opportunity seemed within reach.

(3) The referee handed the ball to Noah's teammate, and play resumed. The ball quickly found its way into Noah's hands. He dribbled toward the basket as the final seconds ticked away.

(4) Then he noticed something unexpected. His teammate Maya stood completely unguarded near the corner. If Noah passed the ball, Maya would have an open shot. If he kept it, he would likely have to shoot over two defenders.

(5) For a split second, Noah hesitated. He had dreamed about taking this shot for years. Yet he also knew Maya had worked just as hard as everyone else on the team.

(6) The defenders closed in. Without any time left to think, Noah passed the ball.

(7) Maya caught it, released the shot, and watched as the ball sailed through the air. The buzzer sounded. A moment later, the ball dropped cleanly through the net.

(8) The gym erupted with cheers. Players rushed onto the court while teammates surrounded Maya. Reporters from the local newspaper snapped photographs as the Wildcats celebrated their victory.

(9) During the ride home, Noah felt proud of the win. Yet part of him also wondered what might have happened if he had taken the shot himself. The next day, several classmates asked why he had passed the ball.

(10) "Because it gave us the best chance to win," Noah replied.

(11) Not everyone agreed. Some students believed athletes should seize important opportunities when they arise. Others admired Noah's willingness to put the team's success ahead of personal recognition.

(12) Weeks later, Coach Daniels spoke about the play during the team's awards banquet. "Great teams succeed because players trust one another," he said. "Sometimes leadership means making the choice that benefits everyone, even when another option might bring personal attention."

(13) Noah still remembered the moment whenever he stepped onto a basketball court. Although he occasionally wondered what would have happened if he had taken the shot himself, he never regretted helping his team succeed.

(14) Looking around the banquet hall, Noah realized that the championship trophy belonged to the entire team, not just the person who scored the final basket.

passages.title: The Science Fair
passages.text:
(1) For months, seventh-grader Priya Shah had worked on her science fair project. While many students selected familiar topics, Priya designed an experiment that required learning new computer software and collecting large amounts of data. The project was challenging, and several times she considered choosing something easier.

(2) Her best friend, Elena, often encouraged her to simplify the project. "You'd probably have a better chance of winning," Elena suggested one afternoon.

(3) Priya understood the advice. Simpler projects often produced more predictable results. Still, she wanted to test an idea she genuinely found interesting.

(4) As the science fair approached, unexpected problems appeared. Some of her data had to be collected again, and one computer program stopped working entirely. While other students seemed ready days before the deadline, Priya continued making adjustments.

(5) The night before the competition, she wondered whether all the extra effort had been worthwhile. Her project looked different from most of the others, but different did not necessarily mean better.

(6) On the morning of the fair, rows of displays filled the school cafeteria. Students presented projects on engineering, biology, environmental science, and technology. Judges moved from table to table asking questions.

(7) When the judges reached Priya's display, she explained her research, described the challenges she had encountered, and shared what she had learned. Some questions were difficult, but she answered them as honestly as she could.

(8) Hours later, students gathered for the awards ceremony. Priya listened as names were announced. First place went to another student whose project had produced impressive results.

(9) Although she felt disappointed, Priya also felt relieved. She had completed the project she wanted to pursue rather than choosing an easier path simply to improve her chances of winning.

(10) After the ceremony, one of the judges approached her table.

(11) "Your project was one of the most ambitious entries we reviewed," he said. "It didn't earn first place this year, but it demonstrated creativity, perseverance, and a willingness to tackle difficult problems."

(12) His words stayed with Priya long after the competition ended. She realized success could be measured in different ways. Winning an award was one form of achievement, but challenging yourself and learning new skills could also be valuable.

(13) In the months that followed, Priya continued developing her project. Eventually, she used what she learned to enter a regional competition, where her work received recognition.

(14) Looking back, Priya believed choosing the more difficult path had been worthwhile. Even though it had not produced immediate success, it helped her grow in ways she had not expected.

Short Stories: complete narratives in brief format

questions.text:
How do the passages present different kinds of achievement?
A. Noah chooses team success over personal recognition, while Priya chooses personal challenge and growth. [CORRECT]
B. Noah refuses to help his team, while Priya refuses to finish her project.
C. Both characters win first place because they choose easy paths.
D. Both passages show that effort has no value without awards.

D. Neither character faces a difficult decision.

title: Maria Mitchell: Reaching for the Stars

passages.title: Maria Mitchell: Reaching for the Stars
passages.text:
(1) In 1847, newspapers across the United States reported exciting news from the world of astronomy. A previously unknown comet had been discovered traveling through the night sky. The discovery earned international attention, and the person responsible was Maria Mitchell, a librarian and teacher from Nantucket, Massachusetts. At a time when few women were recognized in scientific fields, Mitchell's achievement made her one of the most respected astronomers of her era.

(2) Maria Mitchell was born in 1818 on the island of Nantucket. During her childhood, opportunities for advanced education were often limited, especially for girls. However, Mitchell's parents believed strongly in learning and encouraged curiosity. Her father, William Mitchell, was an amateur astronomer who frequently observed the stars and taught his daughter how to use scientific instruments.

(3) As a young girl, Maria spent countless evenings studying the night sky. She learned to identify constellations, track celestial objects, and make careful observations. While many children her age focused on games and entertainment, Mitchell developed a passion for understanding how the universe worked.

(4) Education played an important role in her life. After attending local schools, Mitchell became a teacher and later worked as a librarian. The library gave her access to books about mathematics, astronomy, and science. She often continued studying long after her daily responsibilities were complete.

(5) On the evening of October 1, 1847, Mitchell was observing the sky through a telescope when she noticed an unfamiliar object. After carefully checking her observations, she realized she had discovered a comet. The object later became known as "Miss Mitchell's Comet." Because a prize had been established for the first person to discover a telescopic comet, Mitchell received international recognition for her achievement.

(6) The discovery brought new opportunities. Scientists throughout the United States and Europe praised her work. She became the first woman elected to the American Academy of Arts and Sciences, an organization dedicated to advancing knowledge and research. Her accomplishments demonstrated that scientific ability was not limited by gender.

(7) Despite her success, Mitchell believed that learning should be available to everyone. She often spoke about the importance of education and encouraged young people to pursue their interests. She believed curiosity and determination were essential qualities for anyone seeking knowledge.

(8) In 1865, Mitchell accepted a position at Vassar College, one of the few colleges in the United States that offered higher education opportunities for women. There, she taught astronomy and directed the college observatory. Her classes were known for emphasizing hands-on learning rather than memorization alone.

(9) Students frequently joined Mitchell for nighttime observations. She encouraged them to ask questions, make discoveries, and think independently. Many of her students later pursued careers in science, education, and research.

(10) Mitchell also advocated for greater opportunities for women in professional fields. She believed that talent and hard work should determine success. Throughout her career, she worked to create opportunities for others while continuing her own scientific studies.

(11) Historians often point to Mitchell's influence beyond astronomy. Her accomplishments helped challenge assumptions about who could participate in scientific research. By demonstrating expertise in a field dominated by men, she inspired future generations to pursue careers that had previously seemed out of reach.

(12) Some people admire Mitchell primarily for her scientific achievements, including her comet discovery and contributions to astronomy. Others believe her greatest legacy was her dedication to education and expanding opportunities for future scientists. Both perspectives reflect important parts of her life and career.

(13) Today, Maria Mitchell's name appears in science textbooks, museums, and observatories. Her story continues to be shared because it demonstrates how curiosity, perseverance, and education can lead to remarkable accomplishments.

(14) Looking up at the stars, Mitchell once remarked, "We especially need imagination in science." Her life showed that imagination, combined with hard work and determination, can help people make discoveries that change how others see the world.

questions.text:
Which statement best expresses a central idea of the biography?

D. Mitchell's work mattered only on the night she discovered the comet.

Argumentative

title: Temple Grandin: Seeing the World Differently

passages.title: Temple Grandin: Seeing the World Differently
passages.text:
(1) In the early 1950s, many people had little understanding of autism. Children who communicated or learned differently were often misunderstood, and few educational programs existed to support their needs. During this time, a young girl named Temple Grandin was growing up in Massachusetts. Although she faced challenges that others did not always understand, she would eventually become one of the world's most influential scientists and advocates.

(2) Temple Grandin was born in 1947. As a young child, she spoke very little and often struggled to communicate with others. Doctors at the time had limited knowledge about autism, and some believed she might never learn to speak or succeed in school. Her mother, however, refused to accept those predictions. She worked tirelessly to help Temple develop communication skills and encouraged her to participate in activities that challenged her to learn and grow.

(3) Temple viewed the world differently from many people around her. She often thought in pictures rather than words and noticed details that others overlooked. While some teachers viewed these differences as obstacles, Temple eventually realized they could also be strengths.

(4) During her school years, Temple developed a strong interest in animals. She spent time observing livestock and noticed how animals reacted to their surroundings. She paid close attention to details such as shadows, sounds, fences, and movement patterns. Many people ignored these factors, but Temple realized they could greatly affect animal behavior.

(5) A science teacher named William Carlock played an important role in her life. He encouraged Temple to pursue her interests and challenged her to develop confidence in her abilities. Years later, Temple credited teachers and mentors like Carlock with helping her recognize her potential.

(6) After graduating from high school, Temple continued her education, earning degrees in psychology and animal science. At a time when relatively few women worked in agricultural engineering, she  a field where she could combine her scientific knowledge with her observations of animal behavior.

(7) As she studied livestock facilities, Temple noticed that animals often became frightened by design features that humans failed to notice. Small distractions, such as reflections, sudden noises, or unfamiliar objects, could cause animals to stop moving or become stressed. Temple believed that understanding an animal's perspective could improve both safety and welfare.

(8) Using this knowledge, she designed new systems for handling cattle and other livestock. Her curved pathways and humane handling facilities reduced stress for animals while helping farmers move livestock more efficiently. Many of her designs were eventually adopted throughout the United States and in other countries around the world.

(9) Temple's success attracted attention not only because of her innovations but also because of her personal story. As public understanding of autism increased, many people became interested in how her unique way of thinking contributed to her achievements.

(10) Through books, interviews, and public presentations, Temple explained how autism affected her experiences. She encouraged educators, employers, and families to recognize the strengths that often accompany different ways of learning and thinking. Her message emphasized that individuals should not be judged solely by their challenges.

(11) Over time, Temple became an advocate for both animal welfare and autism awareness. She spoke to audiences around the world and inspired many young people to pursue careers in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics. Her work helped others understand that different perspectives can lead to valuable discoveries.

(12) Many people admire Temple Grandin primarily for her scientific contributions. Her livestock facility designs transformed agricultural practices and improved animal welfare. Others believe her greatest impact came from helping society better understand autism and encouraging acceptance of neurodiversity.

(13) Both aspects of her career continue to influence people today. Farmers still use systems based on her designs, while students, educators, and families continue to learn from her advocacy and educational work. Her achievements demonstrate how one person's experiences can contribute to improvements in multiple fields.

(14) Temple Grandin often encourages people to focus on abilities rather than limitations. Her life story illustrates how perseverance, curiosity, and a willingness to think differently can lead to meaningful change. Whether remembered primarily as a scientist or an advocate, her influence continues to reach people around the world.

questions.text:
Which central idea is developed in the biography?

D. Grandin's work had an effect only in Massachusetts.

How does the biography present Grandin's differences?

B. It presents them as reasons her ideas should be ignored.
C. It suggests they had no connection to her work.
D. It describes them only as problems for farmers.
Argumentative

title: Katherine Johnson: Solving Problems Among the Stars

passages.title: Katherine Johnson: Solving Problems Among the Stars
passages.text:
(1) In the early years of the United States space program, scientists and engineers faced a difficult challenge. They needed to calculate precise flight paths for rockets and spacecraft traveling farther than humans had ever gone before. Among the people helping solve these complex problems was mathematician Katherine Johnson, whose calculations contributed to some of the nation's most important space missions.

(2) Katherine Johnson was born in 1918 in White Sulphur Springs, West Virginia. From an early age, she demonstrated exceptional mathematical ability. She advanced through school quickly and completed high school at a time when educational opportunities for African American students were often limited.

(3) Determined to continue learning, Johnson attended West Virginia State College. There, she studied mathematics and developed skills that would later shape her career. After graduating, she worked as a teacher before pursuing opportunities in scientific research.

(4) In 1953, Johnson joined the National Advisory Committee for Aeronautics, the organization that later became NASA. At the time, many calculations were performed by teams of mathematicians known as "computers." Their work was essential for solving complex engineering problems.

(5) Johnson quickly earned a reputation for accuracy and problem-solving. She analyzed data, calculated trajectories, and helped engineers understand how spacecraft would travel through space. Her work became increasingly important as the United States expanded its space exploration efforts.

(6) One of Johnson's most famous contributions occurred during John Glenn's 1962 orbital mission. Before the flight, new electronic computers generated calculations needed for the mission. However, Glenn requested that Johnson personally verify the numbers before launch. He trusted her mathematical expertise and wanted confirmation that the calculations were correct.

(7) Johnson checked the figures and confirmed their accuracy. The mission proceeded successfully, making Glenn the first American to orbit Earth. Her contributions also supported later projects, including the Apollo missions that eventually landed astronauts on the Moon.

(8) Beyond her technical achievements, Johnson's career helped break barriers. She worked during a period when both racial segregation and gender discrimination limited opportunities for many people. Through determination and excellence, she demonstrated that talent and dedication could overcome obstacles.

(9) Over time, Johnson received numerous awards and honors. In 2015, she was awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom, one of the highest civilian honors in the United States. Her story later reached an even larger audience through books and films that highlighted her contributions.

(10) Many people admire Johnson because her mathematical work directly contributed to historic achievements in space exploration. Others believe her greatest impact came from inspiring future generations to pursue careers in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics.

(11) Whether remembered primarily for her calculations or for the example she set, Katherine Johnson demonstrated how knowledge, perseverance, and careful problem-solving can influence events far beyond the classroom.

passages.title: Jane Goodall: Understanding the Natural World
passages.text:
(1) While Katherine Johnson used mathematics to help humans explore space, another scientist was transforming the way people understood life on Earth. Jane Goodall's groundbreaking research changed scientific views about animals and inspired people around the world to care more deeply about wildlife and conservation.

(2) Jane Goodall was born in London, England, in 1934. As a child, she spent hours observing animals and reading about nature. Her curiosity led her to dream of studying wildlife in Africa, a goal that seemed difficult to achieve at the time.

(3) In 1960, Goodall traveled to what is now Tanzania to study chimpanzees in the wild. At the time, relatively little was known about chimpanzee behavior. Many scientists believed humans were fundamentally different from other animals in ways that could never be bridged.

(4) Rather than observing from a distance for short periods, Goodall spent months patiently watching chimpanzees in their natural environment. She carefully recorded their behavior and gradually earned their trust.

(5) Her observations led to discoveries that surprised the scientific community. Goodall documented chimpanzees using tools, such as modifying sticks to gather insects from nests. Before this discovery, many scientists believed tool use was unique to humans.

(6) She also observed complex social relationships among chimpanzees. The animals formed friendships, cared for family members, cooperated with one another, and sometimes engaged in conflicts. These observations helped researchers better understand similarities between humans and other primates.

(7) As Goodall's research gained attention, she became one of the world's most recognized scientists. Her findings influenced fields ranging from biology and psychology to conservation science.

(8) Over time, Goodall became increasingly concerned about threats facing wildlife and natural habitats. She shifted much of her attention toward conservation efforts and public education. Through speeches, books, and programs, she encouraged people to take action to protect animals and ecosystems.

(9) Goodall's work inspired many young people to pursue careers in science and environmental stewardship. Her message emphasized that individuals can make meaningful contributions, even when addressing large global challenges.

(10) Some people view Goodall's greatest achievement as her scientific research, which transformed understanding of animal behavior. Others believe her most important contribution has been her conservation advocacy and her ability to inspire people to protect the natural world.

(11) Today, Goodall continues to be recognized as both a scientist and a global advocate. Her career demonstrates how curiosity, patience, and dedication can lead to discoveries that change the way people view the world around them.

questions.text:
How are Katherine Johnson and Jane Goodall similar?
A. Both used curiosity and careful work to make discoveries that changed how people understood the world. [CORRECT]

D. Both studied the same animals in Tanzania.

A. Scientific work can produce discoveries and also inspire others to create positive change. [CORRECT]
B. Scientific discoveries matter only if they happen in space.

D. People cannot influence fields that were difficult for them to enter.

Your response must be based on ideas and information that can be found in the passages. title: Wilma Rudolph: Overcoming Obstacles Through Determination

passages.title: Wilma Rudolph: Overcoming Obstacles Through Determination
passages.text:
(1) In the summer of 1960, millions of people around the world watched the Olympic Games in Rome, Italy. Among the athletes competing was Wilma Rudolph, an American sprinter whose speed and determination would make her one of the most celebrated athletes of her time. Her success was remarkable not only because of the medals she won but also because of the challenges she overcame to reach that point.

(2) Wilma Rudolph was born in 1940 in Tennessee. She was one of twenty-two children in her family. During her early childhood, she faced serious health problems, including illnesses that affected her ability to walk normally. At one point, doctors believed she might never walk without assistance.

(3) Rudolph's family refused to give up hope. Her parents and siblings supported her through years of treatment and physical therapy. She wore a leg brace for much of her childhood and worked tirelessly to strengthen her muscles.

(4) Gradually, her condition improved. By the time she reached her teenage years, Rudolph was participating in sports. She discovered a talent for running and quickly became one of the fastest athletes at her school.

(5) Coaches recognized her potential and encouraged her to continue training. Although competition was difficult, Rudolph's determination helped her improve. She often practiced for hours, pushing herself to become faster and stronger.

(6) In 1956, at just sixteen years old, Rudolph qualified for the Olympic Games and earned a bronze medal as part of a relay team. However, she continued working toward even greater goals.

(7) Four years later, at the 1960 Olympics, Rudolph won three gold medals in track and field events. Her accomplishments made her one of the most successful athletes of the Games and brought international attention to her achievements.

(8) Beyond athletics, Rudolph became a symbol of perseverance. Many people admired her ability to overcome obstacles that once seemed impossible. Her story encouraged others to believe that challenges could be overcome through hard work, determination, and support from others.

(9) Throughout her life, Rudolph spoke to students and community groups about the importance of persistence. She believed that setbacks did not have to determine a person's future and often encouraged young people to pursue ambitious goals.

(10) Some people view Rudolph's greatest contribution as her athletic achievements. Others believe her most lasting impact came from inspiring people to overcome difficulties in their own lives. Both perspectives continue to shape how her legacy is remembered today.

passages.title: Norman Borlaug: Feeding a Growing World
passages.text:
(1) While Wilma Rudolph inspired people through sports, another individual was working to solve a global challenge that affected millions of lives. Norman Borlaug, an American scientist, devoted much of his career to improving agriculture and increasing food production around the world.

(2) Borlaug was born in Iowa in 1914 and grew up on a farm. His experiences helping grow crops gave him an appreciation for agriculture and the challenges farmers faced. As he pursued higher education, he became interested in plant science and agricultural research.

(3) During the mid-twentieth century, many experts worried that rapid population growth would lead to widespread food shortages. In some regions, crop yields were too low to meet the needs of growing populations.

(4) Borlaug believed scientific research could help address this problem. He spent years studying wheat plants and developing new varieties that were more productive and resistant to disease. His work required patience, experimentation, and careful observation.

(5) The new wheat varieties proved highly successful. Farmers in several countries were able to grow larger harvests, producing more food from the same amount of land. These improvements helped reduce the risk of famine in many regions.

(6) Borlaug's work became part of what historians often call the Green Revolution, a period of significant agricultural advancement. New farming techniques and improved crop varieties increased food production around the world.

(7) In 1970, Borlaug received the Nobel Peace Prize for his contributions to global food security. The award recognized the impact his work had on improving living conditions for millions of people.

(8) Despite receiving international recognition, Borlaug continued advocating for scientific innovation. He believed research and technology could help solve future challenges related to agriculture, nutrition, and sustainability.

(9) Many scientists regard Borlaug's achievements as some of the most influential agricultural contributions of the twentieth century. His research affected not only farmers but also families who depended on reliable food supplies.

(10) Some people consider Borlaug's greatest contribution to be the practical results of his scientific work. Others believe his legacy lies in demonstrating how innovation and problem-solving can improve lives on a global scale. His story continues to be studied as an example of how one person's efforts can affect millions of people.

questions.text:
How do the two biographies present impact differently?

passages.title: The Day the Water Rose
passages.text:
(1) On the morning of September 8, 1900, twelve-year-old Elena Moreno woke to the sound of wind rattling the shutters of her family's home in Galveston, Texas. Storms were not unusual along the Gulf Coast, and at first the weather seemed like little more than a rough day near the ocean. Outside, clouds moved quickly across the sky, and waves crashed harder than usual against the shoreline.

(2) Elena's father, a dockworker, paused while preparing for work and looked toward the water. The sea appeared restless. Although weather forecasting was far less advanced than it is today, many residents had heard reports of a storm somewhere in the Gulf of Mexico. Few people, however, realized the danger it posed to Galveston.

(3) During the late nineteenth century, Galveston was one of the most important cities in Texas. Its busy port connected businesses to markets throughout the United States and beyond. Ships arrived daily carrying goods and passengers. Many residents believed the city would continue growing and becoming more prosperous.

(4) As the morning continued, the wind intensified. Elena noticed water beginning to cover streets near the harbor. Neighbors gathered outside to discuss the unusual conditions. Some were concerned, while others believed the storm would pass without causing major damage.

(5) By midday, conditions had changed dramatically. Waves pushed farther inland, flooding roads and surrounding buildings. Families hurried to secure doors and windows. Horse-drawn wagons carried people seeking safer locations. The storm's power was becoming impossible to ignore.

(6) Inside her home, Elena helped her mother move belongings away from windows and gather supplies. The family listened as strong gusts shook the walls. Rain pounded the roof so loudly that conversations became difficult. Throughout the neighborhood, families faced similar decisions about whether to stay or seek shelter elsewhere.

(7) As evening approached, the situation worsened. Water continued rising, and many structures could not withstand the powerful winds. Debris swept through flooded streets. The storm surge—a dome of seawater pushed inland by the hurricane—carried tremendous force. Residents who had underestimated the storm now struggled to protect themselves and their families.

(8) Historical records show that the Galveston Hurricane of 1900 became the deadliest natural disaster in United States history. Thousands of people lost their lives, and much of the city was destroyed. Entire neighborhoods disappeared beneath the storm's destructive power.

(9) Elena's family survived by seeking shelter in a sturdier building farther from the shoreline. Throughout the night, they listened to the roar of the wind and the crash of debris. No one knew what remained of the city outside.

(10) When daylight arrived, the storm had passed. Residents emerged to find a landscape transformed. Homes, businesses, and streets had been severely damaged. Debris covered large sections of the city. For many families, the difficult work of rebuilding was only beginning.

(11) In the weeks and months that followed, people throughout Galveston worked together to recover. Volunteers cleared debris, rebuilt structures, and assisted neighbors who had lost homes or loved ones. Communities from other parts of the country also provided support.

(12) City leaders faced important decisions about the future. Some questioned whether rebuilding in the same location was wise. Others believed the city could recover and become stronger. Ultimately, residents chose to rebuild while also investing in protective measures designed to reduce the impact of future storms.

(13) One of the most significant projects was the construction of a seawall along the coast. Engineers and workers spent years building the massive structure. Portions of the city were also raised to higher elevations in an effort to reduce flooding risks. These projects required tremendous effort and resources, but many residents viewed them as necessary investments.

(14) Historians often point to the Galveston Hurricane as an example of both the power of nature and the resilience of communities. The disaster revealed the limitations of weather forecasting at the time, while also demonstrating how people respond when facing extraordinary challenges.

(15) Some people remember the event primarily as a tragedy that caused widespread destruction and loss. Others focus on the determination shown by residents who rebuilt their city and worked to prepare for future disasters. Both perspectives remain important parts of Galveston's history.

(16) Today, visitors to Galveston can still see sections of the seawall that resulted from decisions made after the hurricane. More than a century later, the storm remains a powerful reminder that while people cannot control nature, they can learn from the past and work together to prepare for the future.
