passages.title: Alone Across the Atlantic
passages.text:
(1) On June 14, 1919, a small crowd gathered near an airfield in Newfoundland, Canada, to watch two aviators prepare for a historic journey. The pilots, John Alcock and Arthur Whitten Brown, planned to attempt something no one had ever successfully accomplished: a nonstop flight across the Atlantic Ocean. At the time, airplanes were still relatively new, and long-distance air travel remained extremely dangerous.

(2) Alcock and Brown would fly a modified Vickers Vimy aircraft originally designed during World War I. The airplane carried extra fuel for the journey but lacked many of the navigation tools modern pilots use today. There was no GPS, no radar, and no reliable way to communicate with people on the ground during the flight. Once the aircraft left Newfoundland, the pilots would depend largely on maps, instruments, and their own judgment.

(3) The challenge attracted international attention. Crossing the Atlantic by ship had long been possible, but flying nonstop across thousands of miles of open ocean presented entirely new difficulties. Pilots would face changing weather, freezing temperatures, limited visibility, and mechanical risks. If something went wrong, emergency landing options would be extremely limited.

(4) Shortly after takeoff, Alcock and Brown began encountering problems. Thick clouds reduced visibility, making navigation difficult. Rain and fog often surrounded the aircraft, preventing the pilots from seeing the ocean below. Brown, who served as navigator, struggled to determine their exact location while the airplane bounced through rough weather.

(5) As the hours passed, conditions became even more dangerous. Ice formed on parts of the aircraft, adding weight and affecting performance. Several times, the airplane  clouds so dense that the pilots lost sight of the horizon entirely. Without a visible horizon, it became difficult to determine whether the aircraft was level or beginning to tilt.

(6) During one especially challenging period, the airplane descended unexpectedly through heavy clouds. Alcock later reported that the aircraft emerged much closer to the ocean than he had realized. Quick reactions and careful flying helped prevent disaster.

(7) Meanwhile, Brown continued working to confirm their position. Whenever breaks appeared in the clouds, he used navigation instruments to estimate where they were. These calculations required patience, skill, and concentration. A small mistake could send the aircraft far off course.

(8) The flight lasted more than sixteen hours. Throughout the journey, the pilots dealt with exhaustion, cold temperatures, and constant uncertainty. Yet they continued moving west to east across the Atlantic, determined to complete their mission.

(9) Finally, on June 15, the aircraft reached Ireland. After spotting land, the pilots searched for a suitable place to land. From the air, a large field appeared promising. However, what looked like solid ground was actually a bog, or marshy area.

(10) When the aircraft touched down, its wheels sank into the soft ground. The airplane tipped forward, damaging the aircraft but leaving both pilots unharmed. Despite the rough landing, they had accomplished their goal.

(11) News of the successful flight spread rapidly around the world. Newspapers celebrated the achievement, and many people viewed the journey as proof that long-distance air travel was becoming possible. Alcock and Brown received awards and recognition for their accomplishment.

(12) Historians often describe the flight as a turning point in aviation history. Although airplanes would continue improving for many years, the successful crossing demonstrated that aircraft could travel vast distances over open water. Future pilots and engineers built upon lessons learned from the journey.

(13) Some people admire Alcock and Brown primarily because they achieved a historic first. Others believe their greatest accomplishment was demonstrating perseverance when faced with difficult and dangerous conditions. The pilots encountered numerous obstacles during the flight, yet they continued adapting and solving problems.

(14) Their story remains a true adventure because success was never guaranteed. Every stage of the journey involved uncertainty. Weather, navigation challenges, and mechanical concerns could have ended the attempt at any moment.

(15) More than a century later, modern travelers can cross the Atlantic in a matter of hours aboard advanced aircraft equipped with sophisticated technology. Yet the achievements of Alcock and Brown continue to be remembered because they helped prove what was possible during a time when aviation was still in its early stages.

(16) Their journey serves as a reminder that exploration often requires people to face risks, overcome setbacks, and continue moving forward even when the outcome remains uncertain.

title: The Race to the South Pole

passages.title: The Race to the South Pole
passages.text:
(1) In the early 1900s, much of Antarctica remained unexplored. The continent's extreme cold, powerful winds, and vast ice fields made travel difficult and dangerous. Despite these challenges, explorers from several countries dreamed of becoming the first people to reach the South Pole.

(2) One of those explorers was Roald Amundsen of Norway. Amundsen had spent years preparing for polar expeditions and believed careful planning was the key to success. Before departing for Antarctica in 1910, he studied survival techniques used by Indigenous peoples of the Arctic and learned how to travel efficiently using dog sled teams.

(3) Amundsen's expedition established a base camp on the Antarctic coast. During the long polar winter, his team prepared equipment, organized supplies, and planned routes. Every detail mattered. A small mistake in one of the harshest environments on Earth could have serious consequences.

(4) In October 1911, Amundsen and four teammates began their journey toward the South Pole. They faced freezing temperatures, difficult terrain, and months of isolation. Snow-covered landscapes stretched endlessly in every direction, offering few landmarks to guide them.

(5) The team relied heavily on preparation. Supplies had been carefully placed along the route in advance. Their sled dogs helped transport equipment and food across the ice. Although conditions were difficult, the expedition moved steadily forward.

(6) Along the way, the explorers encountered storms and bitter cold. Some days visibility was limited, making navigation difficult. Yet Amundsen's planning allowed the team to maintain its schedule and conserve resources.

(7) On December 14, 1911, the group reached the South Pole. After confirming their location through careful measurements, they raised the Norwegian flag and documented their achievement. They then began the long journey home.

(8) Reaching the Pole was only part of the challenge. The explorers still needed to return safely. Fortunately, their planning continued to pay off. They successfully retraced their route and arrived back at base camp after traveling roughly 1,860 miles round-trip.

(9) News of the achievement spread throughout the world. Amundsen became famous as the first person to lead an expedition to the South Pole. Historians often point to his careful preparation, attention to detail, and practical decision-making as major reasons for the expedition's success.

(10) Some people view Amundsen's accomplishment as proof that preparation is the most important factor in overcoming challenges. They argue that his success resulted not from luck but from years of planning and disciplined execution.

passages.title: Ernest Shackleton's Impossible Journey
passages.text:
(1) Around the same time that Amundsen was exploring Antarctica, another explorer named Ernest Shackleton was pursuing a different goal. Shackleton hoped to lead the first expedition across the entire Antarctic continent.

(2) In 1914, Shackleton and his crew departed aboard a ship called the Endurance. Their plan was ambitious and dangerous. If successful, they would complete one of the greatest explorations in history.

(3) However, the expedition faced disaster before the crossing could even begin. Deep in the Antarctic ice, the Endurance became trapped. For months, the crew watched as shifting ice squeezed the ship. Eventually, the pressure crushed the vessel beyond repair.

(4) Suddenly, Shackleton and his crew found themselves stranded thousands of miles from home. They had lost their ship, and rescue was impossible. Survival became their new objective.

(5) The men established camps on drifting ice and rationed their supplies carefully. Conditions were harsh. Temperatures remained below freezing, and food became increasingly scarce. Yet Shackleton focused on maintaining hope among the crew.

(6) Months later, the ice beneath their camp began breaking apart. The men launched lifeboats and traveled through dangerous waters until they reached a small, uninhabited island. Although they had escaped immediate danger, they remained stranded.

(7) Shackleton realized that help would not arrive unless someone went to find it. He selected a small group of crew members and set out in a lifeboat for South Georgia Island, more than 800 miles away across some of the roughest seas in the world.

(8) The journey tested the limits of endurance. Massive waves crashed over the boat. Freezing winds battered the crew. At times, survival seemed unlikely. Nevertheless, the group continued forward.

(9) After reaching South Georgia Island, Shackleton still faced one final challenge. Because they landed on the wrong side of the island, he and two companions crossed mountains and glaciers that few people had ever seen.

(10) Eventually, Shackleton reached a whaling station and organized a rescue effort. Months later, every surviving member of the Endurance crew was rescued. Remarkably, despite the loss of the ship and years of hardship, no crew members died during the expedition.

(11) Although Shackleton never completed his original goal of crossing Antarctica, many historians consider the expedition one of the greatest survival stories ever recorded. They point to his leadership, perseverance, and commitment to protecting his crew.

(12) Some people believe Shackleton's achievement demonstrates that success is not always measured by accomplishing an original goal. Instead, they argue that determination, leadership, and the ability to adapt during difficult circumstances can be equally important.

questions.text:
How do the two passages present success differently?

D. Amundsen's team ignored planning and relied on luck.

passages.title: The Midnight Ride
passages.text:
(1) On the evening of April 18, 1775, Boston was tense. For months, disagreements between the American colonists and the British government had grown more serious. Rumors spread through taverns, homes, and meeting halls. Many colonists believed that conflict was becoming unavoidable, while British leaders worked to maintain control over the colonies.

(2) Among those watching events closely was a silversmith named Paul Revere. Revere was an active member of the Patriot movement, a group that opposed many British policies. Along with other colonists, he helped gather information about British military activities and shared important news with local leaders.

(3) That evening, reports reached Patriot leaders that British troops planned to march from Boston toward Concord, Massachusetts. According to the information they received, the soldiers might attempt to seize military supplies stored by the colonists. Some leaders also feared that important Patriot figures could be arrested.

(4) As darkness settled over the countryside, plans were made to warn nearby communities. Revere and other riders prepared to carry messages through the night. Their task was urgent. If the reports were accurate, local militia groups needed time to prepare.

(5) Shortly after nightfall, Revere crossed the Charles River and began riding westward. As he traveled, he stopped at homes and villages, alerting residents that British troops were on the move. Contrary to popular legend, historians note that Revere was not the only rider spreading the warning. Other messengers, including William Dawes and Samuel Prescott, also carried news through the countryside.

(6) Throughout the night, church bells rang, doors opened, and riders moved from town to town. Farmers, shopkeepers, and laborers left their beds to learn what was happening. Some were uncertain whether the reports were accurate, but many believed it was wise to prepare.

(7) Near Lexington, Revere met local militia leaders and shared the latest information. As news spread, more communities became aware of the approaching British troops. The warning system relied on ordinary people communicating quickly and cooperating with one another.

(8) By the following morning, British soldiers encountered growing resistance as they marched through Massachusetts. The battles at Lexington and Concord became the first major military engagements of the American Revolution.

(9) Historians continue to study the events of that night. Some focus on Revere's courage and determination. Others emphasize the efforts of the many riders, messengers, and community members who helped spread information. Regardless of perspective, the midnight ride remains an important example of how communication and cooperation influenced a pivotal moment in history.

(10) Today, Paul Revere is often remembered as the central figure in the story. Yet the events of April 18 and 19, 1775, also remind us that major historical moments often depend on the actions of many individuals working together toward a common goal.

passages.title: Crossing the Delaware
passages.text:
(1) More than a year after the first battles of the American Revolution, the future of the Patriot cause appeared uncertain. By December 1776, General George Washington's army had suffered several defeats and struggled with shortages of supplies, food, and manpower. Many soldiers were exhausted, and some planned to leave the army when their enlistments ended.

(2) Winter conditions made the situation even more difficult. Freezing temperatures swept across the region, and morale among the troops remained low. British forces controlled key areas, and some Americans doubted whether the Patriots could continue fighting successfully.

(3) Faced with these challenges, Washington decided to take a bold risk. He developed a plan to cross the icy Delaware River and launch a surprise attack against Hessian soldiers stationed in Trenton, New Jersey. The Hessians were German troops fighting alongside the British.

(4) On the night of December 25, 1776, soldiers quietly gathered along the riverbank. Snow and sleet fell as strong winds swept across the water. The crossing would be dangerous even under normal conditions, but the harsh weather made the task far more difficult.

(5) Large boats carried soldiers, horses, cannons, and supplies across the river. Ice floated through the water, creating obstacles for the crews. Progress was slow, and some officers worried that the operation might fail before it even began.

(6) Despite the conditions, the troops continued. Many soldiers lacked proper winter clothing, yet they pressed forward. Historians note that completing the crossing required remarkable coordination and determination from everyone involved.

(7) By the time the army reached the opposite shore, it was behind schedule. Nevertheless, Washington decided to continue with the plan. The soldiers marched toward Trenton through snow and freezing temperatures.

(8) The surprise attack succeeded. Patriot forces captured hundreds of Hessian soldiers and achieved an important victory. Although the battle was relatively small compared to later engagements, its impact was significant.

(9) News of the victory spread quickly throughout the colonies. The success boosted morale, encouraged support for the Patriot cause, and demonstrated that Washington's army remained capable of challenging British forces.

(10) Historians often describe the crossing of the Delaware as a turning point in the Revolutionary War. Some emphasize Washington's leadership and willingness to take risks. Others highlight the perseverance of the ordinary soldiers who endured difficult conditions to carry out the mission. Together, their efforts helped shape the course of American history.

passages.title: The Ocean's Great Conveyor
passages.text:
(1) Imagine a river hidden inside the ocean, one that never reaches a shore. This river moves no boats, yet it carries more water than every visible river on Earth combined. Scientists call this vast system the global ocean conveyor belt, and the engine that drives it is something surprisingly ordinary: heat. The transfer of thermal energy between water, air, and ice keeps this enormous current flowing, and in doing so, it helps decide whether a coastline shivers under snow or basks in mild winters.

(2) The story begins with the Sun. Sunlight strikes the equator more directly than it strikes the poles, so tropical waters absorb far more thermal energy than polar waters do. This uneven heating creates a temperature imbalance, and nature works constantly to even it out. Warm surface currents, such as the famous Gulf Stream, carry heat away from the tropics toward higher latitudes. As this warm water travels north along the eastern coast of North America and toward Europe, it releases heat into the air above it. That released energy is why parts of western Europe enjoy winters far milder than their northern position would suggest.

(3) But the conveyor belt does not run on temperature alone. Density, which depends on both temperature and saltiness, plays an equally important role. As warm Gulf Stream water flows toward the Arctic, it cools and gives up its heat to the atmosphere. Cold water is denser than warm water. At the same time, when sea ice forms near the poles, it leaves behind the salt that was dissolved in the seawater, making the surrounding water saltier and therefore even denser. This cold, salty, heavy water sinks toward the ocean floor in a process scientists call deep-water formation.

(4) Once this dense water sinks, it spreads slowly across the bottom of the ocean and creeps along the seafloor for thousands of miles. Eventually, in distant regions such as the Indian and Pacific Oceans, the deep water rises back toward the surface, warms again, and rejoins the surface currents. A single drop of water may take roughly a thousand years to complete the entire loop. Because the system is powered by differences in heat and salt, researchers often describe it with the term thermohaline circulation, combining the Greek words for heat and salt.

(5) The influence of these currents reaches far beyond the water itself. Oceans store and move tremendous amounts of thermal energy, and the atmosphere responds to that energy. Warm currents can fuel storms and add moisture to the air, while cold currents can leave coastlines dry and foggy. The slow churning of the conveyor belt helps distribute heat around the planet, smoothing out temperature extremes that would otherwise make many regions far harsher places to live.

(6) Scientists watch the conveyor belt closely because it is sensitive to change. If large amounts of fresh water from melting ice were to flood the North Atlantic, the surface water there could become less salty and less dense. That water might then resist sinking, which could slow the entire circulation. Understanding how thermal energy moves between the ocean and the atmosphere is therefore not just an academic puzzle. It is a key to predicting the climate patterns that shape daily life for billions of people.

D. Sea ice forms near the poles because polar waters absorb the most sunlight.

Standard: Thermal-energy transfers in the ocean and the atmosphere contribute to the formation of currents, which influence global climate patterns.

title: The Tilt That Makes the Year

passages.title: The Tilt That Makes the Year
passages.text:
(1) Many people assume that summer arrives because Earth swings closer to the sun and that winter comes when our planet drifts farther away. The idea feels logical, yet it is wrong. Earth's distance from the sun barely changes over the year, and, surprisingly, the Northern Hemisphere is actually nearest the sun in early January, the heart of its winter. The real cause of the seasons is not distance at all. It is tilt.

(2) Earth spins on an invisible line called its axis, an imaginary rod running from the North Pole to the South Pole. This axis is not straight up and down relative to Earth's orbit. Instead, it leans at an angle of about 23.5 degrees, and it stays pointed in the same direction in space throughout the year. Because of this steady lean, different parts of the planet receive sunlight at different angles as Earth travels its yearlong path around the sun.

(3) Consider what happens in June. During these months, the Northern Hemisphere tilts toward the sun. Sunlight strikes the ground from high overhead, concentrating its energy on a small area. The days grow long, the sun climbs high in the sky, and warmth accumulates. This is summer. At the very same moment, the Southern Hemisphere leans away from the sun. There, the identical rays arrive at a low, slanting angle, spreading their energy thinly across a wider patch of ground. Days shorten, and winter settles in.

(4) Six months later the situation reverses. By December, Earth has traveled to the opposite side of its orbit, and now the Southern Hemisphere tilts sunward while the north leans away. Australians enjoy a warm holiday season while families in Ohio bundle against the cold. The tilt itself never changes; what changes is which hemisphere happens to be leaning toward the sun as the planet moves along its orbit.

(5) The angle of sunlight matters more than most people realize. When rays come straight down, they pour their heat onto a compact zone. When they strike at a slant, the same amount of light is smeared across a larger surface, so each spot heats up far less. A flashlight beam demonstrates this neatly. Shine it directly at a wall and you get a bright, tight circle. Tip the flashlight, and the circle stretches into a dim oval. The energy has not increased or decreased; it has simply been spread out.

(6) The length of daylight reinforces this effect. The hemisphere tilted toward the sun experiences longer days, giving the ground more hours to soak up warmth and fewer hours of darkness in which to lose it. The hemisphere tilted away gets brief days and long nights, so heat escapes faster than it can build. Together, the angle of the rays and the duration of daylight account for the dramatic swing between July heat and January chill.

(7) Two moments each year mark the extremes of this cycle. The solstices occur when one hemisphere tilts most directly toward or away from the sun, producing the longest and shortest days. Halfway between them lie the equinoxes, when neither pole leans toward the sun and daylight and darkness are nearly equal across the globe. These predictable turning points have guided farmers, sailors, and calendar makers for thousands of years.

(8) Understanding the tilt corrects a stubborn misconception and reveals something elegant about our world. The seasons are not an accident of a wobbling distance but the orderly result of a fixed lean meeting a steady orbit. Earth's modest 23.5-degree slant, faithfully maintained year after year, is the quiet engine behind every harvest and every change of weather we plan our lives around.

D. By describing a problem and then arguing for a solution that readers should adopt.

passages.title: The Tug of Distant Worlds
passages.text:
(6) Anyone who has spent a full day at the shore has watched the water slowly climb the beach and then, hours later, retreat far out again. These daily risings and fallings of the sea are called the tides, and they are driven by gravity reaching silently across space. Every object in the universe pulls on every other object. The Sun is far more massive than the Moon, but the Moon is much closer to Earth, and for tides, nearness matters more than size. For that reason, it is the Moon, not the Sun, that serves as the main force behind the tides.

(7) The Moon's gravity pulls hardest on whichever side of Earth happens to be facing it, drawing the ocean upward into a bulge. A second bulge forms on the opposite side of the planet, where the Moon's pull on the solid Earth is strongest and the water is, in a sense, left behind. As Earth turns on its axis, most coastlines rotate through these two bulges, which is why so many beaches experience roughly two high tides and two low tides every day.

(8) The Sun shapes the tides as well, though with only about half the pull of the Moon. Sometimes the two bodies work together, and sometimes they work against each other. When the Sun and Moon are lined up—at the new moon and the full moon—their forces combine to produce unusually large tides known as spring tides. When they sit at right angles to each other—at the first- and last-quarter moons—the Sun's pull partly offsets the Moon's, and gentler neap tides are the result. In this way, the same alignments that create the Moon's phases also govern the strength of the tides.

(9) Because the movements of the Moon and the Sun are so steady and predictable, scientists can calculate the height and timing of the tides years before they happen. Sailors, fishers, and engineers rely on these tide tables to guide ships safely into harbor, to plan their work along the coast, and to protect low-lying communities from flooding.

(10) The tides are a daily reminder that even the familiar edge of the sea is shaped by distant worlds—one a quarter of a million miles away, the other some ninety-three million miles beyond it—and that the same quiet motions which brighten the Moon overhead reach all the way down to move entire oceans.

passages.title: Reading the Patterns in the Sky
passages.text:
(1) From Earth, the Moon appears to change shape from one night to the next, narrowing to nothing and then swelling back into a bright, round disk. The Moon itself does not actually change. It produces no light of its own; it shines only because it reflects light from the Sun. At every moment, one half of the Moon faces the Sun and is brightly lit, while the other half remains in darkness. What changes from night to night is how much of that lit half we are able to see from Earth, and that depends entirely on where the Moon sits in its orbit in relation to the Sun.

(2) These shifting views are called the phases of the Moon, and they repeat in a dependable cycle of about 29.5 days. When the Moon passes between Earth and the Sun, its lit side faces away from us, and the Moon seems to disappear—a phase known as the new moon. As the Moon continues along its orbit, a thin sliver of the lit half rotates into view and grows a little larger each night. Astronomers say the Moon is waxing while the visible portion increases and waning while it shrinks. About two weeks after the new moon, Earth lies between the Moon and the Sun, the entire lit half faces us, and we see a full moon.

(3) Because this cycle is so regular, people in ancient times used it to build calendars and to keep track of the seasons long before mechanical clocks were invented. To early farmers and travelers, the sky served as a dependable clock and calendar all its own.

(4) Every so often, the Sun, Earth, and Moon line up almost perfectly, and the result is an eclipse. During a solar eclipse, the new moon passes directly in front of the Sun and casts its shadow onto a narrow band of Earth's surface, briefly turning day into an eerie twilight. During a lunar eclipse, a full moon slips into Earth's shadow, and sunlight bent by our atmosphere can stain the Moon a dim, coppery red.

(5) Eclipses are rare not because the alignments themselves are unusual but because the Moon's orbit is slightly tilted. In most months the Moon passes a little above or below the straight line connecting Earth and the Sun, so no shadow falls and nothing unusual appears. Only when the Moon crosses that line at the precise moment of a new or full moon does an eclipse occur—a striking reminder that the sky's most dramatic events follow the very same geometry that produces its quiet, ordinary phases.

title: The Restless Journey of a Carbon Atom

passages.title: The Restless Journey of a Carbon Atom
passages.text:
(5) A single atom of carbon does not stay in the air for long. Although the atmosphere can seem like a permanent reservoir of gases, its contents are constantly being borrowed, transformed, and returned. Carbon offers the clearest example. As carbon dioxide, it drifts in the atmosphere until a plant pulls it from the air during photosynthesis, locking it into leaves, stems, and roots. In this single step, a gas becomes part of the living biosphere, the layer of Earth made up of every living thing.

(6) The atom's travels do not end there. When an animal eats the plant, the carbon moves into the animal's tissues, and when that animal exhales, some of the carbon returns to the atmosphere as carbon dioxide. Other carbon takes a far slower path. Dead plants and animals may be buried, compressed over millions of years, and locked into the rocky lithosphere as coal, oil, or limestone. Carbon can rest underground for ages, removed from the air entirely until geological forces or human activity release it again. A path that begins in seconds, then, can stretch across an almost unimaginable span of time.

(7) Water bodies form yet another stage of the journey. The oceans, part of the hydrosphere, dissolve enormous quantities of carbon dioxide directly from the air. Some of that carbon is used by tiny marine organisms to build shells; when those organisms die, their shells settle on the seafloor and eventually harden into rock. In this way a gas that began in the atmosphere can be transferred through living things, water, and stone, sometimes within days and sometimes across epochs. Each sphere acts as both a source that releases carbon and a sink that stores it.

(8) Scientists call this endless circulation the carbon cycle, and it links all four of Earth's great spheres into one connected system. The cycle once kept atmospheric carbon dioxide in rough balance, with roughly as much gas entering the air as leaving it. Today, however, the burning of buried fossil fuels returns ancient carbon to the atmosphere far faster than natural processes can withdraw it. Tracing the path of a single atom, then, is more than a curiosity. It reveals why the composition of the air above our heads is tied to forests, oceans, and rocks we may never see.

passages.title: Climbing Into Thinner Air
passages.text:
(1) When mountaineers describe the upper reaches of Mount Everest, they often call it the "death zone." The phrase sounds dramatic, but it points to a real and measurable problem. Above roughly 8,000 meters, the air contains so little oxygen that the human body slowly begins to fail no matter how fit the climber. The atmosphere has not changed its chemistry at that altitude; it is still about 21 percent oxygen, the same proportion found at sea level. What has changed is the pressure pushing those gas molecules together. This single fact surprises many people, who assume that high mountains somehow hold less oxygen in the air itself.

(2) The atmosphere is not a uniform blanket. Scientists divide it into layers, and the lowest layer, the troposphere, is where nearly all weather and breathable air exist. Within the troposphere, air pressure drops steadily with height because there is less air stacked above to press down. At sea level, the weight of the entire atmosphere pushes molecules tightly together, so each breath delivers a generous dose of oxygen. By the summit of Everest, atmospheric pressure has fallen to roughly one-third of its sea-level value. Each lungful pulls in the same percentage of oxygen but only about a third as many actual molecules. The lungs work just as hard, yet they capture far less of the gas the body needs.

(3) This thinning explains why elevation, not temperature alone, governs so much of mountain life. Climbers who ascend too quickly may suffer altitude sickness, a condition marked by headaches, dizziness, and dangerous swelling in the lungs or brain. The remedy is often counterintuitive: descend. Returning to thicker, higher-pressure air allows the body to recover. Experienced expeditions therefore build in days of "acclimatization," pausing at intermediate camps so the body can manufacture additional red blood cells to capture scarce oxygen more efficiently. Rushing the process can turn a manageable climb into an emergency within hours.

(4) Pressure is not the only property that shifts with height. Temperature in the troposphere generally falls about 6.5 degrees Celsius for every kilometer of ascent, which is why snow caps tropical peaks. Higher still, in the stratosphere, temperature unexpectedly rises again because a layer of ozone there absorbs ultraviolet energy from the Sun. These contrasting trends reveal that the atmosphere is a structured system, with each layer behaving according to its own rules. Understanding those rules is a matter of survival for pilots, climbers, and engineers alike, all of whom must respect an environment that grows steadily less forgiving with altitude.

title: The Day the Elements Lined Up

passages.title: The Day the Elements Lined Up
passages.text:
(1) In the middle of the 1800s, chemistry had a tidiness problem. Scientists had identified more than sixty different elements, the pure substances from which everything else is built. They knew that gold was not silver and that oxygen was not iron. Yet the elements sat in laboratories like a pile of unsorted puzzle pieces. There seemed to be no rule explaining why certain elements behaved the way they did, or why some were soft metals while others were choking gases. Chemists could describe each element, but they could not see the bigger picture connecting them.

(2) The breakthrough came from a Russian chemist named Dmitri Mendeleev. In 1869, he decided to arrange the known elements in order of their atomic weight, a measure of how heavy each element's atoms are. As he laid them out, he noticed something remarkable. Certain properties repeated at regular intervals. After a very reactive metal came a series of other elements, and then, predictably, another very reactive metal would appear again. The pattern returned again and again, like a melody that circles back to the same note.

(3) Mendeleev used this repeating pattern, called periodicity, to build a chart now known as the periodic table. He placed elements with similar behaviors into the same vertical columns. Elements in a single column tend to react in similar ways. The metals that fizz violently in water sit together; the gases that almost never react sit together in another column. Suddenly the chaotic pile had structure. An element's location on the table now told a chemist a great deal about how it would act.

(4) The most daring part of Mendeleev's work was what he did with the empty spaces. In some places, the pattern of properties did not line up with any known element. Rather than force the wrong element into a slot, Mendeleev left gaps. He insisted that these blanks were not mistakes but signals. He predicted that elements with specific properties were simply yet to be found, and he even described the weight and behavior the missing elements should have.

(5) For a time, some scientists doubted him. It seemed bold to claim that substances no one had ever seen must exist, based only on the holes in a chart. But within fifteen years, chemists discovered several of the missing elements, including one that filled a gap almost exactly where Mendeleev had pointed. Their measured properties matched Mendeleev's predictions with startling accuracy. The empty gaps had been filled exactly as the pattern demanded.

(6) Today the periodic table hangs in nearly every science classroom, often dismissed as something to memorize. Yet it represents one of the great organizing ideas in science. By sorting elements according to their repeating properties rather than treating each as an isolated mystery, Mendeleev turned a confusing collection into a map. That map did more than describe the world that was known; it pointed confidently toward the parts that were not yet discovered.

passages.title: The Sealed Jar of Lavoisier
passages.text:
(1) In the 1770s, a French chemist named Antoine Lavoisier set out to answer a question that had puzzled thinkers for centuries: when something burns or rusts, where does the matter go? Many believed that burning released a mysterious substance and left less material behind. Lavoisier suspected that nothing was truly lost. To test his idea, he relied on one of the most powerful tools in science, the balance, and on a deceptively simple setup: a sealed glass container.

(2) A sealed container creates what scientists call a closed system, a space that allows energy to pass in or out but prevents matter from entering or escaping. This boundary is the heart of Lavoisier's reasoning. If matter could neither leak out nor sneak in, then any change happening inside the jar, whether burning, dissolving, or rusting, had to involve only the atoms already present. The total amount of stuff could not change, even if its appearance did.

(3) In one famous experiment, Lavoisier heated a metal inside a sealed flask containing air. The metal slowly transformed into a crumbly, powdery substance, a chemical change we now recognize as the metal combining with oxygen. Before and after heating, he weighed the entire sealed flask, contents and all. The reading on the balance did not budge. The metal had clearly changed, yet the mass of the closed system stayed exactly the same.

(4) The explanation lies in the atoms themselves. Atoms are not created or destroyed during an ordinary chemical reaction; they are merely rearranged into new combinations. When the metal reacted, oxygen atoms from the air bonded to metal atoms, forming the powder. The oxygen had not vanished into nothing; it had simply moved from the air into the solid. Because every atom was still accounted for inside the sealed flask, the mass remained constant.

(5) This principle became known as the conservation of mass, and it transformed chemistry from guesswork into a precise science. It explains a result that can seem baffling at first. When a log burns in an open fireplace, the ashes left behind weigh far less than the original wood, which once tempted people to conclude that matter was destroyed. But the fireplace is not a closed system. The missing mass escaped as gases and water vapor rising invisibly up the chimney. Capture every one of those gases, and the total mass would not have shrunk at all.

(6) Modern chemists still depend on this idea every day. When they balance a chemical equation, they are essentially keeping the same careful books Lavoisier kept with his balance, making sure the number and types of atoms on each side match exactly. Two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom cannot become anything other than two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom; they can only be rearranged into new arrangements. A single sealed jar, weighed with patience and care, revealed a rule that governs every chemical reaction in the universe.

passages.title: Why Rust Weighs More
passages.text:
(6) Lavoisier worked behind sealed glass, but the same law plays out every day in the open air, where the results can be far more puzzling. Consider a shiny steel tool left outside in a damp yard. Over weeks it grows a flaky orange coating of rust. Surprisingly, if you could weigh the rusted tool against its original clean weight, the rusted version would actually be heavier. It seems impossible for an object to gain weight just by sitting still in the grass.

(7) The explanation lies in the atoms drifting through the surrounding air. Rust forms when iron atoms in the steel bond with oxygen atoms from the atmosphere, creating a new compound called iron oxide. Those oxygen atoms were always present in the air, but they were invisible to us. As they attach themselves to the iron, they add their own mass to the tool. No atoms appear from nowhere—the oxygen simply moves from the air onto the metal and stays there.

(8) Burning shows the opposite effect, which once fooled people into thinking matter could be destroyed. When a candle burns, its wax does not vanish; the wax atoms combine with oxygen and escape as carbon dioxide and water vapor. In an open room, these gases float away unseen, so the candle appears to shrink and lose mass. The atoms have not been destroyed—they have simply left the scene as invisible gas and spread out into the air around the flame.

(9) Scientists can prove all of this by recreating Lavoisier's trick with a seal. If you burn the candle inside a sealed jar and weigh the entire jar, including all of the gases trapped inside, the total mass stays exactly the same. The same is true if iron rusts inside a sealed container: the mass lost by the air equals the mass gained by the metal. Only when a system is open, letting gases slip in or out, can matter seem to come or go.

(10) The lesson connects the laboratory to the backyard. Whether atoms are rearranging inside a chemist's flask or on a rusting wrench in the yard, their number and type never change in a closed system. Within a closed system, every atom can be accounted for from beginning to end. What looks like creation or destruction is really just atoms traveling between substances—and between the visible and the invisible. Once you learn to count atoms instead of trusting appearances, the vanishing log and the heavier wrench stop being mysteries at all.

passages.title: The Chemist Who Weighed the Invisible
passages.text:
(1) In the late 1700s, most people believed that matter could simply vanish. When a log burned down to a small heap of ash, it seemed obvious that the wood had been destroyed. Where had all the rest of it gone? A French chemist named Antoine Lavoisier refused to accept the easy answer. He suspected that nothing truly disappeared during a chemical change—instead, the atoms only moved into forms that the eye could not see.

(2) To test this idea, Lavoisier became devoted to the balance, an instrument that measures mass with great precision. He reasoned that if matter were truly being destroyed, then a sealed container should weigh less after a reaction than it had before. If it weighed the same, then nothing could have escaped or been lost. But to prove anything, he first had to make sure that no substance could secretly enter or leave during the experiment.

(3) His solution was to work inside a closed system—a space sealed off so that no matter could pass in or out. In one famous trial, he heated a metal inside a sealed glass flask that held a fixed amount of air. As the metal grew hot, it gained a crusty coating, and a portion of the air inside was used up. Yet when Lavoisier placed the entire sealed flask on his balance, the total mass had not changed at all. The reading was exactly the same as before he began.

(4) The result was striking. The metal had gained weight, and the air had lost weight, but together they balanced perfectly. Lavoisier concluded that the metal had combined with part of the air rather than creating or destroying any material. Because his apparatus was sealed so that no gas could sneak in or escape, his measurement was convincing—the atoms had not vanished but had merely rearranged themselves into a new arrangement.

(5) From experiments like these, Lavoisier stated what is now called the law of conservation of mass: in a chemical reaction, matter is neither created nor destroyed. The total number and types of atoms present at the start are exactly the same at the end. They may bond together in new ways, forming new substances with new properties, but not a single atom disappears. To Lavoisier, the steady reading on the balance was the clearest evidence of all. By trusting his balance over his eyes, he had measured something invisible and changed chemistry forever.

questions.text:

In the sentence “Because his apparatus was sealed so that no gas could sneak in or escape, his measurement was convincing,” the word “convincing” most nearly means (   ).

passages.title: Catching Energy Before It Escapes
passages.text:
(6) Engineers have long understood that energy cannot be destroyed, and they have turned that knowledge into a practical mission: capturing energy that would otherwise scatter uselessly into the surroundings. The technical name for this pursuit is energy recovery, and it is quietly reshaping the machines that move us. Nowhere is the idea more visible than in the regenerative braking systems found in modern electric and hybrid vehicles.

(7) In an ordinary car, stopping wastes a tremendous amount of energy. Pressing the brake pedal clamps friction pads against the spinning wheels, and the car's kinetic energy is converted into heat. That heat radiates off the hot brakes and vanishes into the air, gone for good. A car that has just burned fuel to reach highway speed throws all of that hard-won motion away every time it slows for a traffic light.

(8) Regenerative braking refuses to accept that loss. Instead of relying only on friction, it runs the electric motor backward, turning it into a generator. As the wheels spin the generator, the vehicle's kinetic energy is transformed into electrical energy rather than heat. That electricity flows back into the battery, where it is stored as chemical energy until the driver accelerates again. The very motion that would have been wasted is recaptured and reused, extending the distance the car can travel on a single charge.

(9) The benefits are real, but they are not unlimited, and honest engineers are quick to point out the catch. No recovery system is perfect, because every transformation surrenders some energy to heat and friction. A typical regenerative system reclaims perhaps half to two-thirds of the braking energy, never all of it. Furthermore, the system works best during the gentle, gradual slowing of city driving; in a sudden emergency stop, friction brakes must still do most of the work, and that energy is lost as before.

(10) Even so, the principle points toward a future of smarter design. Elevators that send power back to the grid as they descend, factories that capture waste heat to warm their offices, and rail systems that share braking energy between trains all rest on the same insight. Because energy is never truly lost, only moved and transformed, the engineer's task is not to create energy from nothing but to guide it, again and again, toward useful work before it slips away into the warmth of the surrounding world.

passages.title: The Long Journey of a Single Calorie
passages.text:
(1) Imagine following a single packet of energy as it travels through the world. The story begins ninety-three million miles away, deep inside the sun, where nuclear reactions release staggering amounts of radiant energy. After a journey of about eight minutes, a sliver of that sunlight strikes the broad green leaf of a cornstalk in an Ohio field. In that instant, the energy does not vanish, nor is it created anew. It is transformed. Through photosynthesis, the plant captures the light and locks it away inside the chemical bonds of sugar molecules. What had been radiant energy is now stored chemical energy, waiting patiently inside the kernel of corn.

(2) This transformation illustrates one of the most powerful ideas in all of science: the law of conservation of energy. The law states that energy can be neither created nor destroyed, only changed from one form into another or moved from one object to another. Physicists call the total amount of energy in a closed system constant. A flashlight battery, a roaring campfire, a sprinting athlete, a falling boulder. In every case, the energy present at the end exactly equals the energy present at the beginning, even though its form may be utterly different.

(3) Return now to our kernel of corn. A farmer harvests it, and eventually a person eats it for breakfast. The chemical energy stored in the sugar is released during digestion and rebuilt into other molecules the body can use. When that person climbs a flight of stairs, the chemical energy is transformed yet again, this time into the energy of motion, what scientists call kinetic energy. At the top of the staircase, having risen against gravity, the climber now possesses a store of potential energy, simply because of his elevated position.

(4) Energy can also be transferred without changing form at all. When a warm hand grips a cold metal railing, heat flows directly from the skin into the metal until both reach the same temperature. This kind of transfer, called conduction, happens because faster-moving particles collide with slower ones, passing along their motion. Energy moves through space in other ways too. The warmth you feel standing near a bonfire arrives as radiation, while the rising column of hot air above the flames carries energy upward through convection.

(5) Yet a puzzle remains. If energy is never lost, why do machines run down and batteries die? The answer is subtle but important. Energy is not destroyed; it is dispersed. Every transformation scatters a portion of the energy as low-grade heat that spreads into the surrounding air and cannot easily be gathered back. The total quantity is unchanged, but its usefulness diminishes. Scientists say the energy has become less concentrated and therefore less able to do work. The calorie that began its life in the heart of the sun is still out there somewhere, mingled invisibly with the warmth of the world.

questions.text:
Which sentence best states a central idea developed across Passage 1?
A. Energy is constantly changing form and moving between objects, but the total amount always stays the same.   [CORRECT]
B. Sunlight takes about eight minutes to travel from the sun to a field in Ohio.
C. Plants are the only living things capable of capturing energy from the sun.
D. Heat always flows from a warmer object into a colder object until they match.

D. Both passages reject the law of conservation of energy as outdated.

passages.title: The Salmon Forest
passages.text:
(1) Along the rivers of the Pacific Northwest, some of the tallest, healthiest trees in the world rise from the soil. For decades, scientists assumed these towering forests grew simply because of rain, sunlight, and rich earth. The truth turned out to be far stranger. A large share of the nutrients feeding these inland trees does not come from the land at all. It comes from the ocean, carried upstream inside the bodies of fish.

(2) Each year, millions of salmon hatch in freshwater streams, swim out to sea, and spend years feeding and growing in the open ocean. During that time, their bodies absorb nitrogen and other nutrients from marine food sources. When the salmon are fully grown, they return to the streams where they were born in order to lay their eggs. After spawning, most of them die. Their bodies, packed with ocean-derived nutrients, pile up along the riverbanks.

(3) This is where the forest enters the story. Bears, eagles, and other predators gather at the rivers to feast on the returning fish. A single bear may catch many salmon in a day, dragging some of them far from the water before eating only the richest parts. The leftover carcasses are scattered across the forest floor, sometimes far from the stream. In this way, the nutrients are ferried inland on the backs of predators, moving from the river into the deep woods.

(4) Once the carcasses are abandoned, decomposers take over. Insects, fungi, and bacteria break down the remains, and the nitrogen locked inside is released into the soil. Tree roots absorb this nitrogen and use it to build leaves, wood, and seeds. Scientists can actually measure this hidden gift: the nitrogen found in ocean fish carries a distinct chemical signature, and that same signature appears in the trunks of streamside trees. By studying tree rings, researchers can even tell which years had strong salmon runs.

(5) The benefits ripple outward through the whole web of life. Healthier trees shade the streams and keep the water cool, which is exactly the condition young salmon need to survive. Fallen leaves feed insects, and those insects feed the next generation of fish. Birds and small mammals grow more numerous where salmon are abundant. A single nitrogen atom from the ocean might pass through a fish, a bear, a beetle, a tree, and a fallen leaf before it ever returns to the soil or the water.

(6) This cycle reveals a basic truth about living systems: matter is transferred from one organism to another and between organisms and their surroundings, rather than created or destroyed within the ecosystem. Energy captured in the ocean travels far inland to power the growth of a forest. The salmon do not simply live in the ecosystem. In a very real sense, they help build it, knitting the sea and the forest into a single connected world.

questions.text:
According to the passage, how do nutrients from salmon end up inside the trees of the forest?

B. Trees drop seeds into the river, which feed the salmon and return nutrients to the land.
C. Predators carry salmon carcasses inland, decomposers release the nitrogen into the soil, and tree roots absorb it.   [CORRECT]
D. Insects fly out to the ocean, collect nitrogen from the fish, and deposit it on the forest floor.

In paragraph 3, the sentence "the nutrients are ferried inland on the backs of predators" uses the word "ferried" to mean that the nutrients are (   ).

D. Bears in the Pacific Northwest eat only salmon and nothing else.
E. Scientists strongly disagree about whether salmon affect forests.

The author organizes the middle of the passage mainly (   ).

passages.title: The Tiny Factories Inside You
passages.text:
(1) Every living thing, from a towering redwood to the bacteria in a puddle, is built from cells. A cell is the smallest unit that can be considered alive, and yet a single one is astonishingly busy. Inside its microscopic boundaries, a cell behaves like a tightly run factory, with specialized parts carrying out specific jobs that, taken together, keep the organism alive. Biologists call these internal parts organelles, a word that means "little organs."

(2) Consider the cell's command center, the nucleus. It stores the cell's DNA, the coded instructions that tell the cell how to build everything it needs. When a cell must manufacture a particular protein, the nucleus does not hand over its only copy of the instructions. Instead, it sends out a working copy, much as a factory manager might photocopy a blueprint rather than risk losing the original. This careful system protects the cell's most valuable information while still allowing daily work to proceed.

(3) Once those instructions leave the nucleus, they travel to the ribosomes, the structures responsible for assembling proteins. Proteins are the workhorses of the cell; they build tissues, speed up chemical reactions, and carry messages between distant parts of the body. A cell that is producing large amounts of protein, such as a cell in your pancreas, may contain millions of ribosomes laboring at once. The pace is relentless, because proteins wear out and must be replaced constantly.

(4) None of this activity could continue without energy, and supplying that energy is the duty of the mitochondria. These bean-shaped organelles break down sugar from the food you eat and convert it into a usable fuel called ATP. Cells with heavy energy demands hold the most mitochondria: a single muscle cell may pack in thousands of them. This explains why muscles that work hard need a steady supply of fuel, for the mitochondria must labor constantly to meet the demand.

(5) Surrounding and protecting all of these structures is the cell membrane, a thin, flexible barrier that controls what enters and leaves. The membrane is not a solid wall. Rather, it is selectively permeable, meaning it admits useful substances like oxygen and nutrients while blocking or expelling harmful ones. Tiny gates and pumps embedded in the membrane decide, moment by moment, which molecules may pass. Without this gatekeeping, a cell would quickly be poisoned or starved.

(6) What makes the cell remarkable is not any single organelle but the way they cooperate. The nucleus issues instructions, the ribosomes build, the mitochondria power the work, and the membrane guards the whole operation. Damage to even one part can throw the entire system into chaos. When scientists study diseases, they often trace the trouble back to a malfunctioning organelle, because a factory cannot run when one of its essential departments breaks down.

(7) The next time you climb a flight of stairs or simply take a breath, remember that trillions of these tiny factories are working in perfect coordination inside you. Each one is far too small to see, yet each performs its specific function faithfully. Life itself, scientists remind us, is the sum of countless cells quietly doing their jobs.

questions.text:

B. The nucleus is the single most important organelle because it controls every other part of the cell.

E. The mitochondria store the cell's DNA and send out working copies of its instructions.

passages.title: Nothing Is Wasted
passages.text:
(6) Energy flows in one direction through an ecosystem, arriving as sunlight and departing as heat, never to return. Matter, however, tells a different story. The atoms that make up a living body, carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, and the rest, are not consumed and lost. They are borrowed, used, and eventually returned, cycling endlessly between organisms and the physical world. While the ten percent rule describes loss, the cycling of matter describes recovery, and the organisms responsible for that recovery are among the most overlooked creatures on the planet.

(7) When a leaf falls or an animal dies, its body does not simply pile up. An army of decomposers, fungi, bacteria, and small invertebrates, sets to work breaking the dead material into ever smaller pieces. As they feed, decomposers release the locked-up nutrients back into the soil and air. Nitrogen returns to forms that plant roots can absorb. Carbon rejoins the atmosphere or the earth. Without this constant dismantling, the planet's supply of essential elements would remain trapped inside dead tissue, and new growth would eventually grind to a halt.

(8) Consider a single nitrogen atom. It might be pulled from the soil by a wheat plant, baked into a grain, and eaten by a mouse. When the mouse dies, fungi reclaim the atom and release it to the soil, where another plant takes it up. The same atom may pass through dozens of organisms across centuries, never wearing out. Matter, unlike energy, is endlessly reusable, which is why ecologists speak of nutrient cycles rather than nutrient chains. A chain has an end; a cycle does not.

(9) Decomposers are easy to ignore because their work is quiet and often unpleasant to picture. Yet they are arguably the most indispensable members of any ecosystem. A forest can survive the loss of its largest predator, but it cannot survive the loss of the organisms that recycle its dead. Some scientists argue that decomposers deserve as much attention and protection as the charismatic animals that dominate nature documentaries, precisely because their role, though invisible, holds the entire system together.

(10) Together, the one-way flow of energy and the round-trip cycling of matter form the twin engines of every ecosystem. Energy must be replaced each day by the rising sun. Matter, by contrast, is conserved and reused, threaded through living and nonliving things in loops that have turned for billions of years. To understand life on Earth is to understand both the river of energy that runs downhill and the wheel of matter that never stops turning.

passages.title: The Ten Percent Rule
passages.text:
(1) Every meal an animal eats is, in a sense, borrowed sunlight. Energy enters nearly every ecosystem on Earth when green plants capture light and convert it into sugars through photosynthesis. Because plants manufacture their own food, ecologists call them producers, and they form the broad base of what scientists describe as a food chain. When a grasshopper chews a blade of grass, some of the energy stored in that plant passes into the insect. When a sparrow swallows the grasshopper, a portion of that energy moves again. Each step in this transfer is called a trophic level, and the journey of energy through these levels follows a rule that surprises many students: at every step, most of the energy disappears.

(2) Ecologists summarize this pattern as the ten percent rule. On average, only about ten percent of the energy stored at one trophic level becomes available to the level above it. The remaining ninety percent is not destroyed, because energy can never simply vanish. Instead, organisms spend it. A deer uses energy to move, to keep warm, to digest food, and to grow muscle that a predator cannot eat. A great deal of it leaves the body as heat, radiating into the surrounding air. By the time a wolf finishes a meal, only a small fraction of the grass's original energy remains locked in tissue the wolf can actually use.

(3) This steep loss explains a feature of ecosystems that might otherwise seem puzzling. Why are top predators so rare? A meadow may support millions of grass plants, thousands of grasshoppers, hundreds of sparrows, and perhaps a single hawk. Because so little energy reaches the upper levels, those levels can sustain only a thin population. Scientists often picture this arrangement as an energy pyramid, wide at the bottom where producers are abundant and narrow at the top where large carnivores stand. The pyramid is not merely a tidy diagram; it reflects an unbending limit set by the physics of energy transfer.

(4) The ten percent rule also reveals why long food chains are uncommon. If each link passes along only a tenth of what it received, the energy available after five or six steps becomes vanishingly small. A chain that began with a vast field of grain might, after several transfers, leave barely enough energy to support one more predator. This is why most food chains in nature contain only four or five links. The rule does not forbid longer chains, but it makes them fragile and difficult to maintain.

(5) Understanding these transfers carries practical weight. Farmers, conservationists, and even city planners use the logic of trophic levels to predict how changes ripple through an ecosystem. Remove the producers, and everything above them starves. Protect them, and the entire pyramid gains a sturdier foundation. The flow of energy, once invisible, becomes a map for understanding why life is distributed the way it is.

questions.text:

passages.title: The Gatekeepers of the Cell
passages.text:
(5) If mitochondria are the power plants that keep a cell running, then the cell membrane is the wall and gate that decides what enters and leaves. Every cell is wrapped in this thin, flexible boundary. Without it, the contents of a cell would simply spill into its surroundings, and the careful chemistry of life would grind to a halt. Yet the membrane is far more than a passive barrier. It is a busy checkpoint that controls the flow of materials with remarkable precision.

(6) The cell membrane is described as selectively permeable, a phrase that means it permits some substances to pass while blocking others. Small molecules such as oxygen and water can often slip through on their own, drifting from areas where they are crowded to areas where they are sparse. This effortless movement is called diffusion, and it costs the cell no energy at all. Larger or more demanding deliveries, however, require active transport. In active transport, the membrane uses energy, supplied as ATP, to pump substances across the boundary, sometimes even forcing them from an area of low concentration to one of high concentration. This ability to move materials against the natural flow is one of the membrane's most powerful tools, because it lets a cell gather nutrients that would otherwise drift away.

(7) Embedded throughout the membrane are thousands of proteins, and these proteins do much of the heavy lifting. Some act as channels, forming narrow tunnels that allow specific particles to flow through. Others behave like locks that open only when the correct molecule arrives, ensuring that the wrong substances stay out. Still others serve as identification tags, displaying markers that let the body recognize the cell as its own. This last job matters a great deal, since it helps the immune system tell the body's own cells apart from invaders that do not belong. A cell stripped of these proteins would be nearly helpless, unable to feed itself or communicate with its neighbors.

(8) The importance of the membrane becomes clear when it fails. If a cell cannot regulate what crosses its boundary, it may swell with too much water and burst, or starve because nutrients never reach its interior. For this reason, the membrane and the mitochondria depend on each other in an elegant loop. The membrane admits the oxygen and sugar that mitochondria need, and the mitochondria supply the ATP that powers the membrane's pumps. Neither structure could sustain life alone. Together, they show how the separate parts of a single cell cooperate to keep an organism alive.

passages.title: The Cell's Power Plants
passages.text:
(1) Every living thing, from a towering redwood to a sprinting cheetah, depends on a constant supply of energy. That energy does not appear out of nowhere. Inside nearly every cell of your body, tiny structures called mitochondria work around the clock to convert the food you eat into a usable form of fuel. Scientists often describe mitochondria as the "power plants" of the cell, and the comparison is fitting. Just as a power plant burns coal or natural gas to generate electricity for a city, a mitochondrion breaks down nutrients to generate energy for the cell.

(2) The fuel that mitochondria produce is a molecule called ATP, short for adenosine triphosphate. When a cell needs to do work, whether contracting a muscle or sending a nerve signal, it spends ATP the way a person spends money. The process that makes ATP is called cellular respiration. During respiration, mitochondria combine sugar molecules with oxygen and, through a series of carefully controlled chemical reactions, release the energy stored in that sugar. The leftover materials, carbon dioxide and water, are then removed from the cell as waste. Because ATP cannot be stockpiled in large amounts, mitochondria must keep making it almost continuously, refilling the cell's supply nearly as fast as the cell uses it up.

(3) Not every cell contains the same number of mitochondria, and that variation reveals something important about how cells specialize. A skin cell, which does relatively little work, may hold only a few hundred mitochondria. A heart muscle cell, which must contract tens of thousands of times each day without rest, can contain several thousand. The pattern is consistent: the harder a cell must labor, the more power plants it tends to carry. Cells can even build new mitochondria when their energy demands rise, which is one reason regular exercise gradually increases the number of mitochondria in muscle cells. In this way, the structure of a cell matches the job it is built to perform.

(4) Perhaps the most surprising fact about mitochondria is their origin. They contain their own small loop of DNA, separate from the DNA stored in the cell's nucleus. Many scientists believe this is a clue to their distant past. According to a widely accepted theory, mitochondria were once free-living bacteria that were absorbed by a larger cell billions of years ago. Rather than being digested, the bacteria survived inside their host and began producing energy in exchange for shelter and nutrients. Over countless generations, the two became inseparable partners. The mitochondria humming inside your cells today are, in a sense, the descendants of an ancient bargain.

questions.text:

title: The Empire That Could Not Cross the Sea

passages.title: The Empire That Could Not Cross the Sea
passages.text:
(1) In the early 1200s, a leader named Temujin united the scattered nomadic tribes of the Mongolian steppe under a single banner. Taking the title Genghis Khan, he forged a disciplined cavalry army that would soon sweep across Asia. Mongol warriors were skilled riders who could shoot arrows from horseback and travel great distances quickly. Within a few decades, the Mongols controlled the largest connected land empire the world had ever seen, stretching from the Pacific coast of Asia toward the edges of Europe. Their conquests reshaped the political map of an entire continent.

(2) The Mongol advance had consequences that lasted long after the fighting stopped. In China, the Mongols toppled rival dynasties and, under Genghis Khan's grandson Kublai Khan, established the Yuan dynasty in 1271. For the first time in centuries, all of China was governed by one ruler. The Korean kingdom of Goryeo, after years of resistance, also fell under Mongol influence and was bound to the empire through tribute and royal marriage. In both lands, the chaos of competing states gave way to a single, unified authority.

(3) Unification brought changes beyond politics. Because the Mongols controlled such a vast territory, merchants could travel the trade routes of the Silk Road with greater safety than before. Goods, ideas, and technologies moved between East and West along roads patrolled by Mongol soldiers. This period of relative stability is sometimes called the Pax Mongolica, or Mongol Peace, and it linked distant civilizations in ways that had not been possible while the region was divided.

(4) Kublai Khan, however, was not satisfied with ruling the mainland. He set his sights on Japan, the island nation lying across the sea to the east. In 1274 and again in 1281, he launched enormous invasion fleets carrying tens of thousands of soldiers. Both times, the Mongol ships were battered by violent storms that wrecked the fleets and drowned much of the invading force. The Japanese called these storms kamikaze, meaning divine wind, believing the gods had protected their islands.

(5) The failed invasions had a lasting effect on Japan's development. Because the Mongols never conquered the islands, Japan was not absorbed into a unified empire as China and Korea had been. Instead, the Japanese feudal system continued, with power divided among warrior lords and their samurai followers rather than concentrated in a single foreign ruler. The shogun, a military leader, governed in the emperor's name, and regional lords maintained their own armies and lands.

(6) The contrast is striking. On the mainland, Mongol conquest erased old divisions and produced unified states governed from a single center. On the islands of Japan, the sea and the storms turned back the invaders, and the older, decentralized order survived. Geography, in the end, shaped the political destinies of these neighboring societies as surely as armies did. The same empire that unified a continent could not cross a narrow stretch of water.

questions.text:

E. Regional lords in Japan maintained their own armies and lands.

A. steady and secure   [CORRECT]
B. wealthy and powerful
C. crowded and busy
D. modern and advanced

passages.title: Crossroads of the Monsoon Seas
passages.text:
(5) Far to the east, on the southwestern coast of the Malay Peninsula in Southeast Asia, a different kind of trading center flourished. The city of Malacca, founded around the year 1400, did not look out over deserts and rivers but over a narrow stretch of sea known as the Strait of Malacca. Through this strait passed nearly all the ships traveling between the Indian Ocean and the waters of China, making it one of the busiest maritime passages on Earth. A city that controlled this narrow gateway could shape the flow of goods between two of the world's great markets.

(6) Malacca's prosperity depended on the rhythm of the monsoon winds. These seasonal winds blew from one direction for half the year and reversed for the other half, carrying sailing ships eastward and then westward. Merchants from India, Arabia, and beyond rode the winds to Malacca, then waited for the winds to change before continuing their voyages or sailing home. Because ships had to pause in the city for weeks or months, Malacca grew into a great entrepot, a port where goods were unloaded, stored, taxed, and shipped onward to distant markets. While they waited, the visiting merchants bought, sold, and traded, filling the city's warehouses and markets with goods from across the seas.

(7) The goods that passed through Malacca were as varied as the people who traded them. From the islands to the east came precious spices such as cloves, nutmeg, and pepper, prized in kitchens from China to Europe. From China came silk and fine porcelain; from India came cotton cloth; from the surrounding region came tin, gold, and forest products. The rulers of Malacca grew wealthy by keeping the peace, offering safe harbor, and collecting fair taxes, which encouraged merchants to choose their port over rivals. By treating traders fairly, the rulers made their city a place where merchants felt safe to do business.

(8) As traders from many lands gathered in its harbor, Malacca became a meeting place of cultures as much as of goods. Merchants speaking dozens of languages settled in the city, and Malay developed into a common trading tongue understood across the region. New faiths, foods, and customs arrived with each fleet and mingled in the markets. Like Timbuktu in the west, Malacca showed how a single well-placed city could turn the movement of trade into lasting wealth and a rich blending of the world's peoples.

passages.title: The Golden City of the Sands
passages.text:
(1) In the fourteenth century, a traveler crossing the vast Sahara Desert in West Africa would have spent weeks among endless dunes before reaching a remarkable destination. Near the southern edge of the desert, where the sands gave way to the wide bend of the Niger River, stood the city of Timbuktu. To merchants and scholars across the medieval world, Timbuktu was a place of legendary wealth and learning, a city that seemed to rise out of the desert like a mirage made real. Stories of its riches traveled so far that, in distant lands, some people doubted such a place could truly exist.

(2) Timbuktu owed its rise to its position along the trans-Saharan trade routes. For centuries, long caravans of camels carried goods across the desert, linking the kingdoms of West Africa with the markets of North Africa and the Mediterranean. A single caravan might include hundreds or even thousands of camels, moving slowly from one oasis to the next. The two most valuable goods traded along these routes were gold and salt. West Africa held some of the richest gold deposits in the world, while the desert supplied great slabs of salt, which people needed to preserve food and stay healthy. In some places, traders exchanged salt for gold by weight, so precious was it in the lands to the south. At Timbuktu, these caravans met the river traffic of the Niger, making the city a crucial hub where the desert and the river economies converged.

(3) The wealth that flowed through Timbuktu reached its height under the Mali Empire and its famous ruler, Mansa Musa. In 1324, Mansa Musa made a pilgrimage to Mecca, traveling with an enormous procession and distributing so much gold along the way that he became known across Europe and the Middle East. His journey put Mali, and Timbuktu, on the maps drawn by distant cartographers. When he returned, he invested in the city's growth, sponsoring the construction of mosques and schools built from sun-dried mud brick. Skilled builders and teachers were invited to settle there, and the city's reputation as a center of faith and study grew alongside its markets.

(4) Yet Timbuktu was never famous for gold alone. The riches of trade attracted scholars, and the city became one of the great intellectual centers of its age. Its university and libraries held tens of thousands of manuscripts on subjects ranging from astronomy and mathematics to law and medicine. Students traveled from across Africa to study with its respected teachers, and the trade in books was said to be among the most profitable businesses in the city. In Timbuktu, the gold of commerce was matched by the gold of knowledge, and the two enriched each other for generations.

questions.text:
Which statement best expresses the central idea of "The Golden City of the Sands" (Passage 1)?

B. Timbuktu was admired mainly because its mosques and schools were built from sun-dried mud brick.
C. Timbuktu became famous only after Mansa Musa made his pilgrimage to Mecca in 1324.
D. Timbuktu mattered chiefly because the desert supplied the salt that people needed to preserve food.

B. factory where raw materials are manufactured into finished goods
