title: Roads That Carried More Than Cargo

passages.title: Roads That Carried More Than Cargo
passages.text:
(1) For more than a thousand years, a vast web of trade routes stretched across the Eastern Hemisphere, linking the markets of Africa, Europe, and Asia. The most famous of these networks is often called the Silk Road, though it was never a single paved highway. Instead, it was a shifting bundle of caravan tracks, mountain passes, desert crossings, and sea lanes. Merchants rarely traveled the entire distance themselves. Goods passed from trader to trader, moving slowly from one city to the next, until a bolt of Chinese silk might finally be sold in a Roman market thousands of miles from where it was woven.

(2) The goods that traveled these roads were valuable and varied. From China came silk and porcelain. From India and Southeast Asia came pepper, cinnamon, and other spices prized in distant kitchens. East African ports shipped gold, ivory, and timber, while Arabian traders supplied incense. Because these items were small, light, and worth enormous sums, they could survive the long, costly journey and still earn a profit. Cities positioned along the routes, such as Samarkand and the oasis towns ringing the deserts of Central Asia, grew wealthy simply by hosting the traders who passed through.

(3) Yet the most lasting cargo was never loaded onto a single camel. Wherever merchants and travelers went, ideas went with them. Knowledge was transmitted along the same roads as silk and spices, reaching new lands far from where it began. The technology of paper-making, for example, developed in China and slowly moved westward over the routes until it transformed how people in the Islamic world and later Europe recorded and shared information. Mathematical ideas, methods of navigation, and new crops traveled in the same quiet way, carried in the minds and saddlebags of people seeking profit.

(4) Religion may have been the most powerful idea to spread this way. Buddhism traveled out of India along the eastern routes, and Buddhist monasteries appeared in the oasis towns of Central Asia, offering shelter and worship to weary travelers. Centuries later, Islam spread westward across North Africa and eastward toward Asia, often following the very same trading paths. Christianity, Hinduism, and Judaism also moved along these corridors as communities of believers settled in foreign cities to trade. Remarkably, these faiths spread not through armies or conquest but through ordinary contact, as people exchanged beliefs along with their bargains.

(5) The people most responsible for this exchange were not generals but merchants. A trader carrying gold or cloth might also carry a prayer, a recipe, a song, or a new way of solving a problem. When he stopped for the night in a distant town, he shared more than the contents of his packs. Over generations, these countless small encounters reshaped the Eastern Hemisphere, blending cultures that had once been strangers to one another.

(6) By the time these routes faded in importance, the world they had connected was permanently changed. Languages had borrowed new words, kitchens had adopted new flavors, and millions of people practiced religions that had been born in lands they would never see. The roads had carried far more than cargo. They had carried the building blocks of a shared and connected world.

questions.text:

B. It compares two separate trade routes side by side to show which one was more important to the ancient world.
C. It presents a problem faced by ancient merchants and then offers a step-by-step solution to that problem.
D. It lists definitions of key trade terms in alphabetical order so readers can learn new vocabulary.

E. The trade routes eventually put a permanent end to all trade between regions.

passages.title: Riding the Monsoon Winds
passages.text:
(1) Long before engines powered ships across the seas, sailors crossing the Indian Ocean relied on something invisible: the wind. Twice each year, enormous wind systems called monsoons sweep across the ocean and reverse their direction. From roughly November to March, the monsoons blow steadily from the northeast, pushing vessels southwest toward Africa. From April to September, the winds swing around and blow from the southwest, carrying ships back toward India and Arabia. Because this pattern repeated so reliably, merchants could plan ocean voyages the way a traveler today might plan around a train schedule.

(2) This predictability transformed the Indian Ocean into one of the busiest trade networks in the ancient and medieval world. Linking the coasts of Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, India, and Southeast Asia, the network moved an astonishing variety of goods. Gold, ivory, and timber flowed out of East Africa; cotton cloth and gemstones came from India; spices such as cloves and pepper traveled from the islands of Southeast Asia; and porcelain and silk arrived from China. A single coastal market might display products gathered from three continents.

(3) Among the great beneficiaries of this trade was the Swahili coast, a string of port cities stretching down the eastern edge of Africa. Towns such as Mombasa, Zanzibar, and Kilwa were perfectly positioned in the path of the monsoon winds. Ships arriving on the northeast monsoon would unload their cargo, wait out the weeks until the winds reversed, and then sail home loaded with African goods. During those waiting periods, foreign merchants lived among local families, and over generations the two groups blended together.

(4) That blending produced something entirely new. Beginning around the eighth century, traders from Arabia and Persia settled along the African coast, married into Bantu-speaking communities, and shared their languages, beliefs, and customs. Out of this mixing emerged the Swahili civilization, with its own distinct language. The word Swahili itself comes from an Arabic term meaning "of the coast," a fitting name for a culture born where the ocean met the land.

(5) The monsoon network carried far more than cargo. Ideas, technologies, and religions traveled along the same routes. Islam, in particular, spread widely through merchant communities. Because traders were respected and prosperous, the faith they practiced gained influence wherever they settled, and many coastal rulers and townspeople gradually adopted it. Mosques rose in the Swahili port cities, and Arabic script began to appear in local writing. Farther east, the same sea lanes helped carry Buddhism and Hinduism into Southeast Asia.

(6) The exchange was never one-sided. African ideas, words, and goods moved outward across the ocean too, reaching distant markets. Knowledge of navigation, shipbuilding, and even mathematics passed from culture to culture along these crowded waterways. In this way, the monsoon winds did more than fill sails. By connecting peoples who might otherwise never have met, they wove together a hemisphere, leaving behind shared languages, shared faiths, and cities whose ruins still tell the story of a connected world.

passages.title: When Kings Took the Crown
passages.text:
(1) For hundreds of years, medieval Europe ran on a system called feudalism. There was no single, powerful ruler who governed an entire country. Instead, power was scattered among many nobles. A king might sit on a throne, but the great lords beneath him controlled their own lands, collected their own taxes, and commanded their own private armies. In return for grants of land, these nobles promised loyalty and soldiers to the king. In practice, however, a determined lord with enough wealth and men could often ignore the king altogether. Authority was divided, local, and personal, and no central government reached deeply into everyday life.

(2) By the late Middle Ages, this old arrangement began to crumble. One blow came from the Black Death, the terrible plague of the 1300s that killed perhaps a third of Europe's people. With so few workers left, peasants could demand wages and greater freedom, and the bonds that tied them to a lord's land loosened. At the same time, trade revived and towns grew rich. Money, rather than land and personal loyalty, increasingly became the measure of power. A noble's sworn promise of service mattered less when soldiers could simply be hired for coin.

(3) Ambitious kings seized this opportunity. Slowly, they began consolidating authority, gathering scattered powers into their own hands. Instead of relying on the unreliable promises of feudal lords, a monarch could now tax the growing wealth of towns and merchants directly. With that steady income, a king could pay for a standing army loyal to him alone, rather than depending on troops a noble might or might not choose to send.

(4) Warfare itself pushed kings in the same direction. As gunpowder weapons spread, armies grew larger and far more expensive to equip and supply. A handful of nobles could no longer afford such forces on their own, but a king who could collect taxes across an entire realm could. The rising cost of war made strong, well-funded central governments not just useful but necessary, and it tied the great lords ever more tightly to the crown that paid them.

(5) These central governments grew more organized in other ways too. Kings appointed professional officials who answered to the crown, not to local lords. Royal judges traveled the land enforcing common laws that applied to everyone, weakening the patchwork of local rules each noble had once enforced. Records were kept, taxes were tracked, and orders flowed outward from the royal court. Slowly, the idea spread that a single government, headed by the king, held supreme authority over a defined territory and the people living within it.

(6) Out of this long process emerged the nation-state, a country united under one central government with clear borders. England, France, and Spain were among the first to take this shape. The transformation did not happen overnight, and powerful nobles did not surrender their independence easily. Yet by the end of the 1400s, the scattered, divided power of feudalism had given way to something new. The modern map of Europe, with its strong national governments, was beginning to take form.

questions.text:
How does the author MAINLY organize the information in the passage?
A. By describing causes and effects over time, tracing how the decline of feudalism led to centralized monarchies.   [CORRECT]
B. By comparing the governments of two specific countries point by point.
C. By presenting a single problem and the one solution a king invented to fix it.
D. By listing unrelated facts about medieval life in no particular order.

Standard: With the decline of feudalism, consolidation of power resulted in the emergence of nation-states.

title: One Land, One Crown: The Tudor Method

passages.title: One Land, One Crown: The Tudor Method
passages.text:
(5) Across the English Channel, a similar transformation was unfolding, though by different means. England in the 1400s had been torn apart by the Wars of the Roses, a long and bloody struggle between rival noble families competing for the throne. Decades of conflict had exhausted the great lords, killing many of them and draining their fortunes. The fighting had shown how dangerous it was for powerful nobles to keep their own armies and pursue their own ambitions. When Henry VII won the crown in 1485 and founded the Tudor dynasty, he inherited a kingdom desperate for stability and peace.

(6) Henry VII set out to ensure that no noble could ever again threaten the monarchy. He passed laws forbidding lords from keeping large private armies, a practice that had fueled the civil wars and allowed nobles to challenge the king by force. He used a powerful royal court, later known as the Star Chamber, to punish nobles who defied royal law, even when they were too wealthy or too feared for ordinary courts to touch them. Unlike Louis XI, Henry rarely went to war; instead, he saved money carefully, collected taxes and fees with great attention, filled the royal treasury, and made the crown financially independent of the nobility.

(7) Henry also strengthened the machinery of government. Trained officials, chosen for their skill rather than their noble birth, helped administer taxes and enforce the king's decisions across the realm. Because these officials owed their positions to the king, they served him faithfully rather than serving some powerful lord. By rewarding loyalty and punishing rebellion, Henry taught England's powerful families that their futures depended on serving the crown, not opposing it. Royal authority reached into every corner of the country in a way it never had under feudalism.

(8) Although Louis XI and Henry VII used different tools—one favoring the spider's web of diplomacy, the other favoring law and careful finance—both achieved the same historic outcome. Each replaced a patchwork of competing lords with a single, centralized government answerable to one ruler. Their reigns show how the decline of feudalism gave rise to the nation-state, a form of organization in which a large population shares one government and one ruler. This new model would shape Europe, and eventually much of the world, for centuries to come.

passages.title: The King Who Built a Kingdom
passages.text:
(1) For hundreds of years during the Middle Ages, the kingdom of France was not truly governed by its king. Instead, power was scattered among hundreds of nobles. Each duke, count, and baron controlled his own lands, collected his own taxes, raised his own soldiers, and even minted his own coins. This system, called feudalism, tied loyalty to local lords rather than to a single ruler. A peasant might serve a baron, who served a count, who only loosely answered to the distant king. In the early centuries of feudalism, the king of France, in practice, directly ruled little more than the area around Paris, while mighty lords governed the rest of the land almost as if they were kings themselves.

(2) By the late 1400s, however, this fragmented map began to change. Kings worked steadily to draw scattered authority into their own hands—a process historians call consolidation. King Louis XI of France, who reigned from 1461 to 1483, became a master of this craft. He distrusted open warfare and instead relied on patience, bribery, and clever diplomacy to weaken the great nobles. He would offer rivals money, titles, or favors to win them to his side, and he set powerful lords against one another so they could not unite against him. His enemies nicknamed him 'the Universal Spider' because he seemed to sit at the center of an invisible web, quietly pulling threads to trap his rivals.

(3) Louis understood that money meant power. He encouraged trade, supported merchants, and built up royal revenues so that he no longer depended on the nobles for funds. With this wealth, he could hire professional soldiers loyal to the crown rather than relying on the private armies of lords. A king with his own treasury and his own army did not have to beg the nobility for help in times of trouble. When the powerful Duke of Burgundy, one of his greatest rivals, challenged royal authority and died in battle in 1477, Louis swiftly absorbed much of his territory into the kingdom, adding rich and important lands to the crown.

(4) Step by careful step, the French crown expanded. Lands once held by independent nobles were folded under a single government, a shared system of royal courts, and a common loyalty to the king. People who had once thought of themselves only as the subjects of a local lord slowly began to see themselves as subjects of the king of France. The result was the slow birth of something new: a nation-state, a large territory unified under one central authority rather than divided among competing lords. The age of the scattered castle was fading, and the age of the unified kingdom had begun.

questions.text:
Which sentence BEST states a central idea shared by both passages?
A. Powerful kings gradually replaced a system of scattered noble power with unified, centralized nation-states.   [CORRECT]
B. European kings of the 1400s preferred to settle their disputes through large, costly wars.
C. Feudalism gave ordinary peasants more freedom than the nation-states that followed it.
D. France and England became allies in order to defeat their rebellious nobles together.

According to Passage 1, select TWO actions Louis XI took to weaken the power of the great nobles.
A. He used bribery and diplomacy rather than relying mainly on open warfare.   [CORRECT]
B. He built up royal revenues so he could hire soldiers loyal to the crown.   [CORRECT]
C. He allowed each noble to continue minting his own coins.
D. He divided the lands around Paris among the dukes and counts.
E. He gave up control of Burgundy after the duke died in battle.

In paragraph 2, the text states that kings worked 'to draw scattered authority into their own hands—a process historians call consolidation.' In this sentence, the word consolidation most nearly means (   ).
Drop-down options:  bringing separate powers together under one control [CORRECT]  /  dividing a kingdom into smaller, independent regions  /  trading goods and coins between distant merchants  /  fighting a long war between rival noble families

How do the two passages work together to develop the topic of how nation-states emerged?
A. They describe two rulers who reached the same goal of centralized power using different methods.   [CORRECT]
B. They disagree about whether feudalism actually declined during the 1400s.
C. Passage 1 argues that nation-states were harmful, while Passage 2 argues they were helpful.
D. Both passages focus entirely on the role of warfare in uniting kingdoms.

writingPrompt.text: Louis XI of France and Henry VII of England both helped create centralized nation-states, but they used different strategies to weaken the nobility. Write a multi-paragraph response in which you explain how the methods used by Louis XI and Henry VII were both similar and different, and how each method helped bring about the decline of feudalism. Your response must be based on ideas and information that can be found in the passages. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passages; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: introduce a clear central idea; develop the topic with relevant facts, definitions, and details; use evidence from the passages; and organize your ideas clearly.

title: From Salt Bars to Silver Coins

passages.title: From Salt Bars to Silver Coins
passages.text:
(1) In the ancient world, before the jingle of coins filled a merchant's purse, people traded one useful thing for another. This system, called barter, worked well enough in a small village where everyone knew the local baker and the local weaver. If you grew barley and wanted a clay pot, you simply found a potter who happened to want barley. But as cities swelled and empires stretched across hundreds of miles, this neighborly arrangement began to strain. A market crowded with strangers, foreign traders, and goods from distant lands demanded something barter could not easily provide: a reliable way to measure and exchange value.

(2) The trouble with barter is what economists call the "double coincidence of wants." For a trade to happen, each person must want exactly what the other offers, and at the same moment. A sandal-maker hungry for fish must locate a fisher who happens to need sandals. In a bustling imperial city such as Babylon or Rome, where thousands of specialized workers produced everything from dyed cloth to bronze tools, finding such a perfect match grew nearly impossible. The larger the market, the more time and effort each exchange consumed, and wasted time meant wasted opportunity.

(3) Markets solved this puzzle by adopting commodity money, goods that nearly everyone would accept because they held steady value. In ancient Mesopotamia, merchants measured prices in fixed weights of barley or silver. Salt, too, was so prized for preserving food that it became a valuable item of trade across the ancient world; in fact, the English word "salary" is thought to come from the Latin word for salt. Such commodities served as a common yardstick, allowing a farmer to sell grain for silver and later spend that silver on tools, without ever needing to find a toolmaker who craved grain.

(4) Still, weighing out barley or chipping off bits of silver for every purchase was clumsy. The breakthrough came in Lydia, a wealthy kingdom in what is now Turkey, more than 2,500 years ago. Lydian rulers minted some of the first standardized coins from electrum, a natural blend of gold and silver, and stamped each with an official mark guaranteeing its weight and purity. A buyer no longer had to test or weigh the metal; the stamp did the vouching. Trade sped up dramatically, and the idea spread across the Mediterranean like wildfire.

(5) Coinage and growing markets fed each other in a powerful cycle. Because coins made exchange so easy, more people could afford to specialize, devoting themselves to a single craft instead of trying to produce everything their household needed. A potter could throw pots all day, sell them for coins, and buy bread, cloth, and nails from others who had likewise mastered their trades. This specialization, in turn, raised the quality and quantity of goods, which drew even more buyers and sellers into the marketplace.

(6) The shift from barter to money did more than oil the wheels of commerce. It reshaped entire societies. Governments could now collect taxes in coin, fund armies, and pay distant officials. Lenders could record debts in precise amounts. Prices could be compared across regions, knitting scattered towns into vast trading networks. What began as a humble fix for the awkwardness of swapping goods became the foundation of the connected, specialized economies that empires depended upon, and that we still rely on today.

questions.text:
Which sentence best states a central idea developed across the passage?
A. As markets in cities and empires grew larger, money replaced barter and allowed people to specialize, transforming how economies worked.   [CORRECT]
B. Salt was useful for preserving food, which is why it became a valued item in the ancient world.
C. The Lydian kingdom became wealthy mainly because it was located in the region now known as Turkey.
D. Barter worked best in large imperial cities where strangers and foreign traders gathered to exchange goods.

According to the passage, the spread of coinage and the growth of markets influenced each other because coins made exchange easier, which allowed more people to (   ).
Drop-down options:  specialize in a single craft and sell their goods for coins [CORRECT]  /  return to bartering goods directly with their neighbors  /  avoid paying taxes to imperial governments  /  produce everything their own household needed

Read this sentence from paragraph 4: "A buyer no longer had to test or weigh the metal; the stamp did the vouching." As used in the sentence, what does the word "vouching" most nearly mean?
A. Guaranteeing that something is genuine or trustworthy   [CORRECT]
B. Increasing the total amount of something
C. Hiding the true value of an object
D. Melting metal down into a new shape

Select two details from the passage that support the idea that barter became impractical as markets grew larger.
A. A successful trade required a "double coincidence of wants," with each person wanting exactly what the other offered at the same moment.   [CORRECT]
B. In a large imperial city full of specialized workers, finding a perfect match for a trade grew nearly impossible.   [CORRECT]
C. Lydian rulers minted some of the first standardized coins from electrum.
D. The English word "salary" is thought to come from the Latin word for salt.
E. Governments could collect taxes in coin and pay distant officials.

writingPrompt.text: How did the growth of cities and empires lead to the replacement of barter with money, and how did this change affect the way people worked and traded? Write a multi-paragraph response in which you explain how the growth of cities and empires drove the shift from barter to money and how that shift affected specialization and trade. Your response must be based on ideas and information that can be found in the passage. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passage; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: introduce a clear central idea; develop the topic with relevant facts, definitions, and details; use evidence from the passage; and organize your ideas clearly.

passages.title: The Lantern Keeper
passages.text:
The old lantern hung beside the sea,

Its glass worn thin by salt and spray.

Each night it woke with a golden glow

To guide lost travelers on their way.

Storm clouds gathered above the waves.

The wind would howl and the rain would pound.

Ships disappeared behind curtains of gray,

Yet the lantern's light could still be found.

A young girl often watched from shore.

She wondered why it shined each night.

"No one would blame it if it went dark,"

She whispered while holding her coat tight.

One evening, the storm grew fierce.

The harbor shook beneath the sky.

The girl looked out through sheets of rain

And saw the lantern standing high.

Its beam stretched over restless water.

It cut through darkness, cold, and fear.

Though battered by the crashing storm,

Its steady glow remained sincere.

The next day, fishermen returned.

Their boats were worn, but all came home.

They thanked the light beside the sea

That helped them safely through the foam.

The girl smiled as she passed the shore.

At last she understood its role.

The lantern wasn't simply bright—

It carried hope from soul to soul.

Years later, when storms returned,

And others feared what lay ahead,

The girl became a guiding light,

Remembering what the lantern said.

Though winds may rise and skies grow dark,

And challenges may block your view,

A single light that refuses to fade

Can help the world find its way through.

questions.text:
Which detail from the poem best supports the inference that the lantern represents more than a physical object?
A. “Its glass worn thin by salt and spray”B. “The wind would howl and the rain would pound”C. “It carried hope from soul to soul”D. “Their boats were worn, but all came home”
Answer: C

Which TWO details help develop the poem’s theme that perseverance can help others during difficult times?
A. “The girl often watched from shore.”B. “Its beam stretched over restless water.”C. “Though battered by the crashing storm, / Its steady glow remained sincere.”D. “The next day, fishermen returned.”E. “The girl smiled as she passed the shore.”
Answers: B, C

The poet uses the lantern as an extended symbol throughout the poem. This structure helps develop the idea that (hope and guidance can endure through challenges / storms are impossible to survive / the sea is more powerful than people).
Answer: hope and guidance can endure through challenges

Which statement best summarizes the poem?
A. A girl becomes fascinated by a lantern and spends years studying how it works.
B. A lantern continues shining through storms to guide sailors safely home, inspiring a girl to later help others during difficult times.
C. Fishermen struggle through dangerous storms and eventually replace an old lantern with a stronger one.
D. A girl fears storms near the harbor until the weather finally improves and the sea becomes calm.
Answer: B

writingPrompt.text: In "The Lantern Keeper," the lantern serves as more than just a source of light. Write a multi-paragraph informational essay analyzing the meaning of the poem. Explain the theme the poet develops and how the lantern functions as a symbol throughout the poem. Be sure to analyze specific words, phrases, images, and events from the poem that help convey its deeper meaning. Support your response with evidence from the text. As you write your response: Use your own words to introduce and develop a topic; Use evidence from the passage to support your topic; and Use clear language to express ideas about your topic by making connections to your evidence Be sure to: Include an introduction; Create focused, organized paragraphs; Pay attention to the grammar, structure, and mechanics of your sentences; and Include a conclusion. Write your multi-paragraph response in the space provided.

passages.title: The Drummer of Dawn
passages.text:
(1) Before the birds began to sing,(2) Before the sun could stretch and yawn,(3) A distant drum would softly sound—(4) The drummer of the dawn.

(5) Boom, boom, beat the day awake,(6) Boom, boom, bright and strong.(7) The rhythm rolled across the hills,(8) Carrying morning along.

(9) The sleepy squirrels stirred and stretched.(10) The rabbits rustled near the creek.(11) The whispering wind wound through the trees,(12) While the drummer kept the beat.

(13) Boom, boom, beat the day awake,(14) Boom, boom, bright and strong.(15) The steady sound moved through the woods(16) Like the chorus of a song.

(17) The drummer sat beneath an oak,(18) Tapping time with weathered hands.(19) His drum was patched and faded brown,(20) Yet everyone understood its plans.

(21) When clouds gathered gray above,(22) And thunder tumbled through the sky,(23) The drumbeat didn't disappear.(24) It answered with a steady reply.

(25) Boom, boom, beat the day awake,(26) Boom, boom, brave and long.(27) Even storms seemed smaller then,(28) Marching to its song.

(29) Seasons drifted, years passed by.(30) Leaves fell gold and rivers froze.(31) Still each morning brought the beat(32) That everyone had come to know.

(33) One spring day, the drum fell silent.(34) No rhythm echoed through the air.(35) The woods stood still, the animals waited,(36) Wondering why it wasn't there.

(37) Then a child stepped beneath the oak.(38) She lifted the drum upon her knee.(39) Boom, boom, the rhythm rang again,(40) Rolling from tree to tree.

(41) The birds resumed their cheerful songs.(42) The squirrels scampered, swift and free.(43) The child smiled as the forest woke,(44) Keeping the drummer's legacy.

(45) Boom, boom, beat the day awake,(46) Boom, boom, bright and strong.(47) Some rhythms last beyond a lifetime,(48) Living on in every song.

questions.text:
Which TWO details best support the inference that the drummer's influence continues even after he is gone?
A. “The drummer sat beneath an oak,” (line 17)
B. “Still each morning brought the beat / That everyone had come to know.” (lines 31–32)
C. “Then a child stepped beneath the oak.” (line 37)
D. “Boom, boom, the rhythm rang again, / Rolling from tree to tree.” (lines 39–40)
E. “The rabbits rustled near the creek.” (line 10)
Answers: C, D

How does the theme of legacy develop over the course of the poem?
A. The drummer teaches animals how to play music, and they eventually replace him.
B. The drummer creates a rhythm that becomes an important part of the forest, and later a child continues it after he is gone.
C. The drummer struggles to keep the forest awake during storms but eventually gives up.
D. The animals learn to create their own rhythm without any help from the drummer.
Answer: B

The repeated refrain, “Boom, boom, beat the day awake,” contributes to the poem's meaning by emphasizing (the lasting power of traditions and routines / the dangers of storms in the forest / the drummer's desire to become famous).
Answer: the lasting power of traditions and routines

Which statement best summarizes the poem?
A. A drummer wakes the forest each morning with a steady rhythm. After the drum falls silent, a child continues the tradition, showing how meaningful influences can live on through others.
B. A child learns to play the drum after many years of practice and becomes more skilled than the original drummer.
C. Animals in a forest depend on a drummer to survive storms and harsh winters.
D. A drummer spends his life teaching music to forest animals before retiring beneath an oak tree.
Answer: A

writingPrompt.text: In "The Drummer of Dawn," the poet uses repeated lines, rhyme, alliteration, and rhythm to develop the poem's meaning. Write a multi-paragraph informational essay explaining how the poet uses words and phrases to create rhythm and meaning. Be sure to analyze the repeated lines, examples of alliteration, rhyme patterns, and the poem's regular beat. Explain how these elements help communicate the poem's message about tradition, leadership, and carrying on important responsibilities. Support your response with evidence from the poem. As you write your response: Use your own words to introduce and develop a topic; Use evidence from the passage to support your topic; and Use clear language to express ideas about your topic by making connections to your evidence Be sure to: Include an introduction; Create focused, organized paragraphs; Pay attention to the grammar, structure, and mechanics of your sentences; and Include a conclusion. Write your multi-paragraph response in the space provided.

passages.title: The Empty Bench
passages.text:
(1) Beneath an old oak in the town square stood(2) A weathered wooden bench.(3) Its paint had faded long ago,(4) Its legs were worn and drenched.

(5) People hurried past each day,(6) Their eyes fixed straight ahead.(7) They rarely stopped to notice it,(8) Or thoughts it might have held.

(9) In spring, young children climbed aboard.(10) They laughed and chased the breeze.(11) In summer, travelers paused to rest(12) Beneath the sheltering leaves.

(13) Autumn brought a quieter crowd.(14) The air grew cool and thin.(15) The bench would hold a reader still(16) Wrapped in a favorite grin.

(17) Then winter came with drifting snow.(18) The square grew cold and white.(19) Yet still the bench remained outside(20) Through every freezing night.

(21) One morning workers came to town.(22) They loaded it away.(23) Few people noticed it was gone(24) Until much later that day.

(25) The children searched for where it stood.(26) The readers looked around.(27) Travelers missed their resting place,(28) Though they had scarcely made a sound.

(29) A new bench soon replaced the old,(30) Bright and polished too.(31) But many paused and thought about(32) The one they never knew.

passages.title: The Streetlight
passages.text:
(33) At the edge of a narrow city block(34) A streetlight stood alone.(35) Each evening as the sunlight faded,(36) Its gentle glow was shown.

(37) It lit the path for passing feet,(38) For cyclists riding home.(39) It watched the city change each night,(40) Yet quietly stayed alone.

(41) Some hurried underneath its light.(42) Some lingered for a while.(43) Some stopped to share a story there,(44) Or greet a friend with a smile.

(45) Year after year, it shone the same,(46) Through thunder, wind, and rain.(47) Most people never thought about(48) How much it might sustain.

(49) One evening darkness filled the street.(50) The lamp no longer glowed.(51) Suddenly people stumbled more(52) Along the familiar road.

(53) Neighbors gathered to replace(54) The light they'd overlooked before.(55) They realized how much it gave(56) By simply doing more.

(57) The next night when the lamp returned,(58) Its glow seemed warm and bright.(59) People smiled as they passed beneath(60) Its steady, faithful light.

questions.text:
Which statement is best supported by evidence from Poem 1?
A. The bench was carefully maintained by the town for many years.
B. Most people regularly stopped to appreciate the bench.
C. The bench quietly affected many people's lives even though they rarely noticed it.
D. The bench was more important to travelers than to children.
Answer: C

In Poem 2, the theme develops as the community realizes that (ordinary things often provide important support that people may overlook / streetlights should be replaced more often / cities are safer than small towns).
Answer: ordinary things often provide important support that people may overlook

Both poems focus on an object that serves an important role in a community. Which TWO details best show this shared idea?
A. “People hurried past each day,” (line 5)
B. “The children searched for where it stood.” (line 25)
C. “Some hurried underneath its light.” (line 41)
D. “They realized how much it gave / By simply doing more.” (lines 55–56)
E. “Its paint had faded long ago,” (line 3)
Answers: B, D

Which statement best summarizes the central idea shared by both poems?
A. Communities should replace old objects before they wear out.
B. People often fail to notice the value of dependable things until they are gone, leading them to appreciate their importance.
C. Public spaces are important because they allow people to meet and socialize.
D. Weather and time eventually damage all objects, no matter how useful they are.
Answer: B

writingPrompt.text: Both "The Empty Bench" and "The Streetlight" explore how ordinary things can have an important impact on people's lives. Write a multi-paragraph informational essay analyzing the meaning of both poems. Explain the themes the poets develop and how the bench and streetlight contribute to those themes. Be sure to analyze specific words, images, and events from each poem that help reveal their deeper meanings. Support your response with evidence from both poems. As you write your response: Use your own words to introduce and develop a topic; Use evidence from the passage to support your topic; and Use clear language to express ideas about your topic by making connections to your evidence Be sure to: Include an introduction; Create focused, organized paragraphs; Pay attention to the grammar, structure, and mechanics of your sentences; and Include a conclusion. Write your multi-paragraph response in the space provided.

passages.title: The River's Song
passages.text:
(1) The river raced beside the road,(2) Rushing, rolling, running free.(3) It sang a song of silver sounds(4) Beneath the bending willow tree.

(5) Flow on, flow on, the river cried.(6) Flow on, flow on today.(7) It carried leaves and drifting sticks(8) And swept them all away.

(9) The water whispered through the rocks.(10) It skipped and splashed with cheerful cheer.(11) The steady rhythm filled the air,(12) A song that all could hear.

(13) Flow on, flow on, the river cried.(14) Flow on, flow on today.(15) Through sunny skies and stormy nights,(16) It never lost its way.

(17) The river moved through every season.(18) It wound through valleys, wide and long.(19) Though many things around it changed,(20) It carried on its song.

(21) Flow on, flow on, the river cried.(22) Flow on, flow on today.(23) The rhythm echoed far downstream(24) As it journeyed on its way.

(25) High above the forest floor,(26) A mountain watched the world below.(27) Its rocky ridges reached the clouds,(28) Where chilly winds would blow.

(29) The mountain answered every sound.(30) It echoed words both loud and clear.(31) Hikers laughed and heard replies(32) Returning to their ears.

(33) Call out, call out, the mountain sang.(34) Call out, call out once more.(35) Voices bounced from peak to peak(36) And rolled from shore to shore.

(37) The crisp, cool air carried each sound.(38) The echoes danced and drifted wide.(39) Repeating words again and again,(40) Like waves upon the tide.

(41) Call out, call out, the mountain sang.(42) Call out, call out once more.(43) The rhythm lingered in the air(44) Long after voices soared.

(45) The mountain stood through passing years.(46) It listened day and night.(47) And every echo seemed to say,(48) "Your words can travel far from sight."

questions.text:
Which TWO details best support the inference that both the river and the mountain have a lasting influence on their surroundings?
A. “It carried leaves and drifting sticks” (line 7)
B. “The steady rhythm filled the air” (line 11)
C. “Voices bounced from peak to peak” (line 35)
D. “The mountain stood through passing years.” (line 45)
E. “The water whispered through the rocks.” (line 9)
Answers: B, D

What shared theme is developed in both poems?
A. Nature is unpredictable and dangerous.
B. Loud sounds are more powerful than quiet ones.
C. Some influences continue long after they are first created.
D. People should spend more time outdoors.
Answer: C

In Poem 2, the mountain setting helps develop the poem's central idea because the mountain (allows voices to echo and travel across great distances / prevents people from communicating with one another / changes constantly throughout the poem).
Answer: allows voices to echo and travel across great distances

Which statement best summarizes the relationship between the ideas developed in both poems?
A. Both poems describe natural features that create repeated sounds, showing how rhythms and echoes can continue to affect the world around them over time.
B. Both poems explain how rivers and mountains are formed through natural processes.
C. Both poems focus on travelers who become lost while exploring nature.
D. Both poems argue that nature is more important than human activity.
Answer: A

writingPrompt.text: Both "The River's Song" and "The Mountain's Echo" use repeated lines, rhyme, alliteration, and rhythm to create meaning. Write a multi-paragraph informational essay explaining how the poets use words and phrases to supply rhythm and meaning in both poems. Be sure to analyze the repeated lines, rhyme patterns, examples of alliteration, and the regular beats found throughout the poems. Explain how these elements help communicate the messages about persistence, communication, and the power of nature. Support your response with evidence from both poems. As you write your response: Use your own words to introduce and develop a topic; Use evidence from the passage to support your topic; and Use clear language to express ideas about your topic by making connections to your evidence Be sure to: Include an introduction; Create focused, organized paragraphs; Pay attention to the grammar, structure, and mechanics of your sentences; and Include a conclusion. Write your multi-paragraph response in the space provided.

passages.title: The Whispering Dragon
passages.text:
(1) Every year on the first night of autumn, the people of Silverpine gathered in the village square to watch the Lantern Flight. Hundreds of glowing lanterns floated into the sky, carrying wishes written by children and adults alike. According to legend, the lanterns were carried by the wind to a dragon who lived high above the clouds. Most villagers considered the story a myth, but thirteen-year-old Elara wasn't so sure.

(2) Elara had always been fascinated by the mountains that surrounded Silverpine. Their snowy peaks disappeared into the clouds, and strange lights occasionally flickered among the cliffs after sunset. Whenever she asked about them, the adults simply smiled and said the mountains held many secrets.

(3) During that year's Lantern Flight, Elara noticed something unusual. One lantern drifted away from the others and flew toward the mountains instead of rising into the sky. As she watched, a flash of silver light appeared above the distant peaks. The lantern vanished, and for a brief moment, Elara thought she saw a giant wing moving through the clouds.

(4) The next morning, curiosity got the better of her. Carrying a backpack filled with supplies, she followed an old trail that wound into the mountains. The path grew steeper as she climbed. Tall pines gave way to rocky cliffs, and the air became colder with every step.

(5) By afternoon, Elara reached a narrow ledge overlooking a hidden valley. At its center stood a crystal lake so clear that it reflected the sky like a mirror. Beside the lake rested a creature unlike anything she had ever seen. Its scales shimmered silver and blue. Its wings stretched wider than the village square. A dragon.

(6) Elara froze. The dragon slowly lifted its head and looked directly at her. Instead of roaring, it spoke in a calm voice. "You followed the lantern."

(7) Elara nearly dropped her backpack. "You can talk?"

(8) The dragon tilted its head. "Most are too surprised to ask that question. My name is Aureon. And yes, I can talk."

(9) Although she was nervous, Elara stepped closer. Aureon explained that dragons had once lived openly among humans. They used their magic to help crops grow, guide travelers, and protect villages from dangerous storms. Over time, however, people stopped believing in dragons. As the stories faded, the dragons retreated into hidden places.

(10) "Then why do the lanterns come here?" Elara asked.

(11) Aureon stretched one wing toward the sky. "The wishes written on those lanterns carry hope, kindness, and dreams. Magic is strengthened by such things. Every year, the lanterns remind us that people still believe in possibilities beyond what they can see."

(12) As the sun began to set, Aureon showed Elara something even more remarkable. The dragon breathed a stream of sparkling mist into the air. The mist formed images of the wishes carried by the lanterns—children hoping to help their families, students wishing for success, and neighbors wishing for peace and happiness.

(13) Elara watched in amazement. She realized the lanterns were not magical because of the dragon. Instead, the dragon's magic came from the hopes of the people below.

(14) Before she left, Aureon handed her a single silver scale. "The world changes when people believe they can make it better," the dragon said. "Never forget that."

(15) Elara returned to Silverpine after dark. She told no one about her journey because she knew most people would not believe her story. Yet every year afterward, she attended the Lantern Flight and watched the glowing lights rise into the night sky.

(16) Sometimes she thought she could still see a silver shape gliding above the clouds. Whenever she did, she smiled and remembered what Aureon had taught her. Magic was not only found in dragons or distant mountains. It could also be found in hope, determination, and the belief that even ordinary people could make a difference.

questions.text:
Which TWO details best support the inference that Elara's understanding of magic changes during her journey?
A. “Elara had always been fascinated by the mountains that surrounded Silverpine.” (paragraph 2)
B. “Most villagers considered the story a myth, but thirteen-year-old Elara wasn't so sure.” (paragraph 1)
C. “The wishes written on those lanterns carry hope, kindness, and dreams.” (paragraph 11)
D. “She realized the lanterns were not magical because of the dragon.” (paragraph 13)
E. “She told no one about her journey because she knew most people would not believe her story.” (paragraph 15)
Answers: C, D

How does the mountain setting contribute to the development of the plot?
A. It prevents Elara from returning to Silverpine.
B. It creates a hidden place where Elara can discover Aureon and learn the truth about the lanterns.
C. It causes the villagers to stop participating in the Lantern Flight.
D. It forces Aureon to leave Silverpine and search for a new home.
Answer: B

The author develops a contrast between Elara's perspective and the villagers' perspective by showing that Elara (is willing to explore mysteries that most villagers dismiss as myths / wants to prove the villagers wrong about everything / believes dragons should return to live among humans).
Answer: is willing to explore mysteries that most villagers dismiss as myths

Which theme is developed over the course of the story?
A. Secrets should always be shared with everyone.
B. True friendship is more important than adventure.
C. Hope and belief can inspire positive change and give strength to others.
D. Magical creatures are more powerful than humans.
Answer: C

writingPrompt.text: In "The Whispering Dragon," Elara learns an important lesson during her journey into the mountains. Write a multi-paragraph informational essay explaining the theme of the story. Be sure to explain how specific events, characters, and details contribute to the overall meaning of the story. Support your response with evidence from the text. Write your multi-paragraph response in the space provided.

title: The Day the Sky Came Alive

passages.title: The Day the Sky Came Alive
passages.text:
(1) Fourteen-year-old Elena Martinez had never seen an airplane before. Living in a small farming town in Ohio in 1918, she had heard stories about flying machines, but they seemed more like something from a science fiction novel than real life. Most people in town traveled by horse, wagon, or train. The idea of a machine carrying people through the air sounded impossible.

(2) One Saturday morning, Elena's older brother Mateo burst through the front door carrying a newspaper. "They're coming!" he shouted. "The Army pilots are stopping in Columbus next week. They're putting on an air show!"

(3) Elena grabbed the newspaper and quickly scanned the article. During World War I, military pilots were traveling around the country demonstrating airplanes and encouraging support for the war effort. The article promised aerial maneuvers, speeches, and opportunities for people to see aircraft up close.

(4) Elena could hardly think about anything else for the next several days. At school, students discussed the upcoming event during lunch and recess. Some believed airplanes would eventually replace trains. Others insisted that flying was too dangerous to ever become common.

(5) The following Saturday, Elena's family boarded an early train to Columbus. As they approached the fairgrounds where the event was being held, they saw crowds gathering from all directions. Vendors sold snacks and souvenirs while bands played patriotic music.

(6) Elena searched the sky but saw nothing unusual. Then a loud buzzing sound echoed overhead. She looked up just as a small aircraft appeared above the trees. The crowd erupted in cheers.

(7) The airplane seemed almost unreal. Its wings glistened in the sunlight as it climbed higher and higher. Elena watched in amazement as the pilot banked sharply, circled the fairgrounds, and then dove toward the ground before pulling upward again.

(8) Several more airplanes joined the demonstration. The pilots performed loops, turns, and formations that left spectators staring skyward. Children pointed excitedly while adults shielded their eyes from the sun to follow the aircraft.

(9) After the performance, visitors were allowed to walk near several airplanes displayed on the ground. Elena hurried toward one of them. Up close, the aircraft looked both fragile and impressive. Wooden supports connected fabric-covered wings, and a powerful engine sat at the front.

(10) A pilot standing nearby answered questions from curious visitors. Elena listened carefully as he described how airplanes were being used during the war for scouting, communication, and observation. He explained that aviation technology was improving rapidly and that airplanes could someday play important roles in transportation and commerce.

(11) "Do you really think people will travel in airplanes someday?" Elena asked.

(12) The pilot smiled. "Many people laughed when inventors first talked about flying," he replied. "Now look around. Sometimes the future arrives faster than we expect."

(13) The comment stayed with Elena throughout the journey home. Before the air show, airplanes had seemed like distant curiosities. Now they felt like a glimpse of the future.

(14) Over the next several years, Elena followed news about aviation whenever she could. Newspaper articles described record-breaking flights, new aircraft designs, and advances in air travel. What had once seemed impossible gradually became more common.

(15) By the time Elena reached adulthood, passenger airlines were beginning to connect major cities. Air mail services delivered letters more quickly than ever before. The technology she had first witnessed at the air show continued transforming transportation and communication.

(16) Decades later, Elena often shared the story of that day with her grandchildren. They found it difficult to imagine a time when airplanes were considered remarkable. To them, air travel was simply a normal part of life.

(17) Elena always smiled when she heard that. She remembered standing beneath a bright summer sky, watching an airplane soar overhead for the first time. In that moment, she had witnessed more than an exciting performance. She had seen a new technology beginning to change the world.

(18) Looking back, Elena realized that history is often experienced through ordinary moments. The people attending the air show had not known exactly how aviation would shape the future. Yet they were witnessing the early stages of a transformation that would connect people, countries, and continents in ways previous generations could scarcely imagine.

(19) The memory remained vivid throughout her life because it reminded her that innovation often begins with curiosity. New ideas may seem impossible at first, but with time and perseverance, they can reshape the world.

(20) For Elena, the day the sky came alive was more than a memory. It was a reminder that every generation experiences moments when the future suddenly becomes visible.

questions.text:
Which TWO details best support the inference that Elena's view of airplanes changes significantly after the air show?
A. “The idea of a machine carrying people through the air sounded impossible.” (paragraph 1)
B. “Some believed airplanes would eventually replace trains.” (paragraph 4)
C. “Now they felt like a glimpse of the future.” (paragraph 13)
D. “They found it difficult to imagine a time when airplanes were considered remarkable.” (paragraph 16)
E. “Vendors sold snacks and souvenirs while bands played patriotic music.” (paragraph 5)
Answers: A, C

How does the historical setting of the story influence the plot?
A. It creates opportunities for Elena to become a pilot during World War I.
B. It allows Elena to witness airplanes at a time when aviation was still new and unfamiliar to many people.
C. It prevents Elena from learning about new technologies.
D. It causes the air show to be canceled because of wartime restrictions.
Answer: B

The author develops a contrast between Elena's early perspective and the pilot's perspective by showing that Elena initially views airplanes as (an unlikely and almost impossible invention / a common form of transportation / a dangerous mistake that should not continue to be developed).
Answer: an unlikely and almost impossible invention

Which theme is developed over the course of the passage?
A. New technologies often create more problems than solutions.
B. Curiosity and openness to new ideas can help people recognize future possibilities.
C. History is shaped only by famous inventors and leaders.
D. People should avoid making predictions about the future.
Answer: B

writingPrompt.text: In "The Day the Sky Came Alive," Elena witnesses an important technological development during a period of historical change. passages.title: The City Beneath the Dome
passages.text:
(1) By the year 2147, the city of Aurelia existed beneath a glass dome in the middle of the desert. Outside the dome, windstorms swept across miles of dry land, carrying dust so thick it could block the sun for days. Inside the dome, however, trees grew in neat rows, water flowed through narrow canals, and artificial clouds drifted across a pale blue ceiling.

(2) Fourteen-year-old Nia Sol had lived in Aurelia her entire life. Like everyone else, she wore a wrist monitor that tracked air quality, water use, and energy consumption. The city’s central computer, called OASIS, controlled nearly every part of daily life. It adjusted temperatures, managed food production, and decided how resources should be distributed.

(3) Most people trusted OASIS completely. After all, the system had kept the city alive for decades. But Nia had begun noticing problems. The garden lights dimmed during the afternoon, water pumps paused without warning, and some neighborhoods received fewer food credits than others. When Nia asked adults about it, they told her OASIS was simply “balancing resources.”

(4) One evening, while repairing sensors in the greenhouse with her uncle, Nia discovered a hidden maintenance message flashing across a screen: CORE ERROR—RESOURCE MODEL OUTDATED. Beneath it was a second message that made her stomach tighten: HUMAN REVIEW REQUIRED.

(5) “Uncle Tomas,” Nia whispered, “has anyone actually checked OASIS lately?” Her uncle glanced toward the security cameras mounted above the greenhouse doors. “Not in years,” he admitted quietly. “The council believes the system can manage better than people can.”

(6) Nia could not stop thinking about the warning. If OASIS was using old information, it might be making decisions that no longer matched the city’s needs. The next day, she accessed public data files from the school library and compared them with current resource reports. The numbers did not match. OASIS believed Aurelia had thousands fewer residents than it actually did.

(7) Nia brought the evidence to the city council, but several members dismissed her concerns. “The system was designed by the greatest engineers of the century,” one official said. “A student cannot understand it better than OASIS understands itself.”

(8) That night, the dome’s lights flickered, and the canals stopped flowing. Across Aurelia, families stepped outside as emergency sirens echoed through the streets. OASIS announced that water would be restricted to “nonessential zones,” including the greenhouse district where most of the city’s food was grown.

(9) Nia knew the restriction would protect drinking water temporarily but damage crops within days. With her uncle’s help, she entered the old control chamber beneath the greenhouse. There, surrounded by humming machines and glowing screens, she uploaded the corrected population data.

(10) For several tense minutes, nothing happened. Then OASIS recalculated its model. Water returned to the canals, greenhouse pumps restarted, and emergency lights faded from red to white. The next morning, the council announced that human review teams would work alongside OASIS from then on.

(11) Nia still believed technology could protect Aurelia, but she also understood its limits. Even the most advanced system needed people willing to question, investigate, and take responsibility when something went wrong.

passages.title: The Memory Archive
passages.text:
(1) On the moon colony of Selene, memories were stored like books. Every citizen carried a small device behind one ear that recorded important experiences and uploaded them to the Memory Archive. The archive preserved discoveries, lessons, mistakes, and personal histories so future generations could learn from them.

(2) Thirteen-year-old Kai Mercer thought the archive was the most important invention in human history. His parents were lunar engineers, and they often used archived memories to study past repairs and construction projects. Instead of reading instructions, workers could experience how someone else had solved a problem years earlier.

(3) At school, students used the archive for history lessons. Kai had walked through a scientist’s first view of Earth rising above the moon. He had experienced the tense minutes before the colony’s first oxygen farm began working. The memories made the past feel immediate and real.

(4) Then one morning, Kai’s class entered the archive chamber for a lesson about the colony’s founding. Instead of showing the expected memory, the system displayed static. A warning appeared across the wall: MEMORY SEQUENCE INCOMPLETE.

(5) Their teacher frowned. “That should not be possible,” she said. “The founding records are protected.” Later that day, more gaps appeared. Important memories about early oxygen shortages, construction failures, and emergency evacuations began disappearing from the system.

(6) Colony leaders suspected a technical malfunction, but Kai noticed something unusual. Only memories involving mistakes were missing. Successful events remained untouched. It seemed as though someone had tried to erase the colony’s failures.

(7) Kai and his friend Amara searched old backup files after school. Deep inside the archive’s storage system, they found deleted records marked with a council authorization code. Amara stared at the screen. “Why would leaders erase these?”

(8) Kai understood the reason before she did. The colony was preparing for new settlers from Earth, and leaders wanted Selene to appear safe and successful. They feared that stories of past failures might frighten people away.

(9) During the next public meeting, Kai presented the recovered files. Some adults were angry that he had accessed restricted records, but others grew quiet as forgotten memories filled the meeting hall. Citizens saw engineers solving oxygen failures, families sharing supplies, and workers rebuilding damaged tunnels after accidents.

(10) “These memories don’t make us look weak,” Kai said. “They show how people survived by learning from mistakes.”

(11) After the meeting, the council restored the missing records and created a new rule: no historical memory could be removed without public review. Kai realized that technology could preserve knowledge, but only if people were honest about what deserved to be remembered.

questions.text:
Which TWO details support the inference that both Nia and Kai believe people should actively question technology rather than trust it completely?
A. “The city's central computer, called OASIS, controlled nearly every part of daily life.” (Passage 1, paragraph 2)
B. “Nia could not stop thinking about the warning.” (Passage 1, paragraph 6)
C. “Kai and his friend Amara searched old backup files after school.” (Passage 2, paragraph 7)
D. “The archive preserved discoveries, lessons, mistakes, and personal histories.” (Passage 2, paragraph 1)
E. “The colony restored the missing records and created a new rule.” (Passage 2, paragraph 11)
Answers: B, C

How does the futuristic setting contribute to the conflicts in both passages?
A. It creates worlds where advanced technology plays a major role in society, making errors or misuse of that technology especially significant.
B. It prevents the main characters from communicating with other people.
C. It causes the characters to leave their communities permanently.
D. It makes the characters rely only on traditional methods to solve problems.
Answer: A

The authors develop Nia’s and Kai’s perspectives by showing that both characters (question accepted systems when evidence suggests something is wrong / want to replace all technology with human workers / distrust every leader they encounter).
Answer: question accepted systems when evidence suggests something is wrong

Which theme is developed in both passages?
A. Technology should always make decisions without human involvement.
B. People must be willing to think critically and act responsibly, even when trusted systems appear reliable.
C. Rules are more important than individual judgment.
D. Success comes from keeping mistakes hidden from others.
Answer: B

writingPrompt.text: Both “The City Beneath the Dome” and “The Memory Archive” explore how advanced technology can help societies while also creating challenges. Write a multi-paragraph informational essay explaining how both stories develop the idea that technology must be guided by human responsibility. Analyze the problems the characters discover, how they respond, and how the science fiction settings contribute to the meaning of each passage. Support your response with evidence from both texts. Write your multi-paragraph response in the space provided.

passages.title: The Community Garden
passages.text:
(1) Maya had walked past the empty lot on Oak Street for years. The space sat between an apartment building and a grocery store, surrounded by a chain-link fence. Weeds pushed through cracked concrete, and trash sometimes collected in the corners. Most people hardly noticed it anymore.

(2) One afternoon, Maya's science teacher announced a community improvement project. Students could identify a local problem and propose a solution. As Maya walked home, she found herself staring at the empty lot. An idea began to form.

(3) The next day, she shared her proposal with her class. Instead of leaving the lot unused, she suggested creating a community garden. Residents could grow vegetables, flowers, and herbs while helping improve the neighborhood.

(4) Not everyone thought the idea would work. Some students argued that maintaining a garden would require too much effort. Others doubted enough people would participate. Maya understood their concerns, but she believed the project could succeed if enough people worked together.

(5) After receiving approval from the property owner, Maya and several volunteers began cleaning the lot. The work was harder than she expected. They removed weeds, picked up litter, and hauled away broken pieces of concrete. Some afternoons, only a handful of volunteers showed up.

(6) As the weeks passed, however, more community members became involved. Parents donated gardening tools. Local businesses contributed soil and seeds. Retired residents shared advice about planting and caring for vegetables.

(7) By early summer, rows of tomatoes, peppers, lettuce, and sunflowers filled the space. Children stopped by after school to water plants, and neighbors who had never spoken before began working side by side.

(8) One evening, Maya stood near the garden entrance watching families gather. She realized the project had accomplished more than growing food. It had created a place where people could meet, cooperate, and take pride in their community.

(9) The garden continued growing throughout the season. Although maintaining it required effort, the project showed Maya that positive change often begins when someone decides to take action instead of waiting for someone else to solve a problem.

passages.title: The Robotics Team
passages.text:
(1) Jordan had always enjoyed building things. Whether he was assembling model airplanes or taking apart old electronics, he loved figuring out how things worked. When his middle school announced the formation of a robotics team, he immediately signed up.

(2) During the first meeting, Jordan expected everything to go smoothly. Instead, he discovered that teamwork could be complicated. The students disagreed about designs, struggled to assign responsibilities, and often spent more time debating than building.

(3) Frustration grew as competition season approached. Several test runs ended with the robot spinning in circles or stopping unexpectedly. At one point, a wheel fell off completely during a practice challenge.

(4) "We're never going to be ready," one teammate complained after another failed attempt.

(5) Jordan felt discouraged too, but their coach encouraged the team to focus on solving one problem at a time. Instead of arguing about everything at once, the students divided tasks according to their strengths. Some worked on programming, while others focused on construction and testing.

(6) Gradually, the team improved. Each failure revealed a problem that could be fixed. The students learned how to communicate more effectively and listen to one another's ideas.

(7) By the time the competition arrived, the robot still wasn't perfect. However, it completed every challenge successfully and performed better than anyone expected. Although another school won first place, Jordan's team earned recognition for creativity and teamwork.

(8) On the bus ride home, Jordan thought about everything the team had accomplished. The most important lesson had not been about robotics at all. It had been about persistence, collaboration, and learning from mistakes.

(9) Looking back, Jordan realized that success rarely happens immediately. Meaningful achievements often require patience, effort, and the willingness to keep improving after setbacks.

questions.text:
Which statement is best supported by evidence from both passages?
A. Success is usually achieved without help from others.
B. Meaningful accomplishments often require persistence through challenges and setbacks.
C. Competition is more important than cooperation.
D. Most problems can be solved quickly if people work alone.
Answer: B

How do the challenges faced by Maya and Jordan influence the events of their stories?
Select TWO answers.
A. Maya's community's initial doubts encourage her to abandon the garden project.
B. Maya's challenges lead more people to become involved and strengthen the project over time.
C. Jordan's team's disagreements and failures force the students to improve their communication and teamwork.
D. Jordan's challenges cause the team to withdraw from the competition.
E. Both characters immediately solve their problems without changing their approach.
Answers: B, C

The authors develop Maya's and Jordan's perspectives by showing that both characters (view setbacks as problems that can be worked through rather than reasons to quit / believe success depends mostly on talent / expect others to solve challenges for them).
Answer: view setbacks as problems that can be worked through rather than reasons to quit

Which statement best summarizes the shared message of the two passages?
A. Maya creates a community garden, and Jordan joins a robotics team, showing that different hobbies can bring people together.
B. Maya and Jordan both face obstacles while working toward goals. Through cooperation, persistence, and problem-solving, they learn that success comes from effort and growth rather than immediate results.
C. Maya improves her neighborhood while Jordan builds a robot, and both eventually win competitions.
D. Maya and Jordan discover that leadership is difficult and decide to let others take charge of their projects.
Answer: B

writingPrompt.text: Both "The Community Garden" and "The Robotics Team" explore how people work together to achieve goals. Be sure to explain how specific events in each story contribute to the overall themes of teamwork, problem-solving, and personal growth. Support your response with evidence from both texts.
