passages.title: The Map in My Pocket
passages.text:
(1) Folded inside my jacket pocket(2) Was a map marked with fading lines.(3) It showed old trails through forests,(4) Rivers winding toward distant hills.

(5) My grandfather carried it first.(6) Then my mother kept it for years.(7) Now it traveled with me,(8) Creased and softened by many hands.

(9) The path ahead seemed clear enough.(10) Arrows pointed where to turn.(11) Notes filled the margins—(12) Warnings, landmarks, and reminders.

(13) Yet when I reached the forest edge,(14) I noticed something strange.(15) New trails crossed the old ones,(16) Twisting in unexpected directions.

(17) Some travelers followed the map exactly.(18) Others wandered onto newer paths.(19) Their footprints disappeared among the trees,(20) Beyond the places the map described.

(21) I paused beneath a tall pine.(22) The paper rustled in the breeze.(23) For a moment, I wondered(24) Whether every journey should be planned.

(25) The map remained in my pocket,(26) But my eyes searched farther ahead.(27) One trail curved toward the unknown.(28) I decided to see where it led.

(29) The old map still mattered.(30) It helped me reach this place.(31) Yet every step forward reminded me(32) That some paths must be discovered.

passages.title: The Lighthouse
passages.text:
(1) At the edge of the rocky shore(2) A lighthouse stood against the wind.(3) Storm after storm swept past,(4) Yet the light continued to shine.

(5) Sailors trusted its steady beam.(6) Families watched for it at night.(7) Generation after generation(8) Looked toward the same guiding light.

(9) Waves crashed against the cliffs.(10) Seasons changed around the harbor.(11) New boats filled the water,(12) And old boats disappeared.

(13) Still the lighthouse remained.(14) Its light crossed the dark water(15) In the same careful pattern(16) That countless travelers had followed.

(17) Some people called it ordinary.(18) Others hardly noticed it at all.(19) Yet whenever storms arrived,(20) Eyes turned back toward the shore.

(21) The lighthouse asked for nothing.(22) It did not chase the waves.(23) It simply stood where it belonged,(24) Offering guidance when it was needed.

(25) Long after the storm clouds passed,(26) The light continued to glow.(27) Its message never changed:(28) Some things are worth preserving.

(29) Though the harbor grew and changed,(30) Though new routes crossed the sea,(31) The lighthouse remained a reminder(32) That wisdom can endure.

questions.text:
How does the theme of Poem 1 ("The Map in My Pocket") develop over the course of the poem?
A  The speaker begins by trusting the map completely and ends by deciding that planning a journey is pointless.
B  The speaker begins by relying on an inherited guide and gradually realizes that valuing the past and discovering new paths can work together.
C  The speaker begins uncertain about the map and ends by concluding that the old trails are the only safe routes to follow.
D  The speaker begins eager to explore and ends by deciding to follow the map exactly as earlier travelers did.

Both poems are organized so that an unchanging object or guide is contrasted with a changing world around it. Select two lines that show how each poem uses this contrasting structure to develop its meaning.
A  "The old map still mattered. / It helped me reach this place." (29-30)
B  "New trails crossed the old ones, / Twisting in unexpected directions." (15-16)
C  "Still the lighthouse remained. / Its light crossed the dark water" (13-14)
D  "Sailors trusted its steady beam. / Families watched for it at night." (5-6)
E  "I paused beneath a tall pine. / The paper rustled in the breeze." (21-22)

Read these lines from Poem 2 ("The Lighthouse"):

"The lighthouse asked for nothing. / It did not chase the waves. / It simply stood where it belonged" (21-23)

The poet's choice to say the lighthouse "did not chase the waves" mainly emphasizes that the lighthouse is [ ▾ ].
steady and content to remain in one place rather than pursuing change
frightened of the powerful storms that surround it
slowly being worn away and damaged by the sea
eager to travel across the water like the boats it guides

How does the point of view in Poem 1 differ from the point of view in Poem 2?
A  Poem 1 is told by a speaker who personally journeys and makes a choice, while Poem 2 is told by an observer who describes how others rely on the lighthouse.
B  Poem 1 is told by an outside observer watching travelers, while Poem 2 is told by the lighthouse describing its own feelings.
C  Both poems are told by speakers who directly take part in the events they describe.
D  Both poems are told by outside observers who never reveal any individual's thoughts or decisions.

writingPrompt.text: Which poem more effectively develops the idea that people can learn from the past while moving toward the future? Write a multi-paragraph response in which you explain your opinion, using specific evidence from both poems to support your analysis. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passages; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: introduce a clear central idea; develop your opinion with relevant details from both poems; use evidence from the passages; and organize your ideas clearly.

passages.title: Drumbeat Morning
passages.text:
(1) Thump, thump, thump went the drum in the square.(2) Thump, thump, thump filled the cool morning air.(3) Neighbors opened windows and doors,(4) Drawn by the familiar sound.

(5) Children hurried down the sidewalks.(6) Shopkeepers stepped outside to listen.(7) The steady beat echoed between buildings,(8) Calling the town awake.

(9) Strong steps followed the sound.(10) Steady steps crossed the street.(11) Quick steps joined the rhythm,(12) Until dozens moved together.

(13) Thump, thump, thump went the drum in the square.(14) Thump, thump, thump like a patient heart.(15) The sound did not rush.(16) It did not fade.

(17) Around and around the crowd gathered,(18) Smiling, swaying, sharing stories.(19) Grandparents greeted old friends.(20) Younger children laughed and danced.

(21) The drummer never spoke a word.(22) Still, everyone understood the message.(23) The rhythm seemed to say,(24) “Come together. Stay awhile.”

(25) Thump, thump, thump went the drum in the square.(26) Thump, thump, thump through the middle of the day.(27) Long after the music ended,(28) The feeling remained behind.

(1) Whispering winds wandered westward,(2) Weaving through willow trees.(3) Soft sounds slipped through branches,(4) Singing across the field.

(5) No drum announced their arrival.(6) No crowd gathered nearby.(7) The breeze moved quietly,(8) Barely noticed at first.

(9) Whisper, whisper, whisper went the wind.(10) Whisper through the tall green grass.(11) Whisper over stones and streams.(12) Whisper beneath the clouds.

(13) A lone hiker paused to listen.(14) Then another stopped along the path.(15) The gentle sounds encouraged silence,(16) Inviting careful thought.

(17) Willow branches waved and whirled.(18) Wildflowers bent and bobbed below.(19) The repeated sounds seemed to linger,(20) Like a memory that would not leave.

(21) Whisper, whisper, whisper went the wind.(22) Whisper through the fading light.(23) Its rhythm moved more slowly now,(24) Calm and steady as evening approached.

(25) The wind carried no instructions.(26) It offered no grand celebration.(27) Yet its quiet song seemed to say,(28) “Pause. Notice. Reflect.”

(29) Whisper, whisper, whisper went the wind.(30) Whisper into the coming night.(31) The sound eventually disappeared,(32) But the feeling remained.

questions.text:
In Poem 1, the repeated comparison in lines (14) and (24)—"Thump, thump, thump like a patient heart" and the rhythm that seems to say "Come together. Stay awhile."—most strongly suggests that the drumbeat is meant to seem
A  welcoming and life-giving, like something that gently sustains the community
B  threatening and demanding, forcing the town to obey a command
C  mysterious and confusing, leaving the neighbors unsure what to do
D  sad and mournful, reminding the crowd of times that have passed

Which statement best describes a theme developed across Poem 2 and how the poet develops it?
A  Quiet, repeated sounds can invite reflection; the poet develops this through the gentle, lingering "Whisper" refrain and the wind's message to "Pause. Notice. Reflect." (28)
B  Nature is dangerous and should be feared; the poet develops this through the wind that "carried no instructions" (25)
C  Crowds are necessary for any experience to matter; the poet develops this by showing "No crowd gathered nearby" (6)
D  Loud announcements are the best way to gather people; the poet develops this through "No drum announced their arrival" (5)

Both poems use a repeated refrain ("Thump, thump, thump" in Poem 1 and "Whisper, whisper, whisper" in Poem 2). Select two ways the structure of these refrains works similarly in BOTH poems to build meaning.
A  Each refrain returns at intervals throughout the poem, framing the action and giving it a steady pulse
B  Each refrain's final repetition is immediately followed by a closing line stating that, even after the sound stops, a feeling "remained" (Poem 1, line 28; Poem 2, line 32)
C  Each refrain grows louder and faster every time it returns until the poem reaches a shout
D  Each refrain is spoken aloud by a named character who explains its meaning to the crowd
E  Each refrain announces a sudden, surprising event that interrupts the calm of the poem

Although both poems end with a "feeling" that remains, the poems present sound differently: Poem 1 treats sound as a public call that gathers people, while Poem 2 treats sound as [ ▾ ].
a private invitation that draws each listener inward toward quiet thought
a warning that drives the listeners apart and sends them home
a competition that the wind is trying to win against the drum
a command that the hikers are required to obey at once

writingPrompt.text: Which poem uses repeated words and sounds more effectively to develop its meaning? Write a multi-paragraph response in which you explain your opinion about how the poet’s use of repeated words, sounds, and rhythm contributes to the meaning of the poem, using specific evidence from both passages. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passages; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: introduce a clear central idea; develop your opinion with relevant details from both poems; use evidence from the passages; and organize your ideas clearly.

passages.title: The Last Crystal
passages.text:
1 Aria tightened her grip on the dragon's harness as they soared above the Cloudpeak Mountains. Below her, jagged cliffs rose through layers of mist like stone towers guarding the kingdom. Beside her, Emberwing's bronze scales shimmered in the morning sunlight. Most dragon riders spent years dreaming of this view, but today Aria barely noticed it.

2 Her thoughts remained fixed on the village below. Three weeks earlier, a strange sickness had spread through the valley. Crops withered overnight, streams dried into narrow trickles, and even the ancient silver trees surrounding the village had begun to lose their leaves. The elders searched for answers, and the healers tried every remedy they knew, but nothing worked.

3 Then an old map was discovered inside the kingdom's library. According to faded notes written along its edges, a magical crystal rested deep within the Dragon Caverns. The crystal supposedly possessed enough power to restore life to the valley. However, a warning accompanied the map. The crystal drew its energy from the same ancient magic that gave dragons their abilities. If the crystal were used, dragon magic would disappear forever.

4 No one knew whether the warning was true. No one wanted to find out. Yet every day the valley grew weaker. Eventually, the kingdom's leaders agreed that someone had to find the crystal. As one of the youngest dragon riders in the kingdom, Aria volunteered to make the journey.

5 As they flew, Aria glanced toward Emberwing. The dragon's golden eyes met hers. "Do you think the warning is real?" she asked. Emberwing released a low rumble. Dragon speech was rare and difficult, but Emberwing had always been different.

6 "Possibly," he replied, his voice echoing like distant thunder.

7 Aria sighed. That wasn't the answer she wanted. Soon a dark opening appeared in the mountainside. The entrance to the Dragon Caverns stretched across the cliff face, surrounded by ancient symbols carved into stone. Emberwing landed near the entrance, and together they stepped inside.

8 The air felt cool and still. Crystals embedded in the walls glowed with soft blue light. The deeper they traveled, the brighter the crystals became. Eventually, the tunnel opened into a vast chamber, and Aria stopped in amazement.

9 A massive crystal floated above a pool of silver water. Light flowed through its center like liquid stars. The entire cavern seemed alive. "It's beautiful," Aria whispered.

10 Emberwing lowered his head. "It is older than the kingdom itself," he said.

11 As they approached, a voice suddenly filled the chamber.

12 "Who seeks the Heart Crystal?"

13 Aria spun around, but no one stood nearby. The voice seemed to come from everywhere at once.

14 "I do," she replied.

15 The crystal brightened. "For what purpose?"

16 Aria hesitated. "My village is dying."

17 Images suddenly appeared within the crystal's light. She saw wilted fields and empty streams. She saw worried families gathered in the village square. Then the images changed. Dragons filled the sky, breathing fire, shaping clouds, and carrying riders across distant lands. Slowly, the images faded. The dragons' magic disappeared, and the sky grew quiet.

18 "One future saves the valley," the voice explained. "The other preserves dragon magic. You may choose only one."

19 Aria's stomach tightened. She had known this decision was coming, but facing it felt entirely different. She looked at Emberwing. "What would you choose?" she asked.

20 The dragon remained silent for several moments. Finally, he spoke. "My kind has protected humans for centuries. Magic is a gift, but lives are precious. Only you can decide which matters more today."

21 Aria stepped closer to the crystal. The silver light reflected in her eyes. She thought about the children who played beneath the silver trees. She thought about the farmers struggling to save their fields. She thought about the dragons who had shared their skies with humans for generations.

22 Neither choice felt right. Saving the valley would protect countless lives, but it might end the magic that dragons had possessed for centuries. Preserving dragon magic would protect an important part of the kingdom's history, but the valley might never recover.

23 Slowly, Aria raised her hand toward the crystal. The chamber fell silent. Even the glowing water seemed to pause. Her fingers hovered inches from the crystal's surface as she considered the future of the valley and the future of the dragons.

24 Aria took a deep breath. Then she made her choice.

questions.text:
How does the conversation between Aria and Emberwing in paragraphs 19-20 most affect the decision Aria faces?
A  It convinces Aria that the warning on the map is definitely false, so she stops worrying about losing dragon magic.
B  It places the full weight of the choice on Aria by making clear that even Emberwing will not decide for her.
C  It reveals that Emberwing secretly wants Aria to preserve dragon magic above all else.
D  It persuades Aria to turn back and let the kingdom's leaders make the decision instead.

Which statement best expresses a theme developed across the passage?
A  Meaningful choices often require weighing one valuable thing against another, with no easy answer.
B  Ancient maps usually contain warnings that turn out to be false.
C  Dragons are wiser than humans and should be trusted to make important decisions.
D  The youngest members of a community are always the bravest.

In paragraph 6, Emberwing's voice is described as 'echoing like distant thunder.' This comparison mainly emphasizes that his voice is [ ▾ ].
deep and powerful
frightening and threatening
quiet and gentle
cheerful and lighthearted

Select two details that BEST support the idea that Aria's decision is a heavy emotional burden for her.
A  "Most dragon riders spent years dreaming of this view, but today Aria barely noticed it." (paragraph 1)
B  "Crystals embedded in the walls glowed with soft blue light." (paragraph 8)
C  "Aria's stomach tightened. She had known this decision was coming, but facing it felt entirely different." (paragraph 19)
D  "It is older than the kingdom itself," he said. (paragraph 10)
E  "The entrance to the Dragon Caverns stretched across the cliff face, surrounded by ancient symbols carved into stone." (paragraph 7)

writingPrompt.text: Should Aria have chosen to save the valley, even if it meant dragons would lose their magic forever? Write a multi-paragraph response in which you explain your opinion about Aria's decision, using specific evidence found in the passage. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passage; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: introduce a clear central idea; develop your opinion with relevant reasons and details; use evidence from the passage; and organize your ideas clearly.

passages.title: The Signal
passages.text:
1 Fourteen-year-old Samuel Carter pulled his wool coat tighter as he stepped onto the train platform. Snow swirled through the air, covering the tracks in a thin layer of white. It was January 1863, and the small railroad station in Pennsylvania seemed quieter than usual. Samuel missed the noise. He missed the steady arrival of trains carrying passengers, newspapers, and supplies.

2 Most of all, he missed his father. Nearly a year earlier, Mr. Carter had left home to serve in the Union Army during the Civil War. Since then, letters arrived whenever they could, but months sometimes passed without any news. Samuel read each letter so many times that he nearly had them memorized.

3 Samuel's uncle, who managed the railroad station, had recently offered him a job delivering messages and helping maintain the telegraph equipment. Samuel accepted immediately. The work kept him busy and allowed him to feel connected to events beyond his small town.

4 On that particular morning, Samuel entered the telegraph office and found his uncle studying a map spread across a wooden desk. Several railroad routes had been marked in red pencil.

5 "Something happened overnight," his uncle said. "A bridge west of town was damaged during the storm."

6 Samuel frowned. "Can it be repaired?"

7 "Eventually," his uncle replied. "The problem is that a military supply train is expected this afternoon. If the train reaches the bridge before receiving a warning, there could be a serious accident."

8 Samuel glanced toward the telegraph machine. The metal instrument sat silent on the desk.

9 "Can't we send a message?" he asked.

10 His uncle shook his head. "The storm damaged the telegraph lines. We've been unable to reach the next station all morning."

11 Samuel looked out the window. Snow continued to fall. Somewhere beyond the hills, a train loaded with supplies for Union soldiers was moving steadily toward the damaged bridge.

12 "There has to be another way," Samuel said.

13 His uncle nodded slowly. "Perhaps. The next station is eight miles away. If someone could reach it before the train arrives, the stationmaster could stop the train."

14 Samuel immediately understood what his uncle was suggesting.

15 The roads would be difficult to travel. Snow covered the countryside, and daylight was already beginning to fade behind thick clouds. Even under ideal conditions, the trip would take hours.

16 "I'll go," Samuel said.

17 His uncle looked surprised. "It's dangerous."

18 "The train is carrying supplies for soldiers," Samuel replied. "And if no one warns them, people could get hurt."

19 For several seconds, the room remained silent. Finally, his uncle handed him a folded note containing details about the damaged bridge.

20 "Then don't waste any time," he said.

21 Samuel borrowed a horse from a nearby farmer and began the journey. Snowflakes stung his face as the horse pushed through drifts that grew deeper with every mile. Several times he considered turning back. The cold seemed to settle into his bones.

22 Yet each time he thought about stopping, he remembered his father's letters. They often described soldiers continuing their duties despite exhaustion, fear, and difficult conditions.

23 "Do the job in front of you," his father had written in one letter. "Others are depending on it."

24 Samuel repeated those words quietly as he rode.

25 Hours later, he spotted the station in the distance. Light glowed from its windows. Relief washed over him.

26 He urged the horse forward and arrived just as a station worker stepped outside.

27 Samuel hurried inside and delivered the note. The stationmaster immediately read the warning and rushed toward the telegraph equipment.

28 Fortunately, the lines east of the station remained operational. Within minutes, messages were being sent along the railroad.

29 Everyone waited.

30 Finally, a reply arrived.

31 The supply train had received the warning.

32 It would stop before reaching the damaged bridge.

33 A cheer filled the room.

34 Samuel sank into a chair, exhausted but smiling.

35 Several weeks later, a letter arrived from his father. News of Samuel's journey had spread through the railroad network and eventually reached the army camp.

36 "I heard about what you did," the letter read. "You helped people you'll probably never meet. That's the kind of courage that keeps a country moving forward."

37 Samuel folded the letter carefully and slipped it into his pocket.

38 Outside, another train whistle echoed through town.

39 The war would continue, and many challenges still lay ahead. But for one winter day, Samuel had found a way to make a difference.

questions.text:
Which incident in the story most directly provokes Samuel's decision to make the dangerous journey himself?
A  His uncle explains that the telegraph lines are down and the next station is eight miles away (paragraphs 10, 13).
B  Samuel reads his father's letters so many times that he nearly has them memorized (paragraph 2).
C  Samuel borrows a horse from a nearby farmer and pushes through the deepening drifts (paragraph 21).
D  A station worker steps outside just as Samuel arrives at the next station (paragraph 26).

Which statement best expresses a theme developed over the course of the story?
A  Quiet acts of duty done for strangers can require real courage and make a meaningful difference.
B  Modern technology such as the telegraph is the most reliable way to keep people safe.
C  Families separated by war can never truly stay connected through letters alone.
D  Young people should avoid dangerous tasks and leave them to experienced adults.

In paragraph 21, the narrator says the cold "seemed to settle into his bones." This phrase shows that the cold was [ ▾ ].
deep and exhausting, weakening him from within
mild and easily ignored as he rode
freezing the horse rather than Samuel
a pleasant relief after the warm office

How does Samuel's point of view about making the journey differ from his uncle's at the moment Samuel volunteers? Select two statements that are true.
A  Samuel focuses on the people who could be hurt, while his uncle focuses first on the danger to Samuel.
B  Samuel sees the trip as worth the risk, while his uncle is hesitant and surprised that Samuel offers.
C  Samuel believes the telegraph will still work, while his uncle is certain it is broken.
D  Samuel wants to wait for better weather, while his uncle insists they leave immediately.
E  Samuel thinks the bridge can be repaired in time, while his uncle thinks an accident is unavoidable.

writingPrompt.text: Did Samuel make the right decision to travel through dangerous conditions to deliver the warning? Write a multi-paragraph response in which you explain your opinion about Samuel's decision, using specific evidence found in the passage. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passage; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: introduce a clear central idea; develop your opinion with relevant reasons and details; use evidence from the passage; and organize your ideas clearly.

passages.title: The Memory Garden
passages.text:
1 In the year 2148, nearly every city on Earth used Memory Gardens. These carefully designed parks contained advanced technology that allowed visitors to relive important moments from their lives. Small silver devices hidden beneath the pathways projected realistic images and sounds, creating experiences that felt almost real.

2 Sixteen-year-old Lena visited the Memory Garden every Saturday. Most people came to revisit birthdays, vacations, or family celebrations. Lena always chose a different memory.

3 She selected moments involving her grandmother, who had passed away several years earlier. Through the garden's technology, Lena could see the familiar smile on her grandmother's face and hear stories she had told countless times.

4 One afternoon, Lena sat beneath a glowing blue tree while a memory played nearby. Her grandmother appeared to be standing beside a lake, laughing as she taught Lena how to skip stones across the water.

5 For several minutes, Lena watched silently. Then a message appeared on her wrist screen.

6 Memory Session Ending in Five Minutes.

7 Lena sighed. Every visit seemed too short.

8 As she walked toward the exit, she noticed a group of students her age discussing the Memory Gardens.

9 "I don't understand why people keep coming here," one student said. "Those moments already happened. Wouldn't it be better to focus on making new memories instead of replaying old ones?"

10 Another student disagreed. "The memories help people remember what matters. My grandfather says the garden keeps important stories alive."

11 Lena continued walking, thinking about their conversation.

12 Later that evening, she opened a photo album at home. The pictures looked simple compared to the advanced projections used in the gardens, yet they still brought back memories.

13 She wondered whether technology truly preserved the past or whether memories lived within people regardless of the tools they used.

14 The next Saturday, Lena returned to the garden. As another memory began to play, she noticed something she had overlooked before. The memory itself never changed, but her understanding of it did.

15 Each visit revealed details she had missed. Sometimes she focused on her grandmother's words. Other times she noticed the setting, the expressions, or the emotions behind the conversation.

16 Standing beneath the blue tree, Lena realized the garden was not simply helping people remember the past.

17 It was helping them discover new meaning in it.

passages.title: The Horizon Project
passages.text:
1 In the year 2148, scientists completed the Horizon Project, a system designed to predict possible future events. Using enormous amounts of data, powerful computers could create detailed simulations showing what might happen weeks, months, or even years ahead.

2 Governments, businesses, and schools quickly adopted the technology.

3 Sixteen-year-old Kai first encountered the Horizon Project during a career-planning course. Students were allowed to view simulations showing several possible versions of their future.

4 Curious, Kai entered the simulation chamber.

5 Moments later, dozens of possible futures appeared on the screen around him.

6 In one future, he became an engineer designing transportation systems on Mars.

7 In another, he worked as a teacher.

8 Several futures showed careers he had never considered.

9 When the session ended, Kai felt overwhelmed.

10 The technology had not provided answers. Instead, it had created more questions.

11 During lunch, students debated whether the Horizon Project was helpful.

12 "I'd rather know what opportunities are ahead," one student said. "The simulations help people make better choices."

13 Another student disagreed. "If you spend too much time looking at possible futures, you might miss what's happening right now."

14 Kai thought about those comments throughout the week.

15 He returned to the simulation center several times. Each visit generated slightly different outcomes because every choice created new possibilities.

16 Eventually, Kai noticed something interesting.

17 The simulations that seemed most successful were not always the ones that appeared happiest.

18 Some futures included achievements but little satisfaction. Others involved unexpected challenges but meaningful relationships and experiences.

19 The more futures Kai explored, the less interested he became in predicting exactly what would happen.

20 Instead, he began paying closer attention to the decisions he made each day.

21 The Horizon Project had been designed to help people see the future.

22 Yet for Kai, its greatest value was reminding him that the future was still being created.

questions.text:
Which statement best expresses a theme developed in Passage 1?
A  Advanced technology will eventually replace simple objects such as photo albums.
B  Revisiting the past can lead a person to discover new meaning rather than only recall facts.
C  People should focus on creating new memories instead of dwelling on old ones.
D  Grief becomes easier to manage when it is shared with others one's own age.

In Passage 2, how does Kai's repeated use of the simulation chamber most affect his thinking?
A  It convinces him to choose the career as a Mars engineer shown in one future.
B  It shifts his attention away from predicting the future and toward the daily choices he makes.
C  It proves to him that the Horizon Project's predictions are usually inaccurate.
D  It makes him decide that looking at possible futures is a waste of time.

Read this sentence from Passage 2: 'The technology had not provided answers. Instead, it had created more questions' (paragraph 10). In this context, the word 'created' most nearly means [ ▾ ].
destroyed
produced
imagined
answered

Select two ways the structures of Passage 1 and Passage 2 are alike.
A  Both passages are arranged as a single continuous flashback to an earlier time in the character's life.
B  Both passages include a debate between minor characters that presents two opposing views of the technology.
C  Both passages are told from the first-person point of view of the main character.
D  Both passages end with a final statement that reveals the technology's deeper purpose to the main character.
E  Both passages open by explaining a real historical event from the year 2148.

writingPrompt.text: Which technology has a greater impact on people's lives: the Memory Garden in Passage 1 or the Horizon Project in Passage 2? Write a multi-paragraph response in which you explain your opinion about which technology is more meaningful or beneficial, using specific evidence from both passages to support your ideas. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passages; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: introduce a clear central idea; develop your opinion with relevant reasons and details; use evidence from the passages; and organize your ideas clearly.

passages.title: The Mural
passages.text:
1 Every afternoon, fourteen-year-old Sofia walked past the blank brick wall behind the community center. The wall stretched nearly the length of a basketball court, and for years it had remained covered with faded paint and graffiti.

2 One Saturday, Sofia noticed a sign posted near the wall. The community center was looking for volunteers to help create a mural that would celebrate the history of the neighborhood. Anyone could submit ideas.

3 Sofia stopped to read the sign twice. She loved drawing, but she had never shared her artwork outside of school. Most of her sketches stayed tucked inside a notebook she carried in her backpack.

4 Later that evening, she mentioned the project to her older brother.

5 "You should enter a design," he said.

6 Sofia shook her head. "There will probably be artists who are much better than I am."

7 "Maybe," her brother replied. "But if you don't try, you'll never know."

8 During the next week, Sofia couldn't stop thinking about the mural. She began sketching ideas after school. Some drawings featured important landmarks. Others included scenes from neighborhood festivals and community events.

9 Eventually, she combined her favorite ideas into one design and submitted it.

10 Several weeks later, Sofia received surprising news. Her design had been selected.

11 At first, she felt excited. Then she felt nervous.

12 Painting in her sketchbook was one thing. Painting a mural that hundreds of people would see every day felt entirely different.

13 Work on the mural began the following month. Volunteers of all ages showed up to help. Some painted backgrounds while others focused on details.

14 Sofia spent hours working alongside people she had never met before. As they painted, neighbors shared stories about the community's past. Older residents pointed out places that had changed over the years.

15 Slowly, the blank wall transformed into something colorful and meaningful.

16 On the day the mural was completed, dozens of people gathered to see it. Sofia stood near the back of the crowd as everyone admired the finished artwork.

17 "You should go stand by it," her brother said.

18 Sofia smiled.

19 For the first time, she felt proud that she had decided to share something she created.

passages.title: The Community Garden
passages.text:
1 Every afternoon, fourteen-year-old Marcus rode his bicycle past an empty lot near his apartment building. The space was filled with weeds and scattered trash, and most people ignored it.

2 One spring, a neighborhood organization announced plans to turn the lot into a community garden. Volunteers were needed to help prepare the space.

3 Marcus signed up immediately.

4 He had never grown vegetables before, but he liked the idea of improving the neighborhood.

5 During the first few weeks, the work was harder than he expected. Volunteers cleared trash, removed weeds, and built planting beds. Some days the progress seemed slow.

6 A few of Marcus's friends questioned why he spent so much time there.

7 "It's just a garden," one friend said.

8 Marcus wasn't sure how to explain it.

9 The garden was about more than planting vegetables. It was about creating something people could use and enjoy together.

10 Throughout the summer, the garden began to change. Green plants filled the beds. Flowers appeared along the fence. Neighbors stopped to ask questions or offer help.

11 Marcus especially enjoyed meeting people he otherwise would never have known.

12 An elderly resident taught him how to care for tomato plants. A younger student helped water seedlings after school.

13 By late summer, the garden was thriving.

14 Families visited regularly. Some harvested vegetables. Others simply enjoyed spending time outdoors.

15 One afternoon, Marcus parked his bicycle near the entrance and looked across the garden.

16 It was difficult to believe the area had once been an empty lot.

17 The project had changed the space, but it had also changed the people who used it.

18 Marcus realized that even small efforts could have a lasting impact when people worked together.

19 He smiled as another group of volunteers arrived to help.

20 The work wasn't finished, but the garden had already become an important part of the community.

questions.text:
In "The Mural," how does the conversation between Sofia and her brother in paragraphs 5-7 most contribute to the development of the plot?
A  It reveals that Sofia's brother is a more skilled artist than she is, which discourages her from entering.
B  It pushes Sofia past her self-doubt and prompts her decision to create and submit a design.
C  It shows that Sofia has always wanted public recognition for the artwork in her notebook.
D  It establishes that the community center will only accept designs from experienced artists.

Read this sentence from paragraph 12 of "The Mural."

"Painting in her sketchbook was one thing. Painting a mural that hundreds of people would see every day felt entirely different."

In this sentence, the phrase "felt entirely different" mainly emphasizes Sofia's sense that [ ▾ ].
the mural would be more public and exposed than her private drawings
painting a mural required completely new artistic skills
her sketchbook drawings were no longer worth keeping
the neighbors would dislike a mural they could see daily

A theme shared by both "The Mural" and "The Community Garden" is that meaningful change happens when individuals contribute to their community. Select two details, one from each passage, that best develop this shared theme.
A  "For the first time, she felt proud that she had decided to share something she created." ("The Mural," paragraph 19)
B  "Most of her sketches stayed tucked inside a notebook she carried in her backpack." ("The Mural," paragraph 3)
C  "A few of Marcus's friends questioned why he spent so much time there." ("The Community Garden," paragraph 6)
D  "Marcus realized that even small efforts could have a lasting impact when people worked together." ("The Community Garden," paragraph 18)
E  "Every afternoon, fourteen-year-old Marcus rode his bicycle past an empty lot near his apartment building." ("The Community Garden," paragraph 1)

How are the structures of "The Mural" and "The Community Garden" most similar?
A  Both passages begin at the moment a project is completed and then flash back to explain how it started.
B  Both passages move from a neglected, unused space toward a transformed space that brings people together.
C  Both passages are told from the first-person point of view of the main character.
D  Both passages are organized as a series of letters exchanged between two neighbors.

writingPrompt.text: Which character makes a greater contribution to the community: Sofia in "The Mural" or Marcus in "The Community Garden"? Write a multi-paragraph response in which you explain your opinion about which character has a greater impact, using specific evidence from both passages to support your ideas. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passages; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: introduce a clear central idea; develop your opinion with relevant reasons and details; use evidence from the passages; and organize your ideas clearly.

title: The Keeper of the Northern Lights

passages.title: The Keeper of the Northern Lights
passages.text:
1 Long ago, before maps covered the world and before people understood the movement of the stars, a small village stood near the edge of a frozen sea. During the long winter months, darkness settled over the land for many hours each day. The villagers depended on firelight and moonlight to guide them through the cold nights. Among the villagers lived a curious girl named Elara, who spent much of her time studying the sky. She knew the names of many constellations and often climbed a snowy hill overlooking the sea so she could watch the stars.

2 One mystery fascinated Elara more than any other. Several times each winter, ribbons of green and silver light appeared across the night sky. The lights shimmered, twisted, and danced above the village before fading away. No one could explain where they came from. Some villagers believed the lights were messages from distant spirits, while others claimed they were reflections from a hidden kingdom beyond the horizon. Although many stories existed, no one knew the truth.

3 One particularly cold evening, Elara climbed the hill once again. The sky was clear, and thousands of stars sparkled overhead. As she watched, a wave of green light spread across the darkness. The ribbons grew brighter and brighter until they seemed close enough to touch. Then, to her surprise, a figure stepped from the glowing light. The stranger wore a cloak that shimmered like the sky itself, and tiny points of light moved across the fabric as if stars had been woven into every thread.

4 "Do not be afraid," the figure said kindly. "I am the Keeper of the Sky." Elara had many questions, but one rose above the rest. She pointed toward the glowing ribbons and asked where they came from. The Keeper smiled and explained that every evening the stars traveled across the sky carrying dreams, hopes, and stories gathered from people around the world. Most nights, the stars moved quietly, but sometimes their burdens became too heavy to carry.

5 The Keeper extended a hand toward the lights. He explained that when the stars could no longer hold everything they had collected, they released a small portion of their brightness into the sky. According to the Keeper, the glowing ribbons were made from those released dreams and stories. They served as reminders that hope still existed, even during the darkest nights. Elara watched the colors dance overhead and wondered why the lights appeared above her village so often.

6 The Keeper explained that the lights visited places that understood darkness. Because the villagers experienced long winters and many hours without sunlight, they especially needed reminders that brighter days would return. Elara listened carefully as the ribbons stretched across the horizon. For a long time, neither she nor the Keeper spoke. Together they watched the lights move across the sky like waves drifting through the night.

7 Eventually, the glowing ribbons began to fade. The Keeper's cloak shimmered more brightly, and Elara realized their conversation was ending. Before disappearing, the Keeper told her to remember the purpose of the lights whenever they returned. Then he stepped back into the glow and vanished. Elara hurried home and shared the story with the villagers. Some believed her immediately, while others remained uncertain.

8 From that day forward, however, the villagers looked at the lights differently. Whenever the ribbons of color appeared overhead, families gathered outside to watch them. Instead of focusing on the long darkness of winter, they remembered the Keeper's message about hope and brighter days ahead. According to the legend, the northern lights still dance across the sky whenever the stars release their collected dreams and stories, filling the darkness with reminders that light can always return.

questions.text:
Which statement BEST expresses a theme that develops over the course of the myth?
A  Curiosity about the natural world will always be rewarded with a magical encounter.
B  Hope can endure and be renewed even during the longest and darkest times.
C  Villages near frozen seas must rely on firelight and moonlight to survive winter.
D  Spirits and hidden kingdoms are the true source of all unexplained lights in the sky.

The myth conveys that the northern lights carry a message of hope specifically for this village. Select two details that provide the STRONGEST evidence for this idea.
A  "Several times each winter, ribbons of green and silver light appeared across the night sky." (paragraph 2)
B  "The Keeper explained that the lights visited places that understood darkness." (paragraph 6)
C  "Because the villagers experienced long winters and many hours without sunlight, they especially needed reminders that brighter days would return." (paragraph 6)
D  "She knew the names of many constellations and often climbed a snowy hill overlooking the sea." (paragraph 1)
E  "Some believed her immediately, while others remained uncertain." (paragraph 7)

Read this sentence from paragraph 4: "Most nights, the stars moved quietly, but sometimes their burdens became too heavy to carry." As it is used in the sentence, the word "burdens" suggests that the stars are [ ▾ ].
carrying loads of dreams and stories that are difficult to hold
traveling more slowly than they normally do across the sky
frightened by the darkness that covers the village in winter
competing with one another to shine the brightest in the sky

How does the Keeper's conversation with Elara in paragraphs 4 through 6 function in the myth as a whole?
A  It creates conflict between Elara and the Keeper that drives the rest of the plot.
B  It reveals that Elara no longer trusts the constellations she once studied.
C  It provides the explanation that changes how the villagers come to view the lights.
D  It shows that the Keeper regrets sharing the secret of the northern lights.

writingPrompt.text: What is the more important purpose of the northern lights in the myth: to inspire hope or to explain a mystery of nature? Write a multi-paragraph response in which you explain your opinion about the purpose of the northern lights in the story, using specific evidence found in the passage. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passage; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: introduce a clear central idea; develop your opinion with relevant reasons and details; use evidence from the passage; and organize your ideas clearly.

passages.title: The Fox and the Well
passages.text:
1 During a long, dry summer, the animals of Oak Meadow struggled to find water. Streams that usually flowed through the forest had slowed to a trickle, and many ponds had nearly disappeared. Each day, animals traveled farther from their homes in search of water. Among them was a fox named Felix, who was known throughout the meadow for his clever ideas and quick thinking.

2 One afternoon, while exploring a part of the forest he had never visited before, Felix discovered an old stone well hidden among a cluster of trees. Curious, he peered over the edge and saw clear water shining far below. Delighted by his discovery, Felix quickly climbed down a narrow path built into the side of the well and drank until he was no longer thirsty.

3 Afterward, Felix looked around and realized he had a problem. The path he had used to climb down had partially crumbled. Loose stones covered several sections, making it impossible to climb back out. Felix tried jumping and scrambling up the wall, but each attempt ended with him sliding back to the bottom.

4 As the afternoon passed, Felix grew worried. He knew the well contained enough water to keep him comfortable, but he could not remain there forever. He needed a way to escape.

5 Just as the sun began to set, a goat named Greta wandered through the trees. Like many animals in Oak Meadow, she had spent the day searching for water. When she noticed the old well, she walked over and looked inside.

6 "Felix!" Greta exclaimed. "What are you doing down there?"

7 Felix immediately saw an opportunity. "I've found the finest water in the entire forest," he replied. "It's cool, fresh, and plentiful. In fact, I think every animal in Oak Meadow should come here."

8 Greta looked down at the sparkling water. "Really?" she asked. "I've been searching all day for a good place to drink."

9 "Then you've found it," Felix said. "Why don't you come down and see for yourself?"

10 Greta hesitated. She trusted Felix's intelligence, but she also noticed that he had not mentioned how he planned to leave the well. "If the water is so wonderful," she asked, "why haven't you climbed out to tell everyone?"

11 Felix quickly searched for an answer. "I was simply enjoying the peace and quiet," he said. "Besides, I thought someone else might appreciate discovering this place before the crowds arrive."

12 Greta studied the fox carefully. She had heard many stories about Felix's cleverness. Most were impressive, but some suggested that he occasionally used his intelligence to benefit himself rather than others.

13 Instead of climbing into the well, Greta sat beneath a nearby tree and thought about the situation. The longer she considered Felix's explanation, the less convincing it seemed. If the water was truly so valuable, why did he appear so eager for someone else to join him?

14 Finally, Greta stood and shook her head. "The water may be excellent," she said, "but I think I'll keep looking."

15 Felix's ears drooped. "Wait!" he called. "Don't leave."

16 Greta looked back.

17 "The truth is that I'm trapped," Felix admitted. "I wanted you to climb down so I could use your back to help me escape."

18 Greta frowned. "You could have simply asked for help."

19 Felix lowered his head. He knew she was right.

20 After a moment, Greta found a long branch and lowered it into the well. Felix grabbed the branch and carefully climbed to safety. Once he reached the top, he thanked Greta for her help.

21 As they walked away, Greta offered one final piece of advice. "Being clever can solve many problems," she said, "but honesty often solves them better."

22 Felix nodded thoughtfully. For perhaps the first time, he realized that intelligence was most valuable when it was combined with trust.

23 From that day forward, the fox remained clever, but he worked harder to earn the confidence of the other animals. And whenever young animals asked about the old well, Felix shared the story of how one honest goat had taught him an important lesson.

questions.text:
How does Greta's decision in paragraphs 13-14 most propel the action of the story?
A  By refusing to climb into the well, she forces Felix to abandon his trickery and admit the truth about his situation.
B  By choosing to keep searching for water, she convinces the other animals of Oak Meadow to avoid the well.
C  By sitting beneath the tree to think, she gives Felix enough time to climb out of the well on his own.
D  By questioning Felix's cleverness, she causes him to become angry and refuse her offer of help.

Which statement best expresses a theme of the fable and how it develops across the story?
A  Cleverness alone can fail, and honesty earns the trust that makes problems easier to solve.
B  Animals must travel far from home to survive when natural resources become scarce.
C  Those who are too cautious to take risks will miss valuable opportunities.
D  Friends should always share their discoveries with the entire community.

The reader understands something about Felix's situation that Greta does not yet know in paragraphs 7-11. Select two statements that explain how this difference in knowledge creates irony in the passage.
A  The reader knows Felix is trapped, so his praise of the well as a place 'every animal in Oak Meadow should come here' (paragraph 7) is really an attempt to use others to escape.
B  The reader knows from paragraph 3 that the path crumbled, so Felix's claim that he stayed because he 'was simply enjoying the peace and quiet' (paragraph 11) is clearly an excuse.
C  The reader knows the water is poisoned, making Felix's invitation to drink a threat to Greta.
D  The reader learns before Greta that the well belongs to another animal who will punish trespassers.
E  The reader sees that Greta is more clever than Felix and has planned the rescue from the very beginning.

Read this sentence from paragraph 7: 'Felix immediately saw an opportunity.' In this context, the word 'opportunity' most nearly means a chance to [ ▾ ].
take advantage of Greta for his own benefit
share fresh water fairly with a friend
escape danger by warning others quickly
rest quietly without being disturbed

writingPrompt.text: Which lesson is more important in the fable: that honesty is better than trickery or that people should think carefully before trusting others? Write a multi-paragraph response in which you explain your opinion about the most important lesson of the story, using specific evidence found in the passage. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passage; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: introduce a clear central idea; develop your opinion with relevant reasons and details; use evidence from the passage; and organize your ideas clearly.

passages.title: The Legend of Iron Jack
passages.text:
1 More than one hundred years ago, a railroad worker named Jack Turner lived in a small mountain town. Historical records confirm that Jack helped build several sections of track that connected remote communities to larger cities. Over time, however, stories about his accomplishments grew larger than life. Eventually, he became known throughout the region as "Iron Jack."

2 According to local legend, Iron Jack possessed extraordinary strength. Townspeople claimed he could lift railroad ties without assistance and carry tools that required several workers to move. Some stories even suggested that he once pushed a stalled train uphill during a snowstorm. Although no evidence supports these claims, they became popular parts of the legend.

3 One of the most famous stories took place during a spring flood. After several days of heavy rain, a river near the railroad rose far above its normal level. The rushing water threatened a bridge that connected two towns. According to the legend, workers feared the bridge would collapse before an important passenger train could cross safely.

4 The story says that Jack rushed to the bridge and noticed a large support beam beginning to crack. While other workers searched for supplies, Jack supposedly climbed beneath the bridge and held the damaged beam in place with his bare hands. Moments later, the passenger train crossed safely. Only after the last train car reached the opposite side did Jack allow the bridge to settle.

5 Historians believe a flood likely occurred and that workers probably repaired the bridge in time to prevent an accident. However, there is no evidence that a single worker held up an entire bridge. Even so, the story continued to spread from town to town.

6 Over the years, the legend of Iron Jack came to represent determination and hard work. Schools, businesses, and community events were named after him. Although many details were undoubtedly exaggerated, people admired the idea of someone willing to do whatever was necessary to help others.

7 Today, visitors can still find statues of Iron Jack throughout the region. Most people understand that the stories are not entirely factual. Nevertheless, the legend continues to inspire those who hear it.

passages.title: The Legend of Captain Elena
passages.text:
1 In the late 1700s, a ship captain named Elena Morales sailed along a rugged coastline known for dangerous storms and hidden rocks. Historical documents show that Captain Elena guided trading vessels through difficult waters and earned a reputation as a skilled navigator. As years passed, however, stories about her achievements became increasingly dramatic.

2 According to legend, Captain Elena possessed an almost magical ability to predict storms. Sailors claimed she could study the clouds for a few moments and know exactly when severe weather would arrive. Some stories even suggested that seabirds followed her ship because they trusted her sense of direction.

3 The most famous tale occurred during a violent storm that threatened several ships near the coast. Strong winds pushed waves high into the air, and visibility became nearly impossible. The story says that sailors feared their ships would crash into the rocky shoreline before morning.

4 As the legend tells it, Captain Elena climbed to the highest point on her ship and raised a lantern above her head. The light reportedly burned brighter than any ordinary flame and remained visible despite the wind and rain. One by one, nearby ships followed the lantern's glow through the darkness.

5 By sunrise, every vessel had safely reached the harbor. Sailors celebrated Captain Elena's bravery, and stories about the glowing lantern quickly spread across the region. Some versions of the tale even claim that the lantern could be seen from miles away through the storm clouds.

6 Historians believe Captain Elena likely helped guide ships during dangerous weather. However, there is no evidence that a single lantern remained visible over such a great distance during a severe storm. Like many legends, the story appears to combine real events with dramatic embellishments.

7 Even so, Captain Elena became a symbol of leadership and courage. Her story was shared by generations of sailors, and her name eventually appeared on maps, schools, and public buildings. Although the legend may not be completely accurate, it continues to remind people of the importance of helping others during difficult times.

questions.text:
Both passages develop a similar theme about how legends form over time. Which statement BEST expresses this shared theme?
A  People create legends mainly to explain natural events, such as floods and storms, that they cannot otherwise understand.
B  Communities admire and remember individuals by exaggerating real accomplishments into stories that celebrate valued human qualities.
C  Legends survive only when the people they describe perform feats that no ordinary person could ever accomplish.
D  The most lasting legends are the ones that historians are eventually able to prove actually happened.

Both passages are organized in the same overall structure. Select two sentences that show how the two passages use a PARALLEL structure to develop their legends.
A  Both passages open by establishing that the central figure was a real, historically documented person before describing the legendary feats.
B  Both passages devote their central paragraphs to a single dramatic rescue during a natural disaster that threatens others.
C  Both passages are arranged as a series of letters written by people who witnessed the hero's actions.
D  Both passages interrupt the action with long passages of dialogue between the hero and the people being rescued.
E  Both passages begin with the hero's death and then move backward in time to explain how the legend started.

In Text 1, paragraph 1, the narrator writes that 'stories about his accomplishments grew larger than life.' In this context, the phrase 'larger than life' is figurative language that means the stories became [ ▾ ].
physically heavier and harder for people to carry from town to town
exaggerated and more impressive than the true events actually were
shorter and easier for townspeople to remember accurately
frightening to the people who lived in the small mountain town

Which detail provides the STRONGEST evidence that the people who tell Captain Elena's legend value her as a leader, not merely as a skilled sailor?
A  "Historical documents show that Captain Elena guided trading vessels through difficult waters and earned a reputation as a skilled navigator" (paragraph 1)
B  "Sailors claimed she could study the clouds for a few moments and know exactly when severe weather would arrive" (paragraph 2)
C  "One by one, nearby ships followed the lantern's glow through the darkness" (paragraph 4)
D  "Captain Elena became a symbol of leadership and courage" (paragraph 7)

writingPrompt.text: Which legendary figure demonstrates greater courage: Iron Jack or Captain Elena? Write a multi-paragraph response in which you explain your opinion about which person is more deserving of admiration, using specific evidence from both passages to support your ideas. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passages; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: introduce a clear central idea; develop your opinion with relevant reasons and details; use evidence from the passages; and organize your ideas clearly.

passages.title: The Glass Forest
passages.text:
1 Long ago, in a kingdom surrounded by mountains, there stood a forest unlike any other. Instead of leaves, the trees were covered with crystal branches that sparkled in the sunlight. The forest was known as the Glass Forest, and according to legend, it had been enchanted centuries earlier by a powerful fairy. Travelers journeyed from distant lands to see its beauty, but few ventured far beyond its edge.

2 In a village near the forest lived a girl named Clara. She had grown up listening to stories about the enchanted trees and the treasures supposedly hidden among them. Some villagers claimed that golden birds nested in the highest branches. Others believed a magical spring flowed through the center of the forest. Although Clara enjoyed the stories, she had never explored the forest herself.

3 One winter, a fierce storm swept across the kingdom. Strong winds damaged homes, blocked roads, and destroyed crops. When the storm finally passed, the villagers discovered that the town's waterwheel had been badly damaged. Without it, the mill could not grind grain, and many families worried about how they would prepare food during the coming months.

4 The village leaders searched for solutions but found none. Replacement parts would take weeks to arrive from the capital. As concern spread through the village, Clara remembered one of the stories she had heard as a child. According to legend, a magical spring deep within the Glass Forest possessed water that could strengthen anything it touched.

5 Most villagers dismissed the tale as folklore, but Clara could not stop thinking about it. If the story was true, perhaps the spring could help repair the waterwheel. After several days of consideration, she packed supplies and set off toward the forest.

6 The deeper Clara traveled, the stranger the forest became. Crystal branches chimed softly whenever the wind passed through them. Sunlight reflected from thousands of surfaces, filling the woods with shifting colors. At times, Clara struggled to determine whether she was following a path or a reflection of one.

7 Near sunset, she reached a clearing where a stream of silver water flowed over smooth stones. Beside the stream stood an elderly woman wearing a cloak decorated with tiny glass leaves.

8 "You have traveled far," the woman said. "What brings you here?"

9 Clara explained the village's problem. The woman listened carefully before dipping a small bottle into the stream.

10 "This water cannot solve every problem," the woman said. "But it can help those who are willing to help others."

11 Clara thanked her and hurried home. When she returned, the villagers used the water while repairing the damaged wheel. Whether through magic, skill, or determination, the repairs succeeded. Within days, the mill was operating again.

12 Years later, people continued to debate whether the water had truly been magical. Some believed the spring possessed extraordinary powers. Others argued that Clara's courage and the villagers' hard work had made the difference. Regardless of the explanation, the story became one of the kingdom's most beloved fairy tales.

passages.title: The Moonstone Tower
passages.text:
1 In another kingdom, beyond a sea of rolling hills, stood a tower built from pale stones that seemed to glow beneath the moonlight. The structure was known as Moonstone Tower, and local legends claimed that it contained a magical gem capable of granting wisdom to anyone who found it.

2 A young prince named Rowan had heard the stories since childhood. Unlike many people, however, he was less interested in treasure than in solving problems. As he grew older, Rowan became known for asking questions and seeking advice from people throughout the kingdom.

3 One summer, a disagreement arose between several farming villages. A long drought had reduced the water supply, and each village believed it deserved a larger share of the remaining water. Arguments became increasingly heated, and some people worried that the conflict would divide the region.

4 The king sent advisors to negotiate, but progress was slow. Frustrated by the situation, Rowan decided to seek the legendary moonstone. If the stories were true, perhaps the gem could provide the wisdom needed to resolve the dispute.

5 Rowan's journey took him across hills, forests, and rivers. Eventually, he arrived at Moonstone Tower. Inside, he climbed winding staircases and passed through silent chambers illuminated by silver light.

6 At the top of the tower, Rowan discovered a single room containing a stone pedestal. Resting upon it was a smooth crystal that glowed softly beneath an opening in the ceiling.

7 Excited, Rowan stepped forward and picked up the crystal. For several moments, nothing happened. Then he noticed words carved into the pedestal.

8 Wisdom is not something that can be given. It must be earned by listening, learning, and understanding others.

9 Rowan read the message several times. At first, he felt disappointed. The crystal had not revealed a magical solution. It had not answered his questions or solved the kingdom's problems.

10 However, as he traveled home, he began thinking differently about the message. Instead of searching for a quick answer, he visited each village involved in the dispute. He listened to farmers, families, and community leaders. Gradually, he gained a clearer understanding of their concerns.

11 Months later, Rowan proposed a plan that balanced the needs of all the villages. Although not everyone received exactly what they wanted, the agreement prevented further conflict and helped the communities work together during the drought.

12 Some people believed the moonstone had granted Rowan wisdom after all. Others argued that the real lesson of the fairy tale was that wisdom comes from effort rather than magic. Whatever the truth, the story continued to be shared throughout the kingdom for generations.

questions.text:
Which statement best expresses a theme developed in BOTH "The Glass Forest" and "The Moonstone Tower"?
A  Genuine achievement comes from a person's own effort and choices, not from magic alone.
B  Powerful objects always reward those who are brave enough to search for them.
C  Ancient legends usually turn out to be false once people investigate them.
D  Leaders should solve problems for their communities rather than expecting ordinary people to act.

Which detail from "The Moonstone Tower" provides the STRONGEST evidence that Rowan resolved the dispute through his own actions rather than through the crystal's magic?
A  "He listened to farmers, families, and community leaders. Gradually, he gained a clearer understanding of their concerns." (paragraph 10)
B  "Resting upon it was a smooth crystal that glowed softly beneath an opening in the ceiling." (paragraph 6)
C  "Excited, Rowan stepped forward and picked up the crystal. For several moments, nothing happened." (paragraph 7)
D  "Some people believed the moonstone had granted Rowan wisdom after all." (paragraph 12)

Read this sentence from paragraph 6 of "The Glass Forest": "Crystal branches chimed softly whenever the wind passed through them." The word "chimed" suggests that the branches [ ▾ ].
produce gentle, bell-like musical sounds
snap and break easily in strong wind
make a harsh, jarring noise that startles Clara
remain completely silent as the wind blows

Select two ways the structures of "The Glass Forest" and "The Moonstone Tower" are SIMILAR.
A  Each story sends a single main character on a journey to find a legendary magical object that might solve a community's problem.
B  Each story ends with people debating whether the magic was real or whether human effort caused the outcome.
C  Each story is told from the first-person point of view of the character who seeks the magical object.
D  Each story interrupts its plot with long flashbacks that explain how the magical object was first created.
E  Each story concludes by revealing that the legendary object was a fake all along.

writingPrompt.text: Which character learns the more important lesson: Clara in "The Glass Forest" or Rowan in "The Moonstone Tower"? Write a multi-paragraph response in which you explain your opinion about which lesson is more valuable, using specific evidence from both passages to support your ideas. Manage your time carefully so that you can: review the passages; plan your response; write your response; and revise and edit your response. Be sure to: introduce a clear central idea; develop your opinion with relevant reasons and details; use evidence from the passages; and organize your ideas clearly.

passages.title: The Championship Clock
passages.text:
1 The clock above the gymnasium scoreboard showed 6:15 when Maya stepped onto the basketball court for practice. The championship game was only three days away, and excitement buzzed throughout the school. Posters decorated the hallways, teachers discussed the upcoming game during class, and students wore the team colors whenever they could. For Maya, however, the excitement came with pressure. As the team's starting point guard, she knew many people expected her to lead the team to victory.

2 Coach Daniels blew his whistle and gathered the players at center court. "We've worked hard all season," he said. "Talent matters, but teamwork matters more. Remember that when the game gets difficult." The players nodded before spreading out for drills. Maya listened carefully, but her thoughts kept drifting elsewhere. She had spent the previous week practicing her shooting and ball-handling skills whenever she had free time. Secretly, she hoped to score more points than anyone else during the championship game.

3 Later that afternoon, the team scrimmaged against a group of older students. Maya played aggressively, driving toward the basket whenever she had the opportunity. Several times she ignored open teammates in order to take difficult shots herself. Although she scored often, the team struggled to keep possession of the ball. By the end of practice, Coach Daniels looked concerned.

4 "Maya, stay for a moment," he said as the other players headed toward the locker room.

5 Maya approached nervously. "Did I do something wrong?"

6 "You're a talented player," Coach Daniels replied. "But basketball isn't won by one person. Right now, you're trying to do everything yourself."

7 Maya frowned. "I just want to help the team win."

8 "I know," said Coach Daniels. "But helping the team doesn't always mean taking the shot. Sometimes it means trusting someone else."

9 Maya thought about his words during the next few days. She understood what he meant, but she also believed she gave the team its best chance to succeed. After all, she had scored the most points all season. If the championship game was close, shouldn't the ball be in her hands?

10 Friday evening arrived faster than expected. The gymnasium filled with students, families, and community members. The noise grew louder as the game began. For most of the first half, Maya played exactly as she always had. She took nearly every shot she could, and although she scored several baskets, the opposing team remained close.

11 Midway through the fourth quarter, the score was tied. Both teams fought for every rebound and loose ball. The crowd cheered whenever either team gained possession. With less than a minute remaining, Maya stole a pass and raced down the court.

12 As she approached the basket, she noticed two defenders moving toward her. For a brief moment, she considered taking the shot anyway. Then she saw her teammate Jordan standing near the corner. Jordan had been open several times throughout the game but had received very few passes.

13 Maya hesitated. Passing the ball meant giving up her chance to score the winning basket. If Jordan missed, the game could continue into overtime—or worse, the other team might gain possession and score.

14 The clock continued ticking.

15 Remembering Coach Daniels's advice, Maya passed the ball.

16 Jordan caught it cleanly. The gym seemed to fall silent as he released the shot. The ball arced high through the air before swishing through the net.

17 The crowd erupted.

18 The opposing team failed to score before time expired, and Maya's team won the championship.

19 After the game, students rushed onto the court to celebrate. Reporters from the local newspaper interviewed several players. Many people congratulated Jordan for making the winning shot.

20 For a moment, Maya felt disappointed. She imagined how exciting it would have been to score the final basket herself.

21 Then she looked around the gym. Her teammates were smiling, laughing, and celebrating together. Coach Daniels caught her eye and nodded.

22 Slowly, Maya smiled too.

23 She realized the championship had not been won because of one player. It had been won because everyone contributed. The pass she made would never appear on a highlight poster, but it had helped her team succeed when it mattered most.

24 As the team gathered for a final photograph beneath the scoreboard, Maya glanced at the championship clock one last time. The score would eventually be forgotten, and new seasons would begin. Yet the lesson she learned about trust and teamwork would stay with her long after the game ended.

questions.text:
Which incident most directly causes Maya to change how she plays during the championship game?
A  Coach Daniels's reminder at center court that "teamwork matters more" (paragraph 2)
B  The private conversation in which Coach Daniels tells Maya she is "trying to do everything yourself" (paragraph 6)
C  Maya stealing a pass and racing down the court with less than a minute left (paragraph 11)
D  The crowd cheering whenever either team gained possession (paragraph 11)

Which statement best expresses a theme developed across the story?
A  Individual talent is what ultimately determines whether a team wins.
B  True success often comes from trusting others rather than acting alone.
C  Winning a championship matters more than any lesson learned along the way.
D  Athletes should ignore their coaches when they believe they know best.

Select two pieces of evidence that BEST show Maya's decision to pass required her to overcome a personal desire.
A  "Secretly, she hoped to score more points than anyone else during the championship game." (paragraph 2)
B  "Passing the ball meant giving up her chance to score the winning basket." (paragraph 13)
C  "Jordan caught it cleanly." (paragraph 16)
D  "The opposing team failed to score before time expired, and Maya's team won the championship." (paragraph 18)
E  "For a moment, Maya felt disappointed. She imagined how exciting it would have been to score the final basket herself." (paragraph 20)

Read this sentence from paragraph 17: "The crowd erupted." As used in this sentence, the word "erupted" most nearly means the crowd [ ▾ ].
suddenly burst into loud cheering
slowly stood up to leave
grew quiet and tense
argued angrily with one another
